title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services relating to "Removal of the Automatic Extension of Employment Authorization Documents".
ai_summary: Congress is voting to cancel a federal rule issued by U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) that eliminated the automatic extension of Employment Authorization Documents (EADs). EADs are work permits that allow certain non-citizens — such as people with pending immigration applications — to legally work in the United States. Under the rule being canceled, those automatic extensions would have been removed, meaning workers whose permits were expiring could have lost their ability to work legally while waiting for their renewal applications to be processed. If this resolution passes and is signed into law, that USCIS rule would be wiped out and have no legal effect, which would restore the prior practice of automatically extending work permits during the renewal process.
ai_headline: Senate moves to restore automatic work permit renewals
last_action_text: Motion to proceed to consideration of measure rejected in Senate by Yea-Nay Vote. 47 - 50. Record Vote Number: 111. (CR S2106)

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting to cancel a federal rule that would have ended automatic work permit extensions for certain non-citizens awaiting renewals.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would cancel a rule issued by U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) that removed automatic extensions of work permits, called Employment Authorization Documents or EADs. If the resolution becomes law, that rule would have no legal effect and the prior practice of automatically extending work permits during the renewal process would be restored.
ai_sections.who: This affects certain non-citizens, such as people with pending immigration applications, who rely on EADs to work legally in the United States.
ai_sections.why: Without the automatic extension, workers whose permits were expiring could lose the legal right to work while waiting for their renewal applications to be processed. Canceling the rule would mean those workers keep their work authorization during that waiting period.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Environmental Protection Agency relating to "Air Plan Disapproval; Colorado; Regional Haze Plan for the Second Implementation Period".
ai_summary: This resolution would cancel a rule that the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) issued in January 2026, which rejected Colorado's plan for reducing haze and improving visibility in national parks and wilderness areas during what regulators call the "second implementation period." If Congress passes this resolution, the EPA's rejection would be wiped out and treated as if it never happened. That would effectively restore Colorado's original state haze-reduction plan, preventing the EPA from forcing Colorado to adopt stricter or different pollution controls under that rejected plan. The people most directly affected would be Colorado residents, visitors to the state's protected natural areas, and businesses or power plants that might have faced new pollution requirements under any replacement plan the EPA could have imposed.
ai_headline: Senate moves to restore Colorado's EPA haze plan
last_action_text: Motion to proceed to consideration of measure rejected in Senate by Yea-Nay Vote. 46 - 52. Record Vote Number: 110. (CR S2106)

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to cancel the EPA's rejection of Colorado's plan to reduce haze in national parks and wilderness areas.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would cancel an EPA rule from January 2026 that rejected Colorado's haze-reduction plan. If passed, the EPA's rejection would be erased and treated as if it never happened, restoring Colorado's original state plan.
ai_sections.who: Colorado residents and visitors to the state's protected natural areas would be most directly affected. Businesses and power plants that could have faced new pollution requirements under any EPA replacement plan would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: If the resolution passes, Colorado keeps its own haze-reduction plan and the EPA cannot force the state to adopt stricter or different pollution controls under the rejected rule. If it does not pass, the EPA's rejection stands and a replacement plan with different requirements could be imposed.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Debt Collection Practices (Regulation F); Deceptive and Unfair Collection of Medical Debt".
ai_summary: Congress is voting on whether to block a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) that canceled protections against unfair and deceptive medical debt collection. In October 2024, the CFPB had finalized a rule that would have placed new limits on how debt collectors can pursue people over medical bills; then in May 2025, the CFPB withdrew that rule, effectively scrapping those protections. This resolution would nullify the CFPB's withdrawal, meaning the agency's cancellation of the medical debt collection rule would have no legal effect. If passed, the practical result would be that the original 2024 medical debt collection protections would be restored. This affects ordinary Americans who owe medical debt, as well as the debt collection companies that contact them.
ai_headline: Senate moves to restore CFPB medical debt rules
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 393.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to restore 2024 rules limiting unfair medical debt collection practices.
ai_sections.what: The CFPB created rules in 2024 to limit how debt collectors can go after people for medical bills. The CFPB then canceled those rules in May 2025. This resolution would undo that cancellation and bring the 2024 protections back.
ai_sections.who: This affects people who owe medical debt and the debt collection companies that contact them.
ai_sections.why: If this resolution passes, the 2024 medical debt collection rules would be restored and legally in effect. If it does not pass, the CFPB's cancellation stands and those rules remain scrapped.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Fair Credit Reporting; Permissible Purposes for Furnishing, Using, and Obtaining Consumer Reports".
ai_summary: This resolution would block a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) in May 2025 to cancel a rule it had originally proposed in 2022 about credit reports. That 2022 rule was intended to set clearer limits on who is allowed to access, share, or use information from a person's credit report and for what reasons. By passing this resolution, Congress would effectively say that the CFPB's cancellation of that rule has no legal effect — meaning the original 2022 rulemaking process could not simply be erased. This would affect everyday Americans who have credit reports, as well as lenders, employers, landlords, and other businesses that request or use credit information, since the question of what counts as a legitimate reason to access someone's credit file would remain an open regulatory matter.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets CFPB's scrapped credit report rule
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 394.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to block the CFPB from canceling a 2022 rule about who can access your credit report.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would cancel the CFPB's May 2025 decision to withdraw a rule it had proposed in 2022. That 2022 rule was meant to set clearer limits on who can access, share, or use information from a person's credit report and why. If this resolution passes, the CFPB's withdrawal of that rule would have no legal effect.
ai_sections.who: This affects everyday Americans who have credit reports. It also affects lenders, employers, landlords, and other businesses that request or use credit information.
ai_sections.why: The question of what counts as a legitimate reason to access someone's credit file would remain an open regulatory matter. The rules around credit report access would not be considered settled.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Truth in Lending (Regulation Z); Consumer Protections for Home Sales Financed Under Contracts for Deed".
ai_summary: This resolution is Congress voting to cancel a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) in 2025 to withdraw consumer protections for people who buy homes through "contracts for deed." A contract for deed is a type of home sale arrangement where the buyer makes payments directly to the seller over time but does not receive the legal title to the home until all payments are complete, which is common in lower-income communities. The original 2024 rule the CFPB withdrew would have required sellers using these contracts to provide buyers with clear, standardized disclosures about loan costs and terms — the same kinds of protections that apply to traditional mortgages under the Truth in Lending Act. By disapproving the CFPB's withdrawal, this resolution would essentially block that withdrawal, meaning the 2024 consumer protections would be restored and treated as still in effect. The people most directly affected are home buyers who use contracts for deed and the sellers or investors who offer them.
ai_headline: Senate moves to restore CFPB contract-for-deed rules
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 396.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting to restore disclosure protections for home buyers who purchase through contracts for deed after the CFPB removed them.
ai_sections.what: This resolution cancels a 2025 CFPB decision that had removed consumer protections for people buying homes through contracts for deed. If the resolution passes, the 2024 rules requiring sellers to give buyers clear, standardized information about loan costs and terms would be restored.
ai_sections.who: The people most directly affected are home buyers who use contracts for deed, especially in lower-income communities, and the sellers or investors who offer them. Contracts for deed are arrangements where the buyer pays the seller over time but does not receive the legal title to the home until all payments are finished.
ai_sections.why: Without these protections, buyers in contract-for-deed arrangements may not receive the same standardized cost and terms disclosures that apply to traditional mortgages. Restoring the rule would bring those disclosure requirements back into effect.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to withdrawal of the rule relating to "Consumer Financial Protection Circular 2024-05: Improper Overdraft Opt-In Practices".
ai_summary: Congress is voting on whether to block a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) — the federal agency that oversees banks and financial products — to cancel a consumer protection guidance it had previously issued. That original guidance, from 2024, addressed "improper overdraft opt-in practices," meaning it warned banks against signing customers up for overdraft coverage in ways that are misleading or without proper consent, which can lead to unexpected fees. The CFPB later withdrew that guidance in May 2025, and this resolution says that withdrawal should have no force or effect — in other words, if passed, it would reverse the CFPB's decision to cancel the guidance. This resolution affects everyday bank customers, particularly those who have checking accounts and may be charged overdraft fees, as well as the banks and financial institutions whose overdraft enrollment practices would again be subject to scrutiny under the original 2024 guidance.
ai_headline: Senate bill would restore CFPB overdraft fee guidance
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 386.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to restore a 2024 rule warning banks against signing customers up for overdraft coverage without proper consent.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would reverse a May 2025 decision by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to cancel its own 2024 guidance on overdraft enrollment. That original guidance warned banks against signing customers up for overdraft coverage in misleading ways or without proper consent. If this resolution passes, the cancellation would have no force or effect, and the 2024 guidance would be restored.
ai_sections.who: This affects everyday bank customers with checking accounts who may be charged overdraft fees. It also affects banks and financial institutions whose overdraft enrollment practices would again be reviewed under the 2024 guidance.
ai_sections.why: Without the guidance in place, banks face less federal scrutiny over how they enroll customers in overdraft coverage. If the resolution passes, the original rules around consent and enrollment practices would apply again.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of  title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Fair Credit Reporting; Name-Only Matching Procedures".
ai_summary: Congress is voting to block a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) in May 2025 to cancel a consumer protection rule about credit reporting. The original rule, finalized in 2021, addressed a practice called "name-only matching," which refers to how credit reporting companies link negative financial information to a person's credit file — specifically, when a match is made using only a person's name rather than additional identifying details. If this resolution passes, the CFPB's cancellation of that rule would be thrown out and have no legal effect, meaning the original 2021 rule would effectively be restored. This primarily affects everyday consumers whose credit reports could be impacted by inaccurate information being attached to their files, as well as the credit reporting companies and lenders that use those reports.
ai_headline: Senate moves to restore CFPB credit report accuracy rule
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 392.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting to undo the CFPB's 2025 cancellation of a 2021 rule protecting consumers from inaccurate credit report matches.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would block a May 2025 decision by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to cancel a 2021 consumer protection rule. That rule addressed name-only matching, a practice where credit reporting companies link negative financial information to someone's credit file using only their name, without checking other identifying details. If this resolution passes, the cancellation would have no legal effect and the original 2021 rule would be restored.
ai_sections.who: Everyday consumers whose credit reports could be affected by inaccurate information are most directly involved. Credit reporting companies and lenders that use credit reports are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without the 2021 rule in place, credit reporting companies could continue matching negative financial information to a person's file based on name alone, which can lead to inaccurate credit reports. If this resolution passes, that matching practice would again be regulated under the restored rule.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Fair Credit Reporting; Background Screening".
ai_summary: Congress is voting to block a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) that canceled a rule about background screening and credit reporting. The original rule, issued in January 2024, set new standards for how background check companies must handle consumer credit information; the CFPB later reversed course in May 2025 and withdrew that rule. This resolution says that withdrawal is invalid and has no legal effect, which would essentially restore the original background screening rule as if the CFPB had never canceled it. The people most directly affected are consumers who undergo background checks — such as job applicants or renters — as well as the companies that conduct and use those background checks.
ai_headline: Senate bill would restore CFPB background check rule
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 389.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting to restore a 2024 rule on background checks that the CFPB canceled in 2025.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would block the CFPB's May 2025 decision to cancel a rule about background screening and credit reporting. If passed, the cancellation would have no legal effect, restoring the original January 2024 rule as if it had never been withdrawn.
ai_sections.who: People who go through background checks, like job applicants and renters, are directly affected. So are the companies that run and use background checks.
ai_sections.why: If the resolution passes, the 2024 standards for how background check companies handle consumer credit information would be back in force. If it does not pass, the CFPB's withdrawal stands and those standards remain canceled.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Truth in Lending (Regulation Z); Consumer Credit Offered to Borrowers in Advance of Expected Receipt of Compensation for Work".
ai_summary: This resolution is Congress voting to reject a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB). Specifically, the CFPB had previously created a rule requiring that certain short-term loans — where workers borrow money in advance of an expected paycheck — be treated as consumer credit under federal lending law, which would have required lenders to provide borrowers with clear disclosures about costs and terms. The CFPB then issued a second rule in May 2025 withdrawing (canceling) that original protection. This resolution disapproves that cancellation, meaning that if it passes, the CFPB's decision to withdraw the rule would be thrown out and have no legal effect — potentially restoring the original protections. The people most directly affected are workers who use earned wage advance or paycheck advance products, as well as the companies that offer those financial products.
ai_headline: Senate moves to restore CFPB paycheck advance protections
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 400.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to restore federal disclosure rules for paycheck advance loans, after the CFPB canceled those rules in 2025.
ai_sections.what: This resolution is Congress voting to reject the CFPB's 2025 decision to cancel a rule about paycheck advance loans. That original rule required lenders to clearly disclose costs and terms to borrowers. If this resolution passes, the CFPB's cancellation would have no legal effect, and the original rule could be restored.
ai_sections.who: Workers who use earned wage advance or paycheck advance products are most directly affected. Companies that offer those financial products are also affected.
ai_sections.why: If the resolution passes, lenders offering paycheck advance products may again be required to provide clear disclosures about loan costs and terms. If it does not pass, the CFPB's cancellation of those protections would remain in place.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Examinations for Risks to Active-Duty Servicemembers and Their Covered Dependents".
ai_summary: Congress is voting to block a decision made by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) in May 2025 to cancel a rule that had directed the agency to conduct special examinations of financial companies to check whether they were treating active-duty military servicemembers and their families fairly. If this resolution passes, the CFPB's cancellation would be wiped out and have no legal effect, meaning the original examination rule from 2021 would be restored. This directly affects active-duty military personnel and their covered dependents, who would once again have the protection of a federal agency actively monitoring whether lenders and financial companies are treating them properly. It also affects financial institutions that serve the military community, which could once again be subject to those targeted CFPB examinations.
ai_headline: Senate bill seeks to restore CFPB military lending oversight
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 388.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting to restore a federal rule that required financial companies serving military families to undergo special oversight examinations.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would cancel a May 2025 decision by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to drop a rule requiring special examinations of financial companies that serve military communities. If the resolution passes, the original 2021 rule would be restored and the CFPB's cancellation would have no legal effect.
ai_sections.who: Active-duty military servicemembers and their covered dependents would again have federal oversight protecting them from unfair treatment by lenders. Financial institutions that serve the military community could again be subject to targeted CFPB examinations.
ai_sections.why: If the resolution passes, the CFPB would be required to resume conducting those special examinations. If it does not pass, the 2021 rule remains canceled and those examinations would not take place.

title: SHARKED Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Secretary of Commerce to create a task force focused on "shark depredation" — meaning situations where sharks interfere with fishing by stealing or damaging fish caught on hooks or in nets. The task force would bring together representatives from regional fishery management councils, state fish and wildlife agencies, marine fisheries commissions, and shark researchers to improve coordination, identify research priorities, and develop strategies for reducing harmful encounters between sharks and people. The group would also create educational materials to help fishermen change their behavior to minimize these interactions. Every two years, the task force must report its findings to Congress, and it would automatically shut down no later than seven years after it is created. This bill primarily affects commercial and recreational fishermen, coastal state agencies, and federal fisheries managers, while also adding shark depredation research as an eligible topic under existing federal fisheries research programs.
ai_headline: Commerce Dept. would lead new shark depredation task force
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 349.

ai_sections.tldr: The SHARKED Act would create a federal task force to study and reduce shark interference with fishing, reporting to Congress every two years.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the Secretary of Commerce to form a task force focused on shark depredation, which is when sharks steal or damage fish caught on hooks or in nets. The task force would coordinate research, develop strategies to reduce harmful encounters between sharks and people, and produce educational materials for fishermen. It would automatically end no later than seven years after it is created.
ai_sections.who: The task force would include representatives from regional fishery management councils, state fish and wildlife agencies, marine fisheries commissions, and shark researchers. Commercial and recreational fishermen, coastal state agencies, and federal fisheries managers would be primarily affected.
ai_sections.why: Shark depredation creates friction between sharks and fishing operations, and there is currently no dedicated federal body coordinating research or responses to these encounters. This task force would fill that gap and add shark depredation as an eligible research topic under existing federal fisheries programs.

title: Fallen Servicemembers Religious Heritage Restoration Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the American Battle Monuments Commission — the federal agency that oversees U.S. military cemeteries abroad — to create a program that identifies Jewish American servicemembers from World War I and World War II who were mistakenly buried under Latin Cross grave markers instead of markers reflecting their Jewish faith. Congress estimates roughly 900 such servicemembers are affected. Over five years, the Commission would hire a qualified nonprofit organization each year, paying it $500,000 annually, to find these mismarked graves and reach out to the servicemembers' surviving family members and descendants. The bill also makes a minor adjustment to a deadline in existing veterans' pension law, moving a cutoff date from November 2031 to January 2032.
ai_headline: Bill would find Jewish WWI and WWII graves mismarked with crosses
last_action_text: Read twice. Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 292.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would launch a 5-year program to find roughly 900 Jewish WWII and WWI veterans buried under the wrong grave markers abroad.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a program to find Jewish American servicemembers from World War I and World War II who were mistakenly buried under Latin Cross grave markers instead of Jewish markers. The program would run for five years. The bill also moves a minor deadline in veterans' pension law from November 2031 to January 2032.
ai_sections.who: The American Battle Monuments Commission, the federal agency that manages U.S. military cemeteries overseas, would run the program. Each year, the Commission would hire a qualified nonprofit organization to locate mismarked graves and contact surviving family members and descendants.
ai_sections.why: Congress estimates around 900 servicemembers may be buried under markers that do not reflect their faith. Finding these graves would allow families to be notified and the record to be corrected.
ai_sections.cost: The Commission would pay a nonprofit $500,000 per year for five years to carry out this work.

title: Shutdown Fairness Act
ai_summary: During a government shutdown — when Congress has not passed a spending bill to fund federal agencies — this bill would automatically keep paychecks flowing to federal workers, certain government contractors, and military members, rather than making them wait until after a shutdown ends. It covers all affected workers whether or not they were required to keep showing up to work during the shutdown (so-called "excepted" employees) or were sent home unpaid (furloughed employees). For shutdowns that have already begun before the bill becomes law, agencies would be required to pay workers within 7 days of the bill's passage; for any future shutdowns, workers would be paid on their normal, regular payday schedules. The money for these paychecks would come directly from the U.S. Treasury and can only be used for that purpose — it cannot be redirected to anything else — and once Congress passes a real funding bill, those expenses would be charged against it.
ai_headline: Federal workers would keep pay during shutdowns
last_action_text: Read the second time. Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 267.

ai_sections.tldr: During a government shutdown, this bill would keep federal workers' paychecks going on their normal schedule instead of making them wait.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires that federal workers, certain government contractors, and military members continue to be paid on their regular payday schedule during a government shutdown. For shutdowns already underway when the bill becomes law, agencies must pay workers within 7 days of passage. Once Congress passes a real funding bill, those paycheck costs are charged against it.
ai_sections.who: The bill covers all federal workers affected by a shutdown, both those required to keep working and those sent home without pay.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, federal workers and military members may go without pay for the full length of a shutdown and receive it only after it ends. This bill changes when the money is delivered, not whether workers are ultimately owed it.
ai_sections.cost: Paycheck funds would come directly from the U.S. Treasury and can only be used for that purpose. The money cannot be redirected to other uses.

title: PUBLIC Lands Act
ai_summary: This bill sets aside and protects large areas of federal land in California — primarily in the northwestern, central coast, and San Gabriel Mountains regions — by officially designating new wilderness areas, expanding existing ones, and protecting certain rivers. It directs federal land managers to carry out forest restoration work (such as reducing wildfire risk, removing debris from illegal marijuana grows, and replanting damaged ecosystems), and it creates or studies new recreational trails for hikers, mountain bikers, and off-highway vehicle users. The bill affects people who live near, work on, or visit these federal lands — including local communities, outdoor recreationists, ranchers with existing grazing rights, tribal nations, and the federal agencies (primarily the Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management) responsible for managing the land. Wilderness designations mean the newly protected areas would be closed to mining, new roads, and most motorized uses, while restoration and recreation provisions open up new opportunities for public enjoyment and ecological recovery.
ai_headline: Senate bill adds wilderness protections to California federal lands
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 525.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1776 protects federal land in California as wilderness and directs agencies to restore forests and create new trails.
ai_sections.what: This bill officially designates new wilderness areas and expands existing ones on federal land in northwestern California, the central coast, and the San Gabriel Mountains. It also protects certain rivers and directs land managers to do forest restoration work, including reducing wildfire risk, cleaning up illegal marijuana grow sites, and replanting damaged land. The bill also creates or studies new trails for hikers, mountain bikers, and off-highway vehicle users.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects local communities, outdoor recreationists, ranchers with existing grazing rights, and tribal nations connected to these lands. The Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management are the main agencies responsible for carrying out the work.
ai_sections.why: Wilderness designation means the newly protected areas would be closed to mining, new road construction, and most motorized uses. Restoration and recreation provisions would open up new opportunities for public use and ecological recovery in affected areas.

title: A bill to transfer administrative jurisdiction over certain parcels of Federal land in Harpers Ferry, West Virginia, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill swaps management responsibility over several pieces of federal land in Harpers Ferry, West Virginia, between two federal agencies — no private land is bought or sold. About 25 acres currently managed by the National Park Service will be handed over to U.S. Customs and Border Protection to be used as part of its Advanced Training Center, while three separate parcels totaling about 71.5 acres currently held by Customs and Border Protection will go in the other direction, becoming part of Harpers Ferry National Historical Park. Neither agency pays the other anything for these transfers. If Customs and Border Protection ever decides it no longer needs those 25 acres for its training center, the land would automatically return to the National Park Service to be folded back into the park.
ai_headline: Federal land in Harpers Ferry shifts between NPS and CBP
last_action_text: Passed Senate without amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S2100; text: CR S2100)

ai_sections.tldr: Two federal agencies swap land in Harpers Ferry, WV: ~25 acres go to Border Protection, ~71.5 acres return to the National Park.
ai_sections.what: This bill moves management of several federal land parcels in Harpers Ferry, West Virginia, between two federal agencies. About 25 acres would shift from the National Park Service to U.S. Customs and Border Protection for use at its Advanced Training Center. About 71.5 acres would move the other way, becoming part of Harpers Ferry National Historical Park.
ai_sections.who: The two agencies involved are the National Park Service and U.S. Customs and Border Protection. No private landowners are involved in any of these transfers.
ai_sections.why: Each agency would end up managing land that fits its current operations. If Customs and Border Protection ever stops using its 25 acres for training, that land automatically returns to the National Park Service.

title: SBA Fraud Enforcement Extension Act
ai_summary: This bill gives the federal government more time — up to 10 years from when the fraudulent act occurred — to bring criminal charges or civil lawsuits against people who committed fraud in two pandemic-era relief programs: the Shuttered Venue Operators Grant program (which helped music venues, theaters, and similar businesses) and the Restaurant Revitalization Fund. Previously, standard fraud cases often had a shorter window for prosecution, so this extension is meant to ensure that people who cheated these programs can still be held accountable even if the fraud is discovered years later. The bill affects anyone who may have illegally obtained grant money from either of these programs — for example, by submitting false information on applications or misusing funds. It also requires the U.S. Attorney General to report to Congress every 90 days for five years on how the Justice Department is pursuing these fraud cases, including how many investigations and prosecutions have been opened, how much money has been recovered, and where cases are coming from across the country.
ai_headline: Senate bill extends fraud deadlines for pandemic venue grants
last_action_text: Passed Senate with an amendment by Unanimous Consent. (text of amendment in the nature of a substitute: CR S2108)

ai_sections.tldr: Federal prosecutors get up to 10 years to charge pandemic relief fraud in two specific grant programs.
ai_sections.what: This bill extends the time the federal government has to file criminal charges or civil lawsuits against people who committed fraud in the Shuttered Venue Operators Grant program or the Restaurant Revitalization Fund. The government now has up to 10 years from when the fraud happened to take action. The Attorney General must also report to Congress every 90 days for five years on how those fraud cases are going.
ai_sections.who: This affects anyone who may have illegally obtained grant money from either program, for example by submitting false information or misusing funds. It also involves the Justice Department and Congress, which will receive regular updates on investigations and prosecutions.
ai_sections.why: Without this extension, some fraud cases discovered years after the fact could have fallen outside the standard window for prosecution. The reporting requirement keeps Congress informed on how many cases are being pursued, how much money has been recovered, and where cases are coming from across the country.

title: PIPELINE Safety Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill updates federal rules and funding for keeping America's gas and hazardous liquid pipelines safe, affecting pipeline companies, government safety regulators, and the communities that live near pipelines. It renews and increases funding for the federal Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration (PHMSA) through 2030, raises the maximum fines companies can face for safety violations, and requires new or updated safety rules covering a wide range of issues — including how tanks and rights-of-way are inspected, how geological hazards like landslides are addressed, and how emerging technologies like drones can be used in inspections. It creates a new voluntary, confidential system where pipeline operators, workers, and safety experts can share safety data and near-miss information with each other without fear that the data will be used against them in lawsuits or enforcement actions. It also adds new consumer and public protections, such as stronger whistleblower rights for pipeline workers who report safety concerns, a public alert notification system for pipeline incidents, new studies on hydrogen and carbon dioxide pipelines, and requirements for greater transparency about safety information.
ai_headline: Senate bill updates PHMSA pipeline safety rules through 2030
last_action_text: Passed Senate with an amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S2109-2121; text of amendment in the nature of a substitute: CR S2109-2121)

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 2975 updates pipeline safety rules, raises fines for violations, and renews federal funding through 2030.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates federal rules for keeping gas and hazardous liquid pipelines safe across the country. It requires new safety rules on topics like tank inspections, geological hazards such as landslides, and the use of drones. It also creates a confidential system for sharing safety data and near-miss reports, strengthens whistleblower protections, and sets up a public alert system for pipeline incidents.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects pipeline companies, federal safety regulators, pipeline workers, and communities that live near pipelines. The federal Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration, known as PHMSA, is the main agency involved.
ai_sections.why: If passed, pipeline companies would face stricter safety requirements and higher fines if they break the rules. Communities near pipelines would have more access to safety information and faster public alerts when something goes wrong.
ai_sections.cost: The bill renews and increases funding for PHMSA through 2030. It also raises the maximum fines that companies can face for safety violations, though specific dollar amounts are not included in the summary.
costChips: Higher max fines for safety violations

title: Expanding the Surety Bond Program Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill raises the maximum amount the federal government can guarantee on a surety bond — a type of insurance that helps contractors get hired for jobs — from $6.5 million to $18 million, making it easier for small businesses to compete for larger contracts. If the Small Business Administration (SBA) ever asks Congress for extra money to keep the program running, that $18 million cap automatically drops by one-third until the funding issue is resolved. The bill also allows up to 2% of the program's revolving fund each year to be used for the SBA's own costs of running the program, such as technology and outreach. The SBA will be required to submit an annual report to Congress on the program's finances and activity, and the Government Accountability Office must complete a separate review with recommendations for making the application process faster and less paperwork-heavy. Small businesses — particularly contractors seeking government or private construction and service contracts — and the surety companies that back them are most directly affected.
ai_headline: SBA surety bond guarantees would rise to $18 million
last_action_text: Passed Senate with an amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S2108-2109; text of amendment in the nature of a substitute: CR S2108-2109)

ai_sections.tldr: This bill raises the federal surety bond guarantee limit from $6.5 million to $18 million so small businesses can compete for bigger contracts.
ai_sections.what: This bill raises the maximum federal guarantee on a surety bond from $6.5 million to $18 million. If the SBA requests extra funding from Congress to keep the program running, that cap automatically drops by one-third until the issue is resolved. Up to 2% of the program's revolving fund can be used each year for the SBA's own costs, and both the SBA and the Government Accountability Office must produce reports on the program.
ai_sections.who: Small businesses that seek government or private construction and service contracts are most directly affected. Surety companies that back those contracts are also affected.
ai_sections.why: A higher guarantee limit changes which contracts small businesses are able to pursue. The automatic cap reduction and reporting requirements create oversight mechanisms tied to the program's financial condition.
ai_sections.cost: Up to 2% of the program's revolving fund may be used each year to cover the SBA's costs of running the program, such as technology and outreach.
costChips: Up to 2% of revolving fund used yearly
Covers SBA tech and outreach costs

title: Expanding Whistleblower Protections for Contractors Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill strengthens protections for people who report wrongdoing connected to federal government contracts and grants, making it harder for their employers to punish them for speaking up. It expands who is covered beyond just regular employees to include contractors, subcontractors, grant recipients, personal services contractors, former employees (if their protected activity happened before they were let go), and even some state and tribal governments that receive federal contracts or grants. A covered person is now also protected if they refuse to follow an order that would require them to break a law related to a federal contract or grant — not just if they report wrongdoing. The bill explicitly states that no executive branch official has the authority to ask a contractor or grantee to retaliate against a whistleblower, and investigators can recommend disciplinary action against any official who does so. Finally, the bill closes a loophole by making clear that these protections cannot be signed away through any employment agreement, policy, or pre-dispute arbitration clause.
ai_headline: Senate bill expands federal contractor whistleblower protections
last_action_text: Passed Senate with an amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S2100-2102; text: CR S2100-2101)

ai_sections.tldr: This bill protects more workers who report wrongdoing on federal contracts and bars employers from punishing them for speaking up.
ai_sections.what: This bill strengthens protections for people who report wrongdoing connected to federal contracts and grants. It also protects people who refuse an order that would require them to break a law tied to a federal contract or grant. No employment agreement, policy, or pre-dispute arbitration clause can take these protections away.
ai_sections.who: Coverage expands beyond regular employees to include contractors, subcontractors, grant recipients, personal services contractors, former employees, and some state and tribal governments that receive federal contracts or grants. No executive branch official may ask a contractor or grantee to retaliate against a whistleblower, and investigators can recommend disciplinary action against any official who does.
ai_sections.why: Without these changes, workers in contractor and grantee roles had fewer legal tools to push back if punished for reporting problems. The bill also addresses cases where protections were previously waived through employment agreements or arbitration clauses.

title: Nationwide Consumer and Fuel Retailer Choice Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill makes two main changes to federal rules about fuel. First, it allows gasoline blended with 10–15% ethanol (known as E15) to be sold year-round, including during summer months when it was previously restricted, by adjusting the rules around a fuel property called Reid Vapor Pressure that limits how much a fuel can evaporate in warm weather; it also requires the EPA to update labeling and underground storage tank rules within 18 months to make it easier for gas stations to sell E15. Second, it changes the Renewable Fuel Standard — the federal program that requires fuel refiners to blend a certain amount of renewable fuel like ethanol into their products — specifically for smaller refiners: starting in 2028, companies that refined no more than 75,000 barrels per day in 2025 get their blending obligations reduced by 75%, the existing system allowing small refineries to seek full exemptions is phased out after 2027, and a new limited exemption process is created for very small refineries (under 10,000 barrels per day) that can prove their renewable fuel costs put them at imminent risk of closing down. The bill primarily affects fuel retailers and gas stations (who gain more flexibility to sell E15 all year), ethanol producers (who benefit from expanded E15 sales), and small petroleum refiners (who face a restructured but reduced set of renewable fuel blending requirements).
ai_headline: Bill would allow year-round E15 sales, ease refinery quotas
last_action_text: Rule H. Res. 1224 passed House.

ai_sections.tldr: E15 ethanol fuel could be sold year-round at gas stations, and small refiners would owe less renewable fuel blending starting in 2028.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets gas stations sell E15 fuel — gasoline mixed with 10 to 15 percent ethanol — in summer months when it was previously banned. It also directs the EPA to update labeling and storage tank rules within 18 months. Separately, starting in 2028, small refiners that processed no more than 75,000 barrels per day in 2025 would see their renewable fuel blending requirements cut by 75%, and a new limited exemption would be available for the very smallest refiners under certain conditions.
ai_sections.who: Gas stations and fuel retailers gain more flexibility in what fuel they can sell year-round. Small petroleum refiners face a restructured set of renewable fuel requirements, and ethanol producers are affected by changes to how much E15 can be sold.
ai_sections.why: Allowing year-round E15 sales changes which fuels are available to drivers at the pump. Reducing blending requirements for small refiners changes how much renewable fuel those companies are required to mix into their products.

title: To expand the sharing of information with respect to suspected violations of intellectual property rights in trade.
ai_summary: When U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) reasonably suspects that imported goods may be violating intellectual property rights — such as being counterfeit — this bill expands what information CBP can share with the owners of those intellectual property rights. Specifically, CBP would now be allowed to share nonpublic information about the suspected goods that came from sources like online marketplaces, shipping and freight companies, or other parties involved in selling or importing the merchandise. The bill also requires CBP to notify the rights holder about exactly what information was shared. Additionally, CBP gains the ability to share information with a broader group of interested parties beyond those already listed in existing law. This primarily affects intellectual property owners (such as brands and manufacturers), customs authorities, online marketplaces, freight forwarders, and importers whose shipments may be flagged as potentially counterfeit or infringing.
ai_headline: CBP gains broader authority to share counterfeit goods data
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: CBP could share more private shipping data with brand owners when goods are suspected of being counterfeit.
ai_sections.what: This bill expands what information U.S. Customs and Border Protection can share with intellectual property owners when imported goods are suspected of being counterfeit or infringing. CBP could share nonpublic information gathered from online marketplaces, shipping companies, and other parties involved in the shipment. CBP would also be required to tell rights holders exactly what information was shared with them.
ai_sections.who: This affects intellectual property owners such as brands and manufacturers, customs authorities, online marketplaces, freight forwarders, and importers whose shipments may be flagged as potentially counterfeit.
ai_sections.why: Rights holders would receive more detailed information about suspected infringing shipments than they currently do. Importers and shipping parties should be aware that nonpublic information they provide could be shared more broadly under this bill.

title: Maverick Act
ai_summary: This bill allows the U.S. Navy to give three specific surplus F-14D Tomcat fighter jets — aircraft that are no longer needed for military use and have had all their combat capabilities removed — to the U.S. Space and Rocket Center in Huntsville, Alabama, completely free of charge. The Navy is not required to fix up the planes beforehand, but it must hand over any available maintenance and operations manuals, and may provide spare parts to help make one of the jets flyable or ready for static display, as long as the Space and Rocket Center reimburses the Navy at fair market value for any parts taken from existing stock. The Space and Rocket Center must follow all FAA rules, keep the planes properly maintained, and cannot sell or transfer the aircraft to anyone else without Navy approval; if those conditions are violated, ownership automatically returns to the federal government. All costs of the transfer, upkeep, and operation fall entirely on the Space and Rocket Center, not taxpayers, and the federal government bears no legal responsibility for any accidents or injuries involving the planes after the transfer. The bill primarily affects the Navy, the Space and Rocket Center Commission, and potentially any nonprofit organizations that might later partner with the Center to restore or display the aircraft at airshows and commemorative events.
ai_headline: Navy to transfer 3 surplus F-14D jets to Alabama museum
last_action_text: Passed Senate with an amendment by Unanimous Consent. (text: CR S2075)

ai_sections.tldr: The Navy can give three decommissioned F-14D Tomcat jets to a museum in Alabama at no cost to taxpayers.
ai_sections.what: This bill allows the U.S. Navy to transfer three surplus F-14D Tomcat fighter jets, with all combat systems removed, to the U.S. Space and Rocket Center in Huntsville, Alabama for free. The Navy must provide available maintenance and operations manuals, and may supply spare parts if the Center reimburses the Navy at fair market value.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects the U.S. Navy and the U.S. Space and Rocket Center Commission. Nonprofit organizations that might later partner with the Center to restore or display the planes could also be involved.
ai_sections.why: The Center must follow FAA rules, keep the planes maintained, and get Navy approval before transferring them to anyone else. If those conditions are not met, ownership of the aircraft automatically returns to the federal government.
ai_sections.cost: All costs of the transfer, upkeep, and operation are the responsibility of the Space and Rocket Center, not taxpayers. The federal government bears no legal responsibility for accidents or injuries involving the planes after the transfer.

title: Reliable Federal Infrastructure Act
ai_summary: This bill removes two existing federal rules that set energy efficiency standards for federal government buildings, and those rules immediately stop being enforced once the bill becomes law. It also changes the rules so that federal buildings cannot be *disqualified* from receiving a "green building" or "high-performance green building" certification simply because they use fossil fuels like natural gas or oil, either directly or indirectly. The Secretary of Energy is required to write new regulations within 180 days to replace the rules that were removed. The bill primarily affects how federal government buildings — such as offices, courthouses, and other facilities owned or leased by the U.S. government — are designed, built, and certified for energy performance.
ai_headline: Federal building energy rules face overhaul under HR 4690
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill removes two federal energy efficiency rules for government buildings and bars those buildings from losing green certification just for using fossil fuels.
ai_sections.what: This bill cancels two existing federal rules about energy efficiency in government buildings, effective immediately. It also means a federal building cannot be denied a green building certification only because it uses fossil fuels such as natural gas or oil. The Secretary of Energy must write replacement regulations within 180 days.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects federal government buildings, including offices, courthouses, and other facilities owned or leased by the U.S. government.
ai_sections.why: Removing the current rules means there are no energy efficiency standards for federal buildings until new regulations are written. Federal buildings that use fossil fuels would remain eligible for green building certification during that period and after.

title: FIRE Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the federal government handles air quality data collected on days when pollution levels spiked because of wildfires or intentional burns used to prevent wildfires (called "prescribed fires"). Specifically, it allows state governors to ask the EPA to set aside that elevated pollution data so it is not counted against the state when the EPA is deciding whether an area has clean enough air, whether it meets federal air quality standards, or whether new construction can proceed. The bill also requires the EPA to update its rules within 18 months to reflect these changes, to conduct shared regional analysis when multiple states are affected by the same smoke event, and to create a publicly accessible website — updated monthly — showing the status of all such state requests. The people most directly affected are state environmental agencies that manage air quality programs, communities in or near areas prone to wildfires, and anyone who lives in a region that might otherwise be labeled as having unhealthy air largely because of wildfire smoke outside local control.
ai_headline: Bill would let states exclude wildfire smoke from EPA air data
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: States could ask the EPA to exclude wildfire smoke data when judging whether local air meets federal clean-air standards.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets state governors ask the EPA to set aside pollution data from days when wildfires or prescribed fires caused spikes in air pollution. That data would then not count against the state when the EPA checks whether an area meets federal air quality standards or can allow new construction. The EPA would also have to update its rules within 18 months, work with other states when smoke crosses regional borders, and keep a public website showing the status of all state requests.
ai_sections.who: State environmental agencies that run air quality programs would be most directly affected. Communities near wildfire-prone areas, and anyone living where wildfire smoke could cause a region to be labeled as having unhealthy air, would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, pollution spikes caused by wildfires or prescribed fires count the same as other pollution when the EPA measures whether an area has clean enough air. That can affect whether a region is considered out of compliance with federal standards, even when the pollution came from sources outside local control.

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 111 South Tremont Street in Tremonton, Utah, as the "Sorensen-Estrada Post Office".
ai_summary: This bill officially renames the U.S. Post Office building at 111 South Tremont Street in Tremonton, Utah, giving it the honorary name "Sorensen-Estrada Post Office." The building would keep this name in all official government documents, maps, and records going forward. The change is purely symbolic — it does not affect how the post office operates, its services, its staff, or its address for mailing purposes. The main people affected are residents of Tremonton, Utah, who use this particular post office location.
ai_headline: Tremonton post office renamed the Sorensen-Estrada Post Office

title: Foster Youth Postsecondary Education Access and Success Act
ai_summary: This bill makes it easier for young people who have been in the foster care system to pay for college or job training by raising the maximum value of education and training vouchers from $5,000 to $12,000 per year. It also adds a "grace period" so that if a youth has to temporarily stop their education or training program, they can continue receiving support while the situation is sorted out through a conversation between the youth and the state. States would be required to actively inform eligible foster youth about these vouchers and to create a simple, standardized, easy-to-use online application form for accessing them. The changes affect current and former foster youth who are eligible for these vouchers, as well as state agencies that run these programs, and the federal Department of Health and Human Services would be responsible for developing guidance — with input from people who have lived in foster care — to help states put the new rules into practice.
ai_headline: Foster youth college vouchers raised to $12,000 yearly
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 555.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7463 raises education vouchers for foster youth from $5,000 to $12,000 per year and adds a grace period if they must pause their studies.
ai_sections.what: This bill raises the yearly maximum for education and training vouchers for foster youth from $5,000 to $12,000. It adds a grace period so youth can keep receiving support if they temporarily stop their program, and it requires states to tell eligible youth about the vouchers and offer a simple online application.
ai_sections.who: Current and former foster youth who qualify for these vouchers are directly affected. State agencies that run these programs and the federal Department of Health and Human Services would also have new responsibilities under this bill.
ai_sections.why: Without these changes, foster youth face a lower voucher cap and may lose support if they pause their education. States would also have no standardized requirement to notify youth or make the application process simple and accessible.

title: PROTECT Kids Act
ai_summary: This bill would require public elementary and middle schools that receive federal education funding to get written permission from a parent or guardian before making certain changes for a student who is a minor. Specifically, schools would need parental consent before changing a student's gender markers, pronouns, or preferred name on any school paperwork, or before changing which locker rooms or bathrooms the student uses. The bill would also ban schools from using federal education funds to teach or promote concepts related to "gender ideology," a term defined by referencing a specific executive order. These rules would apply to any public elementary or middle school that accepts money under the main federal K–12 education funding law, which covers the vast majority of public schools across the country.
ai_headline: Schools would need parent consent for student gender changes
last_action_text: Rules Committee Resolution H. Res. 1224 Reported to House. Rule provides for consideration of H.R. 7567, H.R. 2616, S. Con. Res. 33, S. 1318 and H.R. 1346. The resolution provides for consideration of H.R. 7567 under a structured rule and H.R. 2616, S. Con. Res. 33, S. 1318, and H.R. 1346 under a closed rule, with one hour of general debate on each measure. The resolution provides for one motion to recommit on H.R. 7567, H.R. 2616, and H.R. 1346, and one motion to commit on S. 1318.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2616 would require public K–8 schools to get written parental consent before changing a student's gender markers, pronouns, name, or bathroom access.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require public elementary and middle schools to get written permission from a parent or guardian before changing a student's gender markers, pronouns, or preferred name on school records, or before changing which bathrooms or locker rooms the student uses. The bill would also prohibit schools from using federal education funds to teach or promote concepts related to "gender ideology," a term defined by referencing a specific executive order.
ai_sections.who: These rules would apply to public elementary and middle schools that receive federal funding under the main federal K–12 education law, which covers the vast majority of public schools in the country.
ai_sections.why: Schools that do not comply could put their federal education funding at risk. Students whose parents are not contacted would not have changes made to their records or facility access under this law.

title: Foster Youth Workforce Opportunity Act
ai_summary: This bill expands a federal support program that helps young people who have been in foster care pay for education and job training. Currently, that program is mainly aimed at youth who "aged out" of foster care (typically at age 18), but this bill would extend eligibility to anyone who was in foster care at age 14 or older, and would lower the age at which youth can start participating in the program from 16 to 14. The bill also broadens what the program's funds can be spent on — in addition to traditional colleges and community colleges, young people could use the money for short-term job training programs, apprenticeships, getting a GED, or remedial education (such as tutoring or basic skills instruction needed to reach high school or college-level work, as long as that help isn't already available for free elsewhere). Youth who need remedial education would also be allowed to stay in the program for up to six years instead of the standard five. These changes would take effect one year after the bill is signed into law.
ai_headline: Bill expands foster youth education and training aid
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 556.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7343 lets foster youth join a federal education and job training program starting at age 14 instead of 16.
ai_sections.what: This bill expands a federal program that helps former foster youth pay for education and job training. It lowers the starting age from 16 to 14 and allows funds to be used for apprenticeships, GED programs, short-term job training, and remedial education in addition to traditional college. Youth who need remedial education could stay in the program up to six years instead of five.
ai_sections.who: Young people who were in foster care at age 14 or older would be eligible, including those who aged out of the system. Currently the program focuses mainly on youth who aged out, typically at age 18.
ai_sections.why: More foster youth would be able to access the program earlier and for a wider range of educational paths. Youth needing extra academic support would have more time to complete the program.

title: Support for Expectant and Parenting Foster Youth Act
ai_summary: This bill changes federal rules to better support young people who were in foster care and are now pregnant or raising children. It requires states that receive federal funding for foster care transition programs to inform these young parents about home-visiting services — programs where trained professionals visit families at home to help with parenting skills, child development, and other needs. States would also be allowed to use their federal funding to provide specialized case management and resource coordination specifically designed for expectant and parenting foster youth. These changes would take effect one year after the bill becomes law and would apply to states whose program plans are approved by the federal government after that date.
ai_headline: Foster youth parents gain access to home-visiting services
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 41 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: States getting federal foster care funds must tell young parents who were in foster care about home-visiting services.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates federal rules for states that receive funding for foster care transition programs. Those states would be required to tell expectant and parenting young people who were in foster care about home-visiting services. States could also use that funding to provide specialized case management and resource coordination for these young parents.
ai_sections.who: This affects states that receive federal funding for foster care transition programs. Young people who were in foster care and are now pregnant or raising children would be directly impacted.
ai_sections.why: Without this requirement, states were not obligated to connect parenting foster youth with home-visiting services. These changes take effect one year after the bill becomes law and apply to states whose program plans are approved by the federal government after that date.

title: To designate the facility of the United States Postal Service located at 201 East Grant Avenue in Georgetown, Ohio, as the "Ulysses S. Grant Post Office Building".

Georgetown, Ohio, is the birthplace of Ulysses S. Grant, the Civil War general and 18th President of the United States, which is the reason for the tribute. Once enacted, any official government documents, maps, or records referring to that building would use the new name. The change is symbolic and affects only the name of the building — it does not alter postal services, staffing, or operations there in any way.
ai_headline: Georgetown post office renamed for Ulysses S. Grant

ai_sections.who: This applies to one specific federal building in Georgetown, Ohio. Any official government documents, maps, or records referring to that building would use the new name.
ai_sections.why: Georgetown, Ohio, is the birthplace of Ulysses S. Grant, the Civil War general and 18th President of the United States. That connection is the stated reason for the tribute.

title: Foster Youth Housing Opportunity Act
ai_summary: This bill changes federal rules to give young people who have been in foster care more help as they grow into adulthood, focusing on housing, education, and job training. It raises the maximum education and training voucher from $5,000 to $12,000, lets those vouchers be used for more things like apprenticeships, GED programs, remedial education, and short-term job training, and allows youth to start receiving these benefits at age 14 instead of 16. It also adds new housing supports — such as help with security deposits, moving costs, rental lease guidance, and financial literacy — and requires the federal housing and child welfare agencies to work together and share guidance so foster youth can more easily access federal housing programs. States must also make sure eligible youth actually know these benefits exist, using a simple standardized online application form, and must connect expectant or parenting foster youth to home visiting programs and tailored case management services. The people directly affected are young people who have been in the foster care system, especially those aging out or transitioning to independence, as well as the state agencies and public housing authorities that serve them.
ai_headline: Foster care vouchers would rise to $12,000 under HR 7432
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7432 raises education vouchers for foster youth from $5,000 to $12,000 and adds housing and job training support.

It also adds housing help such as security deposits, moving costs, and financial literacy, and requires federal housing and child welfare agencies to work together so foster youth can more easily access housing programs.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects young people who have been in the foster care system, especially those aging out or moving toward independence. It also affects state agencies and public housing authorities that serve them.
ai_sections.why: Without these changes, foster youth aging out of the system may have fewer resources to find stable housing or continue their education and job training. States would also be required to make sure eligible youth know these benefits exist and can apply through a simple standardized online form.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8193 would rename the post office at 189 East Main Street in Xenia, Ohio, after Gilman "Gil" Whitney.
ai_sections.what: This bill would give the U.S. Post Office building at 189 East Main Street in Xenia, Ohio, the official name "Gilman 'Gil' Whitney Post Office Building." The change affects only the building's name. Postal services and employees would not be affected.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to one specific postal facility in Xenia, Ohio. It has no direct effect on the general public beyond the building's official name.
ai_sections.why: If passed, all official government documents, maps, and records that refer to that building would use the new name. The name change would be permanent.

title: National Quantum Initiative Reauthorization Act
ai_summary: This bill extends and expands the federal government's ongoing effort to advance quantum technology — an emerging field that uses the principles of quantum physics to build more powerful computers, communication systems, and sensors. It updates the agencies, programs, and advisory bodies that coordinate the government's quantum research work, adds new goals around commercializing quantum products and securing the supply chain for quantum hardware, and requires the creation of a national strategy for cooperating with allied countries on quantum technology. The bill also expands education and workforce programs at all levels — from K-12 classrooms to graduate fellowships — to grow the number of Americans trained in quantum fields, and it directs the National Institute of Standards and Technology to help businesses and critical infrastructure operators protect themselves from future quantum computer-based cyberattacks by promoting new encryption standards. The people most directly affected include federal researchers and agencies, universities and community colleges, private quantum technology companies (especially small businesses and startups), and organizations responsible for cybersecurity.
ai_headline: Federal quantum research programs get expanded mandate
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would renew and expand the national quantum technology program, adding workforce training, commercialization goals, and cybersecurity guidance.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews the federal quantum technology program and updates the agencies and advisory groups that run it. It adds new goals around bringing quantum products to market, securing the supply chain for quantum hardware, and working with allied countries on quantum research. It also directs a federal agency to help businesses and critical infrastructure operators prepare for cyberattacks that future quantum computers could carry out.
ai_sections.who: Federal researchers, universities, and community colleges would be involved, along with private quantum companies, startups, and small businesses. Organizations responsible for cybersecurity would also be directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Quantum technology is an emerging field, and without coordinated federal effort, the United States could fall behind in developing it. If quantum computers become powerful enough to break current encryption, businesses and critical infrastructure could face serious cybersecurity risks.

title: CONNECT Act
ai_summary: This bill updates the goals of a longstanding federal program that helps young people who have been in foster care prepare for adult life. Specifically, it adds two new official purposes to the program: helping youth who entered foster care at age 14 or older build and keep lasting relationships with supportive adults, mentors, and peers; and helping those same youth stay involved in decisions about their own future care, including connections to family, peer support, and other services. Within one year of becoming law, the federal Department of Health and Human Services would also be required to issue guidance to states and tribal child welfare agencies — developed with input from people who have personally been in foster care — explaining what kinds of relationship-building and support services can receive federal funding, best practices for mentoring and peer support, how agencies should notify eligible youth about available services, and how these activities should be documented in a young person's case plan. The bill primarily affects youth aged 14 and older who are in or have aged out of the foster care system, as well as the state and tribal agencies that serve them.
ai_headline: Bill adds mentoring goals to federal foster care program
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 559.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7995 updates a federal foster care program to help youth 14+ build lasting relationships and have a say in their own care plans.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds two new goals to an existing federal program for foster youth: helping young people aged 14 and older build and keep lasting relationships with supportive adults, mentors, and peers, and helping those same youth stay involved in decisions about their own care. Within one year of passage, the Department of Health and Human Services must release guidance to states and tribal agencies covering what services qualify for federal funding, best practices for mentoring and peer support, how to notify eligible youth about available services, and how to document these activities in case plans. That guidance must be developed with input from people who have personally been in foster care.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects youth aged 14 and older who are in or have aged out of the foster care system. It also affects the state and tribal child welfare agencies that serve them.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, relationship-building and peer support services for older foster youth have no formal place in this program's stated goals. Adding these goals means agencies have clearer federal direction on supporting these youth and documenting that support.

title: Fresh Starts for Foster Youth Act
ai_summary: This bill makes two changes to how states manage support for young people who are in or have aged out of the foster care system. First, it requires each state's governor to officially certify that the state's case planning process — the roadmap used to help foster youth — actively considers legal problems that could affect a young person's housing, education, job prospects, and family relationships, such as issues with court records, custody, or establishing legal family ties. Second, it allows states to use existing federal funding from the John H. Chafee Foster Care Program — money already set aside to help foster youth transition to adulthood — to pay for legal counseling and legal services for these young people. The people most directly affected are current and former foster youth, particularly those approaching or navigating adulthood, who may face legal barriers that make it harder to find stable housing, get a job, or maintain family connections.
ai_headline: States could use Chafee funds for foster youth legal aid
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 557.

ai_sections.tldr: States would be required to address legal barriers in foster youth case plans and could use existing federal funds for legal services.
ai_sections.what: This bill makes two changes to how states support foster youth. Governors would have to certify that case plans consider legal problems around housing, education, jobs, and family. States could also use existing federal Chafee Program funds to pay for legal help for these young people.
ai_sections.who: Current and former foster youth are most directly affected, especially those approaching or living through adulthood.
ai_sections.why: Some foster youth face legal barriers, such as court record issues or custody questions, that can make it harder to find housing, get a job, or stay connected to family. This bill would require states to account for those barriers and allow them to fund legal support.
ai_sections.cost: No new funding is created. States would be allowed to use money already set aside through the existing John H. Chafee Foster Care Program for legal services.

title: Save Our Sequoias Act
ai_summary: This bill sets up a coordinated, government-wide effort to protect giant sequoia trees on federal lands in California from the growing threats of severe wildfires, insect infestations, and drought. It directs federal agencies (including the Forest Service, National Park Service, and Bureau of Land Management), the State of California, and the Tule River Indian Tribe to work together through a formal agreement and an existing coalition of land managers to assess the health of every giant sequoia grove, carry out emergency forest management work such as clearing dead trees and thinning overgrown areas, and replant groves that have been damaged and are unlikely to recover on their own.
