To speed up that work, Congress declares an emergency on these lands for seven years, allowing many of these forest treatment projects to skip lengthy environmental review steps, as long as they stay within defined size limits. The bill also creates grant programs, special strike teams, an insect-monitoring program, and a private-donation fund — all aimed at supporting the agencies, tribes, rural communities, and private partners involved in keeping these ancient trees alive.
ai_headline: Federal agencies ordered to protect giant sequoias
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2709 declares a 7-year emergency on federal lands to fast-track protection of giant sequoia groves in California.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a coordinated effort across federal agencies, the state of California, and tribal partners to protect giant sequoia trees on federal lands. It allows many forest treatment projects to skip lengthy environmental reviews for seven years, within defined size limits. It also sets up grant programs, strike teams, an insect-monitoring program, and a private-donation fund to support the work.
ai_sections.who: The Forest Service, National Park Service, Bureau of Land Management, the State of California, and the Tule River Indian Tribe would all work together under a formal agreement. Rural communities and private partners are also involved.
ai_sections.why: Giant sequoias on federal lands face growing threats from severe wildfires, insect infestations, and drought. Without action, damaged groves may not recover on their own.

title: A joint resolution to direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Venezuela that have not been authorized by Congress.
ai_summary: This resolution would require the President to stop using U.S. military forces in any combat or hostile operations in or against Venezuela, unless Congress has formally declared war or passed a specific law authorizing such military action. Congress states in the resolution that no such declaration or authorization currently exists, meaning any ongoing U.S. military hostilities there would be happening without congressional approval. The order to withdraw forces would not apply if the U.S. needed to defend itself against an actual or immediately threatened armed attack. This resolution primarily affects the President's authority as commander-in-chief and the U.S. military personnel who might be engaged in operations related to Venezuela.
ai_headline: Senate bill would halt unauthorized U.S. combat in Venezuela
last_action_text: Point of order that the measure is not entitled to expedited procedures under 50 U.S.C. 1546(a) raised against the measure agreed to in Senate by Yea-Nay Vote. 50 - 50. Record Vote Number: 9.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would require the President to pull U.S. troops out of any unauthorized military hostilities involving Venezuela.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would order the President to stop using U.S. military forces in any combat or hostile operations in or against Venezuela. That order would not apply if the U.S. needed to defend itself against an actual or immediate armed attack. Congress says no declaration of war or authorization for such action currently exists.
ai_sections.who: This resolution primarily affects the President's authority as commander-in-chief. It also affects any U.S. military personnel who might be involved in operations related to Venezuela.
ai_sections.why: If passed, any ongoing U.S. military hostilities in or against Venezuela would have to stop unless Congress formally approves them. This shifts decision-making power over military action from the President to Congress.

title: National Landslide Preparedness Act Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill extends and expands a federal program that works to reduce the dangers of landslides across the United States, raising the program's annual funding cap from $25 million to $35 million per year and extending its authorization through 2030, with at least $10 million of that specifically set aside for buying, installing, and repairing landslide early warning systems in high-risk areas. It broadens who the government must work with on landslide preparedness — adding Native Hawaiian organizations, Tribal organizations, and colleges and universities alongside the state, local, and tribal governments already included — and requires the next national landslide strategy to specifically assess the risks posed by atmospheric rivers and extreme rainfall events. The bill also creates a new water monitoring system within the U.S. Geological Survey (called the Next Generation Water Observing System) to collect real-time data on water quantity and quality to help predict floods, droughts, and other water-related hazards, starting in 10 priority areas with $30 million authorized for the first year. Additionally, it updates an existing groundwater monitoring program, increases its authorized funding to $30 million per year through 2033, and expands groundwater monitoring to include the effects of permafrost thaw. People who live in landslide-prone or flood-prone regions, water managers, emergency responders, and Indigenous communities are among those most directly affected.
ai_headline: Senate bill boosts landslide and flood warning funding
last_action_text: Held at the desk.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress proposes raising landslide program funding to $35M/year, adding early warning systems, and creating a new water monitoring network through 2030.
ai_sections.what: This bill would extend a federal landslide preparedness program through 2030 and raise its annual funding cap. It sets aside money specifically for early warning systems in high-risk areas and requires the national landslide strategy to address risks from atmospheric rivers and heavy rainfall. It also creates a new real-time water monitoring system within the U.S. Geological Survey and updates an existing groundwater monitoring program to include effects of permafrost thaw.
ai_sections.who: People in landslide-prone and flood-prone areas, water managers, and emergency responders are among those most directly affected. The bill also expands who the government must partner with, adding Native Hawaiian organizations, Tribal organizations, and colleges and universities to the existing list.
ai_sections.why: Without updated monitoring and early warning systems, communities in high-risk areas may have less time to prepare for landslides, floods, and droughts. Gaps in groundwater data, including changes caused by permafrost thaw, could limit the ability of water managers and emergency teams to respond to hazards.
ai_sections.cost: The bill raises the landslide program's annual funding cap from $25 million to $35 million, with at least $10 million of that set aside each year for early warning systems. The new water monitoring system is authorized at $30 million for its first year, starting in 10 priority areas. The updated groundwater monitoring program is authorized at $30 million per year through 2033.

title: Precision Agriculture Satellite Connectivity Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) to examine its existing rules on satellite communications — specifically the types of satellites used for fixed, mobile, and earth-monitoring services — to see whether any rule changes could help support precision agriculture. Precision agriculture refers to farming techniques that use technology, like GPS and sensors, to make planting, watering, and harvesting more efficient and data-driven. If the FCC finds that rule changes are possible under its current authority, it must also develop recommendations for how to make those changes. Within 15 months of the bill becoming law, the FCC must deliver a written report of its findings and any recommendations to the relevant congressional committees. The bill primarily affects the FCC and, indirectly, farmers and agricultural businesses that rely on satellite technology to manage their operations.
ai_headline: FCC ordered to study satellite rules for farm tech
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: The FCC must study its satellite rules and report to Congress within 15 months on whether changes could support precision agriculture.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the FCC to review its rules on satellite communications to see if changes could support precision agriculture. Precision agriculture uses technology like GPS and sensors to help farmers make more informed decisions about planting, watering, and harvesting. If the FCC finds it has the authority to make changes, it must also write up recommendations and deliver a full report to the relevant congressional committees within 15 months.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects the FCC, which must carry out the review and write the report. Farmers and agricultural businesses that use satellite technology to manage their operations are indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: The review could lead to rule changes that affect how satellite services operate for farming purposes. Without the study, Congress would not have a formal FCC assessment of whether current satellite rules are working for agricultural users.

title: SHARKED Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Secretary of Commerce to create a task force focused on "shark depredation" — meaning situations where sharks steal fish from fishing lines or interfere with fishing activities. The task force would include representatives from regional fishery management councils, state coastal wildlife agencies, marine fisheries commissions, federal fisheries officials, and shark researchers, and it would be responsible for improving communication between fishing managers and shark scientists, identifying research priorities, recommending management strategies, and distributing educational materials to help fishers reduce unwanted shark interactions. The task force must report to Congress every two years and will automatically shut down within seven years of being established. The bill also adds shark depredation research — including studying what causes it and how to address it — to the list of eligible research projects under existing federal fisheries law. People most directly affected include commercial and recreational fishers in coastal states and U.S. territories, as well as researchers and managers who work on shark and fisheries issues.
ai_headline: Commerce Dept. asked to form shark depredation task force
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 207 creates a federal task force to study sharks stealing fish from lines and recommend ways to reduce those conflicts.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the Secretary of Commerce to form a task force focused on shark depredation, which is when sharks take fish off fishing lines or disrupt fishing. The group would coordinate research, recommend management strategies, and share educational materials with fishers. It must report to Congress every two years and will close automatically after seven years.
ai_sections.who: The task force would include fishery councils, state coastal wildlife agencies, marine fisheries commissions, federal fisheries officials, and shark researchers. Commercial and recreational fishers in coastal states and U.S. territories would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without coordinated research and communication, fishing managers and shark scientists have limited ways to share information or address depredation together. This bill creates a structure for that coordination and adds shark depredation research to the list of projects eligible under existing federal fisheries law.

title: Criminal History Access Act
ai_summary: This bill expands who is allowed to access federal criminal history records maintained by the FBI. Specifically, it adds "peace officer standards and training agencies" — state agencies that set the rules for hiring, training, and certifying law enforcement officers — to the list of organizations legally permitted to receive those records for official purposes. The bill also defines these agencies clearly in federal law, and requires the Attorney General to update federal regulations within 180 days to put the change into effect. This affects state-level agencies that oversee law enforcement certification and licensing, allowing them to look up an officer candidate's or officer's criminal background through federal records when making decisions about certification or fitness for duty.
ai_headline: State police licensing boards gain FBI record access
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8352 lets state law enforcement licensing agencies check FBI criminal records when deciding whether to certify officers.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds state "peace officer standards and training agencies" to the list of organizations allowed to access FBI criminal history records. These are state agencies that set the rules for hiring, training, and certifying law enforcement officers. The Attorney General must update federal regulations within 180 days to put this change into effect.
ai_sections.who: State agencies that oversee law enforcement certification and licensing are directly affected. Officer candidates and current officers may have their federal criminal records reviewed as part of certification or fitness decisions.
ai_sections.why: Right now, these state agencies are not on the approved list to receive FBI criminal history records. This change means they would have a legal path to check federal criminal backgrounds when making certification decisions.

title: Deterring American AI Model Theft Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill targets a specific kind of theft: when foreign actors secretly query or probe American companies' private AI systems thousands of times in order to copy how those systems work — a technique called a "model extraction attack." It directs U.S. government agencies to investigate which foreign individuals, companies, or governments have done this, publish a public list of identified bad actors, and share best practices with private AI companies to help them detect and stop these attacks. If bad actors are identified, the government can place them on a trade blacklist that cuts off their access to U.S. goods and technology, and the President can freeze any of their assets or financial interests located in the United States. The bill focuses primarily on actors linked to China, Russia, and a handful of other designated countries, and it carves out clear exceptions for legitimate AI research, humanitarian aid, and intelligence activities. American companies that own private AI systems are the main protected party, while foreign individuals and entities caught stealing AI capabilities face the most significant consequences.
ai_headline: Bill targets foreign AI model extraction attacks
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 43 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: Foreign actors who secretly copy U.S. AI systems could face trade blacklisting and frozen U.S. assets under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill addresses "model extraction attacks," where foreign actors secretly send thousands of queries to private U.S. AI systems to copy how they work. The government would investigate these attacks, publish a list of identified bad actors, and share protective guidance with private AI companies. Identified bad actors could be cut off from U.S. goods and technology, and their U.S. assets could be frozen.
ai_sections.who: American companies that own private AI systems are the main protected party. Foreign individuals, companies, and governments linked to China, Russia, and a handful of other designated countries are the primary targets, though the bill makes exceptions for legitimate research, humanitarian aid, and intelligence activities.
ai_sections.why: If bad actors are identified, they can be placed on a trade blacklist and lose access to U.S. goods and technology. The President can also freeze any of their financial assets held in the United States.

title: BIS IT Modernization Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) — the federal agency that oversees what sensitive goods and technologies can be exported from the United States — to upgrade its computer and data systems through 2030. The upgrades would replace outdated systems with modern tools, including data analytics, artificial intelligence, and supply chain tracking software, to make it faster and more accurate to decide who gets export licenses and to identify companies or individuals trying to illegally obtain U.S. technology, particularly for military use by countries like China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. The bill also calls on BIS to improve how it shares information with other U.S. agencies, the intelligence community, and international partners, and to reassess how many staff it needs as its technology changes. It would primarily affect BIS employees and operations, U.S. businesses that apply for export licenses, and foreign entities that are — or could be — placed on restricted trade lists. Up to $25 million per year is authorized to pay for these upgrades from 2026 through 2029.
ai_headline: BIS would get $25M yearly for tech upgrades under new bill
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 39 - 5.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would require BIS to modernize its tech systems through 2030, with up to $25M/year authorized from 2026–2029.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) to upgrade its computer and data systems through 2030. The new tools would include data analytics, artificial intelligence, and supply chain tracking software. BIS would also be asked to improve information sharing with other agencies, the intelligence community, and international partners, and to review its staffing needs.
ai_sections.who: This primarily affects BIS employees and U.S. businesses that apply for export licenses. Foreign individuals or companies that are on — or could be added to — restricted trade lists would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: BIS currently decides who receives permission to export sensitive goods and technologies, and it identifies those who may be trying to obtain U.S. technology illegally, including for military use by countries such as China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. Upgrading its systems is meant to make those reviews faster and more accurate.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $25 million per year is authorized to fund these upgrades, covering the period from 2026 through 2029.

title: Downwinder Commemoration Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require three federal agencies — the Army, the Air Force, and the Department of the Interior — to obtain and install commemorative plaques honoring New Mexico communities known as "Downwinders," people who were exposed to radioactive fallout after the first atomic bomb was detonated on July 16, 1945. Congress states in the bill that these communities suffered generations of serious health problems, including cancer and infertility, as a result of that exposure. The three plaques must be placed within one year of the bill becoming law at publicly accessible locations at White Sands Missile Range, Holloman Air Force Base, and White Sands National Park, all in New Mexico. The bill primarily affects the federal agencies responsible for those sites and serves as a formal acknowledgment of the harm experienced by the affected New Mexico communities.
ai_headline: Federal sites to honor New Mexico atomic bomb victims
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by Unanimous Consent.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4290 would require three federal agencies to place commemorative plaques honoring New Mexico Downwinder communities within one year.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Army, the Air Force, and the Department of the Interior to obtain and install plaques honoring New Mexico communities called Downwinders. The plaques must be placed at White Sands Missile Range, Holloman Air Force Base, and White Sands National Park within one year of the bill becoming law.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects the three federal agencies responsible for those New Mexico sites. It formally recognizes the Downwinders, community members who were exposed to radioactive fallout after the first atomic bomb was detonated on July 16, 1945.
ai_sections.why: Congress states that exposed communities suffered generations of serious health problems, including cancer and infertility, as a result of that fallout. The plaques would serve as a formal acknowledgment of that harm.

title: FISH Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill creates a formal government "blacklist" of foreign fishing vessels, fleets, and their owners that are caught fishing illegally, using forced labor, or otherwise breaking international fishing rules — and it imposes serious consequences on anyone on that list, including banning their ships from U.S. ports, blocking imports of their seafood into the United States, and potentially freezing their U.S.-held assets and revoking their visas. The bill directs multiple federal agencies — including NOAA, the Coast Guard, Customs and Border Protection, and the State Department — to work together to collect and share data, increase inspections of suspicious vessels on the open ocean, and help other countries build their own capacity to stop illegal fishing. It also calls for several government studies, including an examination of how Russian and Chinese fishing industries interact and affect U.S. seafood markets, and an assessment of what illegal fishing actually costs the U.S. and global economy. The people most directly affected include foreign fishing operators engaged in illegal or forced-labor fishing, U.S. seafood importers who buy from foreign vessels, and coastal fishing communities worldwide that compete with or are harmed by illegal fishing.
ai_headline: Bill targets foreign vessels for illegal fishing
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by Unanimous Consent.

ai_sections.tldr: The FISH Act would blacklist foreign fishing vessels that break international rules and ban their ships, seafood, and owners from the U.S.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates an official government blacklist of foreign fishing vessels, fleets, and their owners caught fishing illegally, using forced labor, or breaking international fishing rules. Ships on the list would be banned from U.S. ports, their seafood blocked from entering the United States, and their U.S.-held assets could be frozen and their visas revoked. The bill also directs federal agencies to share data, increase inspections at sea, help other countries fight illegal fishing, and conduct studies on Russian and Chinese fishing industries and the economic cost of illegal fishing.
ai_sections.who: Foreign fishing operators who engage in illegal or forced-labor fishing would face the most direct consequences. U.S. seafood importers who buy from those vessels and coastal fishing communities worldwide that compete with or are harmed by illegal fishing would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Foreign vessels placed on the blacklist would lose access to U.S. ports and markets and could have their assets frozen and visas canceled. U.S. importers sourcing seafood from blacklisted vessels would be blocked from bringing those products into the country.

title: A Stronger Workforce for America Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill updates and extends the main federal law that funds job training, career services, and adult education programs across the United States. It makes changes to how those programs are organized and run at the state and local level — for example, updating the boards that oversee job training in each area, adjusting how local service regions are drawn, and tightening the rules for measuring whether programs are actually helping people find and keep jobs. It expands the definition of who counts as facing barriers to employment (now including out-of-school youth), broadens the kinds of support people can receive (such as food assistance and help with technology skills), and puts more emphasis on training programs that are backed by evidence and tied to real employer hiring commitments. It also shifts adult education and literacy programs from the Department of Education to the Department of Labor, and creates new tools — like digital records of a person's skills and work history — to help match jobseekers with employers. The people most directly affected are job seekers, unemployed or displaced workers, low-income adults, out-of-school youth, and the employers and training providers who participate in these federally funded programs.
ai_headline: Federal job training law gets first major overhaul in years
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 19 - 14.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal job training and adult education programs would be reorganized, expanded, and moved partly from the Education Department to the Labor Department.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates the federal law that funds job training, career services, and adult education programs across the country. It changes how those programs are run at the state and local level, expands who qualifies for help, adds new types of support like food assistance and technology skills, and shifts adult education programs from the Department of Education to the Department of Labor. It also creates digital records of a person's skills and work history to help connect job seekers with employers.
ai_sections.who: The people most directly affected are job seekers, unemployed or displaced workers, low-income adults, and out-of-school youth. Employers and training providers that take part in these federally funded programs would also see changes.
ai_sections.why: Because the bill changes which agency runs adult education, programs and their staff would go through a reorganization. States and local areas would also need to update their oversight boards, redraw service regions, and meet tighter rules for showing that programs are helping people find and keep jobs.

title: Fiscal Contingency Preparedness Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the U.S. Treasury Department, working together with the White House budget office, to include a new section in their existing annual financial report that examines how prepared the federal government is to handle major unexpected financial crises. The analysis must cover a range of potential emergencies — such as recessions, energy crises, natural disasters, pandemics, wars, cyberattacks, and financial system collapses — and estimate both the short-term and long-term costs to the federal government of responding to each type of event. Officials may look at how the government handled similar crises in the past to help build these estimates. After the Treasury publishes its first such analysis, the Government Accountability Office (an independent congressional watchdog agency) must review the methodology and findings, post a public report on its website, and send that report to the budget committees in both the Senate and the House of Representatives. This bill primarily affects federal agencies — specifically the Treasury Department and the budget office — and is intended to give lawmakers and the public a clearer picture of the country's financial vulnerabilities before a crisis actually occurs.
ai_headline: Treasury directed to assess federal crisis readiness costs
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 39 - 1.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4642 would require the Treasury to add a federal crisis cost analysis to its annual report, then have the GAO review it.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Treasury Department and the White House budget office to add a new section to their annual financial report. That section would analyze how ready the federal government is to handle major crises — such as recessions, pandemics, natural disasters, wars, cyberattacks, and financial collapses — and estimate what each type of crisis could cost the government in the short and long term. Past crises could be used as a reference point for building those estimates.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects the Treasury Department and the White House budget office, who must produce the analysis. The Government Accountability Office, an independent watchdog for Congress, must then review the findings and share a public report with the budget committees in both the Senate and the House.
ai_sections.why: Without this kind of analysis, lawmakers and the public have less information about the country's financial vulnerabilities before a crisis strikes. The GAO review adds an independent check on the quality of the Treasury's methods and conclusions.

title: Florida Safe Seas Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill makes it illegal to feed sharks in the ocean waters off the coast of Florida that fall within the U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone — the federally managed stretch of ocean extending roughly 200 miles from shore. It does this by adding Florida to an existing federal law that already bans shark feeding in the waters off Hawaii. Anyone who feeds sharks in these Florida offshore waters — whether for recreational diving, tourism, or any other purpose — would be violating federal law. This would affect divers, boat operators, tour companies, and anyone else who might offer or participate in shark-feeding activities in those waters.
ai_headline: Federal bill would ban shark feeding off Florida's coast
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported by Unanimous Consent.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3831 would make it a federal crime to feed sharks in Florida's offshore ocean waters, up to about 200 miles from shore.
ai_sections.what: This bill would ban shark feeding in the federally managed ocean waters off Florida's coast. It does this by adding Florida to an existing federal law that already prohibits shark feeding off Hawaii.
ai_sections.who: This would apply to divers, boat operators, tour companies, and anyone else who feeds sharks or takes part in shark-feeding activities in those waters.
ai_sections.why: Anyone who feeds sharks in these waters would be breaking federal law, regardless of whether the purpose is recreational diving, tourism, or anything else.

title: Weather Research and Forecasting Innovation Reauthorization Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill renews and expands the federal government's programs for improving weather forecasting, warnings, and research — primarily through the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA). It updates and funds programs focused on better predicting tornadoes, hurricanes, tsunamis, floods, wildfires, droughts, and other dangerous weather events, and directs NOAA to modernize its tools by investing in artificial intelligence, advanced computing, satellites, and drones. The bill also opens more opportunities for private companies to sell weather data to the government, and requires improvements in how weather warnings and risks are communicated to the public. It affects a wide range of people and organizations — including emergency managers, farmers, pilots, coastal communities, and ordinary Americans who rely on accurate weather forecasts — as well as private weather companies, universities, and other federal agencies that work alongside NOAA.
ai_headline: Senate bill renews and expands NOAA weather programs
last_action_text: Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. Ordered to be reported with amendments favorably.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would renew and expand NOAA's weather forecasting and warning programs, adding AI, satellites, and more private data options.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews federal programs that work to improve weather forecasting, warnings, and research, mainly through NOAA. It directs NOAA to update its tools using artificial intelligence, advanced computing, satellites, and drones. It also creates more opportunities for private companies to sell weather data to the government and requires clearer communication of weather risks to the public.
ai_sections.who: NOAA leads the work under this bill, alongside private weather companies, universities, and other federal agencies. People affected include emergency managers, farmers, pilots, coastal communities, and everyday Americans who depend on accurate weather forecasts.
ai_sections.why: Without renewal, the existing programs supporting tornado, hurricane, tsunami, flood, wildfire, and drought prediction would lose their current authorization. Updating these programs could change how quickly and clearly the public receives warnings about dangerous weather.

title: ACRES Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the U.S. Departments of Agriculture and Interior to more carefully track and publicly report how many acres of federal land — such as national forests and national parks — have had vegetation cleared or burned to reduce wildfire risk each year. Each affected acre can only be counted once, even if multiple treatments were applied to it, and the reports must include details such as the location of the land, its wildfire risk level before and after treatment, the cost per acre, and how effective the work was at actually reducing fire danger. The agencies must set up standardized data-collection procedures within 90 days and make their annual reports available to the public online. The Government Accountability Office (an independent federal watchdog) must also study how well the new reporting system is working within two years. No new money is provided to carry out these requirements — the agencies must use whatever funding they already have available.
ai_headline: Federal agencies must track wildfire treatment acres publicly
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported without amendment favorably.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies must track and publicly report wildfire-treated acreage once per acre, with location, cost, and effectiveness details.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the Departments of Agriculture and Interior to track and publicly report how many acres of federal land are treated each year to reduce wildfire risk. Each acre can only be counted once, and reports must include location, wildfire risk levels before and after treatment, cost per acre, and how well the work reduced fire danger. Agencies must set up standardized data collection within 90 days and post annual reports online.
ai_sections.who: The U.S. Departments of Agriculture and Interior carry out the new reporting requirements. The Government Accountability Office, an independent federal watchdog, must study how well the new system is working within two years.
ai_sections.why: Without standardized tracking, it is harder to know whether wildfire risk reduction work on federal lands is being measured consistently or accurately. This bill creates a public record that shows where treatment happened and how effective it was.
ai_sections.cost: No new funding is provided. The agencies must carry out all requirements using money they already have.

title: Grasslands Grazing Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes a legal definition so that ranchers who graze livestock on national grasslands receive the same type of formal grazing permits — and the protections that come with them — as ranchers who graze on other federal lands like national forests. Right now, a specific federal law governing grazing permits applies to "lands within National Forests," and national grasslands have not clearly fallen under that same language, meaning ranchers there may have less secure grazing rights. The bill fixes that by updating the wording so that all National Forest System land, which includes national grasslands, is covered by the same permit rules. The people most directly affected are ranchers and livestock operators who currently hold grazing agreements on national grasslands across the country. The bill is careful to note that it does not change any other laws that separately govern national grasslands or public rangelands.
ai_headline: Ranchers on national grasslands would gain federal grazing permit protections
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 25 - 14.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6300 updates a legal definition so ranchers on national grasslands get the same formal grazing permits as those on other federal lands.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates the wording of an existing federal grazing law to cover all National Forest System land, which includes national grasslands. Right now, that law clearly applies only to national forests, leaving grassland ranchers with less secure grazing rights. The change brings national grasslands under the same permit rules without altering other laws that govern those lands.
ai_sections.who: Ranchers and livestock operators who currently hold grazing agreements on national grasslands across the country are most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Because national grasslands were not clearly named in the existing law, ranchers there could have less formal protection for their grazing rights. This bill closes that gap so grassland permits carry the same legal standing as permits on other federal lands.

title: Wyoming Education Trust Modernization Act
ai_summary: This bill makes a small but meaningful update to the original 1890 law that established Wyoming as a state and set aside public lands to fund its public schools. Specifically, it changes the wording used to describe how money from those lands can be spent: instead of saying the state may use the "interest of" the funds, it now says the state may use the "earnings on" those funds. This language change reflects how investment income actually works today, where trust funds can generate returns beyond simple interest — such as dividends or capital gains — and ensures that all types of earnings from these educational land trust funds can be used for schools, not just traditional interest payments. The people most directly affected are Wyoming's public school students and the state agencies that manage the school trust lands and funds.
ai_headline: Senate bill updates Wyoming school trust fund language
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported without amendment favorably.

ai_sections.tldr: Wyoming's school trust fund law is updated to allow all types of investment earnings — not just interest — to fund public schools.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates a word in Wyoming's original 1890 statehood law. It replaces "interest of" with "earnings on" when describing how money from public land trust funds can be used for schools. This means all types of investment returns, such as dividends and capital gains, are now clearly covered, not only traditional interest payments.
ai_sections.who: Wyoming public school students are most directly affected. State agencies that manage school trust lands and funds are also affected.
ai_sections.why: The old wording came from 1890, before modern investment tools existed. Updating the language makes the law match how trust funds actually generate income today.

title: Protecting Our Courts from Foreign Manipulation Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill creates new rules about "third-party litigation funding" in federal civil lawsuits — that is, when an outside party (not directly involved in the case) provides money to help fund a lawsuit in exchange for a share of any winnings. Under the bill, if that outside funder is a foreign person, foreign government, or government-owned investment fund, the parties and their lawyers must formally disclose that arrangement to the court, to the other side in the lawsuit, and to the U.S. Attorney General. Going further, the bill completely **bans** foreign governments and their sovereign wealth funds from funding U.S. federal lawsuits in this way at all — any agreement that violates this ban would be legally void and unenforceable. The bill affects anyone involved in a federal civil lawsuit where outside foreign money is helping pay for the litigation, including the parties themselves, their attorneys, and the foreign funders. The Attorney General is also required to report to Congress each year on how much foreign litigation funding is occurring in federal courts and who is behind it.
ai_headline: Federal courts would bar foreign-funded civil lawsuits
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 15 - 11.

ai_sections.tldr: Foreign governments would be banned from funding U.S. federal lawsuits, and other foreign funders must be disclosed to the court.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets new rules for when a foreign person, foreign government, or government-owned fund pays to support a federal lawsuit in exchange for a share of the winnings. Foreign governments and their sovereign wealth funds would be completely banned from doing this, and any agreement that breaks that rule would be legally void. All other foreign funding arrangements must be formally reported to the court, to the other side in the case, and to the U.S. Attorney General.
ai_sections.who: These rules apply to anyone involved in a federal civil lawsuit where outside foreign money is helping pay for it, including the parties, their lawyers, and the foreign funders. The U.S. Attorney General is also required to send Congress a yearly report on how much foreign litigation funding is happening in federal courts and who is behind it.
ai_sections.why: Without these rules, foreign governments could quietly fund U.S. federal lawsuits without the court or opposing side knowing. The ban and disclosure requirements are meant to make that kind of involvement visible or stop it altogether.

title: Weather Act Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill renews and expands the federal government's programs for weather forecasting, research, and public warning systems, primarily directing the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) to continue and improve its work on predicting dangerous weather events like tornadoes, hurricanes, tsunamis, floods, landslides, and atmospheric rivers. It authorizes specific funding amounts for weather research labs, tornado and hurricane forecast improvement programs, and tsunami warning systems through 2030, and it directs NOAA to modernize its tools — including upgrading radar systems, expanding use of artificial intelligence and advanced computing, and building out a network of uncrewed aircraft and ocean vessels to collect data. The bill also opens more opportunities for private companies to sell weather data to the government and encourages partnerships between NOAA and commercial satellite and data providers. It affects a wide range of people: federal weather scientists and forecasters, emergency managers, farmers, pilots, coastal communities, and the general public who rely on accurate weather warnings to protect their lives and property.
ai_headline: Bill renews NOAA weather forecasting and warning programs
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would renew NOAA's weather forecasting and warning programs through 2030, adding AI tools, new radar, and more private-sector data deals.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews and expands federal weather forecasting, research, and public warning programs run by NOAA through 2030. It directs NOAA to upgrade radar systems, use artificial intelligence and advanced computing, and deploy uncrewed aircraft and ocean vessels to collect weather data. It also allows more private companies to sell weather data to the government and encourages partnerships with commercial satellite providers.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects NOAA scientists and forecasters, emergency managers, farmers, pilots, and coastal communities. The general public who depends on weather warnings for tornadoes, hurricanes, tsunamis, floods, and landslides is also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without reauthorization, federal weather research programs and warning systems could lose their legal authority and dedicated funding. Continued gaps in weather prediction can affect public safety decisions made by emergency managers and individuals in the path of dangerous storms.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes specific funding amounts for weather research labs, tornado and hurricane forecast improvement programs, and tsunami warning systems, though exact dollar figures are not provided here.

title: Farm, Food, and National Security Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill reauthorizes and updates a wide range of federal agricultural programs run by the U.S. Department of Agriculture, extending most of them through 2031. It affects farmers, ranchers, rural communities, food assistance recipients, and the broader agricultural industry by continuing or modifying programs that provide financial support for crop production, conservation practices, farm loans, rural development, food aid, crop insurance, forestry, and agricultural research. It also adds new provisions addressing national security concerns around foreign ownership of U.S. farmland, expands food assistance rules (such as allowing hot rotisserie chicken and animal protein to be purchased with SNAP benefits), and strengthens support for specialty crops, dairy farmers, rural broadband, and renewable energy in farming. In short, it is a comprehensive update to the laws governing how the federal government supports American agriculture and rural life for the next several years.
ai_headline: House farm bill extends USDA programs through 2031
last_action_text: Committee of the Whole House on the state of the Union rises leaving H.R. 7567 as unfinished business.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would renew the main federal law governing U.S. farm and food programs through 2031, with several new rules added.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews and updates the federal laws that govern how the U.S. government supports farming, food aid, and rural communities, extending most programs through 2031. It keeps existing programs running and adds new rules on topics like foreign ownership of U.S. farmland, what food SNAP benefits can buy, and renewable energy on farms.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects farmers, ranchers, rural communities, and people who receive food assistance. It also touches the broader agricultural industry, including specialty crop growers, dairy farmers, and those working in forestry and agricultural research.
ai_sections.why: Without reauthorization, many of these federal agricultural programs would expire, potentially interrupting support for crop production, farm loans, conservation, and food aid. The new provisions would change what is allowed under programs like SNAP and add restrictions related to foreign ownership of farmland.

title: Veterans STAND Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to offer a free, voluntary annual health check-up to any veteran who has a spinal cord injury or disorder. During that yearly evaluation — which can be done in person, by referral, or through telehealth — the VA must assess the veteran for risks related to their injury, chronic pain, diet and weight management, and whether they have the right equipment and technology to help them live as independently as possible, including newer devices like powered exoskeletons and non-invasive spinal stimulation tools. The bill also directs the VA to make sure these veterans are informed every year that this evaluation is available to them, and it requires the VA to report to Congress periodically on how many veterans are receiving and being assessed for these assistive technologies. Regional VA networks would be evaluated in part on how well they provide these check-ups, giving the VA an internal incentive to follow through.
ai_headline: VA must offer annual exams to spinal cord veterans
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: The VA would be required to offer free yearly health check-ups to veterans with spinal cord injuries or disorders.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Department of Veterans Affairs to provide a free, voluntary yearly health evaluation to veterans with spinal cord injuries or disorders. The check-up could happen in person, by referral, or through telehealth, and would cover pain, diet, weight, injury-related risks, and access to assistive devices like powered exoskeletons.
ai_sections.who: Veterans with spinal cord injuries or disorders would be eligible for these yearly evaluations. The VA would be responsible for conducting them, notifying veterans annually that they are available, and reporting to Congress on how many veterans receive them.
ai_sections.why: Without this requirement, veterans with spinal cord conditions may not be consistently offered structured yearly care or information about newer assistive technologies. Regional VA networks would also be evaluated on how well they deliver these check-ups, creating an internal accountability measure.

title: Lewis and Clark Regional Water System Expansion Feasibility Study Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Secretary of the Interior to study whether it is practical and worthwhile to expand the Lewis and Clark Regional Water System — an existing water supply network — to serve more municipal, rural, and industrial water users across Iowa, Minnesota, and South Dakota. The Secretary would work alongside Lewis and Clark Regional Water System, Inc. and consult with federal, state, tribal, and local authorities during the study. Once the study is complete, the Secretary must write a report recommending whether the expansion project should be approved for construction and what share of construction costs (at least 25%) should be paid by the non-federal partner, based on that partner's ability to pay. Up to $10 million in federal funds may be used, with the federal government covering no more than half the study's total cost, and the entire study authority expires 10 years after the bill becomes law. People most directly affected are residents, businesses, and communities in those three states who currently lack access to the Lewis and Clark system or need greater water capacity.
ai_headline: Interior Dept. to study Lewis and Clark water expansion
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would order a study on expanding an existing water system in Iowa, Minnesota, and South Dakota, with up to $10M in federal funds.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells the U.S. Secretary of the Interior to study whether expanding the Lewis and Clark Regional Water System is practical and worthwhile. The study would look at serving more municipal, rural, and industrial water users across Iowa, Minnesota, and South Dakota. When the study is done, the Secretary must write a report recommending whether the expansion should be built and how costs should be shared.
ai_sections.who: Residents, businesses, and communities in Iowa, Minnesota, and South Dakota who do not currently have access to the Lewis and Clark system or need more water capacity are most directly affected. The Secretary of the Interior would lead the study alongside Lewis and Clark Regional Water System, Inc., consulting with federal, state, tribal, and local authorities.
ai_sections.why: Without this study, there would be no formal federal review of whether the water system can or should be expanded. The study's findings would shape whether construction is ever approved and how costs would be divided between federal and non-federal partners.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $10 million in federal funds may be used, but the federal government may cover no more than half the study's total cost. The non-federal partner must pay at least 25% of any eventual construction costs, based on that partner's ability to pay. The entire study authority ends 10 years after the bill becomes law.
costChips: Up to $10M federal funds, max 50% of study

title: TRUE Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill requires major federal agencies to create written emergency spending plans ahead of time — before any crisis like a disaster, pandemic, or economic emergency actually hits — so that safeguards against wasteful or fraudulent spending are ready to go the moment Congress releases emergency funds. The Office of Management and Budget (OMB) would write guidelines for these plans within 180 days, drawing on existing government anti-fraud best practices, and each agency would then have one year to submit its own plan to OMB. Each plan must name a senior official personally responsible for overseeing it, spell out how the agency will spot and reduce the risk of improper payments and fraud, and describe real-time tools — like data monitoring and anomaly detection — to catch problems quickly. OMB would send all the agency plans to Congress annually, along with a summary and any suggestions for improving future emergency spending laws. No new money is provided to carry out the bill, so agencies would need to use their existing budgets.
ai_headline: Federal agencies must pre-plan emergency fraud controls
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies would have to write anti-fraud emergency spending plans before a crisis hits, not after.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires major federal agencies to prepare written plans for preventing fraud and wasteful spending before any emergency occurs. Each plan must name a senior official in charge, describe how the agency will detect improper payments, and outline real-time monitoring tools. The Office of Management and Budget would set guidelines, collect all agency plans, and report them to Congress every year.
ai_sections.who: Major federal agencies would create the plans, and the Office of Management and Budget would oversee and set the rules. A named senior official at each agency would be personally responsible for carrying out the plan.
ai_sections.why: Without pre-made plans, agencies have had to build fraud safeguards from scratch after emergencies begin, when money is already moving quickly. This bill is a response to that timing problem.

title: National Veterans Strategy Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill requires the President to create a formal definition of what it means for a veteran to succeed after military service, measured by specific standards covering physical health, mental health, spiritual health, economic security, education, family life, and civic participation. Working with a wide range of partners — including multiple federal agencies, state and local governments, tribal organizations, veterans groups, colleges, businesses, and nonprofits — the President must develop a written National Veterans Strategy at least every four years that lays out how all these different organizations should work together to help veterans meet those standards. The strategy must also explain how to evaluate whether programs are actually working, and the President must send Congress a progress report every year. Congress retains the ability to reject any submitted strategy through a formal disapproval vote within 60 days of receiving it. The bill primarily affects veterans of all backgrounds and demographics, the federal agencies that serve them, and the state, local, nonprofit, and private-sector organizations that provide veterans' services.
ai_headline: Senate bill sets national goals for veteran success
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill requires the President to build a national plan every four years to help veterans succeed, with yearly progress reports to Congress.
ai_sections.what: The bill requires the President to define what veteran success looks like across health, family, education, finances, and civic life. Every four years, a written National Veterans Strategy must be created with input from federal agencies, states, tribes, veterans groups, schools, businesses, and nonprofits. The strategy must include a way to measure whether programs are working, and Congress can reject any submitted strategy within 60 days.
ai_sections.who: Veterans of all backgrounds are the primary focus of this bill. Federal agencies, state and local governments, tribal organizations, colleges, businesses, and nonprofits that serve veterans are also directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without a formal strategy, efforts to support veterans across many different organizations may lack coordination or shared goals. This bill creates a structure meant to align those efforts and make it easier to track whether they are working.

title: FRAUD in VA Disability Exams Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to actively look for fraud in the medical questionnaires that are used to determine veterans' disability benefits, and to report suspected fraud to investigative bodies like the VA's Inspector General. The VA must set up a system for its claims processors to flag suspicious questionnaires, conduct regular audits of those questionnaires, and notify veterans when their own questionnaire has raised a fraud concern. Importantly, the bill protects veterans by prohibiting the VA from reopening or changing a veteran's approved benefits decision based on a fraud investigation — unless the veteran has actually been convicted of fraud in court. The bill affects VA claims processors, the VA Inspector General, and veterans who have filed disability claims, particularly those whose paperwork may come from third-party providers. The VA must also report to Congress at least once a year on how these fraud-detection efforts are working.
ai_headline: VA must flag fraud in veterans' disability questionnaires
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: The VA must search for fraud in disability exam paperwork, but cannot cut a veteran's benefits unless they are convicted in court.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells the VA to look for fraud in the medical questionnaires used to decide veterans' disability benefits. The VA must set up a flagging system, run regular audits, and report suspected fraud to investigators. Veterans must be told if their own paperwork is under suspicion, and their approved benefits cannot be changed because of a fraud investigation unless they are convicted of fraud in court.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects VA claims processors, the VA Inspector General, and veterans who have filed disability claims. Veterans who used third-party providers to fill out their paperwork may be especially affected.
ai_sections.why: If fraud goes undetected in disability exam paperwork, the benefits system may pay out claims that were falsified. This bill creates oversight tools meant to catch that, while also setting a legal limit on when a veteran's benefits can be taken away.

title: Mammography Access for Veterans Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) to make mammography — a type of breast cancer screening — available to veterans in every U.S. state and Puerto Rico within two years of the law taking effect. The VA must offer at least one of three options in each location: a telescreening mammography program (where imaging is read remotely by specialists), a full mammography program inside a VA facility, or a mobile mammography unit that travels to veterans. The bill also turns what was previously a temporary pilot program for telescreening mammography into a permanent, ongoing VA program. Any mammography programs offered under this law must be physically accessible to veterans with paralysis, spinal cord injuries, or other disabilities.
ai_headline: VA must expand mammography access to veterans nationwide
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: The VA would be required to offer breast cancer screening to veterans in every state and Puerto Rico within two years.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the VA to provide mammography screening to veterans across all U.S. states and Puerto Rico within two years of becoming law. Each location must offer at least one of three options: remote reading of scans by specialists, an in-person program at a VA facility, or a mobile unit. It also makes a telescreening pilot program permanent, and requires all mammography services to be accessible to veterans with disabilities.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to the Department of Veterans Affairs and the veterans it serves in all U.S. states and Puerto Rico.
ai_sections.why: Without this law, mammography access through the VA is not guaranteed in every location. Veterans with disabilities could be affected if screening sites are not physically accessible to them.

title: Health Care for Homeless Veterans Act
ai_summary: This bill makes permanent a Department of Veterans Affairs program that provides mental health treatment and rehabilitation services to veterans who are both seriously mentally ill and homeless. Right now, the VA's authority to run this program has an expiration date built into the law — this bill removes that expiration so the program can continue indefinitely without Congress needing to renew it. The change affects homeless veterans who rely on these VA services for mental health care and rehabilitation support. In practical terms, the bill simply eliminates a sunset provision, ensuring the program cannot lapse due to inaction by Congress in the future.
ai_headline: VA homeless veterans mental health program made permanent
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4043 makes a VA mental health and rehab program for homeless veterans permanent by removing its expiration date.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes the expiration date from a Department of Veterans Affairs program. That program provides mental health treatment and rehabilitation services to veterans who are seriously mentally ill and homeless. Without this change, Congress would need to renew the program periodically to keep it running.
ai_sections.who: This affects homeless veterans who are seriously mentally ill and rely on this VA program for mental health care and rehabilitation support.
ai_sections.why: If the expiration date stays in the law, the program could lapse if Congress does not act in time to renew it. Making it permanent means the program continues without needing future congressional action.

title: A bill to prohibit the Secretary of Labor from finalizing, implementing, or enforcing a proposed standard with respect to heat injury and illness prevention, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would permanently block the U.S. Department of Labor from completing, putting into effect, or enforcing a workplace safety rule that the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) proposed in August 2024, which was designed to protect workers from heat-related injuries and illnesses in both outdoor and indoor settings. The bill also prevents OSHA from issuing any other rule that is substantially similar to that proposal. The proposed rule that would be blocked had included requirements such as scheduled rest breaks in high heat, procedures to help workers gradually adjust to working in the heat (called acclimatization), and written heat safety plans for employers. The bill would affect any employer that OSHA oversees — across all industries and work environments — by ensuring that no new federal heat safety standards based on the 2024 proposal can be created or enforced. Workers in jobs with significant heat exposure, such as construction, agriculture, manufacturing, and warehousing, would also be directly affected, as the federal protections that rule was intended to provide would not go into effect.
ai_headline: Senate bill bars OSHA heat safety rule from taking effect
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would permanently stop the federal government from ever finishing or enforcing the 2024 OSHA heat safety rule for workplaces.
ai_sections.what: This bill would stop the Department of Labor from completing, enforcing, or even issuing a similar version of a heat safety rule that OSHA proposed in 2024. That rule had called for rest breaks in high heat, plans to help workers adjust to heat over time, and written heat safety plans from employers. The bill would block that rule and any future rule that looks substantially like it.
ai_sections.who: Every employer that OSHA oversees, across all industries, would be affected because no new federal heat safety standard based on that 2024 proposal could be created or enforced. Workers in high-heat jobs such as construction, agriculture, manufacturing, and warehousing would also be directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without this rule going into effect, the federal protections it was designed to provide against heat-related injuries and illnesses would not exist. Workers in those jobs would not gain the specific safeguards the 2024 proposal had outlined.

title: To nullify certain rules related to foreign assistance.
ai_summary: This bill would cancel three specific federal rules issued by the Department of State in January 2026 that place certain restrictions and requirements on U.S. foreign aid programs. The three rules being canceled deal with: (1) policies described as "protecting life" in foreign assistance (which relate to abortion-related restrictions on aid recipients), (2) policies targeting what the rules call "discriminatory equity ideology," and (3) policies targeting what the rules call "gender ideology" in foreign assistance. If passed, these rules would be treated as if they never existed, and no federal agency would be allowed to enforce them or create similar replacement rules. The bill primarily affects U.S. government agencies that distribute foreign aid — such as the State Department — as well as the foreign organizations and governments that receive that aid and would have been subject to the canceled rules.
ai_headline: Bill would repeal three State Dept. foreign aid rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8582 would cancel three State Department rules from January 2026 that set restrictions on U.S. foreign aid programs.
ai_sections.what: This bill would cancel three federal rules issued by the State Department in January 2026. Those rules placed restrictions on foreign aid related to abortion policies, "discriminatory equity ideology," and "gender ideology." If the bill passes, the rules would be treated as if they never existed, and no agency could enforce them or issue similar rules in their place.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects U.S. agencies that distribute foreign aid, especially the State Department. It also affects foreign organizations and governments that receive U.S. aid and were subject to the canceled rules.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the restrictions those three rules placed on aid recipients would no longer apply. Agencies would also be blocked from creating replacement rules with similar requirements.

title: Reducing Arbitrary Barriers to Apprenticeship Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill changes how veterans' education benefits are paid out when a veteran chooses an apprenticeship or on-the-job training program instead of a traditional college degree. Under current law, veterans in these programs receive a reduced housing stipend compared to those attending college full-time; this bill would fix that by paying apprentices the full housing allowance based on where their employer is located, putting them on equal financial footing with college-going veterans. The bill also removes a minimum attendance requirement — meaning veterans in construction-trade apprenticeships (such as carpentry, electrical work, or plumbing) would still receive their monthly benefits even in months when they fall below a certain number of training hours, a flexibility not previously available. These changes apply to veterans using Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits, veterans using the Montgomery GI Bill (active duty), and members of the Selected Reserve using their education benefits.
ai_headline: GI Bill apprenticeship benefits would match college rates
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: Veterans in apprenticeships would get the same full housing allowance as veterans attending college under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill would give veterans in apprenticeship or on-the-job training programs the same full housing allowance as veterans attending college, based on where their employer is located. It would also let veterans in construction-trade apprenticeships keep receiving their monthly benefits even in months when their training hours fall below the current minimum.
ai_sections.who: These changes would apply to veterans using Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits, veterans using the Montgomery GI Bill on active duty, and members of the Selected Reserve using their education benefits.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, veterans in apprenticeships receive a lower housing stipend than those in college, and can lose monthly benefits in low-hour months. This bill would remove those two differences in how the programs are treated.

title: To require the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to establish an education program on unleaded aviation gasoline, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to create and run a public education program about unleaded aviation gasoline — the fuel used in small, non-commercial aircraft — within one year of the law taking effect. The program must publish easy-to-find information online covering which unleaded fuels are approved for use, how compatible those fuels are with different aircraft and engines, safety guidance, and any available tax credits or other government incentives. The FAA must also work with the aviation industry and fuel suppliers to offer annual hands-on training for the people who handle and dispense aviation fuel at small airports. The program affects a wide range of people in general aviation, including pilots, flight instructors, aircraft owners, mechanics, airport managers, and fuel service providers. The program would run until the end of 2036, and the FAA must periodically report to Congress on how the program is going, including how much unleaded fuel is being sold compared to the traditional leaded fuel known as 100LL.
ai_headline: FAA ordered to educate pilots on unleaded aviation fuel
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: The FAA must launch a public education program on unleaded aviation fuel within one year and run it through 2036.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the FAA to create a public education program about unleaded aviation gasoline within one year of becoming law. The program must post clear online information about approved fuels, aircraft compatibility, safety guidance, and available government incentives. The FAA must also partner with the aviation industry and fuel suppliers to offer annual hands-on training for people who handle fuel at small airports.
ai_sections.who: The program is aimed at people involved in general aviation, including pilots, flight instructors, aircraft owners, mechanics, airport managers, and fuel service providers. The FAA must periodically report to Congress on the program's progress, including data on unleaded versus leaded fuel sales.
ai_sections.why: Small aircraft have traditionally relied on leaded fuel known as 100LL, and this program is meant to help the people who work with that fuel understand their unleaded options. Without clear information and training, the transition to unleaded fuel could be slower or less safe.

title: To strengthen the public-private partnerships and policy efforts of the Department of State to advance women's economic security in South and Central Asia, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Secretary of State to maintain and grow partnerships between the U.S. government, businesses, universities, and civil society groups aimed at improving women's economic opportunities in South and Central Asian countries. It creates a new unit inside the State Department's Bureau of South and Central Asian Affairs, led by a Special Advisor for Women's Economic Security with the rank of Ambassador, who would oversee these partnerships and report to senior State Department leadership. The partnerships are meant to help women in the region gain better access to jobs, entrepreneurship resources, education, financial services, mentorship, and ownership of assets. U.S. diplomatic posts in relevant countries would also be required to designate a staff member to track private-sector commitments made through these partnerships. Within one year of the bill becoming law, the State Department would need to report to Congress on what steps were taken, what partnerships exist, and what economic impact the work has had.
ai_headline: State Dept. would gain women's economic envoy for Asia
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: The State Department would create a new unit and a senior Ambassador-level role focused on women's economic opportunities in South and Central Asia.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Secretary of State to build and maintain partnerships with businesses, universities, and civil society groups to expand women's access to jobs, education, financial services, and entrepreneurship in South and Central Asia. A new unit would be created inside the Bureau of South and Central Asian Affairs, led by a Special Advisor for Women's Economic Security at the Ambassador rank. Within one year of passage, the State Department would report to Congress on partnerships formed and their economic impact.
ai_sections.who: The Secretary of State and a newly appointed Special Advisor for Women's Economic Security would lead this effort. U.S. diplomatic posts in the relevant countries would each need to assign a staff member to track private-sector commitments.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the State Department would take on new reporting and coordination duties related to women's economic conditions in the region. Congress would receive a formal update on results within one year.

title: To reauthorize the active transportation infrastructure investment program, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would continue federal funding for a program that builds sidewalks, bike lanes, trails, and other infrastructure meant for people who walk, bike, or use other non-motorized ways of getting around. Specifically, it would authorize $250 million per year from the Highway Trust Fund for each year from 2027 through 2031 — a total of up to $1.25 billion over five years — to keep the program running after its current funding period ends. The money cannot be moved to other programs and does not expire until it is spent. The people most directly affected are communities and local governments that apply for these grants to build or improve walking and biking infrastructure, as well as residents who use those facilities.
ai_headline: Bill would fund walking and biking trails through 2031
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to keep federal funding alive for sidewalk, bike lane, and trail projects through 2031.
ai_sections.what: This bill would renew a federal program that pays for sidewalks, bike lanes, trails, and other paths for people who walk, bike, or use other non-motorized ways of getting around. It would keep the program running after its current funding period ends.
ai_sections.who: Local governments and communities that apply for these grants would be directly affected, as would residents who use the walking and biking infrastructure those grants help build or improve.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, the program would lose its federal funding when the current period ends. The bill sets aside dedicated money that cannot be moved to other programs and does not expire until it is spent.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize $250 million per year from the Highway Trust Fund for each year from 2027 through 2031, for a total of up to $1.25 billion over five years.

title: To prohibit the enforcement of certain contractual clauses that restrict disclosure of sexual abuse of minors, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill makes it illegal to enforce any contract clause that prevents a victim of child sexual abuse — or someone supporting that victim — from speaking out about the abuse. In plain terms, if someone was sexually abused as a minor, any "keep quiet" or confidentiality agreement they signed (or that was signed on their behalf) cannot be used to stop them from telling law enforcement, child protection agencies, courts, or anyone else about what happened. The bill applies to contracts already in existence, not just new ones, meaning old settlement or nondisclosure agreements cannot be used to silence survivors going forward. Importantly, the bill still allows contracts to keep certain details confidential — such as the dollar amount of a legal settlement — as long as those restrictions do not prevent a survivor from disclosing the abuse itself. This affects sexual abuse survivors who are minors or were minors at the time of abuse, people with knowledge of such abuse, and anyone — such as an institution or alleged abuser — who might otherwise try to enforce a secrecy clause against them.
ai_headline: NDAs shielding child sex abuse lose legal enforceability
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Silence agreements cannot be used to stop child sexual abuse survivors from speaking out, even if the contract already exists.
ai_sections.what: This bill makes it illegal to enforce any contract clause that stops a child sexual abuse survivor, or someone supporting them, from disclosing the abuse. This applies to old contracts too, not just new ones. Contracts can still keep certain details private, like a settlement amount, as long as they do not block the survivor from describing the abuse itself.
ai_sections.who: This affects people who were sexually abused as minors and anyone with knowledge of that abuse. It also applies to institutions, alleged abusers, or anyone else who might try to use a secrecy clause to prevent disclosure.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, existing nondisclosure agreements could be used to stop survivors from reporting abuse to law enforcement, child protection agencies, or courts. This bill removes that legal barrier going forward.

title: A bill to establish requirements for financial aid offers made by institutions of higher education, and for other purposes.
