The bill does not prevent doctors from discussing treatment options or referring patients to mental health professionals, and it carves out exceptions for treatments related to certain biological conditions, medical emergencies, and naturally occurring early puberty.
ai_headline: Bill would ban gender care for minors under 18
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would ban doctors and hospitals from providing gender transition care to anyone under 18, with fines and lawsuits as enforcement.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal for doctors, hospitals, or other providers to give gender transition medical care to anyone under 18. That includes puberty blockers, cross-sex hormones, and related surgeries. Exceptions exist for medical emergencies, certain biological conditions, and naturally occurring early puberty, and providers may still discuss options or refer patients to mental health professionals.
ai_sections.who: Only providers and those who assist them can face penalties. Minors and their parents or guardians are fully protected from any fines or punishment under this bill.
ai_sections.why: Providers who violate the ban could face federal fines and civil lawsuits. Affected minors or their families could sue in federal court for up to 25 years after the minor turns 18.
ai_sections.cost: Fines start at $100,000 per violation and are paid by providers. Fine money would go into a special fund for grants to nonprofit organizations that help people reverse prior gender transition procedures, offering medical, mental health, and employment services.
costChips: Fine funds go to detransition nonprofits

title: Federal Fraud Prevention Workforce Training Act
ai_summary: This bill would require federal employees who manage government programs, handle payments, or oversee grants to complete a training course on how to detect and prevent fraud and improper payments — and to repeat that training at least every two years. The training would be developed jointly by the Treasury Department and the Office of Management and Budget, and would cover topics like spotting fraud risks, using government tools to verify payments, setting up internal controls, and reporting suspected waste or abuse. State, local, and tribal government workers who run programs funded by federal money could also access the same training, and federal agencies would have the option to make it a requirement for anyone receiving a federal grant. The government would be authorized to spend up to $5 million per year to run the program, and officials would be required to report to Congress every year on how well the program is working and how many people have completed it.
ai_headline: Federal workers would train to detect improper payments
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

ai_sections.tldr: Federal workers who manage programs or payments would be required to take fraud-detection training every two years under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require certain federal employees to complete a training course on spotting and preventing fraud and improper payments. The training would need to be repeated at least every two years. It would cover topics like identifying fraud risks, verifying payments, setting up internal controls, and reporting suspected waste or abuse.
ai_sections.who: Federal employees who manage government programs, handle payments, or oversee grants would be required to take the training. State, local, and tribal government workers running federally funded programs could also access it, and agencies could require it for federal grant recipients.
ai_sections.why: Without consistent training, employees across agencies may apply different or incomplete standards when reviewing payments and grants. This bill would create a shared, government-wide approach to fraud detection and require annual reports to Congress on how the program is working.
ai_sections.cost: The government would be authorized to spend up to $5 million per year to run the program.

title: Timely and Accurate Benefits Act
ai_summary: This bill would require every state, U.S. territory, and the District of Columbia to start using a new computerized income-checking system within one year, or risk losing federal funding for any benefit program that uses income to determine who qualifies or how much they receive — programs like Medicaid, food assistance, housing aid, and others. The system, called an Enhanced Income Verification Platform, would automatically cross-check applicants' reported income against a wide range of sources, including wages, gig economy earnings, rental income, child support, gifts, and bank account transaction data, to look for income that may have been unreported or underreported. Applicants would be able to give permission for the system to access their bank account transactions, and would have the option to review and confirm that the data is accurate. The bill affects state governments, which must adopt the system, as well as any person who applies for or receives a federal benefit that is based on their income.
ai_headline: States must adopt new income verification system for benefits
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held

ai_sections.tldr: States must adopt a new income-checking system within one year or risk losing federal funding for benefit programs.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require states, U.S. territories, and Washington D.C. to use a new computer system called an Enhanced Income Verification Platform. The system would automatically compare what applicants report as their income against sources like wages, gig work, rental income, child support, gifts, and bank account transactions. Applicants could choose to allow access to their bank account data and would have a chance to review the information for accuracy.
ai_sections.who: State governments would be required to adopt the new system. Anyone who applies for or receives a federal benefit based on their income would be affected by this change.
ai_sections.why: States that do not adopt the system within one year could lose federal funding for benefit programs that use income to decide who qualifies or how much they receive, such as Medicaid, food assistance, and housing aid.

title: A bill to increase cooperation with countries in the Eastern Mediterranean region in order to strengthen energy security and defense capabilities, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. government to deepen its diplomatic, energy, and defense relationships with four specific countries — Egypt, Greece, Cyprus, and Israel — as part of a broader effort to build a trade and infrastructure corridor linking India, the Middle East, and Europe. The Secretary of State is required to make the Eastern Mediterranean a priority in U.S. foreign policy, and may create formal, ongoing multilateral talks with these countries. Within one year of passage, several government reports must be produced, covering topics such as energy projects in the region, the usefulness of a security center in Cyprus as a model for wider cooperation, and whether research-and-development partnership programs that currently exist between the U.S. and Israel could be created or expanded to include other countries in the region. The bill affects U.S. diplomatic and energy officials, the governments of Egypt, Greece, Cyprus, and Israel, and — indirectly — other nations involved in the India-Middle East-Europe corridor such as India, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and several European countries.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets Eastern Mediterranean foreign policy
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: The U.S. would deepen ties with Egypt, Greece, Cyprus, and Israel on energy and defense under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the U.S. to strengthen diplomatic, energy, and defense relationships with Egypt, Greece, Cyprus, and Israel. It requires the Secretary of State to make the Eastern Mediterranean a foreign policy priority and allows the creation of ongoing multilateral talks with those four countries. Within one year of passage, the government must produce reports on regional energy projects, a possible security center in Cyprus, and whether existing U.S.-Israel research partnerships could expand to include other countries nearby.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects U.S. diplomatic and energy officials, along with the governments of Egypt, Greece, Cyprus, and Israel. Countries involved in the India-Middle East-Europe corridor, including India, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and several European nations, would also be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Passing this bill would require specific government reports to be completed within one year and would shift U.S. foreign policy attention toward the Eastern Mediterranean. It could also lead to new or expanded multilateral agreements involving the named countries.

title: Justice for ALS Veterans Act of 2025
ai_summary: When a veteran dies from ALS (also known as Lou Gehrig's disease), this bill ensures that the veteran's surviving spouse receives a higher monthly payment — called Dependency and Indemnity Compensation — no matter how recently the veteran was diagnosed with ALS before dying. Under existing law, this increased payment is tied to how long a veteran had a qualifying condition, which could disqualify spouses of veterans who were diagnosed shortly before death. This bill removes that time requirement specifically for ALS, treating all ALS-related deaths the same way regardless of how long the veteran lived with the disease. The change would apply retroactively to veterans who died from ALS on or after October 1, 2022, meaning some surviving spouses may become newly eligible for benefits they were previously denied.
ai_headline: Senate bill expands DIC benefits for ALS survivors
last_action_text: Committee on Veterans' Affairs. Hearings held.

ai_sections.tldr: Surviving spouses of ALS veterans can receive higher monthly payments regardless of how long the veteran had the disease before dying.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes a time requirement for ALS-related deaths when deciding if a surviving spouse qualifies for higher Dependency and Indemnity Compensation payments. All ALS deaths are treated the same, no matter how soon after diagnosis the veteran died. The change applies retroactively to veterans who died from ALS on or after October 1, 2022.
ai_sections.who: This affects surviving spouses of veterans who died from ALS. Some spouses who were previously denied benefits may become newly eligible under the retroactive date.
ai_sections.why: Under existing law, the higher payment is tied to how long a veteran had a qualifying condition, which could leave out spouses of veterans diagnosed shortly before death. Removing that requirement for ALS means the length of time a veteran lived with the disease no longer affects whether their spouse qualifies.

title: Fallen Servicemembers Religious Heritage Restoration Act
ai_summary: This bill does two main things. First, it adds new oversight and accountability rules for how the FBI uses a federal surveillance law (known as Section 702) that allows the government to collect intelligence on foreign targets — the new rules require the FBI to get attorney approval before searching that collected data for information about Americans, send monthly reports to a civil liberties watchdog who can flag potential abuses, and impose criminal penalties of up to five years in prison on FBI employees who illegally search that data or lie about following the rules; it also extends that surveillance law through April 2029 and orders an independent audit of how it works. Second, it permanently prohibits the Federal Reserve from creating, testing, or issuing a central bank digital currency (a government-run digital dollar) — whether directly to individuals, through banks, or as a tool for setting monetary policy — though it does not restrict privately issued digital currencies that are open and protect user privacy. These provisions affect ordinary Americans whose personal data could be swept up in government surveillance, federal law enforcement and intelligence agencies, the Federal Reserve, and Congress members who oversee intelligence programs.
ai_headline: Senate bill tightens FBI surveillance rules, bars digital dollar
last_action_text: Message on House action received in Senate and at desk: House amendment to Senate bill.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill adds new FBI surveillance rules, extends a spying law to 2029, and permanently bans a government digital dollar.
ai_sections.what: The bill sets new rules for how the FBI can search data collected under a federal surveillance law, including requiring lawyer approval and monthly oversight reports, and extends that law through April 2029. It also permanently bars the Federal Reserve from creating or issuing a central bank digital currency in any form. Private digital currencies that are open and protect user privacy are not affected.
ai_sections.who: These rules affect ordinary Americans whose personal data could be caught up in government surveillance, as well as the FBI, the Federal Reserve, and members of Congress who oversee intelligence programs.
ai_sections.why: The new surveillance rules change how federal agents access data about Americans and create criminal penalties for FBI employees who break those rules or lie about following them. The digital currency ban prevents the Federal Reserve from ever launching or testing a government-run digital dollar.
ai_sections.cost: FBI employees who illegally search surveillance data or lie about following the rules could face criminal penalties of up to five years in prison.
costChips: FBI staff face up to 5 years in prison
For illegal searches or lying about rules

title: To designate a building of the Chancery of the United States in Pristina, Kosovo, as the "Eliot L. Engel Building".
ai_summary: This bill gives an official name to a specific U.S. government building located at the American Chancery (a diplomatic office complex) in Pristina, the capital of Kosovo — designating it the "Eliot L. Engel Building." Once enacted, all official U.S. government documents, maps, and records that refer to that building would use that new name. The bill does not change how the building operates, affect U.S. diplomatic activities, or involve any spending. It primarily affects how the U.S. government formally refers to this particular building in official records going forward.
ai_headline: U.S. diplomatic building in Kosovo named for Eliot Engel
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8562 officially names a U.S. diplomatic building in Pristina, Kosovo the "Eliot L. Engel Building."
ai_sections.what: This bill gives an official name to a building at the U.S. Chancery, a diplomatic office complex in Pristina, Kosovo. The building would be formally known as the "Eliot L. Engel Building" in all U.S. government records, maps, and documents going forward.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects how U.S. government agencies refer to this specific building in official records. It does not affect the staff or diplomatic work carried out there.
ai_sections.why: Once enacted, official U.S. documents and maps that reference this building must use the new name. No other operations or activities at the building are changed.

title: A joint resolution to direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Republic of Cuba that have not been authorized by Congress.
ai_summary: This resolution would require the President to pull U.S. military forces out of any fighting involving Cuba unless Congress has formally declared war on Cuba or passed a specific law approving that military action. It also covers the U.S. Coast Guard and specifically calls out actions like a naval blockade or quarantine of Cuba as the kind of military activity that would need congressional approval. The resolution makes clear, however, that it would not stop the U.S. from defending itself if America is attacked or faces the threat of an imminent attack, and it would not interfere with legal anti-drug operations. This measure primarily affects the President's authority to use military force, the U.S. Armed Forces, and any ongoing or future U.S. military operations related to Cuba.
ai_headline: Senate seeks approval rule for Cuba military action
last_action_text: The motion to discharge fell when the point of order was well taken.

ai_sections.tldr: This resolution would require Congress to approve any U.S. military action involving Cuba before the President can order it.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would require the President to remove U.S. military forces from any fighting involving Cuba unless Congress has formally declared war or passed a law approving the action. It covers the U.S. Coast Guard and specifically includes naval blockades or quarantines of Cuba as actions that would need congressional approval. Self-defense in response to an attack or imminent threat, and legal anti-drug operations, would still be allowed.
ai_sections.who: This resolution primarily affects the President's authority to use military force and the U.S. Armed Forces, including the Coast Guard. It would apply to any ongoing or future U.S. military operations related to Cuba.
ai_sections.why: If this resolution passes, the President would need to get approval from Congress before taking most military actions involving Cuba. Without that approval, U.S. forces involved in such hostilities would have to be withdrawn.

title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to allow for limited full expensing of certain reforestation expenditures.
ai_summary: This bill changes federal tax rules to make it less expensive — on paper — for landowners to replant trees on their property. First, it more than doubles the amount of reforestation spending that a landowner can immediately write off on their taxes in a single year, raising the limit from $10,000 to $30,000 (or from $5,000 to $15,000 for married couples filing separately), and it ties those new limits to inflation so they adjust over time. Second, it creates a brand-new, larger tax deduction specifically for landowners who need to replant trees on land where standing, unharvested timber was damaged or destroyed by a presidentially declared natural disaster — such as a wildfire, hurricane, or flood — within the past five years, allowing up to $500,000 per property and $1,000,000 total to be deducted, also adjusted for inflation going forward. However, if a landowner sells or transfers that disaster-replanted land within ten years of claiming the deduction, a portion of the tax benefit must be paid back. These changes apply to individual landowners, family forest owners, partnerships, S corporations, and controlled groups of businesses that incur qualifying reforestation costs starting in tax year 2027.
ai_headline: HR 8538 raises reforestation tax deductions for landowners
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2027, landowners can write off up to $30,000 a year in tree-replanting costs, or up to $500,000 after a declared natural disaster.
ai_sections.what: This bill raises the amount of tree-replanting costs a landowner can immediately deduct on their federal taxes, from $10,000 to $30,000 per year. It also creates a larger deduction for replanting on land where unharvested timber was destroyed by a presidentially declared natural disaster in the past five years, up to $500,000 per property and $1,000,000 total. Both limits adjust for inflation over time, and if disaster-replanted land is sold within ten years, part of the tax benefit must be paid back.
ai_sections.who: These changes apply to individual landowners, family forest owners, partnerships, S corporations, and certain business groups. They apply to qualifying reforestation costs starting in tax year 2027.
ai_sections.why: Raising the deduction limits means landowners pay less in federal taxes in the years they replant trees. The payback rule means landowners who sell disaster-replanted land within ten years will owe some of those tax savings back to the federal government.

title: A bill to amend the Countering America's Adversaries Through Sanctions Act to expand review by Congress of actions relating to sanctions imposed with respect to the Russian Federation.
ai_summary: This bill gives Congress greater power to review and potentially block actions by the executive branch that would ease or lift U.S. sanctions on Russia — particularly sanctions related to Russian energy products like oil, natural gas, and petroleum. Under current law, Congress can already review certain sanction-related decisions, but this bill expands that oversight to include any executive orders tied to the national emergency declared over Russia's harmful activities, and specifically covers decisions about energy trade with Russia (including special permits called licenses). The energy-related oversight lasts until the Secretary of State — working with other top officials — officially certifies that Russia has ended its war in Ukraine and made a credible commitment to a fair peace deal that includes compensating Ukraine for war damages. There is a narrow exception that allows energy-related actions to proceed without waiting for congressional review if they are strictly needed to protect the safety of a ship's crew, handle an emergency repair or environmental situation, or urgently address an economic impact in a country other than Russia. The bill primarily affects the executive branch's flexibility to adjust Russia sanctions, and it indirectly affects businesses or countries that might otherwise seek U.S. permission to trade in Russian energy products.
ai_headline: Senate bill tightens oversight of Russia sanctions relief
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would gain new power to review or block executive decisions to ease Russia sanctions, especially on energy trade, until Russia ends its war in Ukraine.
ai_sections.what: This bill expands Congress's ability to review and potentially block White House decisions that would loosen or remove U.S. sanctions on Russia. The expanded oversight specifically covers Russian energy products like oil, natural gas, and petroleum, including special trade permits. The energy oversight stays in place until the Secretary of State certifies that Russia has ended the war in Ukraine and made a credible commitment to a fair peace deal that includes compensating Ukraine.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects the executive branch's ability to adjust Russia sanctions on its own. It also indirectly affects businesses and other countries that might seek U.S. permission to trade in Russian energy products.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, the executive branch can make certain sanctions decisions with less congressional review. Adding oversight means those decisions could be delayed or blocked while Congress weighs in.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Environmental Protection Agency relating to "Modification to the Start of the Submission Period for Perfluoroalkyl and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) Reporting and Recordkeeping Under TSCA 8(a)(7)".
ai_summary: This resolution is Congress voting to cancel a rule that the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) published in April 2026. That EPA rule had changed the timeline for when certain businesses had to start submitting reports and records about PFAS chemicals — a large family of man-made chemicals sometimes called "forever chemicals" — under a federal law requiring companies to disclose information about them. If this resolution passes both the House and Senate and is signed into law, the EPA's timeline change would be wiped out and treated as if it never existed. The businesses and other entities that manufacture, process, or use PFAS chemicals would be most directly affected, since the reporting schedule they were following under the revised rule would no longer apply.
ai_headline: Senate moves to void EPA's PFAS reporting delay
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting to cancel an EPA rule that changed the deadline for businesses to report information about PFAS "forever chemicals."
ai_sections.what: This resolution is Congress voting to cancel a rule the EPA published in April 2026. That rule had shifted the timeline for when certain businesses had to start submitting reports about PFAS chemicals. If this resolution becomes law, the EPA's timeline change would be erased and treated as if it never existed.
ai_sections.who: Businesses and other entities that manufacture, process, or use PFAS chemicals would be most directly affected. The reporting schedule they were following under the revised EPA rule would no longer apply to them.
ai_sections.why: The reporting timeline that businesses had been using would revert to what it was before the EPA made its change. Companies would need to follow the original schedule for disclosing information about PFAS chemicals under federal law.

title: Increasing Access to Foster Care Through Age 21 Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it easier for young people who have been in the foster care system to continue receiving support past age 18, up through age 21 — and removes a rule that previously tied eligibility to whether the youth's parents or guardians had qualified for a specific older welfare program. States that choose to participate would be required to let eligible young adults voluntarily re-enter foster care if they left after turning 18, and to actively help them do so. Young people covered under certain adoption or guardianship assistance agreements — if those agreements began when they were at least 16 — would also be eligible for extended support up to the age the state selects (19, 20, or 21). The federal government would also be required to provide guidance to state caseworkers on how to connect these young adults to job training and workforce development programs.
ai_headline: Bill expands foster care aid for young adults to age 21
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8498 would let young adults stay in or return to foster care support up to age 21, dropping an old eligibility rule.
ai_sections.what: This bill would extend foster care support up to age 21 and remove the rule that tied eligibility to an older welfare program. States that join would have to let eligible young adults voluntarily re-enter foster care after age 18 and help them do so. Young people covered by certain adoption or guardianship agreements starting at age 16 or older could also receive extended support.
ai_sections.who: Young adults who were in foster care, as well as those under qualifying adoption or guardianship agreements starting at age 16 or older, would be affected. State caseworkers would also receive federal guidance on connecting these young adults to job training and workforce programs.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, some young adults lose foster care support at 18 and may not qualify for extended help under current rules. Removing the old eligibility requirement would allow more young people to remain connected to support and services.

title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Environmental Protection Agency relating to "National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants: Coal- and Oil-Fired Electric Utility Steam Generating Units: Final Repeal".
ai_summary: Congress is voting to block an Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) rule that would have eliminated air pollution limits for coal- and oil-fired power plants. Specifically, the EPA issued a rule in February 2026 that repealed existing standards controlling hazardous air pollutants — toxic substances like mercury and other harmful emissions — from those types of power plants. If this resolution passes both the House and Senate and is signed into law, that EPA repeal would be canceled, meaning the original pollution limits would remain in place. This would primarily affect companies that operate coal- and oil-fired power plants, as well as the communities living near those facilities who breathe the air those plants affect.
ai_headline: Senate moves to restore EPA power plant pollution limits
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to block an EPA rule that removed air pollution limits for coal- and oil-fired power plants.
ai_sections.what: The EPA issued a rule in February 2026 that removed existing limits on hazardous air pollutants — including mercury and other toxic emissions — from coal- and oil-fired power plants. This resolution would cancel that EPA rule, keeping the original pollution limits in place.
ai_sections.who: This affects companies that operate coal- and oil-fired power plants. It also affects communities living near those plants who breathe the air the plants impact.
ai_sections.why: If the resolution passes and is signed into law, the original pollution limits stay in effect. If it does not pass, the EPA rule removing those limits remains in place.

title: A joint resolution proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States relative to the fundamental right to vote.
ai_summary: This resolution proposes adding a new amendment to the U.S. Constitution that would give every American citizen of legal voting age a fundamental right to vote in public elections where they live. Under the proposed amendment, neither the federal government nor any state or local government could restrict that right unless it could prove it had a critically important reason for doing so and that its restriction was the most limited, least burdensome way possible to achieve that reason — a very high legal bar to clear. The proposal also removes a provision currently in the Constitution that allows states to take away voting rights from people convicted of crimes other than participating in a rebellion against the government, which would limit states' ability to strip voting rights from people with felony convictions. Congress would be given the power to pass laws to enforce and protect these voting rights. For this amendment to actually become part of the Constitution, it would need to be approved by two-thirds of both the Senate and House of Representatives and then ratified by at least three-fourths of all state legislatures.
ai_headline: Senate resolution seeks constitutional right to vote
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary. (text: CR S2055)

ai_sections.tldr: Proposed constitutional amendment would make voting a guaranteed right for U.S. citizens and limit governments' power to restrict it.
ai_sections.what: This resolution proposes a new constitutional amendment that would give every U.S. citizen of legal voting age a fundamental right to vote in local public elections. No federal, state, or local government could restrict that right unless it could prove a critically important reason and show its restriction was the least burdensome option possible. The proposal would also limit states' ability to take away voting rights from people with felony convictions.
ai_sections.who: The amendment would affect all U.S. citizens of legal voting age. To become law, it would need approval from two-thirds of both the Senate and House of Representatives, then ratification by at least three-fourths of all state legislatures.
ai_sections.why: If passed, governments that want to restrict voting would face a very high legal standard to justify those restrictions. States would also lose some of their current authority to strip voting rights from people convicted of certain crimes.

title: Supporting Energy and Economic Development (SEED) Act
ai_summary: This bill extends existing federal tax breaks for biodiesel and renewable diesel fuel — fuels made from sources like vegetable oils, animal fats, or recycled grease — that had been set to expire after 2024, keeping them in place through 2029. These incentives come in two forms: a credit that reduces income taxes for businesses that produce or blend these fuels, and credits or payments that reduce or refund certain federal fuel excise taxes. The bill also adds a rule to prevent anyone from claiming both these biodiesel/renewable diesel tax breaks *and* a separate, newer clean fuel tax credit (known as the "45Z" credit) for the same batch of fuel — they can only use one or the other. The people most directly affected are fuel producers, blenders, and distributors who work with biodiesel and renewable diesel, as well as farmers and other suppliers who provide the raw materials for these fuels.
ai_headline: Bill extends biodiesel tax credits through 2029
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would extend biodiesel and renewable diesel tax breaks through 2029, but businesses could not use those breaks and the newer 45Z clean fuel credit on the same fuel.
ai_sections.what: This bill extends federal tax breaks for biodiesel and renewable diesel fuel through 2029. Those breaks had been set to expire after 2024. It also adds a rule that prevents anyone from claiming both these tax breaks and a separate clean fuel tax credit, known as the 45Z credit, for the same batch of fuel.
ai_sections.who: Fuel producers, blenders, and distributors who work with biodiesel and renewable diesel are most directly affected. Farmers and other suppliers who provide raw materials like vegetable oils, animal fats, or recycled grease are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without the extension, these tax breaks would have ended after 2024. The new rule means businesses must choose between two available tax credits rather than using both at once for the same fuel.

title: A joint resolution to direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that have not been authorized by Congress.
ai_summary: This resolution would order the President to pull U.S. military forces out of any fighting involving Iran unless Congress has formally declared war on Iran or passed a specific law allowing such military action — neither of which has happened. It is based on the idea that, under the Constitution, only Congress has the power to authorize war, and that the President cannot use the military in combat against Iran without that approval. The order would affect U.S. service members currently involved in any hostilities with or inside Iran. However, the resolution does carve out three things the U.S. could still do: defend American personnel or facilities if directly attacked, share intelligence with countries that have been attacked by Iran since late February 2026, and help those same countries intercept Iranian attacks or receive defensive military equipment.
ai_headline: Senate bill seeks Iran war powers limits
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting on whether to order the President to pull U.S. troops out of any fighting with Iran without congressional approval.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would require the President to withdraw U.S. military forces from any hostilities involving Iran unless Congress has formally declared war or passed a law allowing it. Neither of those steps has happened. The U.S. could still defend American personnel if attacked, share intelligence with countries attacked by Iran since late February 2026, and help those countries intercept Iranian attacks or receive defensive military equipment.
ai_sections.who: This resolution affects the President and U.S. service members currently involved in any hostilities with or inside Iran. It also touches countries that have been attacked by Iran since late February 2026.
ai_sections.why: If passed, this resolution would limit the President's ability to use military force against Iran without a vote from Congress. It is based on the constitutional idea that only Congress holds the power to authorize war.

title: To direct the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to establish a precision oncology program for cancer of the prostate, and for other purposes.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs.

title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to reverse certain energy-related modifications enacted by Public Law 119-21.
ai_summary: This bill would undo several changes that a recent law made to tax breaks related to clean energy. Specifically, it would restore a tax deduction for businesses that make their commercial buildings more energy-efficient, extend a tax credit for builders of new energy-efficient homes through 2032 (rather than letting it expire in mid-2026), give companies more time to build facilities that qualify for a clean hydrogen production tax credit, and restore tax credits for clean electricity production and investment that the recent law had cut back or ended early. The bill also ties the phase-out of the clean electricity production credit to an emissions milestone — meaning the credit would not begin winding down until U.S. electricity generation greenhouse gas emissions drop to 25% of their 2022 levels, or until 2032, whichever comes later. The people most directly affected would be commercial property owners, homebuilders, energy companies, and investors involved in clean hydrogen and electricity projects who would benefit from these restored or extended tax incentives.
ai_headline: Bill would restore clean energy tax credits cut by recent law
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8477 would restore and extend several clean energy tax breaks that a recent law reduced or ended.
ai_sections.what: This bill would undo changes made by a recent law to clean energy tax breaks. It would bring back a deduction for energy-efficient commercial buildings, extend a tax credit for builders of energy-efficient homes through 2032, give companies more time to qualify for a clean hydrogen production credit, and restore clean electricity production and investment credits. The bill also sets a specific emissions milestone that must be reached before the clean electricity production credit can begin to phase out.
ai_sections.who: Commercial property owners, homebuilders, energy companies, and investors in clean hydrogen and electricity projects would be directly affected. These groups would have access to restored or extended tax incentives under the bill.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, the tax breaks reduced or ended by the recent law would remain unavailable or would expire sooner. With it, eligible businesses and investors would face a different tax landscape when making decisions about energy-related construction and projects.

title: Western Tribal Water Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill updates an existing federal program that helps Native American tribes on reservations get better access to safe drinking water. It adds a new requirement that at least 10 projects funded by the program must be located within the Upper Colorado River Basin, a region that includes parts of Colorado, Utah, Wyoming, and other western states — specifically addressing water infrastructure needs for tribes in that area. It also sets aside $60 million per year for fiscal years 2027 and 2028 to fund the program. The tribes most directly affected are those living on reservations in drought-prone areas of the western United States, particularly those in the Upper Colorado River Basin, such as the Ute Mountain Ute Tribe, which the bill singles out as having significant aging water infrastructure in need of repair.
ai_headline: Bill targets tribal water access in Upper Colorado Basin
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8461 updates a federal water program to fund safe drinking water projects for western tribes, setting aside $60M/year for 2027–2028.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates a federal program that helps Native American tribes on reservations access safe drinking water. It requires that at least 10 funded projects be located in the Upper Colorado River Basin, which covers parts of Colorado, Utah, Wyoming, and other western states.
ai_sections.who: Tribes living on reservations in drought-prone parts of the western United States are most directly affected. The bill specifically names the Ute Mountain Ute Tribe as having aging water infrastructure in need of repair.
ai_sections.why: Without updated infrastructure, tribes in the Upper Colorado River Basin may continue to lack reliable access to safe drinking water. The bill directs federal attention and funding toward a region where water needs have not been fully addressed.
ai_sections.cost: The bill sets aside $60 million per year for fiscal years 2027 and 2028 to fund the program.

title: No Rogue Jurors Act
ai_summary: This bill would bar the federal government from giving money — through grants, contracts, awards, or any other financial support — to any organization, school, or group that promotes "jury nullification." Jury nullification is when a juror votes to acquit (find not guilty) a defendant even though the evidence shows the law was broken, essentially ignoring the law on purpose. The ban specifically covers training programs, written materials, and outreach efforts aimed at encouraging people to seek jury duty in federal or Washington, D.C. courts with the goal of voting against the evidence or the law. Organizations that run such programs — including any program with a name or framework similar to "Equity & Root Cause Jury Training" — would no longer be eligible for federal funding if this bill becomes law.
ai_headline: Federal funds barred for jury nullification programs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8485 would cut federal funding to any group that teaches or promotes jury nullification in federal or D.C. courts.
ai_sections.what: This bill would stop the federal government from giving money to organizations that promote jury nullification. Jury nullification is when a juror votes not guilty even if the evidence shows a law was broken, choosing to ignore the law on purpose. The ban covers training programs, written materials, and outreach efforts that encourage people to seek jury duty in order to vote against the evidence or the law.
ai_sections.who: Any organization, school, or group that runs such programs would lose eligibility for federal grants, contracts, awards, or other financial support. This includes programs similar in name or structure to something called "Equity & Root Cause Jury Training."
ai_sections.why: If passed, groups that currently receive federal funding and run these kinds of programs would have to stop them or give up that funding. Organizations not yet receiving federal money would be blocked from applying for it.

title: Funding Early Childhood is the Right IDEA Act
ai_summary: This bill would set new, higher spending limits for two federal programs that help young children with disabilities get early support services. The first program pays for special education services for children ages 3 to 5, and the bill would authorize spending to grow from $503 million in 2027 to $1.22 billion by 2031. The second program supports early intervention services for infants and toddlers with disabilities and their families, with authorized funding rising from $932 million in 2027 to $1.72 billion by 2031. The children and families who would be affected are those with infants, toddlers, or preschoolers who have disabilities and rely on these federally funded programs for things like therapy, developmental support, and special education. It is important to note that authorizing these spending amounts does not automatically provide the money — Congress would still need to pass separate legislation to actually appropriate the funds.
ai_headline: Bill proposes higher funding caps for disability early intervention
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill raises the spending caps for two federal programs serving young children with disabilities, but does not guarantee the money will be spent.
ai_sections.what: This bill would raise the maximum amounts Congress is allowed to spend on two federal programs that help young children with disabilities. One program covers special education for children ages 3 to 5. The other supports early intervention services for infants and toddlers with disabilities and their families.
ai_sections.who: Families with infants, toddlers, or preschool-age children who have disabilities and use these federal programs would be affected. These families rely on the programs for services like therapy, developmental support, and special education.
ai_sections.why: Raising the spending caps makes higher funding possible, but does not guarantee it. Congress would still need to pass separate legislation to actually release the money.
ai_sections.cost: For the preschool special education program, authorized spending would grow from $503 million in 2027 to $1.22 billion by 2031. For the infant and toddler early intervention program, authorized funding would rise from $932 million in 2027 to $1.72 billion by 2031. These are spending limits, not guaranteed expenditures.

title: Savings Opportunity and Affordable Repayment Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a new federal student loan repayment option called the Savings Opportunity and Affordable Repayment (SOAR) plan, available to borrowers with federal student loans. Under this plan, borrowers would pay nothing on income up to 250% of the federal poverty level for their family size, then 5% of income above that threshold for undergraduate loans and 10% for other loans (such as graduate loans), with the monthly amount adjusted based on the share of their debt from each category. The government would not charge borrowers for any interest their monthly payment doesn't cover, and half of every payment would go directly toward reducing the loan's principal balance. Borrowers repaying only loans from short undergraduate programs (two years or less) could have their remaining balance canceled after 10 years of qualifying payments, while borrowers with other loans could receive cancellation after 15 years. Two existing income-driven repayment plans — the Pay As You Earn (PAYE) plan and the older Income-Contingent Repayment (ICR) plan — would be phased out for new enrollees two years after the bill becomes law, though current enrollees could generally remain in those plans.
ai_headline: New SOAR plan would cap student loan payments at 5–10%
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8475 creates a new federal student loan repayment plan with income-based payments, no interest buildup, and balance cancellation after 10–15 years.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new repayment option called the SOAR plan for federal student loan borrowers. Borrowers pay nothing on income up to 250% of the federal poverty level, then 5% on income above that for undergraduate loans and 10% for other loans. The government covers any interest a monthly payment does not fully pay, and half of every payment goes directly toward the loan's principal balance.
ai_sections.who: Any borrower with federal student loans could enroll in the SOAR plan. Borrowers currently in the Pay As You Earn (PAYE) or Income-Contingent Repayment (ICR) plans can generally stay in those plans, but new enrollees would no longer be able to join them two years after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.why: Borrowers repaying only loans from short undergraduate programs of two years or less would have remaining balances canceled after 10 years of qualifying payments, and borrowers with other loans after 15 years. Two existing repayment plans would be phased out for new enrollees over time, which would reduce the repayment options available to future borrowers.

title: Territorial Protection and Sovereignty Act
ai_summary: This bill would permanently end the Temporary Protected Status (TPS) program, which currently allows people from certain countries experiencing ongoing crises — such as natural disasters or armed conflicts — to live and work legally in the United States on a temporary basis. The law that created TPS would be completely removed from the books, meaning no new TPS designations could ever be granted in the future. Anyone who already holds TPS when this bill becomes law would have their status canceled immediately on that same day. Those people would then have 60 days to leave the United States, and after that 60-day window closes, they would no longer be considered legally present in the country. The bill affects the hundreds of thousands of foreign nationals currently living in the U.S. under TPS protections from countries such as El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, Venezuela, and others.
ai_headline: Bill would abolish Temporary Protected Status program
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8460 would end the TPS program permanently and cancel existing TPS protections the same day it becomes law.
ai_sections.what: This bill would permanently eliminate the Temporary Protected Status program, removing it entirely from U.S. law so it could never be used again. Anyone currently holding TPS would lose that status immediately on the day the bill is signed. Those people would then have 60 days to leave the United States before they would be considered unlawfully present.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects hundreds of thousands of foreign nationals currently living in the U.S. under TPS from countries including El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and Venezuela, among others.
ai_sections.why: People who currently rely on TPS for the legal right to live and work in the U.S. would lose that authorization immediately. After the 60-day window, those who remain would no longer have legal status in the country.

title: Protect our Pets Act
ai_summary: This bill makes two main changes to federal law to increase protections for animals. First, it raises the maximum prison sentences for "animal crushing" — a federal crime that already exists — so that a first offense can result in up to 10 years in prison and a repeat offense can result in up to 15 years, along with fines in either case. Second, it creates a new federal crime: knowingly abandoning a domesticated animal or committing a violent act that endangers a domesticated animal, when that conduct crosses state lines, involves foreign commerce, or takes place on federal land; the same prison terms and fines apply. The new crime covers domesticated mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians kept as pets or for commercial purposes, but it does not apply to standard veterinary care, farming practices, food slaughter, hunting, fishing, pest control, medical research, acts necessary to protect a person or their property, or euthanasia. These changes would affect anyone in the United States who harms or abandons a domesticated animal in a way that falls under federal jurisdiction, and they increase the legal consequences compared to what current federal law provides.
ai_headline: Bill would stiffen penalties for animal cruelty and abandonment
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8480 raises prison sentences for animal crushing and creates a new federal crime for abandoning or harming domestic pets across state lines.
ai_sections.what: This bill makes two changes to federal law. It increases the maximum prison time for animal crushing to 10 years for a first offense and 15 years for a repeat offense. It also creates a new federal crime for knowingly abandoning or violently harming a domesticated animal when the act crosses state lines, involves foreign commerce, or happens on federal land.
ai_sections.who: This applies to anyone in the United States whose mistreatment or abandonment of a domesticated animal falls under federal jurisdiction. The law covers mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians kept as pets or for commercial purposes.
ai_sections.why: People convicted of animal crushing would face longer prison sentences than current federal law allows. Those who abandon or harm a pet across state lines or on federal land could now face federal criminal charges where none existed before.
ai_sections.cost: The summary mentions fines as part of the possible penalties for both crimes, but does not specify amounts or who collects them.

title: Support for Ownership and Investment in Land Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) to create a test program that speeds up the application and approval process for two types of farm loans: loans made directly by USDA to farmers who want to buy land, and loans backed (guaranteed) by USDA when a specially certified private lender is handling them for a qualified borrower. The goal is to cut down the time it takes for farmers to get through the paperwork and approval steps — but importantly, the program cannot waive or change any of the actual loan requirements themselves, only the timing of the application process. USDA must report to Congress every year on how the program is working. The program would automatically end on September 30, 2031. This primarily affects farmers and ranchers who are seeking to purchase farmland and are applying for USDA farm ownership loans, as well as the approved lenders who work with those borrowers.
ai_headline: USDA farm loan applications would move faster under pilot plan
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: USDA would run a pilot program to speed up farm land loan paperwork, ending September 30, 2031.
ai_sections.what: This bill would have USDA create a test program to shorten the time it takes to apply for and receive approval on certain farm ownership loans. It covers loans made directly by USDA and loans backed by USDA through approved private lenders. The program cannot change actual loan requirements, only the timing of the process.
ai_sections.who: Farmers and ranchers applying for USDA loans to buy farmland would be affected, along with the approved private lenders who work with those borrowers.
ai_sections.why: Currently, the application and approval process for these loans takes time that may affect when farmers can act on land purchases. USDA would also be required to report to Congress each year on how the program is working.

title: Surveillance Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill would require government agencies to obtain a warrant — a court order based on probable cause — before conducting almost any search that intrudes on a person's privacy, including accessing personal data held by companies like banks, phone providers, or cloud storage services. Importantly, it closes what is sometimes called the "third-party loophole," meaning that just because your data is stored with a private company does not mean the government can get it without a warrant, and the fine print in a user agreement cannot take away that protection. The bill also specifically prohibits warrantless use of facial recognition systems, automated license plate readers, and similar surveillance tools to track people in public spaces. If a government official — including a federal employee — violates these rules, the person whose rights were violated can sue that official in court and may have their legal fees covered. The bill includes narrow exceptions for situations already recognized under the law, such as emergencies, plain-view observations by officers lawfully on the scene, checking government-issued ID during a traffic stop, and truly public information that anyone can freely access online.
ai_headline: Bill would require warrants for government data access
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8470 would require government agencies to get a court warrant before accessing your personal data or using facial recognition to track you.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require government agencies to get a court order before searching personal data, even data stored with private companies like banks or cloud services. It would ban warrantless use of facial recognition, license plate readers, and similar tracking tools in public spaces. Exceptions exist for emergencies, things officers can plainly see, checking ID during a traffic stop, and information that is freely available to anyone online.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to government agencies and officials, including federal employees, who conduct searches or use surveillance tools. People whose rights are violated could sue the official responsible and may have their legal fees covered.
ai_sections.why: Right now, data stored with a private company can sometimes be accessed by the government without a warrant, and user agreements can be used to allow that access. This bill would change that, meaning government access to personal data and public surveillance would face stricter legal requirements.

title: Workforce Data Enhancement Act
ai_summary: This bill sets up a federal grant program to help states build better systems for collecting, linking, and sharing data about workers, job training programs, and employment outcomes. State agencies — particularly those that run unemployment insurance programs, collect labor market data, or administer job training programs — can apply for these grants individually or team up with other states to share data across state lines. Grant money can be used for things like connecting education records with employment records, improving the accuracy and timeliness of information about in-demand jobs and skills (including those related to artificial intelligence), creating secure digital records that workers can own and share with employers, and training staff to better use workforce data. Groups of states working together, especially those that include at least one state that has never received this type of funding before, are given priority when grants are awarded. The goal is to give workers, employers, and training providers better, more up-to-date information to help match people with jobs and skills that the economy needs.
ai_headline: Federal grants would modernize state workforce data systems
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would create a federal grant program to help states build better systems for tracking workers, job training, and employment outcomes.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal grant program for states to improve how they collect, link, and share data about workers, job training programs, and employment outcomes. States could use the money to connect education and employment records, create secure digital records workers can share with employers, improve information about in-demand jobs and skills, and train staff who work with workforce data.
ai_sections.who: State agencies that run unemployment insurance, collect labor market data, or manage job training programs can apply. States can apply alone or team up with other states, and groups that include at least one state that has never received this type of funding before get priority.
ai_sections.why: Without current and connected data, workers, employers, and training programs may have a harder time finding accurate information about available jobs and needed skills. This bill is meant to make that information more complete and up to date.

title: WISE Act
ai_summary: This bill requires that at least 20 percent of the federal water infrastructure loan funds that states receive must be spent on environmentally focused water projects, such as green infrastructure, improvements that save water or energy, or other innovative environmental activities. These funds come through a program where the federal government gives money to states, which then use it to make low-interest loans to communities for water and wastewater projects. The 20 percent requirement only applies if there are enough qualifying project applications available — states are not required to turn away other projects to meet the threshold if demand for green projects falls short. The people most directly affected are state water agencies that manage these funds and the local governments, utilities, and communities that apply for loans to build or upgrade water systems.
ai_headline: Federal water loans to require 20% green spending
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: States must use at least 20% of federal water loan funds on green or innovative water projects, if enough qualifying applications exist.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets a 20 percent minimum for how states spend certain federal water infrastructure loan funds. That portion must go toward environmentally focused projects, like green infrastructure, water-saving improvements, or innovative environmental activities. States are not required to meet the threshold if there are not enough qualifying applications to fill it.
ai_sections.who: State water agencies that manage these federal loan funds are directly affected. So are local governments, utilities, and communities that apply for low-interest loans to build or upgrade water and wastewater systems.
ai_sections.why: States will need to track how their loan funds are directed and prioritize green project applications up to the 20 percent threshold. Communities seeking loans for green or innovative water projects may find more funding available to them under this structure.

title: RETAIN Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill makes changes to how military pilots and other aviation officers in the Air Force are paid and retained. First, any aviation officer who has completed more than 8 years of flying service would automatically receive the highest possible monthly aviation incentive pay, instead of potentially receiving a lower amount. Second, the bill expands an existing Air Force pilot retention program by making it easier for pilots to qualify, shortening the required commitment in a written retention agreement from four years to one year, and raising the maximum annual retention bonus that can be offered to up to $100,000 — with the full maximum required to be offered to any pilot who agrees to stay on active duty for at least one additional year. The bill also requires the Air Force to give pilots more flexibility in where they are assigned and to match or beat the bonus amounts and contract terms offered by the Air National Guard and Air Force Reserve, so active-duty pilots are not financially pressured to leave for those branches. The program is also extended through 2031.
ai_headline: Air Force pilot pay and retention bonuses raised
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: Air Force pilots with 8+ years of flight service would get top incentive pay, and retention bonuses could reach $100,000 a year.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how the Air Force pays and keeps its pilots. Pilots with more than 8 years of flying service would automatically receive the highest monthly aviation incentive pay. A current retention program would be expanded, with shorter commitment periods, higher bonuses up to $100,000 a year, and a requirement that the Air Force match or beat bonus offers from the Air National Guard and Air Force Reserve.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects Air Force aviation officers, especially active-duty pilots. The Air Force would be required to follow the new pay and assignment rules.
ai_sections.why: Active-duty pilots currently may receive lower incentive pay or face longer commitment requirements than pilots in reserve components. Without these changes, some pilots may choose to leave active duty for the Air National Guard or Air Force Reserve, where terms may be more favorable.

title: Cold War Military Force Repeal Act
ai_summary: This bill would cancel a 1957 law — commonly known as the "Eisenhower Doctrine" — that gave the President standing authority to use U.S. military force and provide military assistance to Middle Eastern countries requesting help against communist aggression during the Cold War. That original law was passed at the height of tensions between the U.S. and the Soviet Union, and this bill would wipe it entirely off the books. In practical terms, repealing it would remove one of the older standing legal authorities that a president could potentially point to when taking military action in the Middle East. The bill would primarily affect the executive branch's legal toolkit for authorizing military operations in that region, though Congress and the President would still have other laws and authorities available to them.
ai_headline: Bill repeals 1957 Eisenhower Doctrine on Mideast force
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8436 would erase a 1957 Cold War law that lets the President use military force in the Middle East without new approval from Congress.
ai_sections.what: This bill would repeal a 1957 law known as the Eisenhower Doctrine. That law gave the President standing authority to use U.S. military force and provide military assistance to Middle Eastern countries facing communist aggression. Repealing it would remove that authority entirely from the books.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects the executive branch, specifically the President's legal options for authorizing military action in the Middle East. Congress and the President would still have other laws and authorities available to them.
ai_sections.why: Removing this law would reduce the number of legal justifications a president could point to when taking military action in the Middle East. The law being repealed dates to the Cold War era and the height of U.S. and Soviet tensions.

title: Baby Food Safety Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill requires the federal government to set legal limits on four toxic metals — lead, arsenic, cadmium, and mercury — in baby and toddler food (products marketed for children under 24 months) and in fruit and vegetable purees and juices, with those limits to be reviewed and updated at least every four years. Companies that make or process these foods would be required to regularly test their products for these contaminants, keep detailed records of that testing for at least two years, and use accredited laboratories to conduct the tests; the FDA would also run its own independent testing program. A food product that exceeds the established limits, or whose manufacturer fails to follow the testing and recordkeeping rules, can be declared legally "adulterated," and the FDA would gain clearer authority to order a mandatory recall of such products. The bill primarily affects manufacturers and processors of baby food, toddler food, and fruit/vegetable purees and juices, as well as importers of those products, who must verify that foreign suppliers meet the same standards. Ordinary consumers — especially parents of infants and young children — are the intended beneficiaries of the new safety requirements.
ai_headline: FDA would set toxic metal limits in baby food
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: The federal government would set legal limits on lead, arsenic, cadmium, and mercury in baby and toddler food.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the federal government to set legal limits on four toxic metals — lead, arsenic, cadmium, and mercury — in baby and toddler food and in fruit and vegetable purees and juices. Those limits would be reviewed and updated at least every four years. Foods that exceed the limits, or come from companies that fail to follow testing and recordkeeping rules, could be declared legally adulterated and subject to a mandatory recall.
ai_sections.who: Manufacturers, processors, and importers of baby food, toddler food, and fruit and vegetable purees and juices would need to follow new testing and recordkeeping rules. Parents of infants and young children are the intended beneficiaries of the new safety requirements.
ai_sections.why: If a company does not follow the rules, its products can be labeled adulterated and removed from the market. The FDA would also gain clearer authority to order mandatory recalls of unsafe products.

title: To amend the Immigration and Nationality Act to provide for a pause on the issuance of H-1B visas until certain limitations on the issuance thereof are implemented.
ai_summary: This bill would immediately stop the issuance of H-1B visas — the work visas most commonly used by U.S. employers to hire foreign workers in specialty occupations like technology and engineering — for three years from the date it becomes law. After that pause, new rules would take effect: the number of H-1B visas allowed each year would be cut from 65,000 to 25,000; each employer would have to pay a $100,000 fee per visa petition; workers could only be paid if they earn at least $200,000 per year; and visas would be awarded to the highest-paid applicants rather than through a lottery. The bill also eliminates the ability of staffing agencies to sponsor H-1B workers, bars the federal government from hiring any nonimmigrant visa holders (directly or through contracts), ends work authorization for foreign students on F, M, and certain J visas (the Optional Practical Training program), and blocks most temporary visa holders from applying to become permanent residents while living in the United States. These changes would affect foreign workers currently in or seeking H-1B status, U.S. employers who rely on those workers, foreign students working legally in the U.S. after graduation, and federal agencies that currently contract with nonimmigrant visa holders.
ai_headline: Bill targets H-1B visas with steep caps and new fees
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would pause H-1B work visas for three years, then permanently cut the annual cap and add strict new requirements.
ai_sections.what: This bill would stop the issuance of H-1B visas for three years. After that, annual visas would be cut from 65,000 to 25,000, only workers earning at least $200,000 per year could be hired, and visas would go to the highest-paid applicants instead of through a lottery. The bill also ends work permits for foreign students in certain visa programs, bars staffing agencies from sponsoring H-1B workers, and blocks most temporary visa holders from applying for permanent residency from inside the United States.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects foreign workers currently holding or applying for H-1B visas, U.S. employers who hire those workers, foreign students working legally in the U.S. after graduation, and federal agencies that contract with nonimmigrant visa holders.
ai_sections.why: Employers who currently rely on H-1B workers would need to find other ways to fill those roles during the pause and under the tighter limits afterward. Foreign students who depend on work authorization after graduation would lose that option under this bill.
ai_sections.cost: Employers would be required to pay a $100,000 fee for each H-1B visa petition they file.

title: Critical Infrastructure Airspace Defense Act
ai_summary: This bill gives private owners and operators of certain critical infrastructure facilities — such as nuclear power plants, electrical substations, transformer stations, and power grid control centers — the legal right to use drone-detection and drone-neutralization technology to protect their sites from threatening unmanned aircraft (drones). Currently, only government agencies and some law enforcement can legally take action against drones in this way, but this bill extends that authority to qualifying private facility owners and their designated security staff or contractors, provided they complete a government-approved training and certification program. Drones seized under this authority can be forfeited under applicable law, and the people taking these actions are shielded from civil or criminal liability as long as they are not grossly negligent or acting with willful misconduct. A grant program is established, with up to $250 million authorized over roughly five years, to help eligible facility owners and operators purchase and install approved counter-drone equipment. The new authority for private owners is temporary, expiring at the end of September 2031.
ai_headline: Private infrastructure owners gain counter-drone authority
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: Private owners of nuclear plants and power facilities could legally use drone-detection and counter-drone tools to protect their sites until 2031.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets private owners and operators of certain critical facilities — like nuclear power plants, electrical substations, and power grid control centers — legally detect and neutralize threatening drones. Right now, only government agencies and some law enforcement can do this. The new authority would expire at the end of September 2031.
ai_sections.who: Qualifying private facility owners and their designated security staff or contractors would gain this authority, as long as they complete a government-approved training and certification program. People acting under this law are protected from civil or criminal liability unless they are grossly negligent or acting with willful misconduct.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, private operators of these facilities have no legal way to act against threatening drones on their own. Drones seized under this authority can be forfeited under applicable law.
ai_sections.cost: A grant program is established to help eligible facility owners and operators purchase and install approved counter-drone equipment, with up to $250 million authorized over roughly five years.

title: Youth Climate Leadership Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require five federal agencies — the Environmental Protection Agency, the Department of the Interior, the Department of Energy, the Department of Agriculture, and the Department of Commerce — to each create a Youth Advisory Council made up of 15 to 25 young people between the ages of 16 and 29 who would advise those agencies on environmental issues affecting young people. At least half of each council's members must come from, live in, or work in communities that have been identified as disadvantaged due to environmental or economic hardships, and members would be drawn from a variety of backgrounds such as business, schools, local governments, and environmental groups. Each council would research environmental topics, write reports, and submit recommendations to its agency at least once a year on issues like climate change, pollution reduction, and environmental fairness — particularly for struggling communities. Each agency would receive up to $250,000 per year from 2027 through 2037 to run its council.
ai_headline: Five agencies would form youth environmental advisory councils
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: Five federal agencies would each form a youth council of 16-to-29-year-olds to advise on environmental issues, funded through 2037.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require five federal agencies to each create a Youth Advisory Council of 15 to 25 people between the ages of 16 and 29. These councils would research environmental topics, write reports, and send recommendations to their agency at least once a year on issues like climate change, pollution, and environmental fairness.
ai_sections.who: Council members would come from backgrounds such as business, schools, local governments, and environmental groups. At least half of each council's members must come from, live in, or work in communities identified as disadvantaged due to environmental or economic hardships.
ai_sections.why: The five agencies affected would be the EPA, the Department of the Interior, the Department of Energy, the Department of Agriculture, and the Department of Commerce. Each agency would receive formal input from young people on environmental decisions, particularly those affecting struggling communities.
ai_sections.cost: Each of the five agencies could receive up to $250,000 per year to run its council. Funding would run from 2027 through 2037.

title: PCTEC Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a federal grant program to fund "primary care team education centers" — community-based clinics and health centers where medical, nursing, and other health profession students can get hands-on training alongside practicing healthcare providers. Eligible health care organizations could receive up to $1 million per year for up to five years to do things like pay the salaries of the experienced clinicians who supervise students ("preceptors"), help students with housing or transportation during their training, and bring in additional health workers — such as community health workers or school nurses — as part of the care team. The program prioritizes funding for centers that work in areas with health worker shortages and that help people from disadvantaged backgrounds enter health professions. The bill sets aside funding starting at $10 million in 2027 and rising to $50 million by 2029, and requires the federal government to report annually to Congress on how the money is being used and how many students are being trained. Organizations that already receive certain other federal teaching health center grants would not be eligible for this new funding.
ai_headline: Senate bill funds hands-on primary care training sites
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4370 would create a federal grant program to fund community clinics where health profession students train alongside real care teams.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a federal grant program to support community-based health centers where medical, nursing, and other health profession students get hands-on training.
