Eligible centers could use the money to pay supervisors, help students with housing or transportation, and bring in workers like community health workers or school nurses.
ai_sections.who: Community-based clinics and health organizations could apply for grants. Centers that already receive certain federal teaching health center grants would not be eligible. The program gives priority to centers in areas with health worker shortages and those that help people from disadvantaged backgrounds enter health professions.
ai_sections.why: Without enough supervised training sites, health profession students may have fewer places to gain hands-on experience. Areas that already lack enough health workers could see more or fewer trained providers depending on whether local organizations qualify and apply.
ai_sections.cost: Funding would start at $10 million in 2027 and rise to $50 million by 2029. Eligible organizations could receive up to $1 million per year for up to five years. The federal government would also be required to report annually to Congress on how the money is spent and how many students are trained.

title: Save Willy Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would create a four-year trial program that sets up a small, dedicated team — called a "Cetacean Desk" — inside the existing Coast Guard vessel traffic control center for the San Francisco Bay area, staffed by no more than two full-time employees. The team's job would be to track the locations of large whales, orcas, and sperm whales in the Bay and the Golden Gate strait, then communicate that information in real time to ship and boat operators to help them avoid hitting or disturbing those animals. The program would also allow authorities to establish local rules about vessel speed, buffer zones around whales, and restricted areas, and it could use new technologies — including artificial intelligence tools — to better detect and predict where whales are located. It would require coordination with California state agencies, Tribal governments, ports, universities, and environmental groups, and it directs the Coast Guard to report to Congress each year on how well the program is working, including how many whale strikes were prevented. The bill primarily affects commercial shipping companies, vessel operators, and the Coast Guard personnel working in the San Francisco Bay region, as well as the whale populations that migrate through that area.
ai_headline: Coast Guard would track Bay Area whales in real time
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8450 would create a small Coast Guard team in San Francisco Bay to track whales and warn nearby ships in real time.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a four-year trial program with a small team, called a Cetacean Desk, inside the existing Coast Guard vessel traffic control center for the San Francisco Bay area. No more than two full-time employees would track large whales, orcas, and sperm whales and share that location information with ship and boat operators in real time. The program could also set local rules on vessel speed and buffer zones, use AI tools to detect whales, and would require the Coast Guard to report to Congress each year on results, including how many whale strikes were prevented.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects commercial shipping companies and vessel operators working in the San Francisco Bay region, along with Coast Guard personnel there. It also involves California state agencies, Tribal governments, ports, universities, and environmental groups, as well as the whale populations that migrate through the area.
ai_sections.why: Without the program, ship and boat operators in the Bay area would have less real-time information about where whales are located, which can lead to vessels striking or disturbing those animals. The program exists because whale strikes are a known risk in this busy shipping corridor.

title: Absentee and Mail Voter Protection Act
ai_summary: This bill would cancel a March 2026 executive order signed by President Trump (Executive Order 14399) that dealt with citizenship verification in federal elections, declaring that order to have no legal effect and blocking any federal money from being used to carry it out. Beyond that specific order, the bill also permanently prohibits federal agencies — including the Departments of Homeland Security, Justice, Commerce, and the Social Security Administration — from creating any national voter registration or citizenship database for election purposes, sharing state voter registration lists between federal agencies, or using federal funds to regulate who can vote by mail. It also bars the U.S. Postal Service from refusing to deliver mail-in or absentee ballots or from helping determine whether a voter is eligible to vote by mail. The bill primarily affects federal agencies, the Postal Service, and the roughly 48 million or more Americans who vote by mail or absentee ballot, as well as military members and U.S. citizens abroad who rely on mail-in voting under existing federal law.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets Trump voter citizenship order
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would cancel a 2026 Trump executive order on voter citizenship checks and ban federal agencies from building voter or citizenship databases.
ai_sections.what: This bill would cancel Executive Order 14399, signed in March 2026, and declare it legally void. It would permanently ban federal agencies from creating national voter registration or citizenship databases, sharing state voter rolls across agencies, or using federal funds to control who can vote by mail. It would also stop the U.S. Postal Service from refusing to deliver mail-in ballots or helping decide whether a voter qualifies to vote by mail.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects federal agencies including the Departments of Homeland Security, Justice, and Commerce, the Social Security Administration, and the U.S. Postal Service. It also affects the roughly 48 million or more Americans who vote by mail or absentee, as well as military members and U.S. citizens living abroad who rely on mail-in voting under existing federal law.
ai_sections.why: If passed, federal agencies would lose the authority to build or share voter and citizenship databases for election purposes. The Postal Service would be required to continue delivering mail-in and absentee ballots without screening voters for eligibility.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would block federal money from being used to carry out Executive Order 14399, but the summary does not specify a dollar amount or funding source.

title: Countering Russia’s War on Faith Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the Secretary of State and the Secretary of Defense, working with the Director of National Intelligence, to produce a detailed report — within 120 days of passage and once a year for three more years — documenting how Russia has violated the religious freedom of people living in Ukrainian territories it occupies, including actions against Christians, Jews, Muslims (such as Crimean Tatars), and other religious groups not connected to the Russian Orthodox Church. The report must identify destroyed or seized houses of worship, count people who have been imprisoned or displaced because of their faith, and name specific Russian officials or entities responsible for these abuses. After each report is delivered, the President must formally assess whether the named individuals and organizations likely engaged in those abuses, and if the answer is yes, the President is required to impose economic sanctions on those people or groups using existing U.S. sanctions laws. The sanctions can later be lifted if a future report shows the person or entity is no longer engaged in such conduct. The bill primarily affects U.S. government agencies that must carry out the reporting and sanctions, foreign individuals and entities in Russia or Russian-occupied Ukraine who could face financial penalties, and indirectly the religious communities in occupied Ukraine whose situations would be documented.
ai_headline: Bill targets Russian religious abuses in occupied Ukraine
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committees on Financial Services, and Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: If passed, this bill would require annual U.S. reports on religious persecution in Russian-occupied Ukraine and trigger mandatory sanctions.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Secretary of State and the Secretary of Defense, working with the Director of National Intelligence, to produce reports on religious freedom violations in Russian-occupied Ukrainian territories. The reports must document destroyed or seized houses of worship, people imprisoned or displaced for their faith, and name specific Russian officials or groups responsible. If the President determines those named likely committed abuses, sanctions must be imposed using existing U.S. law, and can be lifted if later reports show the conduct has stopped.
ai_sections.who: U.S. government agencies would carry out the reporting and sanctions process. Russian officials and entities found responsible for abuses could face economic sanctions, and religious communities in occupied Ukraine would have their situations formally documented.
ai_sections.why: The reporting requirement creates a formal, recurring record of alleged religious persecution in occupied territories. The mandatory sanctions provision means that a presidential finding of likely abuse would automatically trigger financial penalties under existing law.

title: Airpower Acceleration Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill gives the Defense Department the power to sign multi-year purchase contracts for two types of military fighter jets — the F-35 (in its A, B, and C versions) and the F-15EX — and to buy key parts for those planes in bulk ahead of time to save money. It also sets new legal minimums for how many fighter jets the Air Force must keep in its fleet: at least 1,800 total fighters between 2026 and 2035, with specific targets for "combat-ready" aircraft by 2030 and 2035. Separately, the bill allows the Air Force to grow its F-15EX fleet from 129 planes up to 329, but any jets beyond the first 129 must be used specifically to replace the older F-15E aircraft already in service. The people most directly affected are the Air Force, defense contractors and their suppliers who build these aircraft and components, and — more broadly — U.S. military readiness.
ai_headline: Senate bill sets Air Force fighter jet minimums
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: The Air Force must keep at least 1,800 fighter jets and can expand its F-15EX fleet from 129 to 329 planes.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets the Defense Department sign multi-year contracts to buy F-35 and F-15EX fighter jets and purchase key parts in bulk ahead of time. It sets a legal minimum of 1,800 total Air Force fighters from 2026 to 2035, with specific combat-ready targets by 2030 and 2035. It also allows the F-15EX fleet to grow from 129 to 329 planes, but any jets beyond the first 129 must replace older F-15E aircraft already in service.
ai_sections.who: The Air Force is directly affected, along with defense contractors and suppliers who build these aircraft and components. U.S. military readiness more broadly is also at stake.
ai_sections.why: Setting fleet minimums in law means the Air Force must maintain a certain size regardless of future budget or policy decisions. Allowing bulk purchases and multi-year contracts changes how and when the government spends money on these aircraft.

title: Patient Refunds for Bad Denials Act of 2026
ai_summary: Starting in 2027, this bill would allow the federal government to fine health insurance companies that deny too many of their customers' medical claims. If an insurer denies 25% or more of claims — not counting denials that are correctly flagged as fraudulent or truly medically unnecessary — it can be fined at least $10 million, with an additional $2 million added for every percentage point above that 25% threshold. The money collected from those fines would be paid back directly to the people who were enrolled in that insurer's plans during the year the violations occurred. The bill also requires insurers to tell patients exactly what medical necessity standards they use and specifically why a claim failed to meet those standards when coverage is denied on that basis, and insurers must report their overall denial rates to the government each year. This bill affects anyone with private health insurance — whether through an employer or purchased individually — as well as the insurance companies that sell those plans.
ai_headline: Insurers face fines for high medical claim denial rates
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2027, insurers who deny 25% or more of valid claims can be fined at least $10 million, with refunds going to their customers.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets a limit on how often health insurers can deny medical claims. Insurers who deny 25% or more of valid claims — not counting fraud or truly unnecessary care — face fines starting at $10 million, plus $2 million for each percentage point above that threshold. The money collected goes back to the people enrolled in that insurer's plan during the year the violations happened. The bill also requires insurers to explain their medical necessity rules upfront and tell patients specifically why a claim was denied, and to report their denial rates to the government each year.
ai_sections.who: This affects people with private health insurance, whether they get it through a job or buy it on their own. It also applies to the insurance companies that sell those plans.
ai_sections.why: Insurers who deny too many claims would face large financial penalties. Patients who were enrolled during a violation year would receive a share of the money collected from those fines.
ai_sections.cost: Fines start at $10 million for insurers at the 25% denial threshold, with $2 million added for each percentage point above it. Fine money is paid directly back to the insurer's customers from that year.
costChips: Fines repaid to that year's customers

title: To prohibit the manufacture and conveyance of certain products for children that incorporate an artificial intelligence chatbot, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would ban the making, importing, selling, and distributing of children's toys and child care products that have an AI chatbot built into them. A chatbot, as defined in the bill, is any technology that uses artificial intelligence or machine learning to hold interactive conversations with a user. The ban would take effect 180 days after the bill becomes law, giving manufacturers and sellers time to adjust. The rule would apply to anyone in the supply chain — manufacturers, importers, retailers, and distributors. Companies that break this rule would face the same legal penalties that apply to other violations of federal consumer product safety law.
ai_headline: Bill would ban AI chatbots in children's toys and products
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR H3000-3001)

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would ban AI chatbot toys and child care products, taking effect 180 days after it becomes law.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal to make, import, sell, or distribute children's toys and child care products that have an AI chatbot built in. A chatbot is any technology that uses artificial intelligence or machine learning to hold conversations with a user. The ban would start 180 days after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: The rule would apply to everyone in the supply chain, including manufacturers, importers, retailers, and distributors. Anyone who breaks the rule would face penalties under existing federal consumer product safety law.
ai_sections.why: Companies that do not comply would face the same legal consequences that apply to other violations of federal consumer product safety law. The 180-day window gives businesses time to adjust before the rule takes effect.

title: STAR Act
ai_summary: This bill removes an existing federal restriction that prevented certain federal transit funding from being used to pay for artwork incorporated into transit facilities, such as bus stations or subway stops. Under current law, those funds cannot cover the extra costs of adding art to these facilities; this bill would eliminate that prohibition. Transit agencies that receive federal funding for construction or improvement projects would be affected, as they could then use a portion of those funds for artwork as part of their facility designs. The change applies to the federal public transportation grant programs that fund capital projects like building or upgrading transit infrastructure.
ai_headline: Federal transit funds could cover public art in stations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8399 removes a federal rule that blocked transit agencies from spending federal construction funds on artwork in stations.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes a federal rule that stopped transit agencies from using federal construction funds to pay for artwork in bus stations, subway stops, and other transit facilities. That restriction would no longer apply if this bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: Transit agencies that receive federal funding for building or upgrading transit facilities would be affected. They could choose to include artwork costs as part of those funded projects.
ai_sections.why: Without the restriction, transit agencies would have the option to direct a portion of existing federal construction funds toward artwork. This changes what those funds are allowed to cover under federal public transportation grant programs.

title: Hazard Pay for Health Care Heroes Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal grant program allowing the government to give money to public and nonprofit hospitals, clinics, and home health agencies so they can pay extra "hazard pay" to certain health care workers during declared emergencies or disasters — such as a pandemic, a major hurricane, or other crises. Workers who qualify include doctors, nurses, direct care aides, medical technologists, public health workers, orderlies, and janitors or custodial staff in health care settings, as long as their job cannot be done remotely. The extra pay would be capped at $13 per hour on top of whatever the worker already earns, and no single worker could receive more than $25,000 in hazard pay per year through this program. Grant funds could also be used for other worker protections, such as personal protective equipment or alternative transportation if a worker's commute is considered hazardous. No specific total dollar amount is set aside; Congress would decide actual funding levels separately.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund hazard pay for health workers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would create federal grants so hospitals and clinics can pay qualifying health care workers up to $13/hr extra during declared emergencies.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a federal grant program for public and nonprofit hospitals, clinics, and home health agencies. The grants would let those employers pay extra hazard pay to certain health care workers during declared emergencies or disasters, such as a pandemic or major hurricane. Grant money could also cover protective equipment or alternative transportation for workers with hazardous commutes.
ai_sections.who: Eligible workers include doctors, nurses, aides, medical technologists, public health workers, orderlies, and janitors in health care settings, as long as their job cannot be done remotely.
ai_sections.why: Declared emergencies can create added physical risk for health care workers who must still report in person. This program would give employers a funding source to offer extra pay and protections during those periods.
ai_sections.cost: Extra pay would be capped at $13 per hour above a worker's regular wages, and no single worker could receive more than $25,000 in hazard pay per year through this program. No total funding amount is set in the bill; Congress would decide actual spending levels separately.
costChips: Extra pay capped at $13/hr above wages

title: Energy Consumer Protection Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill gives federal energy regulators — specifically the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) — stronger tools to punish companies or individuals who cheat in the electricity and natural gas markets. If someone is caught manipulating electricity markets or lying about natural gas prices to influence the data that government or private agencies use to set prices, FERC can ban them — temporarily or permanently — from buying or selling electricity, electricity-related financial products, natural gas, or related transmission services. The bill also creates a new explicit rule making it illegal to knowingly report false natural gas price or supply information to government or private reporting agencies with the intent to manipulate those records. These changes primarily affect energy traders, brokers, pipeline companies, and other businesses that participate in wholesale electricity and natural gas markets. Ordinary consumers are not directly regulated by this bill, but could benefit indirectly if stronger enforcement helps prevent the kind of market manipulation that can drive up energy prices.
ai_headline: FERC gains power to ban energy market manipulators
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal regulators could ban energy traders who cheat or lie about prices from participating in electricity and gas markets.
ai_sections.what: This bill gives federal energy regulators at FERC (the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission) stronger power to punish cheating in electricity and natural gas markets. It also creates a new rule making it illegal to knowingly report false price or supply information to agencies that track natural gas data.
ai_sections.who: This bill mainly affects energy traders, brokers, pipeline companies, and businesses that operate in wholesale electricity and natural gas markets. Ordinary consumers are not directly regulated by this bill.
ai_sections.why: FERC could ban companies or individuals from buying or selling electricity, natural gas, or related services if they are caught manipulating markets or reporting false data. Stronger enforcement may affect whether the kind of market manipulation that can drive up energy prices continues to occur.

title: Engineering Biology Readiness Act
ai_summary: This bill does two main things related to protecting the United States from biological threats, particularly those involving "engineering biology" — the deliberate design or modification of living organisms using scientific tools. First, it extends an existing requirement for government officials to give Congress yearly updates on the national strategy for defending against biological threats, pushing the deadline for those briefings several years further into the future. Second, it requires that the next routine government report on biological threats include a specific deep-dive analysis of the dangers posed by engineering biology — such as the potential misuse of gene-editing or synthetic biology — along with concrete recommendations for how the government can better prepare against those dangers, fill gaps in current rules, and reduce overlapping or conflicting efforts across agencies. The report must also recommend any new laws or programs needed, estimate their costs, and suggest ways to support responsible scientific innovation while guarding against misuse. This primarily affects federal agencies involved in national security, public health, and biodefense, as well as researchers and companies in the life sciences, and Congress itself, which would receive and act on the findings.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets engineered biology in biodefense reports
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill extends deadlines for biodefense briefings and requires a new government report on the risks of engineered biology.
ai_sections.what: This bill pushes back the deadline for yearly government briefings to Congress on the national biodefense strategy. It also requires the next routine government report on biological threats to include a deep analysis of risks from engineering biology, such as gene-editing and synthetic biology, plus recommendations for new laws, programs, cost estimates, and ways to support responsible science while preventing misuse.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects federal agencies working in national security, public health, and biodefense. It also affects researchers and companies in the life sciences, and Congress, which would receive and act on the findings.
ai_sections.why: Congress would receive more detailed information about biological risks tied to engineered organisms and where current rules may fall short. Federal agencies could face new guidance about how to reduce overlapping or conflicting efforts in this area.

title: Post-Disaster Protection Act
ai_summary: If the federal government denies or limits your disaster assistance after a declared disaster, this bill gives you more time to challenge that decision. Currently, people have 60 days to file an appeal after receiving an unfavorable assistance ruling; this bill extends that window to 90 days. The change applies to anyone who receives disaster aid — or is denied it — under the federal disaster relief law, which covers things like money for temporary housing, home repairs, and other recovery help after major disasters. The extra 30 days would give disaster survivors more time to gather documents, get help understanding the process, and submit a stronger appeal, especially while they may still be dealing with the immediate aftermath of a disaster.
ai_headline: FEMA disaster aid appeals window extended to 90 days
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8409 extends the disaster aid appeal window from 60 to 90 days for people denied or limited federal assistance.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes the deadline to appeal a federal disaster assistance decision from 60 days to 90 days. It applies after a federally declared disaster and covers aid such as temporary housing, home repairs, and other recovery help.
ai_sections.who: Anyone who applies for federal disaster relief and receives an unfavorable decision — including a denial or a limited award — would have the longer window to appeal.
ai_sections.why: The extra 30 days gives survivors more time to gather documents, understand the process, and submit an appeal while they may still be managing the immediate effects of a disaster.

title: Protecting Moms and Babies Against Climate Change Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal Department of Health and Human Services to create several programs aimed at protecting pregnant people, new mothers, and babies under age 3 from health risks linked to climate change — such as extreme heat, air pollution, and severe storms — with a particular focus on communities where these risks are greatest and on reducing racial and ethnic health disparities. It establishes a competitive grant program, funded at up to $100 million over four years, through which groups of local organizations (such as community groups, health clinics, and local governments) can apply for money to provide direct help to vulnerable individuals — including air conditioning units, housing assistance, evacuation support, and mental health counseling — and to train health care workers to recognize and address climate-related risks. A separate, smaller grant program (up to $5 million) funds medical and nursing schools to build climate and maternal health topics into their training programs. The bill also directs the National Institutes of Health to coordinate research across its agencies on how climate change affects pregnant people and infants, and requires the CDC to develop a public strategy for identifying geographic areas where these health risks are especially high, taking into account factors like air quality, poverty, health care access, and race.
ai_headline: HHS would fund climate protections for pregnant women, infants
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4356 would fund local programs and medical training to protect pregnant people and babies from climate-related health risks.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create grant programs to help local organizations provide direct support to pregnant people, new mothers, and babies under age 3 facing health risks from extreme heat, air pollution, and severe storms. Funded organizations could offer things like air conditioning, housing help, evacuation support, mental health counseling, and training for health care workers. It would also direct federal health agencies to conduct research and identify high-risk areas across the country.
ai_sections.who: The bill focuses on pregnant people, new mothers, and babies under age 3, especially those in communities with greater climate risks and racial or ethnic health disparities. Local organizations such as community groups, health clinics, and local governments could apply for funding, and medical and nursing schools would be eligible for a separate, smaller program.
ai_sections.why: Climate-related events like extreme heat and air pollution can create serious health risks for pregnant people and infants. Without coordinated programs, high-risk communities may have fewer resources to respond to those threats.
ai_sections.cost: The larger grant program would be funded at up to $100 million over four years. The separate program for medical and nursing schools would be funded at up to $5 million.
costChips: Main grant program: up to $100M over 4 years

title: INSIGHT Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the federal agency that oversees workplace retirement and benefit plans — the Employee Benefit Security Administration (part of the Department of Labor) — to submit annual reports to Congress about two things: the status of its ongoing investigations into potential violations, and any agreements it makes to share information or provide assistance to private lawyers who are suing employers or plan managers on behalf of workers. For each investigation, the report must include when it was opened, when the agency first asked for documents, and whether it was wrapped up within three years — and if not, why not and when it's expected to finish. For any agreements to help private attorneys, the agency must put the arrangement in writing beforehand, notify the employers or plan managers who could be harmed by it, and report detailed records of what information was shared and when. The people most directly affected are employers who sponsor retirement and benefit plans, the plan managers (fiduciaries) who oversee those plans, and private lawyers who may work with the government agency on lawsuits — though workers who participate in those plans are also indirectly affected since the bill aims to make the government's enforcement and legal cooperation activities more transparent to Congress.
ai_headline: EBSA would face new reporting rules on investigations
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: The INSIGHT Act would require a federal benefits agency to give Congress yearly reports on its investigations and its deals with private lawyers.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells the Employee Benefit Security Administration, the federal agency that oversees workplace retirement and benefit plans, to send Congress annual reports. Those reports must cover the status of open investigations and any written agreements the agency makes to share information with or assist private attorneys suing employers or plan managers. For each investigation, the report must include when it started, when documents were first requested, and whether it closed within three years, and if not, why not and when it is expected to end.
ai_sections.who: Employers who offer retirement and benefit plans and the fiduciaries who manage those plans are most directly affected, along with private lawyers who may work alongside the agency on lawsuits. Workers enrolled in those plans are also indirectly affected since the bill focuses on transparency around the agency's enforcement and legal cooperation activities.
ai_sections.why: Congress would receive more detailed information about how long investigations take and how the agency works with private attorneys. Employers and plan managers would also have to be notified before the agency enters into any information-sharing arrangement with a private lawyer.

title: Promoting Access to Local Agriculture Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Department of Agriculture to make it easier for local farmers and ranchers who sell directly to consumers — such as at farmers markets — to sign up as approved vendors for multiple federal food assistance programs at once, instead of filling out a separate application for each program. The programs covered include SNAP (food stamps), WIC, the Senior Farmers' Market Nutrition Program, and a nutrition incentive program. To reduce this paperwork burden, USDA would either create one single application that works for all these programs, or build a system where getting approved for one program automatically pre-qualifies a farmer for the others. The bill also requires USDA to set up a streamlined way for these farmers to actually process customers' benefits using standardized technology — like a single device or mobile app — and specifically requires that farmers markets and similar direct-to-consumer sellers receive wireless or mobile equipment suited to how they operate.
ai_headline: USDA would unify farmer sign-ups for food aid programs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Agriculture, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: USDA would create one shared signup process so local farmers can join SNAP, WIC, and other food benefit programs without separate applications.
ai_sections.what: This bill would direct USDA to streamline how local farmers and ranchers sign up to accept federal food benefits. USDA could build one combined application or make approval in one program automatically pre-qualify a farmer for the others. The bill also requires USDA to provide standardized technology, such as a single device or mobile app, so farmers can process customers' benefits at farmers markets.
ai_sections.who: Local farmers and ranchers who sell directly to consumers, such as at farmers markets, would be affected. The programs involved are SNAP, WIC, the Senior Farmers' Market Nutrition Program, and a nutrition incentive program.
ai_sections.why: Right now, farmers must fill out a separate application for each federal food program they want to join. This bill would reduce that paperwork burden by replacing multiple processes with one.

title: Consumer Protection and Corporate Accountability in Bankruptcy Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill changes the rules around corporate bankruptcy to make it harder for companies to misuse the process at the expense of people with injury or damage claims against them. It allows a bankruptcy court to throw out a company's bankruptcy case if the filing was pointless from the start or made in bad faith — for example, if the company restructured itself specifically to dodge paying victims, moved assets to insiders to avoid debts, or chose a friendly courthouse rather than one it genuinely belongs in. It also prevents bankruptcy courts from blocking lawsuits against related companies (such as parent companies or spinoffs) when those related companies may share responsibility for mass harm claims — like personal injury or contamination cases involving at least 100 people — that stem from a corporate restructuring done in the four years before the bankruptcy filing. The people most directly affected are individuals with injury, environmental, or product-harm claims against large companies, as well as corporations that use complex restructuring tactics (sometimes called "divisional mergers" or "Texas Two-Steps") to try to limit their legal liability through bankruptcy court.
ai_headline: Senate bill tightens rules on corporate bankruptcy abuse
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill makes it harder for companies to use bankruptcy court to escape paying people harmed by their products or actions.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes corporate bankruptcy rules in two main ways. First, courts can dismiss a bankruptcy case if the company filed in bad faith — for example, by restructuring itself to avoid paying victims or moving assets to insiders. Second, bankruptcy courts cannot block lawsuits against related companies, like parent companies or spinoffs, when those companies may share responsibility for mass harm claims involving at least 100 people and linked to a restructuring done within four years before the bankruptcy filing.
ai_sections.who: People with injury, environmental, or product-harm claims against large companies are directly affected. So are corporations that use complex restructuring strategies — sometimes called divisional mergers or Texas Two-Steps — to limit their legal liability through bankruptcy.
ai_sections.why: Without these changes, companies can reorganize themselves specifically to make it harder for victims to collect on legal claims. This bill gives courts more tools to push back on filings that appear designed to shield assets rather than genuinely resolve financial trouble.

title: Dietary Supplement Listing Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require companies that make, package, or distribute dietary supplements sold in the United States to register each of their products with the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) by submitting key information — including the product's name, ingredients, serving sizes, directions for use, allergen warnings, and a copy of the label. The FDA would then create a publicly searchable online database where consumers could look up this information for any listed supplement, though certain details like contact information for company personnel and the exact amounts of individual ingredients within proprietary blends would be kept confidential. Companies already selling supplements when the law takes effect would have 18 months to complete their listings, while newly launched products would need to be listed before they go on sale. Any supplement sold without a proper listing on file would be considered mislabeled under federal law, meaning the company could face legal consequences. This bill primarily affects dietary supplement manufacturers, distributors, and retailers, while also giving everyday consumers easier access to information about the supplements they buy.
ai_headline: FDA would list all dietary supplements in public database
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8370 would require supplement makers to register each product with the FDA before it can be legally sold in the U.S.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require companies that make, package, or distribute dietary supplements to register each product with the FDA. Registration means submitting the product name, ingredients, serving sizes, directions, allergen warnings, and a copy of the label. The FDA would build a public online database so consumers can search this information, though some details like proprietary blend amounts and staff contact info would stay private.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects dietary supplement manufacturers, distributors, and retailers. Everyday consumers who buy supplements would also be affected, as they would gain access to a searchable public database of product information.
ai_sections.why: Supplements already on the market when the law takes effect would have 18 months to complete their listings, while new products would need to be listed before going on sale. Any supplement sold without a proper listing on file would be considered mislabeled under federal law, which could expose companies to legal consequences.

title: Answering the Call Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal government to take specific steps to encourage first responders — including police officers, firefighters, emergency medical technicians, and 911 dispatchers — to use the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline when they need mental health support. The Department of Health and Human Services would be required to run outreach campaigns, award grants to fund awareness efforts, train 988 hotline counselors to better understand the unique experiences of first responders, and collect anonymous data to see how well the hotline is actually being used by this group. The bill also establishes a pilot program in which the federal mental health agency and the U.S. Fire Administration would work together with first responder organizations to make sure these outreach efforts are well-suited to the people they're trying to reach. Privacy protections are built in, meaning any data collected cannot identify individual callers and must follow existing federal and state privacy laws. Within three years of the law taking effect, the government would be required to report to Congress on how the pilot program worked.
ai_headline: Federal push to connect first responders with 988 crisis line
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies would be required to promote the 988 crisis line to first responders and report results to Congress within three years.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the federal government to run outreach campaigns, offer grants for awareness efforts, train 988 counselors on first responder experiences, and collect anonymous data on how first responders use the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline. A pilot program would bring together federal agencies and first responder organizations to shape these efforts. Any data collected must protect caller privacy and follow existing federal and state privacy laws.
ai_sections.who: The Department of Health and Human Services and the U.S. Fire Administration would carry out the requirements. Police officers, firefighters, emergency medical technicians, and 911 dispatchers are the intended people this bill tries to reach.
ai_sections.why: Without this law, there would be no formal federal effort to connect first responders with the 988 crisis line. If passed, Congress would receive a report within three years on how well the pilot program worked.

title: RECOVER Act of 2026
ai_summary: Under current Medicare rules, when a patient receives multiple therapy services (such as physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy) in the same session, Medicare pays the full rate for the first service but reduces payment by 50 percent for additional services provided at the same time. This bill would eliminate that 50 percent payment cut starting January 1, 2027, so that Medicare would pay the full rate for all therapy services provided in a single session, regardless of how many are given at once. The change applies to services billed under both the standard Medicare physician fee schedule and the outpatient therapy and rehabilitation program. The people most directly affected are Medicare patients who need multiple types of therapy at once — such as stroke survivors or people recovering from serious injuries — as well as the therapists and outpatient rehabilitation facilities that treat them, who currently receive reduced reimbursement for those additional services.
ai_headline: Medicare would end therapy payment cuts for combined sessions
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting Jan. 1, 2027, Medicare would pay full rate for all therapy services in one session, not just the first.
ai_sections.what: Right now, Medicare cuts payment by 50 percent for any therapy service given at the same time as another. This bill would end that cut starting January 1, 2027, so Medicare pays the full rate for every therapy service in a single session, no matter how many are provided at once. The change applies to both the standard Medicare physician fee schedule and the outpatient therapy and rehabilitation program.
ai_sections.who: Medicare patients who need more than one type of therapy at the same time — such as stroke survivors or people recovering from serious injuries — would be affected. So would the therapists and outpatient rehabilitation facilities that treat them and currently receive reduced payment for those additional services.
ai_sections.why: Therapists and facilities are currently paid less when they provide more than one therapy in the same session. This bill would change what Medicare pays for those combined sessions.

title: PEARL Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow any licensed pharmacist — whether working for a private pharmacy or a government agency — to refuse to dispense or sell the drugs mifepristone and misoprostol when those drugs are being used for abortion services, if doing so conflicts with the pharmacist's sincerely held religious beliefs. Under this protection, a pharmacist who refuses on those religious grounds could not be penalized in any way, including losing their pharmacy license or having federal funding withheld from their employer. If a pharmacist is nonetheless punished or forced to fill such a prescription despite this protection, the bill gives them the right to sue in federal court to stop that punishment or requirement. The bill affects licensed pharmacists across the country, as well as the employers, licensing boards, and government agencies that currently have authority over pharmacists' professional conduct.
ai_headline: Pharmacists could refuse abortion drug sales on religious grounds
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

ai_sections.tldr: Pharmacists could legally refuse to fill abortion-related prescriptions for religious reasons and could not be penalized for doing so.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets licensed pharmacists refuse to dispense mifepristone or misoprostol for abortion services if doing so conflicts with their sincere religious beliefs. A pharmacist who refuses on those grounds cannot lose their license, face penalties, or have their employer lose federal funding. If a pharmacist is punished anyway or forced to fill such a prescription, they can sue in federal court.
ai_sections.who: This bill applies to licensed pharmacists across the country, whether they work for a private pharmacy or a government agency. It also affects the employers, licensing boards, and government agencies that oversee pharmacists' professional conduct.
ai_sections.why: Patients seeking these medications for abortion services may have difficulty filling their prescriptions if their pharmacist declines. Employers and licensing boards would lose authority to require pharmacists to fill these prescriptions.

title: Economy of the Future Commission Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates a temporary, bipartisan advisory group — made up of 12 members appointed by Congressional leaders plus four nonvoting Deputy Secretaries from the Departments of Education, Labor, Commerce, and Treasury — to study how artificial intelligence is changing the U.S. economy and recommend specific laws Congress could pass in response. The group is required to produce two public reports: a shorter one within 7 months estimating how AI might affect jobs and federal tax revenue over the next 5 to 10 years, and a fuller final report within 13 months with concrete legislative recommendations on topics such as worker retraining, unemployment insurance, education, taxation, manufacturing, transportation safety, and energy demand. The four cabinet departments receiving the final report must each respond with their own written assessment within 60 days. The group shuts down 120 days after it delivers its final report, and the entire effort is funded with $5.25 million set aside for this purpose. This bill most directly affects workers whose jobs could be disrupted by AI, businesses — especially small and medium-sized ones — that use or compete with AI, and policymakers who would act on the group's recommendations.
ai_headline: Bipartisan panel would study AI's impact on U.S. jobs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committees on Energy and Commerce, Transportation and Infrastructure, Oversight and Government Reform, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would create a 12-month advisory group to study how AI is changing jobs and the economy, then recommend new laws.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a temporary, bipartisan advisory group to study how artificial intelligence is affecting the U.S. economy. The group would write two public reports — an early one within 7 months and a full final one within 13 months — with specific recommendations for new laws. It would shut down 120 days after delivering its final report.
ai_sections.who: The group would have 12 members appointed by Congressional leaders, plus four nonvoting representatives from the Departments of Education, Labor, Commerce, and Treasury. Workers whose jobs could be disrupted by AI, businesses that use or compete with AI, and policymakers would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Congress would receive concrete proposals on topics such as worker retraining, unemployment insurance, education, taxation, manufacturing, transportation safety, and energy demand. The four cabinet departments would each be required to respond in writing within 60 days of receiving the final report.
ai_sections.cost: The effort would be funded with $5.25 million set aside for this purpose.

title: Prison Libraries Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal grant program, run by the Attorney General, that gives money to states and U.S. territories to build or improve libraries inside prisons. The grants could pay for things like books, computers, internet access, librarians, educational classes, job training, and cultural programs — all available to incarcerated people at no cost to them. States that apply must show they have a real plan, demonstrate a need based on data about their prison population, and report back each year on how the money was spent. The program is capped at $10 million per year for six years, and no single grant can last longer than six years total.
ai_headline: Senate bill funds prison libraries across U.S. states
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4320 would fund prison libraries in states and territories through federal grants capped at $10M per year for six years.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a federal grant program to help states and U.S. territories build or improve libraries inside prisons. Grants can cover books, computers, internet access, librarians, classes, job training, and cultural programs, all free to incarcerated people. States must submit a plan, show need using data, and report yearly on how funds were used.
ai_sections.who: The Attorney General would run the grant program. States and U.S. territories could apply for funding.
ai_sections.why: Without this program, many prisons may lack the resources to offer library services or educational programs to incarcerated people. This bill creates a structured way for states to request federal support for those services.
ai_sections.cost: The program is capped at $10 million per year and would run for six years. No single grant can last longer than six years.

title: To prohibit liability against those engaged in the mining, extraction, production, refinement, transportation, distribution, marketing, manufacture, or sale of energy for damages or injunctive or other relief from the use of their products, and for other purposes.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Breast Cancer Stamp Reauthorization Act
ai_summary: This bill extends the life of a special U.S. postage stamp that raises money for breast cancer research. The stamp — called a "semipostal" stamp — costs a little more than a standard stamp, and that extra amount goes toward breast cancer research funding rather than just paying for postage. Under current law, the Postal Service's authority to sell this stamp was set to expire in 2027; this bill would push that deadline to 2037, allowing the stamp to remain on sale for an additional ten years. Anyone who buys postage stamps is potentially affected, since they would continue to have the option to purchase this stamp and contribute to breast cancer research through their everyday mail.
ai_headline: Senate bill extends breast cancer stamp sales to 2037
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill extends the breast cancer research postage stamp program from 2027 to 2037, keeping it available for ten more years.
ai_sections.what: This bill extends the expiration date for a special postage stamp that raises money for breast cancer research. The stamp costs slightly more than a regular stamp, and that extra amount goes to breast cancer research. Without this bill, the Postal Service's authority to sell the stamp would end in 2027; this bill moves that deadline to 2037.
ai_sections.who: Anyone who buys postage stamps could be affected, since they would continue to have the option to buy this stamp.
ai_sections.why: If the bill passes, the stamp remains available for purchase for an additional ten years. If it does not pass, the Postal Service loses the authority to sell the stamp after 2027.
ai_sections.cost: Buyers of the stamp pay a little more than the standard postage price, and that extra amount goes toward breast cancer research funding.

title: Stop Climate Shakedowns Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for anyone — including state governments, cities, or private citizens — to sue oil, gas, coal, and other fossil fuel companies in either federal or state court for harms connected to climate change, such as flooding, wildfires, or extreme heat. Any such lawsuits already in progress when the bill becomes law would have to be immediately thrown out, and any existing state or local laws designed to make energy companies pay for climate-related costs would be cancelled and made unenforceable. The bill declares that only the federal government — not states, cities, or individuals — has the authority to regulate greenhouse gas emissions and climate change. This would affect energy companies (which would be shielded from these types of legal claims), as well as states, cities, and people who might otherwise try to use the courts to seek compensation or other relief for climate-related damages.
ai_headline: Senate bill bars climate suits against fossil fuel firms
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would ban all climate-change lawsuits against fossil fuel companies and cancel existing state laws meant to hold them accountable.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal to sue oil, gas, coal, and other fossil fuel companies over climate-related harms like flooding, wildfires, or extreme heat. Any lawsuits already underway would be immediately dismissed, and existing state or local laws meant to make energy companies pay for climate costs would be cancelled.
ai_sections.who: Fossil fuel companies would be shielded from these types of legal claims. States, cities, and individuals who have filed or planned to file climate-related lawsuits would lose the ability to do so.
ai_sections.why: The bill declares that only the federal government has authority over greenhouse gas emissions and climate change, removing that power from states, cities, and courts. As a result, people and communities seeking money or other relief for climate-related damages would no longer have a legal path to pursue those claims.

title: Clean Slate through Consolidation Act
ai_summary: If a federal student loan borrower is in default and then takes out a Federal Direct Consolidation Loan to pay off that defaulted loan, this bill would require the government or whoever holds the loan to ask credit bureaus to remove the default record from the borrower's credit history. In other words, consolidating the defaulted loan would effectively give the borrower a "clean slate" on their credit report for that default, as if it never happened. This affects federal student loan borrowers who have defaulted on loans originally made, insured, or guaranteed under federal student loan programs and who choose to consolidate those loans. Currently, even after a borrower resolves a default through consolidation, the record of that default can remain on their credit report and hurt their credit score — this bill would eliminate that outcome.
ai_headline: Federal loan consolidation would erase default from credit reports
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: If you consolidate a defaulted federal student loan, this bill would require lenders to remove the default from your credit report.
ai_sections.what: This bill says that when a borrower pays off a defaulted federal student loan by taking out a Direct Consolidation Loan, the government or loan holder must ask credit bureaus to delete the default record from the borrower's credit history. The default would be removed as if it never appeared.
ai_sections.who: This affects federal student loan borrowers who have defaulted on loans that were originally made, insured, or guaranteed under federal student loan programs. It applies to those who choose to resolve that default through consolidation.
ai_sections.why: Right now, a default can stay on a borrower's credit report even after they consolidate, which can continue to hurt their credit score. This bill would end that outcome by requiring removal of the default record after consolidation.

title: FAITH Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it a federal crime to charge someone money — or withhold goods, services, or opportunities from someone — because of their religious beliefs, membership in a religious group, or refusal to join one. For example, it would be illegal for any person or organization to impose a fee, fine, or financial penalty on someone simply because they do or don't belong to a particular religion. The punishment ranges from a fine and up to one year in prison for smaller amounts (up to $1,000), up to three years in prison for larger amounts. The bill makes clear that religious organizations and schools can still ask members for voluntary donations for their own internal purposes — that remains fully legal. It also adds this new crime to the list of offenses that can be prosecuted under federal organized crime (RICO) laws, meaning the crime could be pursued as part of a larger criminal enterprise if applicable.
ai_headline: Bill targets financial penalties based on religion
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8353 makes it a federal crime to charge someone money or deny them goods and services because of their religion.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new federal crime. It would be illegal for any person or organization to charge someone money, or to withhold goods, services, or opportunities from them, because of their religious beliefs or membership in a religious group. Religious organizations and schools can still collect voluntary donations from their own members.
ai_sections.who: This law would apply to any person or organization in the United States. People who are charged money or denied something because of their religion would be protected.
ai_sections.why: Anyone found guilty could face fines and up to one or three years in prison, depending on the amount of money involved. Because the offense is added to federal organized crime laws, it could also be prosecuted as part of a larger criminal operation.

title: Drug Origin Transparency Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill requires drug companies to provide more detailed information about where their drugs and drug ingredients come from — and to make that information visible to the public on drug labels. Specifically, manufacturers would have to report to the federal government not just that they made a drug, but also who supplied each ingredient and how much of the drug was made using ingredients from each supplier; these reports could be required up to four times a year instead of just once. In addition, drug labels and accompanying documents would have to show the name, location, and a unique identifying number for the original manufacturer of the drug or its active ingredients, as well as any other companies involved in manufacturing, packing, or distributing it. The people most directly affected are drug manufacturers, ingredient suppliers, and distributors, who would face new reporting and labeling obligations; consumers and pharmacists would benefit from greater visibility into where drugs and their components are actually made. The Food and Drug Administration would be responsible for writing the specific rules that spell out exactly what information must be reported and how it must appear on labels.
ai_headline: FDA would require drug supply chain labels to go public
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8339 would require drug makers to report ingredient sources to the government and display that information on drug labels.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires drug manufacturers to tell the federal government who supplies each ingredient in their drugs and how much of the drug came from each supplier. Reports could be required up to four times a year. Drug labels and package documents would also have to show the name, location, and a unique ID number for every company involved in making, packing, or distributing the drug.
ai_sections.who: Drug manufacturers, ingredient suppliers, and distributors would face new reporting and labeling requirements. Consumers and pharmacists would be able to see more detail about where drugs and their ingredients are made.
ai_sections.why: Because more sourcing information would appear on labels and in government reports, the origin of a drug and its ingredients would be more traceable. The Food and Drug Administration would be responsible for writing the rules that define exactly what must be reported and how it must appear on labels.

title: Securing America’s Drug Supply from Communist China Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to screen drug applications — both new ones and those submitted since January 1, 2016 — to determine whether the company seeking approval has financial or organizational ties to the Chinese government, Communist Party, or military. If a company is found to have such ties, the FDA would be blocked from approving its drug applications, and U.S. Customs and Border Protection would be required to turn away and destroy any of its drugs already approved and being shipped into the country. Companies flagged under this law would have a chance to prove they've cut those ties or sell their approved drug licenses to an unaffiliated company within 180 days to avoid import bans. There is a limited exception: if blocking a drug would cause or worsen a drug shortage in the United States, Customs could allow it in anyway. The bill primarily affects pharmaceutical companies with connections to China, as well as U.S. agencies responsible for drug approvals and border enforcement, and could indirectly affect American patients and pharmacies that rely on drugs made or owned by Chinese-linked companies.
ai_headline: FDA would ban drugs tied to Chinese government
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would block the FDA from approving drugs from companies tied to the Chinese government, party, or military.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the FDA to review drug applications and check whether the company behind them has financial or organizational ties to the Chinese government, Communist Party, or military. Companies found to have those ties would be blocked from getting drug approvals, and their already-approved drugs could be turned away and destroyed at the border. A company could avoid the ban if it proves it cut those ties or sells its drug licenses to an unrelated company within 180 days, and drugs could still enter if blocking them would cause a shortage.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects pharmaceutical companies with ties to China and the U.S. agencies that handle drug approvals and border enforcement. American patients and pharmacies that rely on drugs made or owned by Chinese-linked companies could also be affected.
ai_sections.why: If passed, some drugs currently available in the United States could be blocked from entering the country or lose their approval status. Patients and pharmacies depending on those drugs could face reduced access, especially if a shortage exception does not apply.

title: Hemp Safety Enforcement Act
ai_summary: This bill gives states and Native American tribes the option to step outside the federal hemp regulatory system and run their own rules for growing and selling hemp and hemp-derived products (such as CBD gummies or other cannabinoid goods). Instead of submitting a detailed plan to the U.S. Department of Agriculture and following federal hemp rules, a state or tribe can simply send a notice saying it wants to handle regulation on its own — as long as it requires a minimum age for buying hemp-derived products. Once a state or tribe takes that path, it keeps its own definitions of "hemp" and related products, meaning it can set stricter or different standards than the federal government does. The bill also protects the flow of hemp products across state lines: no state or tribe can block the shipment of hemp goods to or from a self-regulating state or tribe, though products moving between two self-regulating places must follow the rules of both. This affects hemp farmers, hemp product businesses, and consumers in any state or tribal territory that chooses to opt out of the federal program.
ai_headline: States could manage their own hemp rules under new bill
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: States and tribes can opt out of federal hemp rules by sending a notice, as long as they require a minimum purchase age.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets states and Native American tribes leave the federal hemp regulatory system and run their own rules for growing and selling hemp and hemp products like CBD goods. To opt out, they send a notice instead of submitting a detailed plan to the U.S. Department of Agriculture. They must require a minimum age to buy hemp-derived products, and they keep their own definitions of hemp and related goods.
ai_sections.who: This affects hemp farmers, hemp product businesses, and consumers in any state or tribal territory that chooses to opt out of the federal program.
ai_sections.why: States and tribes that opt out can set standards that differ from or are stricter than federal rules. No state or tribe can block hemp shipments to or from a self-regulating state or tribe, though products moving between two self-regulating places must follow the rules of both.

title: FLEX Act
ai_summary: This bill changes the federal rules for how money is distributed and used to support charter schools across the country. It adjusts how federal funding is divided among different charter school programs — raising the minimum shares set aside for certain purposes — and allows that money to be used for a broader range of activities, including adding new academic programs or expanding offerings at already high-performing charter schools, not just opening new ones. The bill also makes it easier for charter schools to receive grant money upfront (rather than waiting to be reimbursed), reduces paperwork requirements for schools and the organizations that oversee them, and limits the federal government to issuing only regulations that are strictly necessary. It also requires that states consult with charter school operators before new rules are proposed, and adds a requirement that charter schools receiving federal funds address how their students will get to and from school. The people most directly affected are charter school operators, the state agencies that manage charter school grant programs, and the students who attend or may in the future attend charter schools.
ai_headline: Senate bill rewrites federal charter school grant rules
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: The FLEX Act changes how federal charter school money is divided and spent, and adds a transportation requirement for funded schools.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates federal rules for how charter school funding is split up and what it can be used for, including expanding programs at already high-performing schools, not just opening new ones. It also makes it easier for schools to get grant money upfront, cuts paperwork, limits new federal regulations, and requires states to ask charter operators for input before proposing new rules. Schools receiving federal funds must also show how students will get to and from school.
ai_sections.who: Charter school operators and the state agencies that run charter school grant programs are most directly affected. Students who attend or may one day attend charter schools are also affected.
ai_sections.why: These changes shift how federal dollars flow to charter schools and what those schools are allowed to do with them. The new transportation requirement means federally funded charter schools must have a plan for how students travel to and from school.

title: Securing Local Communities Input in Broadband Development Act
ai_summary: This bill would cancel two specific FCC decisions from 2018 that were designed to speed up the rollout of 5G and other broadband infrastructure by limiting what state and local governments could require from telecommunications companies seeking to install equipment. By wiping out those FCC rulings, the bill would restore the ability of state and local governments — such as city councils and county boards — to set their own rules and conditions when companies want to place cell towers, antennas, or other broadband equipment on public property or rights-of-way. The people most directly affected would be local governments, residents whose communities host that infrastructure, and telecommunications companies that currently operate under the looser federal standards the 2018 rulings established. In short, the bill shifts decision-making power over broadband infrastructure placement back toward local communities and away from the federal government.
ai_headline: Bill would restore local control over 5G tower siting
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8354 would cancel two 2018 FCC rulings and return local governments' power to set rules for broadband equipment placement.
ai_sections.what: This bill would cancel two FCC decisions from 2018 that limited what state and local governments could require from telecom companies installing 5G and other broadband equipment. Canceling those rulings would restore the ability of city councils, county boards, and other local governments to set their own conditions for placing cell towers, antennas, and related equipment on public property.
ai_sections.who: Local governments, telecommunications companies, and residents in communities that host broadband infrastructure would be most directly affected. Telecom companies currently operate under the looser federal standards the 2018 rulings put in place.
ai_sections.why: If passed, decision-making power over where and how broadband equipment is placed would shift from the federal government back to local governments. Telecom companies would need to meet local rules and conditions rather than the existing federal standards.

title: Ending the Carried Interest Loophole Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how certain investment fund managers — such as those who run private equity, hedge, or venture capital funds — are taxed on a special type of pay called "carried interest," which is the share of a fund's profits they receive as compensation for managing the fund. Under current law, that profit share is often taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rate; this bill would instead require those managers to pay ordinary income tax rates on the portion of their profits that exceeds what a normal financial return on their own invested money would produce. To calculate how much of a manager's earnings count as compensation (rather than as a return on investment), the bill uses a formula based on a benchmark interest rate plus nine percentage points applied to the fund's total invested capital. The bill also closes related workarounds, such as using loans from the partnership or restructuring the interest through financial contracts, so that managers cannot avoid the new rules through alternative arrangements. This primarily affects people who earn carried interest from managing investment partnerships — not ordinary investors, employees, or small business owners.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets carried interest tax break for fund managers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would tax investment fund managers' profit-based pay at regular income tax rates instead of the lower capital gains rate.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how investment fund managers are taxed on carried interest, which is the share of a fund's profits they earn for managing it. Instead of paying the lower long-term capital gains tax rate, managers would pay ordinary income tax rates on the part of their earnings that goes beyond a normal return on their own invested money. The bill also closes workarounds, such as using partnership loans or financial contracts, that could let managers avoid the new rules.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects managers of private equity, hedge, and venture capital funds who receive carried interest as compensation. It does not affect ordinary investors, employees, or small business owners.
ai_sections.why: Carried interest is currently taxed at the lower capital gains rate; this bill would treat the compensation portion of those earnings more like a regular paycheck for tax purposes. Managers who use certain financial arrangements to get around the rules would also be covered under the new law.

title: Pedophile Financial Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the head of a federal financial watchdog agency — the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN), part of the U.S. Treasury Department — to investigate whether banks and their employees broke federal anti-money laundering laws in connection with financial transactions involving Jeffrey Epstein. The investigation would specifically look at whether banks, including JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America, were too slow to file required reports on suspicious transactions, failed to question large payments involving Epstein and certain wealthy individuals named in the bill, and whether senior bank executives allowed employees to keep doing business with Epstein even after the banks had dropped him as a client due to money laundering concerns. Within 100 days of the bill becoming law, FinCEN would have to deliver a report to Congress on what it found, and that report could include information drawn from confidential suspicious activity reports filed about Epstein-related transactions. If the investigation uncovers evidence that any bank employee may have willfully broken federal anti-money laundering laws, FinCEN would be required to refer those individuals to the U.S. Attorney General for possible further legal action.
ai_headline: FinCEN ordered to probe Epstein bank transactions
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would order a federal agency to investigate whether banks broke money laundering laws in their dealings with Jeffrey Epstein.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network, known as FinCEN, to investigate whether banks broke federal anti-money laundering laws in connection with Jeffrey Epstein. The investigation would look at whether banks filed required reports too slowly, failed to question large payments, and whether senior executives let employees keep doing business with Epstein after the banks had already dropped him. FinCEN would have to deliver a report to Congress within 100 days, and would have to refer any employee suspected of willfully breaking the law to the U.S. Attorney General.
ai_sections.who: FinCEN, which is part of the U.S. Treasury Department, would lead the investigation. Banks named in the bill include JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America, along with their employees and senior executives.
ai_sections.why: If the investigation finds that bank employees willfully broke anti-money laundering laws, those individuals could face further legal action. Congress would receive a formal report on the findings, which could include information from confidential suspicious activity reports.

title: HERO Child Care for Military Families Act
