Foreign actors found responsible for sabotage could face frozen U.S.-held assets and visa bans.
ai_sections.why: Congress states that underwater cables near Taiwan have been repeatedly cut or damaged by ships linked to China. The bill sets up tools to respond to that damage and to prepare for larger crises such as a blockade or military conflict in the Taiwan Strait.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $20 million per year is authorized for rapid-response repair work, covering the years 2027 through 2032.

title: Fair Credit Reporting Reseller Accuracy Act
ai_summary: This bill adds a new rule for **resellers** — companies that buy credit report information from major credit bureaus and then sell or pass it along to lenders, landlords, employers, or other businesses. Under the new rule, before a reseller forwards someone's credit information to whoever ordered it, the reseller must take reasonable steps to make sure that information is as accurate as possible. However, the bill also protects resellers from being sued if they accurately passed along information exactly as they received it from the original credit bureau — meaning the responsibility for bad data stays with the source, not the middleman. This change would primarily affect reseller companies in the credit reporting industry, as well as everyday consumers whose credit information moves through those companies before reaching a lender or other decision-maker.
ai_headline: Bill targets accuracy rules for credit report resellers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: Resellers must check credit report accuracy before sharing it, but can't be sued if they passed along bad data exactly as received.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new rule for resellers, companies that buy credit report data from major bureaus and sell it to lenders, landlords, or employers. Resellers must take reasonable steps to check that information is accurate before passing it along. If a reseller shared data exactly as received from the original bureau, they are protected from lawsuits over that data.
ai_sections.who: This affects reseller companies in the credit reporting industry. It also affects everyday consumers whose credit information travels through a reseller before reaching a lender or other decision-maker.
ai_sections.why: When credit data moves through a reseller, errors in that data could affect decisions about loans, housing, or employment for consumers. This rule places the responsibility for inaccurate data on the original source bureau rather than the reseller that passed it along unchanged.

title: Restoring College Access and Affordability Act
ai_summary: This bill would undo a set of recent changes to federal student aid programs that were enacted as part of a 2025 budget law, essentially restoring the rules that existed before those changes took effect. Specifically, it would reverse cuts to how much students can borrow in federal loans, reverse changes to repayment plans and deferment options, reverse restrictions on Public Service Loan Forgiveness, and restore the prior rules for Pell Grant eligibility and how Pell Grants interact with other aid — all of which would affect current and future college students who rely on federal financial assistance. The bill also cancels delays that had been placed on two borrower-protection rules: one allowing students to seek loan cancellation if their school misled them, and another allowing automatic loan discharge if their school closed. Additionally, the bill expands an existing law that can cut off federal financial aid to educational programs whose graduates consistently earn too little to repay their loans, applying that rule more broadly across certificate, associate's, bachelor's, and graduate programs. Finally, it fixes the endowment tax that wealthy private universities pay — locking it at a flat rate of 1.4 percent and removing a tiered rate structure that had been added by the 2025 law.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets 2025 cuts to federal student aid
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would reverse 2025 cuts to federal student loans, repayment options, and Pell Grants, and restore protections for borrowers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would undo changes made by a 2025 budget law to federal student aid, bringing back earlier rules for borrowing limits, repayment plans, deferment, Public Service Loan Forgiveness, and Pell Grant eligibility. It would also restore two borrower protections that were delayed — one letting students cancel loans if their school misled them, and one allowing automatic loan discharge if their school closed. The bill also applies an existing rule more broadly, requiring that educational programs across certificate, associate's, bachelor's, and graduate levels show their graduates earn enough to repay their loans, and it sets the endowment tax on wealthy private universities at a flat 1.4 percent rate.
ai_sections.who: Current and future college students who rely on federal financial assistance would be affected, including those seeking loan forgiveness through public service work. Graduates of educational programs and wealthy private universities would also be affected by parts of this bill.
ai_sections.why: If passed, students would have access to the same borrowing limits, repayment options, and grant rules that existed before the 2025 changes. Educational programs that do not meet the earnings standard could lose access to federal financial aid for their students.

title: Workforce Investments Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill changes the rules for how federal job training programs are measured, reported on, and funded under the main federal workforce law. It updates the yardsticks used to judge whether job training programs are succeeding — for example, by tracking whether workers stay employed longer after leaving a program and by counting how many participants complete hands-on training like apprenticeships or on-the-job training. It strengthens consequences for states and local areas whose programs fall short of those targets, including funding reductions, while rewarding states that show the most improvement by directing extra money their way. It also requires that at least half of certain local job training funds be spent directly on actual training services — such as individual training accounts or training contracts — rather than on other program costs. These changes primarily affect state and local workforce agencies, job training providers, and the workers and jobseekers who use federally funded employment and training programs.
ai_headline: Bill tightens job training standards and funding rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8102 changes how federal job training programs are measured and funded, with penalties for low performers and rewards for most-improved states.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates how federal job training programs are judged, reported on, and funded. New measures track whether workers stay employed longer after finishing a program and how many complete hands-on training like apprenticeships. It also requires that at least half of certain local training funds go directly to actual training services, not other program costs.
ai_sections.who: This affects state and local workforce agencies, job training providers, and workers or jobseekers who use federally funded employment and training programs.
ai_sections.why: States and local areas that fall short of performance targets face funding reductions. States that show the most improvement receive extra funding.
ai_sections.cost: Funding can be reduced for programs that miss performance targets. States showing the most improvement are directed extra money, and at least half of certain local funds must go directly to training services.
costChips: Missed targets can cut program funding
50%+ of local funds must go to training

title: Fertilizer Transparency Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would require fertilizer manufacturers and wholesalers to report their prices and sales quantities to the U.S. Department of Agriculture every week, so that the government can regularly publish that information to the public. The goal is to give farmers and other market participants clearer, more timely information about what fertilizer actually costs, so they can make better buying decisions. Cooperatives and retailers (unless a retailer is also a manufacturer) would be exempt from the mandatory reporting but could choose to share their price and quantity information voluntarily. The Department of Agriculture would also run a weekly retail price survey and publish all of this data through an easy-to-access online dashboard, while keeping individual companies' confidential business details private. The bill does not change any existing antitrust laws.
ai_headline: USDA would track weekly fertilizer prices under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: Fertilizer makers would report prices weekly to USDA, which would post that data on a public dashboard for farmers to use.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require fertilizer manufacturers and wholesalers to report their prices and sales quantities to the U.S. Department of Agriculture every week. The USDA would then publish this information on a public online dashboard and run its own weekly retail price survey. Individual companies' confidential business details would stay private.
ai_sections.who: Fertilizer manufacturers and wholesalers would be required to report. Cooperatives and retailers would be exempt from the requirement but could choose to report voluntarily, unless a retailer is also a manufacturer.
ai_sections.why: Farmers and other buyers would have more timely information about fertilizer prices when making purchasing decisions. The bill would not change any existing antitrust laws.

title: Permanent Housing Affordability Act
ai_summary: This bill creates several government programs to make homeownership permanently affordable for low- and moderate-income people by supporting "shared equity" housing models — arrangements where homes are sold at below-market prices with legal agreements (like ground leases or deed restrictions) requiring them to stay affordable for at least 99 years whenever they are resold. It sets up a fund at the Treasury Department to give grants to state agencies and certain nonprofit lenders, who would then make low-interest construction loans (capped at 3% interest) to nonprofits and local governments to build or renovate homes that are then sold only to buyers earning up to 120% of the local median income. A separate pilot grant program through the Department of Housing and Urban Development would help eligible nonprofits and local governments buy vacant land or existing properties to develop and sell as permanently affordable homes, primarily to households earning up to 80% of the local median income (or 120% in rural areas). The bill also directs the federal government to make surplus federal properties available to community land trusts and similar organizations at a 75% discount off market value, and launches public education and research programs to raise awareness of shared equity homeownership among potential buyers, lenders, and real estate professionals. The people most directly affected are lower- and moderate-income prospective homebuyers, nonprofit housing organizations, community land trusts, local governments, and lenders who work in affordable housing.
ai_headline: Bill would fund permanently affordable homeownership programs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8127 creates loans, grants, and land discounts to keep homes affordable for lower-income buyers for at least 99 years after each sale.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets up programs to build and sell homes at below-market prices to lower- and moderate-income buyers, with legal agreements requiring those homes to stay affordable for at least 99 years each time they are resold. It creates a Treasury fund for low-interest construction loans, a HUD pilot grant program to buy land and properties, and a discount program giving community land trusts access to surplus federal land at 75% off market value. It also funds public education and research about shared equity homeownership.
ai_sections.who: The people most directly affected are lower- and moderate-income homebuyers, nonprofit housing organizations, community land trusts, and local governments. Lenders and real estate professionals involved in affordable housing would also be reached through the education programs.
ai_sections.why: Homes built under this bill could only be sold to buyers earning up to 120% of the local median income, or up to 80% in most cases under the pilot program. This limits who can purchase these homes and shapes how the housing market operates in areas where the programs are used.
ai_sections.cost: The bill creates a Treasury fund to issue grants to state agencies and certain nonprofit lenders, which would then make construction loans capped at 3% interest. It also establishes a separate HUD pilot grant program, though the summary does not specify total dollar amounts for either program.
costChips: Treasury fund issues grants to states

title: Save Struggling Hospitals Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how Medicare calculates payments to hospitals located in areas where wages are relatively low. Specifically, hospitals whose local wage index score falls below the 25th percentile (meaning they are among the lower-paid quarter of hospitals) would receive a boost — their wage index score would be raised halfway toward the 25th percentile level, resulting in higher Medicare reimbursement payments. The adjustment would apply retroactively starting October 1, 2019, and would be carried out in a way that keeps total Medicare spending the same overall (budget neutral), meaning some other hospitals would see slight reductions to offset the increases. To protect all hospitals from steep cuts, the bill sets guardrails: hospitals below the 75th percentile cannot have their scores reduced to fund the adjustment, and no hospital's score can drop below 95% of what it was the prior year. In short, this primarily helps lower-wage, often rural or underserved hospitals receive more Medicare money, while limiting how much higher-wage hospitals can be affected.
ai_headline: Medicare wage index boost proposed for low-pay hospitals
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8109 raises Medicare payment scores for the lowest-paid quarter of hospitals, starting retroactively from October 1, 2019.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how Medicare calculates payments to hospitals in lower-wage areas. Hospitals with a wage index score below the 25th percentile would have that score raised halfway toward the 25th percentile, increasing their Medicare payments. The change applies retroactively starting October 1, 2019, and total Medicare spending stays the same overall.
ai_sections.who: This primarily affects hospitals in lower-wage areas, which are often rural or underserved communities. Other hospitals, particularly higher-wage ones, may see small reductions to offset the increases, though guardrails limit how steep those cuts can be.
ai_sections.why: Hospitals below the 75th percentile cannot have their scores reduced to fund the adjustment, and no hospital's score can fall below 95% of its prior-year level. This means lower-wage hospitals receive more Medicare funding while most other hospitals are largely protected from significant cuts.
ai_sections.cost: The bill is designed to be budget neutral, meaning total Medicare spending does not change. Increases for lower-wage hospitals are offset by small reductions to some higher-wage hospitals.

title: Chesapeake Bay Watershed Advancement for Training, Education, Restoration, and Science (WATERS) Act
ai_summary: This bill renews and updates the federal government office within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) that focuses on the Chesapeake Bay — the large estuary shared by Virginia, Maryland, and several surrounding states. The office would be led by a Director with hands-on experience in the Bay's research or resource management, and would be responsible for scientific monitoring of water quality and wildlife, coordinating with state and federal partners, and working with a network of buoys that track weather and ecological conditions in the Bay. The bill also authorizes two new formal programs: one to fund education and teacher training about the Bay for K-12 students and schools, and another to support the protection and restoration of important species and habitats such as oysters, blue crabs, striped bass, underwater grasses, and fish communities. People most directly affected include researchers, educators, fishermen, coastal communities, and state and local agencies around the Chesapeake Bay watershed, as well as students and teachers in the region whose environmental education programs could receive grant funding under the bill.
ai_headline: NOAA's Chesapeake Bay Office renewed with new education programs
last_action_text: Subcommittee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would renew the federal Chesapeake Bay office at NOAA and create new programs for education and habitat restoration.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews and updates the federal office inside NOAA that focuses on the Chesapeake Bay. It would create two new programs: one supporting Bay-related education and teacher training for K-12 schools, and another supporting protection and restoration of species and habitats like oysters, blue crabs, striped bass, and underwater grasses.
ai_sections.who: Researchers, educators, fishermen, coastal communities, and state and local agencies around the Chesapeake Bay watershed would be most directly affected. Students and teachers in the region could also be affected through grant funding for environmental education programs.
ai_sections.why: Without renewal, the existing federal office focused on the Bay could lose its current structure and leadership requirements. The new programs would change how restoration efforts and school-based environmental education in the region are organized and funded.

title: American Seafood Competitiveness Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill opens up U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) loan and grant programs — which have historically been available only to farmers and ranchers — to commercial fishermen and fish processors as well. Under the bill, people who catch fish for a living or who operate fish processing businesses could apply for USDA loans to buy fishing permits, purchase or upgrade fishing vessels, or acquire and improve fish processing facilities, and could also use operating loans to cover the day-to-day costs of running those operations. Wild-caught seafood would also become eligible under a USDA program that promotes local and regional food markets, and the agency would have the flexibility to waive or reduce matching-fund requirements for fishing and processing businesses applying for those marketing grants. Separately, the bill allows Farm Credit banks and production credit associations — a network of lenders that provide financing to agricultural producers — to extend credit to businesses that supply services directly to fish and seafood harvesters. Within one year of passage, the USDA would be required to take steps to bring the fishing industry into these programs, including reaching out to fishermen, training agency staff, and coordinating with other federal and state agencies.
ai_headline: USDA loans would expand to cover commercial fishing
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: Commercial fishermen and fish processors could apply for USDA loans and grants that were previously only available to farmers.
ai_sections.what: This bill lets commercial fishermen and fish processors apply for USDA loan and grant programs that have only been open to farmers and ranchers until now. They could use loans to buy fishing permits, vessels, or processing facilities, and use operating loans for everyday business costs. Wild-caught seafood would also qualify for a USDA program that supports local and regional food markets.
ai_sections.who: People who catch fish commercially, fish processing businesses, and businesses that provide services to seafood harvesters would be affected. Farm Credit banks and production credit associations would be able to lend to seafood-related service businesses under this bill.
ai_sections.why: Fishing and processing businesses would have access to a broader set of federal financing and marketing tools that were not previously available to them. Within one year of passage, USDA would be required to reach out to fishermen, train staff, and coordinate with other federal and state agencies to put these changes into effect.

title: Permanent Housing Affordability Act
ai_summary: This bill creates several federal programs to help make homeownership permanently affordable for low- and moderate-income people by supporting "shared equity" models — arrangements where a nonprofit or government entity keeps homes affordable long-term by limiting how much a seller can profit when reselling. It sets up a $100 million loan fund managed by the Treasury Department that channels low-interest construction loans (at no more than 3% interest) to local nonprofits and similar organizations to build or fix up homes that must be sold to buyers earning no more than 120% of the local median income. A separate pilot grant program, authorized at $100 million per year for five years through the Department of Housing and Urban Development, lets those same kinds of organizations buy land or existing properties to develop affordable homes, primarily for buyers earning up to 80% of the local median income (or 120% in rural areas). The bill also allows the federal government to transfer surplus federal land to community land trusts or similar organizations at a 75% discount off market value. Finally, it directs the government to conduct research, public education campaigns, and outreach to homebuyers, lenders, builders, and real estate professionals about how these shared equity homeownership models work.
ai_headline: Senate bill backs shared equity homeownership funds
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill creates federal loan and grant programs to help nonprofits build homes that stay affordable for buyers with low to moderate incomes.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets up several federal programs to support shared equity homeownership, where nonprofits or government groups limit how much a seller can profit so homes stay affordable over time. It creates a loan fund, a pilot grant program, discounted land transfers from the federal government, and public education efforts about how these models work.
ai_sections.who: The programs are aimed at nonprofits and similar local organizations that build or sell affordable homes. Homebuyers earning up to 80%, 120%, or in some cases higher percentages of their local median income may be eligible depending on the program and location.
ai_sections.why: Without these programs, low- and moderate-income buyers may have fewer options to purchase homes at prices they can afford. Shared equity rules also limit future sale profits, which affects how much wealth a homeowner can build over time.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes a $100 million loan fund managed by the Treasury Department, with loans capped at 3% interest. It also authorizes up to $100 million per year for five years through the Department of Housing and Urban Development for a pilot grant program.
costChips: Up to $100M/year for 5 years for pilot grants

title: Plug Offshore Wells Act
ai_summary: Every year, the Secretary of the Interior would be required to compile and publish a public report tracking the cleanup and shutdown of old offshore oil and gas wells, platforms, and pipelines in U.S. waters. The report would cover how many of these structures were supposed to be shut down, how many actually were, how many missed their required deadlines, and what enforcement actions — such as fines or penalties — were taken against companies that didn't comply. It would also track details about structures that were left in place underwater versus those that were physically removed. This bill primarily affects the Department of the Interior, the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement, and the oil and gas companies that own or operate offshore drilling equipment — but the resulting reports would be freely available to any member of the public on the department's website.
ai_headline: Interior Dept. must report yearly on offshore well cleanups
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill requires a yearly public report tracking whether offshore oil and gas companies are cleaning up old wells and platforms on time.
ai_sections.what: Each year, the Secretary of the Interior would have to publish a public report on old offshore oil and gas wells, platforms, and pipelines in U.S. waters. The report would show how many structures were supposed to be shut down, how many actually were, how many missed deadlines, and what enforcement actions were taken against companies that did not comply. It would also note which structures were left underwater versus physically removed.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects the Department of the Interior, the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement, and oil and gas companies that own or operate offshore drilling equipment. The published reports would be freely available to any member of the public on the department's website.
ai_sections.why: Without a regular public report, it can be hard to know whether companies are meeting their cleanup deadlines or facing consequences when they don't. This bill creates a consistent record that anyone can look up.

title: Plug Offshore Wells Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the Secretary of the Interior to produce a public annual report on the progress of shutting down and cleaning up old offshore oil and gas wells, platforms, and pipelines in U.S. waters. The report must track how many companies were required to submit decommissioning applications, how many actually did, and how many wells, platforms, and pipelines missed their legally required deadlines for being closed down or removed. It must also cover how many structures were approved to be left in place on the ocean floor rather than physically removed, and the total length of pipelines either left in place or removed. The report must include information on any enforcement actions taken against companies that failed to meet their decommissioning obligations, such as fines or bans from future offshore operations. This bill primarily affects the Department of the Interior, offshore oil and gas companies operating in U.S. waters, and members of the public or policymakers who want to track whether these companies are properly cleaning up their old offshore infrastructure.
ai_headline: Interior Dept. must report on offshore well cleanup progress
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8099 would require a public yearly report tracking whether offshore oil and gas companies are cleaning up old wells, platforms, and pipelines on time.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the Secretary of the Interior to publish a yearly public report on the cleanup and shutdown of old offshore oil and gas wells, platforms, and pipelines in U.S. waters. The report must track missed deadlines, structures left on the ocean floor, pipeline removals, and any enforcement actions taken against companies that did not meet their obligations.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects the Department of the Interior and offshore oil and gas companies operating in U.S. waters. It also affects members of the public and policymakers who want to monitor whether companies are properly handling their old offshore infrastructure.
ai_sections.why: Without a regular public record, it can be difficult to know whether companies are meeting their legal cleanup deadlines or facing consequences when they do not. This report would make that information available to the public each year.

title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Labor relating to the Adverse Effect Wage Rate.
ai_summary: This resolution would cancel a Department of Labor rule that was published in October 2025 and deals with how wages are set for temporary foreign farmworkers who come to the U.S. on H-2A visas for non-ranch jobs (such as crop harvesting). The rule being canceled established a method for calculating the "Adverse Effect Wage Rate," which is the minimum hourly pay that employers must offer H-2A workers to avoid driving down wages for American workers doing similar jobs. If this resolution passes, that wage-setting rule would be wiped out and treated as if it never took effect. This would primarily affect agricultural employers who hire H-2A temporary workers, the foreign workers themselves, and U.S. farmworkers whose wages are protected by those minimum pay requirements.
ai_headline: House moves to repeal DOL's H-2A farmworker wage rule
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress is voting to cancel a 2025 Labor Department rule that set minimum pay rates for temporary foreign farmworkers on H-2A visas.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would cancel a Department of Labor rule from October 2025 that set a minimum hourly wage for H-2A temporary foreign farmworkers in non-ranch jobs like crop harvesting. That minimum wage, called the Adverse Effect Wage Rate, was designed to keep H-2A hiring from lowering pay for American farmworkers. If the resolution passes, the rule would be erased and treated as if it never took effect.
ai_sections.who: This primarily affects agricultural employers who hire H-2A temporary workers, the foreign workers themselves, and American farmworkers in similar jobs whose wages were protected by the canceled rule.
ai_sections.why: Without the rule, there would be no longer a federally set method for calculating the minimum wage H-2A employers must pay. American farmworkers doing similar work would lose the wage floor that was meant to protect their pay from being undercut.

title: INSULIN Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would limit how much people with private health insurance pay out of pocket for insulin to no more than $35 per month's supply, with no deductible required, starting in 2027 — and from 2028 onward, that cap could be even lower if the plan's actual negotiated price works out to less. It also requires that any rebates or discounts that drug manufacturers give to pharmacy benefit managers or insurers related to insulin must be passed back entirely to the health plan, rather than kept by middlemen. For people who are uninsured, the bill would create a 5-year grant program giving 10 states up to $100 million total to help uninsured people access insulin for no more than $35 per month, and it would fund a resource center and 24/7 hotline to connect uninsured individuals with assistance programs. Finally, the bill includes measures to make it harder to use legal petitions to delay the approval of lower-cost generic or biosimilar versions of insulin, and it allows the FDA to fast-track review of biosimilar insulin products when there are fewer than three competing biosimilars on the market.
ai_headline: Senate bill caps insulin costs at $35 for insured patients
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting 2027, privately insured people would pay no more than $35/month for insulin, with no deductible required.
ai_sections.what: This bill would cap out-of-pocket insulin costs at $35 per month for people with private health insurance, with no deductible, beginning in 2027. From 2028 onward, that cap could be lower if the plan's negotiated price comes in under $35. The bill also requires that rebates and discounts from drug manufacturers be passed fully to the health plan, not kept by middlemen, and it would make it harder to use legal petitions to delay cheaper generic or biosimilar insulin products.
ai_sections.who: People with private health insurance who use insulin would be directly affected by the cost cap. Uninsured people could also be helped through a grant program available in up to 10 states, along with a resource center and a 24/7 hotline connecting them to assistance programs.
ai_sections.why: Without a cap, out-of-pocket insulin costs under private insurance vary widely and can be much higher than $35 per month. Delays in approving lower-cost insulin versions can limit how many affordable options are available on the market.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would fund grants totaling up to $100 million over five years across up to 10 states to help uninsured people access insulin at no more than $35 per month.
costChips: Up to $100M in grants over 5 years

title: AI/AN CAPTA
ai_summary: This bill changes how federal money for child abuse prevention and treatment programs is shared, specifically to direct more funding to American Indian and Alaska Native communities. Currently, a small slice of these funds (1 percent) is set aside for migrant programs, but nothing is specifically reserved for tribal communities. Under this bill, 5 percent of the available funding would be set aside for Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations, while the existing 1 percent for migrant programs would be kept. The bill also explicitly adds Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations to the list of groups that must receive an equitable share of assistance under the law. The people most directly affected are American Indian and Alaska Native children and families, as well as the tribal governments and organizations that run child abuse prevention and treatment services in their communities.
ai_headline: Bill sets aside 5% of child abuse funds for tribal groups
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4179 sets aside 5% of federal child abuse prevention funds specifically for Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how federal child abuse prevention and treatment funds are divided. It reserves 5 percent of available funding for Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations and keeps the existing 1 percent set aside for migrant programs. It also adds Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations to the list of groups that must receive an equitable share of assistance under the law.
ai_sections.who: American Indian and Alaska Native children and families are most directly affected. Tribal governments and organizations that run child abuse prevention and treatment services in their communities are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Currently, no portion of these federal funds is specifically reserved for tribal communities. This bill creates a dedicated share so that tribal communities are explicitly included in the funding structure.
ai_sections.cost: The bill directs 5 percent of the existing available funding to Indian Tribes and Tribal organizations, shifting how current funds are distributed rather than specifying a new total dollar amount.
costChips: 5% of existing funds for Tribal groups

title: SPARK Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the Small Business Administration (SBA) to create two new programs aimed at helping startups and small businesses in communities that typically struggle to access business support and funding — including low-income neighborhoods, rural areas, and communities with large minority populations. The first program would allow the SBA to provide funding agreements (at least $500,000 per year each) to organizations like nonprofits, community banks, credit unions, and community colleges that run incubators or accelerators — programs that mentor and support new and growing businesses — as long as those organizations serve groups such as women, minorities, veterans, people with disabilities, formerly incarcerated individuals, and rural entrepreneurs. The second program would allow those same types of organizations to receive up to $1,000,000 per year from the SBA and use that money to make small grants (capped at $20,000 per business) or low-interest loans directly to qualifying small businesses owned by people from those underserved groups or located in economically distressed areas, all at no fee to the businesses receiving help. Both programs include requirements for annual reporting to Congress, financial oversight, privacy protections for participants, and training support for the organizations running the programs.
ai_headline: SBA would fund incubators serving underserved entrepreneurs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Small Business.

ai_sections.tldr: The SPARK Act would create two SBA programs sending money to local organizations that support startups in underserved communities.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the Small Business Administration to set up two new programs. The first would fund organizations that run business incubators or accelerators serving underserved entrepreneurs. The second would let those same organizations give small grants of up to $20,000 or low-interest loans directly to qualifying small businesses, at no fee to the businesses.
ai_sections.who: Organizations eligible to receive SBA funding include nonprofits, community banks, credit unions, and community colleges. The small businesses and entrepreneurs they serve include women, minorities, veterans, people with disabilities, formerly incarcerated individuals, and rural entrepreneurs in low-income or economically distressed areas.
ai_sections.why: Without a program like this, organizations serving underserved communities may have limited access to dedicated federal funding for mentorship and startup support. Small businesses in these communities may also have fewer options for grants or affordable loans.
ai_sections.cost: The SBA would provide at least $500,000 per year to organizations under the first program. Under the second program, organizations could receive up to $1,000,000 per year from the SBA to fund grants and loans to small businesses.

title: To direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Republic of Cuba that have not been authorized by Congress.
ai_summary: This resolution would require the President to pull U.S. military forces out of any fighting involving Cuba unless Congress has formally declared war on Cuba or passed a specific law approving military action there. It also explicitly states that using the U.S. Coast Guard or other military forces to blockade or quarantine Cuba counts as the kind of military action that requires Congressional approval. The resolution would affect the President's ability to conduct military operations against or inside Cuba without first getting permission from Congress. There is one exception built in: nothing in the resolution would stop the U.S. from defending itself against an actual or imminent attack, or from carrying out lawful drug-interdiction operations.
ai_headline: Bill would require Congress to approve Cuba military action
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: This resolution would require Congress to approve any U.S. military action involving Cuba before the President can order it.
ai_sections.what: This resolution would require the President to withdraw U.S. military forces from any fighting involving Cuba unless Congress has formally declared war or passed a law approving that action. It also states that using the Coast Guard or other military forces to blockade Cuba counts as military action requiring Congressional approval. Two exceptions apply: the U.S. could still defend itself against an actual or imminent attack and could still carry out lawful drug-interdiction operations.
ai_sections.who: This resolution would limit the President's authority to order military operations against or inside Cuba. It would apply to U.S. Armed Forces and the U.S. Coast Guard.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the President would need Congressional approval before taking most military actions involving Cuba. Without that approval, any ongoing military involvement would have to stop.

title: AI/AN CAPTA
ai_summary: This bill changes how federal money for child abuse prevention and treatment programs is shared, specifically to direct more funding to American Indian and Alaska Native communities. Under current law, a small share of these funds must go to programs serving migrant families, but Native tribes and tribal organizations are not specifically guaranteed their own dedicated portion. This bill fixes that by requiring that 5 percent of available funds be set aside for Indian Tribes and tribal organizations, while keeping 1 percent for migrant programs. It also adds Indian Tribes and tribal organizations by name to the list of groups that must receive an equitable share of federal child abuse prevention assistance. The people most directly affected are children and families in Native American and Alaska Native communities, as well as the tribal governments and organizations that run child welfare and abuse prevention programs serving those communities.
ai_headline: Tribal groups gain dedicated child abuse prevention funding
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8048 sets aside 5% of federal child abuse prevention funds specifically for Indian Tribes and tribal organizations.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how federal child abuse prevention and treatment funds are divided. It requires that 5 percent be set aside for Indian Tribes and tribal organizations, and keeps 1 percent for programs serving migrant families. It also adds Indian Tribes and tribal organizations by name to the list of groups that must receive an equitable share of these funds.
ai_sections.who: Children and families in Native American and Alaska Native communities are most directly affected. Tribal governments and organizations that run child welfare and abuse prevention programs are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Under current law, tribes and tribal organizations are not specifically guaranteed their own dedicated portion of these funds. This bill creates that guarantee.
ai_sections.cost: The bill redirects a portion of existing federal child abuse prevention funds, setting 5 percent aside for Indian Tribes and tribal organizations and maintaining 1 percent for migrant programs.
costChips: Redirects existing federal child abuse funds

title: FAIC Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how Medicare pays hospitals for certain newer cancer drugs and biologics given in outpatient settings. Right now, payment for some of these treatments is "bundled" or folded into a single lump payment for a broader outpatient visit or procedure, which can leave hospitals underpaid for expensive drugs. Under this bill, if a cancer treatment was FDA-approved on or after January 1, 2008, and its estimated daily cost reaches or exceeds $350 (a threshold that would rise each year with inflation), Medicare must pay for it separately rather than bundling it into the overall visit payment. The separate payment would generally be based on the drug's average sales price, similar to how Medicare already pays for other outpatient drugs. The bill primarily affects hospitals and other outpatient providers that administer these cancer treatments, as well as Medicare patients who may otherwise lose access to costly newer therapies if providers cannot cover their costs under bundled payment rates.
ai_headline: Medicare would pay separately for newer outpatient cancer drugs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Medicare would pay separately for newer cancer drugs costing $350/day or more instead of folding them into one bundled payment.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how Medicare pays for certain cancer drugs and biologics given in outpatient settings. If a cancer treatment was FDA-approved on or after January 1, 2008, and its estimated daily cost reaches or exceeds $350, Medicare must pay for it separately rather than bundling it into the overall visit payment. That $350 threshold would increase each year with inflation, and the separate payment would generally be based on the drug's average sales price.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects hospitals and other outpatient providers that give these cancer treatments. Medicare patients who receive costly newer cancer therapies are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Under the current bundled system, hospitals may not be fully covered for the cost of expensive newer drugs. If providers cannot cover those costs, some patients could lose access to these therapies.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare would make separate payments for qualifying cancer drugs based on average sales price, rather than including those costs in a single bundled payment.

title: Feeding Families Not Fear Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill would undo two things that a previously enacted law (referred to in the bill as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act") put in place. First, it would cancel the extra funding that law gave to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), taking back any of that money that has not yet been spent. Second, it would restore food stamp (SNAP) benefits to their earlier, higher levels by wiping out the portion of that prior law that reduced those benefits. The people most directly affected are SNAP recipients — low-income individuals and families who rely on food assistance — who would see their benefit amounts return to what they were before the cuts, as well as the federal immigration enforcement agency, which would lose the additional funding it had received.
ai_headline: Bill targets ICE funds and restores SNAP benefit levels
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Agriculture, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would restore SNAP food benefits to higher levels and remove extra funding that ICE received under a recent law.
ai_sections.what: This bill would reverse two parts of a recent law. It would bring SNAP food stamp benefits back up to their earlier, higher amounts and take back any unspent extra money that was given to Immigration and Customs Enforcement.
ai_sections.who: Low-income people and families who receive SNAP benefits would see their food assistance amounts go back up. Immigration and Customs Enforcement would lose the additional funding it had received.
ai_sections.why: SNAP recipients had their benefits reduced under the earlier law, so this bill would undo that reduction. The extra ICE funding that has not yet been spent would be cancelled.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would pull back unspent funds that had been directed to ICE and would increase SNAP benefit levels compared to what the prior law set.

title: GUARD VA Benefits Act
ai_summary: This bill restores a federal criminal penalty for anyone who charges a veteran an unauthorized fee to help file a VA benefits claim. Under current law, certain accredited attorneys and claims agents are allowed to charge fees, but anyone else — such as unaccredited individuals or companies — is prohibited from doing so. If this bill becomes law, anyone who solicits, charges, or collects an unauthorized fee related to preparing or filing a VA benefits claim could be fined under federal law. The people most affected are veterans seeking benefits help and any individuals or businesses that offer claims assistance services without proper government accreditation.
ai_headline: Bill would restore fines for unauthorized VA claims fees
last_action_text: Committee Hearings Held

ai_sections.tldr: This bill makes it a federal crime again to charge veterans unauthorized fees for help filing VA benefits claims.
ai_sections.what: This bill brings back a federal criminal penalty for charging veterans fees that are not allowed by law when helping them file VA benefits claims. Only accredited attorneys and claims agents are currently permitted to charge fees for this help. Anyone else who asks for or collects an unauthorized fee could face a federal fine.
ai_sections.who: Veterans who seek help filing benefits claims are most directly affected. Individuals and businesses that offer claims help without proper government accreditation are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Without this penalty in place, unauthorized people or companies can charge veterans fees for claims help without facing federal consequences. This bill restores that consequence under federal law.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not specify fine amounts, but people who charge unauthorized fees could be fined under federal law.

title: Save the World Cup Act
ai_summary: This bill would ban the use of federal money by the Department of Homeland Security or the Department of Justice to conduct civil immigration enforcement — such as detaining or arresting people for immigration violations — within one mile of any 2026 FIFA World Cup soccer match or official Fan Festival held in the United States. The restriction would apply to the areas immediately surrounding those events, meaning immigration agents could not use federal funds to carry out those activities in that zone during those events. There are four exceptions where enforcement would still be allowed: if there is an immediate threat of death, violence, or physical harm to someone (including terrorism); an imminent threat to national security; the active pursuit or arrest of someone posing an immediate public safety danger; or the risk that evidence in an ongoing criminal case is about to be destroyed. This bill would primarily affect federal immigration enforcement agencies and, indirectly, the large number of fans — including foreign nationals — expected to attend World Cup matches and Fan Festivals across the country.
ai_headline: Bill would bar DHS immigration arrests near World Cup sites
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7986 would ban federal immigration arrests within one mile of 2026 World Cup matches and Fan Festivals, with four narrow exceptions.
ai_sections.what: This bill would stop the Department of Homeland Security and the Department of Justice from using federal money to detain or arrest people for immigration violations within one mile of any 2026 FIFA World Cup match or official Fan Festival in the United States. Four situations would still allow enforcement: an immediate threat of death, violence, or physical harm including terrorism; an imminent threat to national security; the active pursuit of someone posing an immediate public safety danger; or the risk that evidence in a criminal case is about to be destroyed.
ai_sections.who: This bill would directly affect federal immigration enforcement agencies. It would also indirectly affect the many fans, including foreign nationals, expected to attend World Cup matches and Fan Festivals across the country.
ai_sections.why: Without this restriction, federal immigration agents could conduct civil enforcement actions in the areas surrounding World Cup events. With it, those agencies would be required to pause that activity near the events unless one of the four exceptions applies.

title: Energy Bills Relief Act
ai_summary: This bill is a broad package of energy policy changes aimed at lowering electricity and heating costs for American households, particularly lower-income families. It would restore clean energy tax credits that were recently rolled back, expand and increase funding for programs that help low-income households pay their heating and cooling bills, and create rebates for energy-saving home improvements like reflective roofing and weatherization upgrades. It would also require that wind, solar, and energy storage projects go through the same federal permitting process as oil, gas, and coal projects — no easier, but no harder — and would invest in modernizing the electricity grid, including faster connections for new power generators and stronger transmission lines. The bill affects a wide range of people and organizations: low-income families who rely on heating and cooling assistance, homeowners seeking energy efficiency help, energy companies and utilities, federal agencies overseeing energy and environmental rules, and communities near public lands and waters where energy projects are developed.
ai_headline: Bill would cut home energy costs and restore clean-energy tax credits
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Agriculture, Ways and Means, Natural Resources, Financial Services, Transportation and Infrastructure, Education and Workforce, Oversight and Government Reform, and Science, Space, and Technology, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7977 would change energy tax credits, heating aid, home rebates, and permitting rules to affect household energy costs.
ai_sections.what: This bill would restore clean energy tax credits that were recently removed and create rebates for home improvements like weatherization and reflective roofing. It would expand programs that help lower-income households pay heating and cooling bills. It would also require wind, solar, and storage projects to follow the same federal permitting process as oil, gas, and coal projects, and would invest in modernizing the electricity grid.
ai_sections.who: Lower-income families who rely on heating and cooling assistance, homeowners seeking energy efficiency help, and energy companies and utilities would all be affected. Federal agencies overseeing energy and environmental rules and communities near public lands where energy projects are developed would also be impacted.
ai_sections.why: Restoring tax credits and expanding assistance programs could change how much households pay for energy. Applying the same permitting rules to all energy sources could affect how quickly different types of energy projects move forward.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would increase funding for programs that help low-income households pay their heating and cooling bills, though the summary does not specify exact amounts.

title: 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
ai_summary: This wide-ranging bill is designed to increase the supply and affordability of housing across the United States through dozens of separate measures affecting homebuyers, renters, landlords, builders, lenders, and government agencies at all levels. On the homeownership side, it creates pilot programs to make small mortgages (under $100,000) more accessible, strengthens housing counseling for people at risk of foreclosure, and takes steps to make it easier for lenders — especially community banks and credit unions — to offer home loans. For renters and low-income households, it funds home repair grants for low-income homeowners and small landlords, supports affordable housing construction, and launches a pilot program to install temperature sensors in federally assisted rental units to help ensure safe living conditions. For builders and local governments, it directs federal agencies to publish guidelines on zoning reforms (such as reducing parking requirements and allowing more types of housing), streamlines environmental reviews for certain rural housing projects, and encourages repurposing vacant buildings and publicly owned land into housing. The bill also addresses manufactured and modular housing, veteran homelessness, oversight of federal housing programs, and banking rules — making it one of the broadest federal housing policy packages in recent memory.
ai_headline: Bill targets housing costs with zoning and loan reforms
last_action_text: Message on Senate action sent to the House.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6644 uses dozens of separate measures to try to increase housing supply and lower costs for buyers, renters, and builders across the U.S.
ai_sections.what: This bill tackles housing from many angles at once. It creates pilot programs for small mortgages and temperature sensors in rental units, funds home repairs, supports affordable housing construction, and pushes local governments to reform zoning rules. It also addresses manufactured housing, veteran homelessness, and banking rules for lenders.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects homebuyers, renters, low-income households, landlords, homebuilders, community banks, credit unions, local governments, and federal housing agencies. Veterans experiencing homelessness and people at risk of foreclosure are also specifically addressed.
ai_sections.why: If passed, more types of lenders could offer home loans and more land could be converted into housing, which would change how much housing is available in many communities. Local governments would receive federal guidance on zoning changes, which could affect what kinds of homes are allowed to be built.
ai_sections.cost: The bill funds home repair grants for low-income homeowners and small landlords and supports affordable housing construction, though the summary does not specify exact dollar amounts or identify who provides the funding.

title: State Veterans Homes Inspection Simplification Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow nursing homes run by states specifically for veterans — called State Veterans Homes — to skip a separate federal inspection process for Medicare and Medicaid if they have already been inspected and certified by the Department of Veterans Affairs, as long as the VA's inspection standards meet or exceed federal Medicare requirements. Right now, these facilities can face overlapping inspections from both the VA and federal Medicare/Medicaid authorities, and this bill would let a passing VA inspection count for both, reducing that duplication. The federal government would still be able to investigate complaints, impose penalties, or pull a facility's "deemed compliant" status if problems are found, and inspection results would still be made available to the public online. This affects veterans living in state-run veterans nursing homes, the states that operate those homes, the VA, and the federal agency that oversees Medicare and Medicaid. An independent government watchdog would be required to review how well the new system is working after three years and report back to Congress.
ai_headline: VA inspections would satisfy Medicare rules at state veterans homes
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

ai_sections.tldr: States running VA-certified veterans nursing homes could skip a separate Medicare/Medicaid inspection under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill would let state-run veterans nursing homes skip a separate federal Medicare and Medicaid inspection if the Department of Veterans Affairs has already inspected and certified them. That VA inspection would count for both, as long as VA standards meet or exceed federal Medicare requirements. The federal government could still investigate complaints, apply penalties, or remove a facility's compliant status, and results would stay public online.
ai_sections.who: This affects veterans living in state-run veterans nursing homes, the states that operate those homes, the VA, and the federal agency that oversees Medicare and Medicaid. An independent government watchdog would review how the new system is working after three years and report to Congress.
ai_sections.why: Right now these facilities can face overlapping inspections from both the VA and federal Medicare and Medicaid authorities. This bill would reduce that duplication by allowing one passing VA inspection to satisfy both requirements.

title: Clarence Mitchell, Jr. Statue Act
ai_summary: This bill directs a congressional committee — the Joint Committee of Congress on the Library — to obtain a statue of Clarence Mitchell, Jr. and place it permanently in the U.S. Capitol building, with two years to arrange that acquisition. Clarence Mitchell, Jr. was a Baltimore-born civil rights leader who directed the NAACP's Washington office for nearly three decades and played a major role in the passage of landmark civil rights legislation in the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s; he received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1980. The bill authorizes whatever federal funding is needed to cover the cost of the statue. It primarily affects the congressional and Capitol administrative officials responsible for carrying out the acquisition and installation, while the statue itself would be visible to the general public who visit the Capitol.
ai_headline: Capitol to receive statue of civil rights leader Clarence Mitchell
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would place a permanent statue of civil rights leader Clarence Mitchell, Jr. in the U.S. Capitol.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs a congressional committee to obtain a statue of Clarence Mitchell, Jr. and install it permanently in the U.S. Capitol. The committee would have two years to arrange the acquisition.
ai_sections.who: The Joint Committee of Congress on the Library is responsible for carrying out the acquisition and installation. Capitol and congressional administrative officials would handle the process, and the statue would be visible to anyone who visits the Capitol.
ai_sections.why: Clarence Mitchell, Jr. was a Baltimore-born civil rights leader who led the NAACP's Washington office for nearly three decades. He played a major role in passing landmark civil rights laws in the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s, and received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1980.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes federal funding to cover whatever the statue costs, though no specific dollar amount is stated.

title: ACTION for National Service Act
ai_summary: This bill converts the existing Corporation for National and Community Service — the federal body that oversees AmeriCorps and related volunteer programs — into a new executive agency called the AmeriCorps Administration, led by a Director with cabinet-level rank rather than a corporate board structure. It significantly expands educational awards for people who complete national service, setting the award amount equal to twice the average annual in-state tuition at a public four-year college, and makes both those awards and volunteers' living allowances tax-free. The bill aims to grow national service participation to at least one million people per year within ten years, and it creates an outreach program to notify every American between ages 17 and 30 about opportunities to serve in AmeriCorps, the Peace Corps, or the military. It also establishes a new nonprofit National Service Foundation to accept private donations in support of service programs. These changes would primarily affect current and future AmeriCorps and VISTA volunteers, nonprofit organizations that run service programs, and young Americans who might be recruited into national service.
ai_headline: Senate bill would expand AmeriCorps awards and reach
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S1048-1049)

ai_sections.tldr: S 4068 would rename and reshape the agency that runs AmeriCorps, double education awards, and aim to enroll 1 million volunteers a year.
ai_sections.what: This bill replaces the Corporation for National and Community Service with a new agency called the AmeriCorps Administration, led by a cabinet-level Director. It doubles education awards for people who complete national service, ties the award amount to twice the average in-state tuition at a public four-year college, and makes both those awards and living allowances tax-free. It also sets a goal of one million national service participants per year within ten years, creates outreach to Americans ages 17 to 30, and sets up a new nonprofit called the National Service Foundation to accept private donations.
ai_sections.who: Current and future AmeriCorps and VISTA volunteers would be most directly affected, along with nonprofit organizations that run service programs. Young Americans between ages 17 and 30 would be targeted by a new outreach effort about service opportunities in AmeriCorps, the Peace Corps, or the military.
ai_sections.why: Restructuring the agency under a cabinet-level Director would change how national service programs are governed and made accountable. Expanding education awards and removing taxes on those awards and living allowances would change the financial picture for people who choose to serve.

title: ACTION for National Service Act
ai_summary: This bill would transform AmeriCorps — the federal agency that runs national volunteer and community service programs — from a government corporation into a full executive department led by a Director (similar in rank to a Cabinet secretary), renaming it the "AmeriCorps Administration." It would increase the education awards that volunteers earn for their service, setting them equal to twice the average annual in-state tuition at public four-year colleges, and would make both those education awards and the living allowances paid to participants tax-free. The bill also sets a goal of expanding national service opportunities to reach at least one million participants per year within ten years, and creates an outreach program to contact every American between ages 17 and 30 to inform them about service opportunities. Additionally, it establishes a new private nonprofit National Service Foundation to accept donations supporting the agency's mission, and creates an interagency working group to study whether national service veterans should receive preference in federal hiring. The bill primarily affects current and future AmeriCorps volunteers and participants in related programs like VISTA and the National Senior Service Corps, as well as the organizations and communities they serve.
ai_headline: Bill would expand AmeriCorps and raise service awards
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7910 would restructure AmeriCorps into a full executive department, raise education awards, and aim for 1 million service participants per year within 10 years.
ai_sections.what: This bill would turn AmeriCorps into a full executive department called the AmeriCorps Administration, led by a Director at Cabinet-level rank. It would raise education awards for volunteers to twice the average in-state tuition at public four-year colleges and make both those awards and living allowances tax-free. The bill also sets a goal of one million national service participants per year within ten years, creates outreach to Americans ages 17 to 30, establishes a private nonprofit National Service Foundation to accept donations, and starts a study on whether service veterans should get preference in federal hiring.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects current and future AmeriCorps volunteers and participants in related programs like VISTA and the National Senior Service Corps. It also affects the communities and organizations those participants serve.
ai_sections.why: Restructuring AmeriCorps as an executive department would change how the agency is governed and how much authority its leader holds. Raising education awards and removing taxes on awards and living allowances would change the financial picture for participants.

title: Justice for Hind Rajab Act
ai_summary: This bill responds to the killing of a 5-year-old girl named Hind Rajab, six of her family members, and two paramedics in Gaza City on January 29, 2024, and requires specific U.S. government actions to investigate what happened. Within 45 days of becoming law, the Secretary of State must submit a detailed report to Congress covering what U.S. officials know about the attack — including whether the weapons used were supplied by the United States, whether any U.S. citizens were among the soldiers involved, and whether those soldiers received U.S. training. If credible information suggests the attack may have been a war crime and involved U.S. weapons, citizens, or U.S.-trained personnel, the Secretary of State must refer that information to the Attorney General, who must then certify that the Justice Department will review it and potentially open a criminal investigation and prosecution under existing U.S. war crimes law. The bill also expresses Congress's view that Israel should compensate the victims' families, and that the U.S. should do the same if American citizens serving in the Israeli military are found to have been involved.
ai_headline: Bill would probe U.S. role in Gaza girl's killing
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 7903 requires U.S. officials to report on and potentially investigate the January 2024 killing of Hind Rajab in Gaza.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the Secretary of State to report to Congress within 45 days about the January 29, 2024 killing of five-year-old Hind Rajab, six family members, and two paramedics in Gaza City. The report must address whether U.S. weapons, U.S. citizens, or U.S.-trained soldiers were involved. If credible information points to a possible war crime with U.S. connections, the case must be referred to the Attorney General for potential criminal investigation.
ai_sections.who: The Secretary of State and the Attorney General are the main officials with duties under this bill. The bill also expresses Congress's view that Israel and potentially the U.S. government should compensate the victims' families.
ai_sections.why: If the required report is not completed, Congress would lack an official accounting of any U.S. role in the attack. If a referral to the Attorney General is not made when the standard is met, existing U.S. war crimes law could go unenforced in this case.

title: FOOD for Health Act
ai_summary: This bill would direct the U.S. Department of Agriculture, working with the Department of Health and Human Services, to create a pilot grant program that funds community organizations working to improve people's health through food. Eligible organizations — such as food banks and community health clinics working together — could use the money for things like delivering medically appropriate meals, writing "produce prescriptions," teaching cooking skills, and providing transportation to program participants. The program targets people who have, are at risk of, or are recovering from health conditions linked to diet, including diabetes, heart disease, obesity, cancer, and mental illness, among others. Up to $20 million is authorized for the program from 2027 through 2031, and the Agriculture Department would be required to submit progress reports to Congress assessing how well the program works and whether it improves participants' health.
ai_headline: USDA food-as-medicine pilot grants proposed for 2027
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Agriculture, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: The FOOD for Health Act would create a pilot grant program funding community groups that use food to help people with diet-related health conditions.
ai_sections.what: This bill would direct the USDA, working with the Department of Health and Human Services, to create a pilot grant program for community organizations that use food to support people's health. Funded activities could include delivering medically appropriate meals, writing produce prescriptions, teaching cooking skills, and providing transportation to participants.
ai_sections.who: Eligible organizations include partnerships like food banks and community health clinics working together. The program targets people who have, are at risk of, or are recovering from diet-related conditions such as diabetes, heart disease, obesity, cancer, and mental illness.
ai_sections.why: The USDA would be required to submit progress reports to Congress assessing how well the program works and whether participants' health improves. Those results would inform Congress about whether the program is meeting its goals.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $20 million is authorized for the program covering the years 2027 through 2031.

title: Economy of the Future Commission Act of 2026
ai_summary: This bill creates a temporary, bipartisan advisory commission made up of 10 members appointed by congressional leaders — some of whom are current members of Congress and others who are outside experts in areas like artificial intelligence, education, workforce training, or taxation — along with four non-voting deputy secretaries from the Departments of Education, Labor, Commerce, and Treasury. The commission's job is to study how the spread of artificial intelligence is changing the economy — including effects on jobs, education, job retraining programs, taxes, manufacturing, transportation, and government services — and then produce recommendations for new laws to address those changes. Within about seven months the commission must release a public interim report that includes estimates of how AI may affect employment and federal tax revenue five and ten years out, and within about thirteen months it must deliver a final report with specific legislative recommendations to key congressional committees and four Cabinet secretaries, who then have 60 days to respond with their own assessments. The commission is funded with $5.25 million, and it shuts down 120 days after delivering its final report. Ordinary Americans are affected indirectly, since the commission's work could shape future laws on job retraining programs, unemployment insurance, education, and how AI-driven industries are taxed.
ai_headline: Bill would create AI economic impact advisory panel
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would create a temporary commission to study how AI is changing jobs and the economy, then recommend new laws within about 13 months.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a short-term advisory commission to study how artificial intelligence is reshaping jobs, education, taxes, manufacturing, transportation, and government services. The commission would publish an interim report after about seven months and a final report with specific legislative recommendations after about thirteen months.
ai_sections.who: The commission would have 10 members appointed by congressional leaders, including some current members of Congress and some outside experts in AI, education, workforce training, and taxation. Four non-voting deputy secretaries from the Departments of Education, Labor, Commerce, and Treasury would also participate. Key congressional committees and four Cabinet secretaries would receive the final report and have 60 days to respond.
ai_sections.why: The commission's recommendations could shape future laws on job retraining, unemployment insurance, education, and how AI-driven industries are taxed. Ordinary Americans would be affected indirectly through whatever legislation might follow from those recommendations.
ai_sections.cost: The commission would be funded with $5.25 million and would shut down 120 days after delivering its final report.

title: Healthy Competition for Better Care Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal for health insurance companies and employer health plans to sign contracts with hospitals, doctors' networks, or other health care providers that contain certain clauses limiting competition. Specifically, it bans contract terms that prevent an insurer from steering patients toward cheaper or higher-quality providers, force an insurer to also contract with a hospital's or provider's affiliated facilities at set prices, or stop other insurers from negotiating lower prices than what one insurer already agreed to pay. There are exceptions for certain coordinated-care arrangements — such as HMOs that work through exclusive physician groups, accountable care organizations, and centers of excellence — where such restrictions may still be allowed because those models are designed around integrated, quality-focused care. States can also temporarily shield certain specific contracts that were signed around 2019–2020 from the new rules for up to ten years, but only if the state determines those contracts are unlikely to significantly harm competition. The rules would apply to new, renewed, or amended contracts starting 18 months after the law is enacted, and the relevant federal agencies must issue regulations within one year of enactment.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets anticompetitive health insurance contracts
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would ban certain contract clauses between insurers and health providers that limit competition, starting 18 months after passage.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal for health insurance companies and employer health plans to sign contracts with hospitals or doctor networks that include clauses limiting competition. The banned clauses include ones that stop insurers from directing patients to lower-cost providers, force insurers to accept set prices across affiliated facilities, or block other insurers from negotiating lower prices.
