ai_sections.why: Courts and opposing parties would know when an outside financial interest exists in a case. Without this rule, that information could remain hidden from the people and institutions deciding or involved in the lawsuit.

title: Protect American Fisheries Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill expands the situations in which the federal government can officially declare a "fishery resource disaster," which can unlock federal assistance for struggling fishing communities. Currently, a disaster can be declared when fish populations collapse due to natural causes or human activities, but this bill adds a new category: economic harm caused by **foreign entities**. Specifically, if a foreign government, company, or individual is found to be distorting the market for American seafood — through practices like illegal fishing, predatory pricing (selling seafood at artificially low prices to undercut competitors), or unfair subsidies — that could now qualify as grounds for a disaster declaration. The bill affects U.S. commercial fishing communities, particularly those who believe foreign competition is undermining their livelihoods, and it requires that requests for such a disaster declaration include documented evidence of the foreign activity and its economic effects on the fishery.
ai_headline: Bill expands fishery disaster rules to cover foreign harm
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6150 would let the federal government declare a fishery disaster when foreign entities harm American seafood markets through illegal or unfair practices.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds a new reason the federal government can officially declare a fishery resource disaster. That declaration can unlock federal assistance for fishing communities. The new category covers economic harm caused by foreign governments, companies, or individuals through practices like illegal fishing, predatory pricing, or unfair subsidies.
ai_sections.who: U.S. commercial fishing communities are most directly affected, especially those facing competition they believe is driven by unfair foreign practices. Anyone requesting this type of disaster declaration would need to provide documented evidence of the foreign activity and its economic effects.
ai_sections.why: If passed, fishing communities would have a new legal pathway to seek federal help when they can show foreign market distortion is hurting them. It also places a documentation requirement on those seeking such a declaration.

title: RIDER Safety Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow public transit agencies to use existing federal grant money — money already designated for crime prevention and security on public transportation — to pay for the day-to-day operating costs of a new category of workers called "transit support specialists." These are unarmed employees who would work on buses, trains, stops, and stations to make riders feel safer by monitoring the environment, helping passengers, reporting suspicious activity, handling minor conflicts without involving police, and connecting people in crisis with appropriate services. Currently, that grant funding can only be used for capital purchases (like equipment or infrastructure), so this change would expand it to cover the ongoing personnel costs of hiring and deploying these workers. The bill primarily affects public transit agencies, their riders, and potentially law enforcement — since one stated goal of these specialists is to handle lower-level issues so that police can focus on more serious incidents.
ai_headline: Bill expands transit security grants to fund unarmed staff
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6069 lets transit agencies use existing federal security grants to hire unarmed support workers on buses and trains.
ai_sections.what: This bill would let public transit agencies use federal grant money that is already set aside for transit safety to pay the ongoing wages of a new type of worker called a transit support specialist. These unarmed employees would work on buses, trains, stops, and stations to monitor the environment, help passengers, handle minor conflicts, report suspicious activity, and connect people in crisis with services. Right now, that grant money can only be spent on equipment or infrastructure, not on staffing costs.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects public transit agencies and the riders who use their systems. Local law enforcement could also be affected, since these specialists would handle lower-level situations that police currently respond to.
ai_sections.why: If passed, transit agencies would have a new way to staff these positions without finding separate funding sources. Police resources could shift toward more serious incidents as lower-level issues are handled by the support specialists instead.
ai_sections.cost: The bill does not create new federal funding. It would allow existing federal security grant money to be spent on personnel costs, which is not currently permitted.
costChips: Allows grants to cover personnel costs

title: Bringing Assistance for Rural Needs During Shutdowns Act
ai_summary: This bill would require Farm Service Agency (FSA) offices to stay open and keep operating during a federal government shutdown. It does this by legally classifying all FSA work as "essential," the same category used for services that protect people's lives or property — which are the services allowed to continue even when the government runs out of funding authority. The Farm Service Agency is the part of the U.S. Department of Agriculture that helps farmers and ranchers access loans, disaster assistance, crop insurance, and other financial programs. This bill would primarily affect farmers and rural communities who depend on FSA services, as well as the FSA employees who would be required to continue working during a shutdown rather than being sent home.
ai_headline: Bill would keep Farm Service Agency open in shutdowns
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 6018 would require Farm Service Agency offices to stay open and keep running during a federal government shutdown.
ai_sections.what: This bill would classify all Farm Service Agency work as "essential," meaning FSA offices could not close during a federal government shutdown. Essential services are those allowed to keep running when the government loses its funding authority, because they protect people's lives or property.
ai_sections.who: The Farm Service Agency is the part of the U.S. Department of Agriculture that connects farmers and ranchers with loans, disaster assistance, crop insurance, and other financial programs. This bill would affect farmers and rural communities who rely on those services, as well as FSA employees who would be required to keep working during a shutdown.
ai_sections.why: Currently, FSA offices can be shut down during a federal funding lapse, which can interrupt access to loans and disaster assistance for farmers and rural communities. This bill would change that by giving FSA the same legal standing as other services that are not allowed to stop during a shutdown.

title: CARE Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires Medicare's Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation to test a new payment model in which Medicare would pay ground ambulance providers for responding to a 911 emergency call **even if the patient is treated on the scene and not transported to a hospital**. Currently, Medicare generally only pays ambulance providers when they actually transport a patient, which can create pressure to take people to the hospital even when it may not be necessary. Under this five-year pilot program, ambulance crews could instead treat patients at the scene — potentially including remote medical guidance through video technology — and still receive payment roughly in line with what they would have earned for a transport. The program primarily affects Medicare patients (generally people 65 and older, or those with certain disabilities) and the ground ambulance providers who serve them. Within four years of the program launching, the Government Accountability Office must report to Congress on how the pilot affected patient outcomes, access to emergency services, and other factors, along with recommendations for improvement.
ai_headline: Medicare bill would fund on-scene ambulance care
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S7964)

ai_sections.tldr: Medicare could pay ambulance crews for treating patients on the scene even when no hospital trip is needed, under a 5-year pilot program.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a five-year pilot program testing a new way for Medicare to pay ground ambulance providers. Under the pilot, ambulance crews could treat a patient at the scene after a 911 call and still receive payment, even if the patient is never transported to a hospital. Treatment could include remote medical guidance through video technology.
ai_sections.who: This primarily affects Medicare patients, generally people 65 and older or those with certain disabilities, and the ground ambulance providers who serve them. The Government Accountability Office would also be required to report to Congress on how the pilot performed.
ai_sections.why: Currently, Medicare generally only pays ambulance providers when they transport a patient, which can create pressure to take people to the hospital even when it may not be necessary. This pilot tests whether paying for on-scene care changes that dynamic, and a required report will assess the effects on patient outcomes and access to emergency services.

title: MATCH Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes the rules for a federal emergency watershed protection program so that state and local governments and tribal nations can get credit for cleanup work they pay for *before* signing a formal agreement with the U.S. Department of Agriculture. Right now, sponsors typically must wait to finalize a cost-sharing agreement with the federal government before their expenses count toward their required share of a project's cost; this bill allows pre-agreement spending to count toward that share. Within 180 days of passage, the Agriculture Secretary would be required to publish a list of approved emergency watershed measures that sponsors can start paying for early, and to create a process for sponsors to request that additional measures be added to that list for specific natural disasters. Importantly, any sponsor that starts work before an agreement is signed takes on full financial risk — meaning the federal government is not obligated to reimburse those costs or even to sign an agreement at all. This primarily affects state and local governments and tribal nations that respond to watershed emergencies such as floods, wildfires, or mudslides and want to act quickly without waiting for federal paperwork to be completed first.
ai_headline: USDA watershed sponsors could get credit for early cleanup costs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: States, tribes, and local governments could count emergency watershed cleanup costs paid before a federal agreement is signed toward their required share.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes federal rules so that state, local, and tribal sponsors of emergency watershed projects can get credit for cleanup work they pay for before signing a cost-sharing agreement with the U.S. Department of Agriculture. Within 180 days of passage, the Agriculture Secretary must publish a list of approved measures sponsors can begin paying for early, and create a process to add measures for specific disasters.
ai_sections.who: This primarily affects state and local governments and tribal nations that respond to watershed emergencies such as floods, wildfires, or mudslides.
ai_sections.why: Sponsors who act before an agreement is signed take on full financial risk, because the federal government is not required to reimburse those costs or to sign an agreement at all. This allows faster local action during emergencies without waiting for federal paperwork, but with no guarantee of federal cost-sharing.
ai_sections.cost: Any sponsor that begins work early bears the full cost if the federal government does not sign an agreement. The bill does not guarantee federal reimbursement for pre-agreement spending.

title: Ensuring Timely Access to Generics Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill tightens the rules around a legal process called a "citizen petition," which allows drug companies or others to ask the FDA to take some action — and which can be used to slow down the approval of cheaper generic or biosimilar drugs. Under the bill, anyone who wants to file a lawsuit to block or delay a generic drug approval must first submit a citizen petition to the FDA, and that petition must be filed within 180 days of learning the information it is based on — if they miss that window, a court must permanently throw out their lawsuit. The bill also gives the FDA a specific list of factors it can use to determine whether a petition was filed mainly to delay a competitor's drug rather than for a genuine safety or scientific reason, such as whether the petition was filed very close to a key approval date, whether it lacked supporting data, or whether the company has a history of filing petitions found to be delay tactics. The people most directly affected are brand-name drug manufacturers who use citizen petitions as a competitive tool, generic and biosimilar drug manufacturers trying to bring lower-cost alternatives to market, and ultimately patients and payers who could benefit from faster access to those cheaper drugs.
ai_headline: Senate bill targets FDA citizen petition delays
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill sets strict deadlines and rules for using a legal petition process that can delay cheaper generic drug approvals.
ai_sections.what: This bill tightens rules around "citizen petitions," which let companies ask the FDA to take action and can be used to slow down approval of generic or biosimilar drugs. Anyone who wants to sue to block a generic drug approval must first file a citizen petition within 180 days of learning the relevant information, or a court must permanently dismiss their case. The FDA also gets a specific list of factors to help identify petitions filed mainly to delay a competitor rather than for genuine safety or scientific reasons.
ai_sections.who: Brand-name drug manufacturers who use citizen petitions as a competitive tool are most directly affected by the new limits. Generic and biosimilar drug makers, patients, and payers are also affected because the rules could change how quickly lower-cost drugs reach the market.
ai_sections.why: If the bill becomes law, courts would be required to throw out delay-related lawsuits that do not follow the new petition rules. The FDA would also have clearer authority to identify petitions filed as competitive tactics rather than for legitimate reasons.

title: Physical Therapist Workforce and Patient Access Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill makes two main changes aimed at getting more physical therapists to work in underserved communities and making it easier for certain Medicare patients to receive physical therapy. First, it adds physical therapists to the National Health Service Corps Loan Repayment Program, which means physical therapists who agree to work in areas with a shortage of healthcare providers could receive federal help paying off their student loans; the bill also sets aside at least $15 million specifically for those loan repayments by increasing a funding pot from about $173 million to about $188 million. Second, starting January 1, 2027, Medicare will be required to cover physical therapy services provided at Rural Health Clinics and Federally Qualified Health Centers — the types of clinics that often serve rural, low-income, or otherwise underserved patients — which currently are not required to cover those services. The people most directly affected are physical therapists carrying student debt who might be willing to work in underserved areas, and Medicare patients in rural or low-income communities who currently have limited access to physical therapy through these clinics.
ai_headline: Bill would add physical therapists to NHSC loan program
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2027, Medicare must cover physical therapy at rural and low-income clinics, and physical therapists can get federal student loan help.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds physical therapists to a federal program that helps pay off student loans for health workers who work in areas short on providers. It also requires Medicare to cover physical therapy services at Rural Health Clinics and Federally Qualified Health Centers beginning January 1, 2027.
ai_sections.who: Physical therapists with student debt who are open to working in underserved areas would be eligible for loan repayment help. Medicare patients in rural or low-income communities who currently have limited access to physical therapy through these clinics would gain new coverage options.
ai_sections.why: Areas with too few health providers may attract more physical therapists if those therapists can get help paying off their loans. Requiring Medicare to cover physical therapy at these clinics means more patients in underserved areas could access that care.
ai_sections.cost: The bill increases a funding pool from about $173 million to about $188 million, with at least $15 million of that set aside specifically for physical therapist loan repayments.

title: Patients Deserve Price Tags Act
ai_summary: This bill requires hospitals, health insurance plans, diagnostic labs, imaging centers, and outpatient surgery centers to publicly post their actual prices — in plain language and easy-to-read formats — for medical services, so that patients can see what things cost before receiving care. Hospitals would need to publish their full list prices, the discounted cash price they accept from patients who pay out of pocket, and the specific rates they have negotiated with each insurance company, updated monthly. Health insurance plans would also have to provide patients with a real-time online tool showing exactly what the patient will owe for a specific service from a specific doctor, including how much of their deductible they have already met, and the plan could not charge the patient more than that estimate. Providers who fail to comply can be fined daily, with higher fines for larger hospitals and repeat violators, and those fines cannot be waived. The bill affects virtually anyone who receives medical care or has health insurance in the United States, as well as the hospitals, labs, imaging centers, surgery centers, and insurers that serve them.
ai_headline: Hospitals and insurers must publish real prices under HR 5582
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Education and Workforce, and Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Hospitals and insurers would have to post real prices for medical services so patients know their costs before receiving care.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require hospitals, health insurance plans, labs, imaging centers, and outpatient surgery centers to publicly post their actual prices for medical services in plain, easy-to-read formats. Hospitals would need to list their full prices, cash prices for patients paying out of pocket, and the specific rates negotiated with each insurance company, updated monthly. Insurance plans would also have to provide a real-time online tool showing exactly what a patient would owe for a specific service from a specific provider, and the plan could not charge more than that estimate.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects virtually anyone in the United States who receives medical care or has health insurance. It also applies to hospitals, labs, imaging centers, outpatient surgery centers, and health insurance companies.
ai_sections.why: Patients currently may not know what a medical service will cost until after they receive care and get a bill. This bill would change that by requiring prices to be available before care is received.
ai_sections.cost: Providers who do not follow the rules can be fined on a daily basis. Fines are higher for larger hospitals and for repeat violators, and those fines cannot be waived.

title: TRICARE Transition Transparency Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires the Department of Defense to send advance warning notices to military healthcare (TRICARE) beneficiaries whenever they are approaching a point where they must take action to keep their TRICARE coverage — for example, when a dependent child reaches an age that requires them to switch to a different plan. The notices must be sent electronically at three specific points: one year before the required change, 180 days before, and 30 days before. The Defense Department must also run a broader public awareness campaign about these transition requirements using the TRICARE website, social media, and family readiness groups. Each year, the Defense Department must report to Congress on how well these notification and outreach efforts are working, along with any suggestions for improvement. This bill affects all TRICARE beneficiaries — including active-duty service members, military retirees, and their dependents — who will at some point be required to change their enrollment to stay covered.
ai_headline: DoD must warn TRICARE users before coverage lapses
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5586 requires the Pentagon to send TRICARE beneficiaries advance notices at 1 year, 180 days, and 30 days before required coverage changes.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the Department of Defense to send electronic notices to TRICARE beneficiaries before they must take action to keep their coverage. Notices go out at three points: one year, 180 days, and 30 days before the required change. The Pentagon must also run public outreach through the TRICARE website, social media, and family readiness groups, and report to Congress each year on how well these efforts are working.
ai_sections.who: This affects all TRICARE beneficiaries, including active-duty service members, military retirees, and their dependents. Anyone who will eventually be required to change their enrollment to stay covered is included.
ai_sections.why: Without advance notice, beneficiaries may miss a required enrollment change and lose their healthcare coverage. The reporting requirement means Congress receives yearly updates on whether the outreach is reaching people.

title: National Guard Proper Use Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it illegal to order members of the U.S. military — including active-duty forces and the National Guard — to carry out or assist with enforcing immigration laws. Under current law, a principle called the Posse Comitatus Act already generally limits the military's role in civilian law enforcement, and this bill would explicitly extend that restriction to immigration enforcement specifically. It would apply to both federal military personnel and National Guard members when performing certain types of duty. The people directly affected would be military and National Guard service members, who could not be directed to perform tasks such as detaining, arresting, or otherwise enforcing immigration rules, as well as federal officials who would no longer have the legal authority to give such orders.
ai_headline: Bill would bar military from enforcing immigration laws
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5604 would make it illegal for the military or National Guard to enforce immigration laws.
ai_sections.what: This bill would ban ordering U.S. military members or National Guard troops to carry out immigration enforcement. That includes tasks like detaining or arresting people for immigration reasons. It builds on an existing law called the Posse Comitatus Act, which already limits the military's role in civilian law enforcement.
ai_sections.who: Military service members and National Guard troops would be protected from being ordered into immigration enforcement roles. Federal officials would lose the legal authority to give those orders.
ai_sections.why: If passed, immigration enforcement would remain solely a civilian responsibility. Military and National Guard members could not be directed to perform those duties, even if ordered by federal officials.

title: Statutes of Limitation for Child Sexual Abuse Reform Act
ai_summary: This bill uses federal money to push states to get rid of time limits on lawsuits and criminal charges related to child sexual abuse, exploitation, and sex trafficking. Right now, many states have "statutes of limitations" — deadlines by which a victim must file a lawsuit or prosecutors must bring criminal charges — and this bill tries to convince states to eliminate those deadlines entirely and to reopen cases that were previously thrown out because the deadline had passed. States that make these changes would be eligible for federal grant money, with bigger awards going to states that make more of the changes; up to $20 million per year through 2033 is authorized for those grants. This bill primarily affects child sexual abuse survivors who previously couldn't take legal action because too much time had passed, as well as accused individuals, institutions, and prosecutors in states that choose to adopt the new rules.
ai_headline: Federal grants tied to child sex abuse lawsuit deadlines
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal grants would reward states that remove time limits on child sexual abuse lawsuits and criminal charges, up to $20M/year through 2033.
ai_sections.what: This bill uses federal grant money to encourage states to remove deadlines — called statutes of limitations — for filing lawsuits or criminal charges related to child sexual abuse, exploitation, and sex trafficking. It also pushes states to reopen cases that were previously dismissed because those deadlines had passed. States that make more of these changes would receive larger grant awards.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects child sexual abuse survivors who were previously blocked from taking legal action because too much time had passed. It also affects accused individuals, institutions, and prosecutors in any state that chooses to adopt the new rules.
ai_sections.why: States that do not change their laws would not be eligible for the federal grant money. States that do change their laws could face new lawsuits and criminal cases involving older allegations.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $20 million per year in federal grant money for participating states, with funding authorized through 2033.

title: Katie Meyer’s Law
ai_summary: This bill would require colleges and universities that receive federal funding to give students a support person — called an adviser — when those students are accused of violating their school's code of conduct. When a student is notified of an alleged violation, the school must inform them that they can either choose their own outside adviser or ask the school to provide one for free; the school-provided adviser could come from a trained staff coordinator, a peer support program, or an alumni support program. Whichever adviser the student uses must be trained on the school's disciplinary process, can receive regular updates on the case (with the student's written permission), and can participate in the disciplinary proceedings on the student's behalf. The bill also requires colleges to add student suicide incidents — when reported to campus security or local police — to the campus crime and safety statistics they are already required to publish each year. These changes would affect all colleges and universities that participate in federal student aid programs, as well as the students who attend them.
ai_headline: Students accused of misconduct would get campus advisers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Colleges receiving federal aid must give accused students a trained support adviser and report student suicides in campus safety statistics.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires colleges and universities that get federal funding to provide a trained adviser to any student accused of breaking their school's code of conduct. The student can choose their own adviser or ask the school to provide one for free, drawn from trained staff, peer support, or alumni programs. The bill also requires schools to include student suicide incidents reported to campus or local police in their annual campus safety statistics.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects all colleges and universities that participate in federal student aid programs. It also affects the students who attend those schools.
ai_sections.why: Students facing disciplinary proceedings would have access to someone trained on the school's process who can speak on their behalf. Schools would also be required to track and publish student suicide incidents as part of their existing annual safety reporting.

title: Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require large tech companies and other businesses that use certain AI and automated decision-making systems to regularly study and document how those systems affect people — a process the bill calls an "impact assessment." The requirement applies to companies above certain revenue or data thresholds that use algorithms to make or influence important decisions about people's lives, such as whether someone gets a loan, a job, housing, healthcare, or access to education. Before and after deploying these algorithms, covered companies would have to evaluate risks of bias or harm, test performance across different groups (such as by race, sex, or age), consult with outside experts and affected communities, and try to fix any serious negative impacts they find. Companies would submit regular reports summarizing their findings to the Federal Trade Commission, which would maintain a publicly searchable database of those reports so that consumers and researchers can see which algorithms are being used and how people can challenge decisions made about them. The FTC would enforce the rules, state attorneys general could also sue companies that violate them, and a new Bureau of Technology would be created within the FTC to help carry out the law.
ai_headline: Bill would make firms disclose AI decision-making risks
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: Large tech companies would have to test their AI decision-making tools for bias and report findings to the FTC under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require certain large companies to study and document how their AI and automated decision-making systems affect people before and after using them. Companies would have to test for bias across groups defined by race, sex, age, and similar factors, consult outside experts and affected communities, and try to fix serious problems they find. The FTC would keep a public database of company reports so consumers and researchers can see which algorithms are in use and how people can challenge decisions made about them.
ai_sections.who: The rules would apply to large companies above certain revenue or data thresholds that use algorithms to make or influence important decisions about loans, jobs, housing, healthcare, or education. The FTC would enforce the law, state attorneys general could also sue companies that break it, and a new Bureau of Technology would be created inside the FTC to help carry it out.
ai_sections.why: Without these requirements, automated systems that affect major life decisions could go unexamined for bias or harm. This bill creates a process meant to make those systems more visible and to give people a way to challenge decisions made about them.

title: Safe Step Act
ai_summary: Many employer-sponsored health insurance plans require patients to try cheaper or preferred drugs before they can get coverage for the specific drug their doctor prescribed — a practice called "step therapy." This bill would require those plans to have a clear, formal process that lets patients or their doctors request an exception to skip that requirement, and the plan must respond within 72 hours (or 24 hours in urgent medical situations). An exception must be approved in specific situations, such as when the required drugs have already failed, when they are dangerous or medically inappropriate for that patient, when delay could cause serious or irreversible harm, or when the patient is already stable on their current medication. If an exception is granted, the coverage must remain in place for at least one year. The bill also requires health plans to report data annually to the federal government about how many exceptions were requested, approved, and denied, and the government must share a summary of that data with Congress and the public.
ai_headline: Patients could appeal insurer step therapy rules within 72 hours
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Employer health plans would have to let patients skip "step therapy" drug requirements under certain conditions and respond within 72 hours.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets rules for employer-sponsored health plans that require patients to try cheaper drugs before covering the one their doctor prescribed. Plans would need a formal process for patients or doctors to request an exception, with a response required within 72 hours, or 24 hours in urgent situations. Exceptions must be approved when prior drugs failed, are unsafe, or when delay could cause serious harm, and approved coverage must last at least one year.
ai_sections.who: This affects patients enrolled in employer-sponsored health insurance plans and the doctors who treat them. Health plans covered by this bill would also be required to report exception data annually to the federal government.
ai_sections.why: Without a required exception process, patients whose doctors prescribed a specific drug may face delays or denials getting that medication. This bill would create a legal requirement for plans to respond and decide within set time limits.

title: Protecting Free Vaccines Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require health insurance plans and government health programs to continue covering — at no cost to patients — all vaccines that were recommended by the federal government's vaccine advisory committee (a group of medical experts) as of October 25, 2024, even if those recommendations are later removed or changed. It applies to a wide range of coverage, including private employer health plans, individual health insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, and the Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP). The requirement would last through the end of 2029. In plain terms, if a vaccine was on the official recommended list as of that October 2024 date, insurers and programs covered by this bill could not start charging people copays or other out-of-pocket costs for it, and could not stop covering it, even if the government's recommended list is updated to remove it during that period.
ai_headline: Bill would lock in free vaccine coverage through 2029
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Vaccines recommended by October 25, 2024 must stay free to patients through 2029, even if removed from the official list.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires health insurance plans and government health programs to keep covering vaccines that were on the federal recommended list as of October 25, 2024, at no cost to patients. That coverage requirement holds through the end of 2029, even if those vaccines are later removed from the official list.
ai_sections.who: The rule applies to private employer health plans, individual health insurance, Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP. Patients enrolled in any of those programs would keep their no-cost access to those vaccines during that period.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, changes to the federal recommended list could allow insurers and programs to begin charging patients out-of-pocket costs for vaccines or to stop covering them. This bill would prevent that from happening for the vaccines on the October 2024 list until 2029.

title: Dismantle Foreign Scam Syndicates Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a new interagency government task force, led by the Secretary of State and including officials from the Departments of Justice, Homeland Security, and Treasury, to fight large-scale online scams — particularly "pig butchering" cryptocurrency fraud schemes — that are run by criminal organizations primarily based in Southeast Asia and that have cost Americans billions of dollars. Within six months of passage, the task force would be required to deliver a comprehensive strategy to Congress that could include imposing financial sanctions on specific named individuals and organizations, using offensive cyber tools to disrupt scam operations, pressuring foreign governments that allow these operations, and working with banks, social media platforms, cryptocurrency exchanges, and other private companies to cut off the scammers' infrastructure. The bill also authorizes up to $30 million per year for 2026 and 2027 to fund this work, and it specifically protects the people who are themselves trafficked and forced to run these scams from being treated as criminals, instead directing that support services be provided to them. Americans who have lost money to these scams, people who have been trafficked into scam compounds, and foreign individuals and organizations named in the bill as potential sanction targets are all directly affected by this legislation.
ai_headline: Bill targets Southeast Asia crypto fraud networks
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5490 creates a federal task force to fight overseas crypto scams that have cost Americans billions of dollars.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new government task force led by the Secretary of State, with officials from the Departments of Justice, Homeland Security, and Treasury. Within six months of passage, the task force must deliver a strategy to Congress that could include sanctions, cyber operations, pressure on foreign governments, and working with banks and tech companies to cut off scammers. People who are trafficked and forced to run these scams would be protected from criminal charges and directed toward support services instead.
ai_sections.who: Americans who have lost money to these scams and people trafficked into scam operations are directly affected. Foreign individuals and organizations identified as potential sanction targets are also affected by this bill.
ai_sections.why: Large-scale cryptocurrency fraud schemes, often called pig butchering scams, are run by criminal organizations primarily based in Southeast Asia and have cost Americans billions of dollars. Without a coordinated response, these operations and the harm they cause may continue to grow.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $30 million per year for 2026 and 2027 to fund the task force and its work.

title: Tyler Clementi Higher Education Anti-Harassment Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require colleges and universities that receive federal student aid to adopt and publish clear written policies against harassment of students based on their race, color, national origin, sex (including sexual orientation and gender identity), disability, or religion — covering harassment that happens on campus, off campus at school-related events, and online through school-owned or school-operated technology. Those policies must spell out how students can report harassment, what the school will do once a report is made, what disciplinary consequences are possible, and where victims and accused students can find counseling or other support services. The bill also creates a competitive federal grant program that colleges — individually or in partnerships with nonprofits or other schools — can apply for to fund programs that prevent harassment, support affected students, or train students, faculty, and staff to recognize and respond to it; up to $50 million per year could be spent on these grants from 2026 through 2031. The bill affects all students, faculty, and staff at federally funded colleges and universities, and it makes clear that its requirements are added on top of — not instead of — existing civil rights protections already in federal law.
ai_headline: Colleges would face new anti-harassment policy mandates
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Colleges receiving federal aid must publish anti-harassment policies or risk losing compliance, and may apply for grants up to $50M/year through 2031.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require colleges and universities that get federal student aid to publish clear written policies against harassment based on race, color, national origin, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, disability, or religion. The policies must cover harassment on campus, at school events, and online through school-owned technology, and must explain how to report it, what happens after a report, possible discipline, and where to find support. The bill also creates a grant program colleges can apply for to fund harassment prevention, student support, or training for students, faculty, and staff.
ai_sections.who: All students, faculty, and staff at colleges and universities that receive federal student aid are affected. Colleges can apply individually or with nonprofit organizations or other schools.
ai_sections.why: Schools that receive federal aid would need to meet these requirements in addition to civil rights protections already in federal law. Without a published policy that meets the bill's standards, a school could fall out of compliance with federal rules.
ai_sections.cost: The grant program could spend up to $50 million per year from 2026 through 2031. Colleges and universities would also carry the cost of creating and publishing the required policies.

title: SHINE for Autumn Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs the federal government to improve how the United States tracks, studies, and communicates information about stillbirths — the death of a baby before birth. It allows the government to give grants totaling up to $5 million per year (for five years) to states to collect and analyze stillbirth data, including risk factors, while protecting the privacy of patients and their doctors. It also requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to create guidelines for state and local health departments on how to consistently collect stillbirth data, and to develop publicly available educational materials on the topic — consulting with a wide range of medical professionals, mental health experts, statisticians, and people who have personally experienced a stillbirth. Within five years of becoming law, the government must publish a public report summarizing those guidelines and educational materials. The people most directly affected include state public health agencies, health care providers who handle pregnancy-related care, and families who have experienced or may experience a stillbirth.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets stillbirth data collection and education
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal bill would fund state stillbirth data collection and require public education materials within five years.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the federal government to improve how the U.S. tracks and studies stillbirths — when a baby dies before birth. States could receive grants to collect and analyze stillbirth data, including risk factors, while protecting patient privacy. The Secretary of Health and Human Services would also be required to create data collection guidelines and publicly available educational materials.
ai_sections.who: State public health agencies and pregnancy care providers would carry out much of the work under this bill. Families who have experienced or may experience a stillbirth are among those most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without consistent guidelines, states may collect stillbirth data in different ways, making it harder to study the issue nationally. A public report summarizing the guidelines and educational materials would be required within five years of the bill becoming law.
ai_sections.cost: The bill allows grants of up to $5 million per year for five years to be given to states for collecting and analyzing stillbirth data.

title: Faster Labor Contracts Act
ai_summary: This bill sets up a step-by-step timeline to speed up the process of reaching a first union contract after workers vote to be represented by a union. Once a union is recognized, the employer must sit down and start bargaining within 10 days of receiving a written request; if the two sides haven't reached a deal after 90 days of negotiations, either side can ask a federal agency called the Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service to help them reach an agreement; and if mediation still hasn't produced a deal after another 30 days, the dispute goes to a three-person arbitration panel — one member chosen by the union, one by the employer, and one neutral member agreed upon by both — whose majority decision is binding on both sides for two years. The arbitration panel must base its decision on factors such as the employer's financial situation and size, workers' cost of living, workers' ability to support themselves and their families, and what similar employers pay their workers. The bill also requires that while bargaining is ongoing, the employer must maintain current wages, hours, and working conditions until a contract is signed. This primarily affects private-sector employers and their newly unionized workers. The bill also directs a federal watchdog agency to study and report to Congress on how long it takes, on average, to reach a first contract after this law takes effect.
ai_headline: Bill sets union contract bargaining timeline and deadlines
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: If no union contract is reached after 90 days, a binding arbitration panel steps in to decide the deal.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a set timeline for reaching a first union contract after workers vote to unionize. If bargaining stalls after 90 days, a federal agency helps mediate. If 30 more days pass with no deal, a three-person panel makes a final, binding decision that lasts two years.
ai_sections.who: This bill mainly affects private-sector employers and the workers they newly employ under union representation. It also directs a federal watchdog agency to study and report to Congress on how long first contracts take to reach under this law.
ai_sections.why: Without a set timeline, first contract negotiations can stretch on indefinitely after a union vote. This bill creates fixed deadlines and a backstop decision so that both sides reach an agreement within a defined period.

title: Fertilizer Research Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires the U.S. Department of Agriculture to produce and publicly post a detailed research report on the American fertilizer industry within one year of the law being enacted. The report must cover a wide range of topics, including how fertilizer prices have changed over the past 25 years, which companies and countries are supplying fertilizer to the U.S. market, how the fertilizer supply chain works from factory to farm, and whether a small number of large companies controlling the market has led to unfair pricing. It must also look at newer fertilizer technologies, the rules and regulations affecting the industry, and whether more public price reporting would help farmers and other buyers make better-informed decisions — including a recommendation to Congress on whether companies should be required to regularly report fertilizer prices at different points in the supply chain. This bill most directly affects farmers who buy fertilizer, fertilizer manufacturers and distributors, and policymakers, since the report is intended to give Congress and the public a clearer picture of how the fertilizer market works and what might be driving costs for American agricultural producers.
ai_headline: USDA would study fertilizer prices and market competition
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: USDA must publish a report on the U.S. fertilizer industry within one year, covering prices, suppliers, and market competition.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the U.S. Department of Agriculture to research and publicly post a detailed report on the American fertilizer industry within one year of becoming law. The report must cover how prices have changed over 25 years, who supplies fertilizer, how the supply chain works, whether market concentration has led to unfair pricing, newer technologies, industry regulations, and whether more public price reporting would help buyers. The report must also include a recommendation to Congress on whether companies should be required to regularly report fertilizer prices at different points in the supply chain.
ai_sections.who: This bill most directly affects farmers who buy fertilizer, fertilizer manufacturers and distributors, and policymakers. Congress and the public are the intended audience for the finished report.
ai_sections.why: The report is intended to give Congress and the public a clearer picture of how the fertilizer market works. This could inform future decisions about rules or requirements affecting fertilizer pricing and reporting.

title: Enhancing COPS Hiring Program Grants for Local Law Enforcement Act
ai_summary: This bill expands the types of expenses that local law enforcement agencies can pay for using existing federal COPS (Community Oriented Policing Services) grants. Specifically, it would allow agencies to use that grant money to offer bonuses to attract new officers or to keep current officers from leaving — but only if the agency is experiencing a drop in recruitment or a high number of retirements and resignations. Currently, these grants can be used for a range of policing purposes, but paying recruitment or retention bonuses is not on the approved list; this bill would add that option. The change would affect local law enforcement agencies across the country that receive COPS grants and are struggling to hire or hold onto officers.
ai_headline: COPS grants could fund police recruitment bonuses
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5403 would let local police agencies use existing federal COPS grants to pay bonuses for hiring and keeping officers.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds a new approved use for federal COPS grants. Local law enforcement agencies could use that money to offer bonuses to recruit new officers or keep current ones from leaving. This option would only be available to agencies already seeing fewer recruits or more officers retiring and resigning than usual.
ai_sections.who: Local law enforcement agencies across the country that receive COPS grants would be affected. The change would apply only to those agencies facing staffing challenges through low recruitment or high turnover.
ai_sections.why: Right now, COPS grants cannot be used to pay recruitment or retention bonuses. This bill would add that as an allowed expense, giving qualifying agencies another tool to address staffing changes.

title: Taxpayer Funds Oversight and Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how the federal government manages and reports on public money by updating the rules for financial officers at federal agencies and for a government-wide financial planning document. Each agency's top financial officer (the Chief Financial Officer) would be given a clearer, broader set of duties — including overseeing internal financial controls, linking budget spending to performance results, and coordinating with other agency leaders such as the Chief Data Officer and Chief Information Officer. The government-wide financial plan that the Office of Management and Budget produces would be shortened from a 5-year to a 4-year plan, issued at the start of each presidential term, and developed with input from a wider group of councils and experts; a separate annual financial status report to Congress would also be required. Agencies would be required to identify key financial information they need for good decision-making — including spending data and improper payment information — and annually assess whether their internal financial controls are actually working. Federal auditors, including agency Inspectors General, would be required to more thoroughly test those internal controls and report any weaknesses they find.
ai_headline: Bill updates duties of federal agency financial officers
last_action_text: ASSUMING FIRST SPONSORSHIP - Mr. Min asked unanimous consent that he may hereafter be considered as the first sponsor of H.R. 1558, a bill originally introduced by Representative Connolly, for the purpose of adding cosponsors and requesting reprintings pursuant to clause 7 of rule XII. Agreed to without objection.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1558 updates how federal agencies manage and report public money, with stronger oversight rules for financial officers and auditors.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates the duties of Chief Financial Officers at federal agencies, giving them a clearer and wider set of responsibilities over budgets, controls, and coordination with other agency leaders. It also changes the government-wide financial plan from a 5-year to a 4-year cycle, timed to each presidential term, and requires a separate annual financial report to Congress. Agencies would need to regularly check whether their internal financial controls are working, and auditors would be required to test and report on those controls more thoroughly.
ai_sections.who: Federal agencies and their Chief Financial Officers would face new requirements, along with federal auditors and Inspectors General. The Office of Management and Budget would produce a revised financial plan with input from a broader group of councils and experts.
ai_sections.why: These changes would require agencies to more closely track how public money is spent and whether financial safeguards are functioning. Congress would receive more regular reporting on the government's financial condition.

title: Title VIII Nursing Workforce Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill renews and expands federal programs that help train nurses in the United States through 2030. It clarifies that federal grant money can go to students in specialized nursing programs — including nurse practitioners, nurse-midwives, nurse anesthetists, and clinical nurse specialists — and allows those grants to cover costs like clinical training and preceptors (the experienced nurses who supervise students on the job). The bill also broadens the types of improvements nursing schools can make with federal funding, such as buying simulation equipment, using telehealth technology, hiring more faculty, and partnering with clinics and hospitals to give students more hands-on training experience. It updates the list of vulnerable patients that nurses are trained to serve, adding survivors of sexual assault alongside survivors of domestic violence. To fund all of this, the bill sets aside approximately $184 million per year for one set of nursing programs and about $121 million per year for another set, both running from 2026 through 2030 — increases from the amounts authorized in the previous five-year period.
ai_headline: Federal nursing training programs renewed through 2030
last_action_text: Forwarded by Subcommittee to Full Committee by Voice Vote.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress would renew federal nursing education programs through 2030 and increase their funding above previous levels.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews federal programs that help train nurses through 2030. It expands what schools and students can use grant money for, including simulation equipment, telehealth tools, more faculty, and payments to the experienced nurses who supervise students during clinical training. It also updates training requirements to include care for survivors of sexual assault, alongside survivors of domestic violence.
ai_sections.who: Nursing students in specialized programs — including nurse practitioners, nurse-midwives, nurse anesthetists, and clinical nurse specialists — would be eligible for these federal grants. Nursing schools and the clinics or hospitals they partner with would also receive support.
ai_sections.why: Renewing these programs continues federal investment in growing the nurse workforce and improving hands-on training. Adding sexual assault survivors to the list of patients nurses are trained to serve broadens the scope of care covered by the program.
ai_sections.cost: The bill sets aside approximately $184 million per year for one set of nursing programs and about $121 million per year for another, running from 2026 through 2030. Both amounts are higher than what was authorized in the previous five-year period.

title: American Franchise Act
ai_summary: This bill sets a specific legal test that must be met before a franchisor (like a corporate brand headquarters) can be held legally responsible as a "joint employer" alongside a franchisee (the local, independent business owner who runs a franchise location) for how that franchisee treats its workers. Under the bill, a franchisor can only be considered a joint employer — meaning it would share legal liability for wages, working conditions, or labor law violations — if it actually and regularly makes real decisions about workers' pay, benefits, hours, hiring, firing, discipline, supervision, or daily task assignments. The bill draws a clear line between actions that do *not* count as joint employment, such as setting brand standards, providing training materials, establishing safety rules, or setting store operating hours, versus actions that do count, such as directly setting an individual worker's pay rate or personally deciding to fire a specific employee. This affects franchise companies (such as restaurant chains, hotel brands, and retail franchises), the independent business owners who operate franchise locations, and the employees who work at those locations. The new standard would apply only to cases filed after the bill becomes law.
ai_headline: HR 5267 narrows franchisors' joint employer liability
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: A franchisor is only a joint employer if it directly controls workers' pay, hours, or firing — not just by setting brand rules.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets a legal test for when a franchisor, like a corporate brand, can be held jointly responsible with a franchisee for how workers are treated. A franchisor only qualifies as a joint employer if it actually and regularly makes real decisions about things like pay, hours, hiring, or firing. Setting brand standards, safety rules, or store hours does not count as joint employment under this bill.
ai_sections.who: This affects franchise companies such as restaurant chains, hotel brands, and retail franchises, along with the independent owners who run franchise locations and the workers employed there. The new standard applies only to legal cases filed after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.why: Franchisors who do not directly control workers would face less legal exposure for labor violations at franchise locations. Franchisees and their workers would need to look primarily to the local business owner, not the corporate brand, to resolve workplace disputes.

title: Pakistan Freedom and Accountability Act
ai_summary: This bill would require the President to identify — within 180 days of becoming law — specific senior or former senior officials of Pakistan's government, military, or security forces who have committed serious human rights violations or taken actions that undermine democracy in Pakistan, as well as any companies or organizations those individuals own or control. Once that list is compiled and reported to Congress, the President would have the authority (though not a strict requirement) to impose financial and travel sanctions on those individuals — the same type of "Global Magnitsky" sanctions the U.S. already uses against human rights abusers in other countries, which can freeze assets and ban people from entering the United States. The bill carves out exceptions so that sanctions cannot block humanitarian aid, agricultural goods, food, medicine, or medical devices, and cannot interfere with U.S. intelligence or national security operations. Ordinary Pakistani citizens are not targeted — the bill is aimed only at specific named officials found responsible for abuses. The entire law would automatically expire on September 30, 2030.
ai_headline: Bill targets Pakistan officials for Magnitsky sanctions
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would let the U.S. freeze assets and ban travel for Pakistani officials found responsible for human rights abuses or undermining democracy.
ai_sections.what: Within 180 days of becoming law, the President must identify specific current or former Pakistani government, military, or security officials who committed serious human rights violations or undermined democracy, plus any companies they control. The President would then have the authority to impose Global Magnitsky sanctions on those individuals, which can freeze their assets and ban them from entering the United States. The law would automatically expire on September 30, 2030.
ai_sections.who: The bill targets only specific named Pakistani officials found responsible for abuses, not ordinary Pakistani citizens. Exceptions protect humanitarian aid, food, medicine, agricultural goods, medical devices, and U.S. intelligence or national security operations.
ai_sections.why: If sanctions are imposed, targeted individuals could lose access to assets and be barred from entering the United States. Because exceptions are built in, aid and essential goods flowing to Pakistan would not be blocked.

title: RESULTS Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how Medicare decides what to pay clinical laboratories for diagnostic blood tests and other lab tests. Starting in 2028, instead of requiring labs to report what private insurance companies paid them (the current system), the government would collect that pricing data directly from a large independent nonprofit database containing billions of insurance claims — but only for common tests performed by more than 100 providers. For rarer, specialized tests, labs would still report their own payment data, and for the most cutting-edge diagnostic tests, existing rules would continue to apply. The bill also narrows what counts as a valid payment (excluding denied claims, payments under appeal, or payments made in error), caps how much Medicare payment rates can be cut in any given year, and requires the government to publicly explain how each test's payment rate was calculated. The people most directly affected are clinical laboratories, the patients who rely on Medicare-covered lab testing, and the private insurers whose claims data will be used to set payment rates.
ai_headline: Medicare lab test payment rules would shift to claims databases
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2028, Medicare would pull lab test pricing from an independent database instead of requiring labs to report it themselves.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how Medicare sets payment rates for diagnostic lab tests. For common tests, the government would collect pricing data from a large independent nonprofit database instead of asking labs to report what private insurers paid them. Rarer tests and cutting-edge diagnostic tests would follow separate rules.
ai_sections.who: Clinical laboratories and the patients who use Medicare-covered lab testing are most directly affected. Private insurance companies are also affected because their claims data would be used to set payment rates.
ai_sections.why: Changing the data source could affect how much Medicare pays labs for tests. The bill also caps how much payment rates can be cut in a single year and requires the government to publicly explain how each rate was calculated.

title: Federal Firefighter Cancer Detection and Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require the Department of Defense to provide free cancer screenings to its firefighters — meaning military and civilian employees whose primary job is firefighting. Covered screenings include mammograms for female firefighters, colon cancer tests for firefighters 40 and older, prostate-specific antigen (PSA) tests for male firefighters 50 and older (or 40 and older if they are considered high-risk), and screenings for any other cancer that the CDC has identified as occurring at higher rates among firefighters than in the general public. Firefighters can choose to opt out of any of these tests. The Department of Defense would be required to track how often tests are offered and taken, and to record results to monitor cancer trends among this workforce, while removing personal identifying information before analyzing the data; it may also share that data with the CDC to improve understanding of cancer risks in the firefighting profession.
ai_headline: DoD firefighters would get free cancer screenings
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2745 requires the Dept. of Defense to offer free cancer screenings to its firefighters and track the results.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require the Department of Defense to offer free cancer screenings to its firefighters. Covered tests include mammograms, colon cancer screenings, PSA tests, and screenings for any cancer the CDC has linked to higher rates in firefighters. Firefighters can opt out of any test.
ai_sections.who: This applies to military and civilian employees of the Department of Defense whose primary job is firefighting.
ai_sections.why: Requiring these screenings means firefighters in this workforce would have regular access to cancer detection at no personal cost. The Department of Defense would also be required to collect and analyze data on screening and results, and may share that data with the CDC.

title: Peace Officer Standards and Training Agency Information Access Clarification Act
ai_summary: This bill allows state agencies that set standards and issue certifications for law enforcement officers — such as state Police Officer Standards and Training (POST) boards — to officially access federal criminal history records held by the FBI. Currently, federal law lists which government bodies can exchange these records, and this bill adds those state certification agencies to that list. The change matters because these agencies are responsible for deciding whether someone is qualified to become or remain a law enforcement officer in their state, and access to criminal history records would help them make those decisions. The bill covers not just the 50 states but also Washington D.C., Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. The FBI's parent agency, the Justice Department, would have 180 days to update its own regulations to put this change into practice.
ai_headline: Bill expands FBI record access for POST agencies
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5211 lets state law enforcement certification agencies access FBI criminal history records when deciding who can become or stay a police officer.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds state agencies that certify law enforcement officers to the list of government bodies allowed to access federal criminal history records held by the FBI. The Justice Department would have 180 days to update its regulations to carry out this change.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to state Police Officer Standards and Training (POST) boards and similar agencies across all 50 states, Washington D.C., Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands.
ai_sections.why: These agencies decide whether a person is qualified to become or remain a law enforcement officer in their state. Access to FBI criminal history records would factor into those decisions.

title: Modernizing Rural Physician Assistant and Nurse Practitioner Utilization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes a Medicare rule that currently requires certain rural health clinics to have a formal arrangement with a **physician** in order to qualify for Medicare coverage. Under the new rule, those same clinics — specifically ones that are not physician-directed — could instead have a formal arrangement with a **physician assistant (PA) or nurse practitioner (NP)**, as long as that arrangement follows the state laws and regulations that govern how PAs and NPs are allowed to practice in that state. In plain terms, the bill lets rural clinics rely on PAs and NPs — rather than requiring a physician — to meet Medicare's baseline staffing requirements. This primarily affects rural health clinics, the PAs and NPs who work in them, and the patients in rural communities who depend on those clinics for care. The change would take effect on January 1, 2027.
ai_headline: Rural Medicare clinics could swap physician rule for PAs, NPs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2027, rural health clinics can use a physician assistant or nurse practitioner — not just a doctor — to meet Medicare's staffing rule.
ai_sections.what: Medicare currently requires certain rural health clinics to have a formal arrangement with a physician to qualify for coverage. This bill allows clinics that are not physician-directed to instead have that arrangement with a physician assistant or nurse practitioner, as long as it follows state law. The change takes effect January 1, 2027.
ai_sections.who: This affects rural health clinics that are not physician-directed, the physician assistants and nurse practitioners who work in them, and patients in rural communities who rely on those clinics.
ai_sections.why: Clinics that cannot secure a physician arrangement currently may not qualify for Medicare coverage. Under the new rule, those same clinics could meet the requirement through a PA or NP instead.

title: Puerto Rico Nutrition Assistance Fairness Act
ai_summary: This bill would bring Puerto Rico into the federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) — the program commonly known as food stamps — which would make Puerto Rico residents eligible for the same food assistance benefits that people in the 50 U.S. states and other territories like Guam already receive. Right now, Puerto Rico gets a fixed block grant (a lump sum of federal money) to run its own separate nutrition assistance program, rather than participating in SNAP directly. Under this bill, Puerto Rico's government would develop and submit a transition plan, the U.S. Department of Agriculture would review and approve it, and the full switch to SNAP would take effect 10 years after the bill becomes law, with the existing block grant program allowed to continue during that transition period. Once Puerto Rico joins SNAP, its residents would be able to receive benefits based on the same rules and eligibility standards used nationwide, rather than through a separate, fixed-funding system. American Samoa, which currently shares a funding arrangement with Puerto Rico under the block grant, would continue to receive nutrition assistance funding after Puerto Rico's transition is complete.
ai_headline: Puerto Rico would join federal SNAP food aid program
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5168 would move Puerto Rico from a fixed federal block grant into the full federal SNAP food assistance program over 10 years.
ai_sections.what: This bill would bring Puerto Rico into SNAP, the federal food assistance program commonly known as food stamps. Puerto Rico currently runs its own separate nutrition program funded by a fixed federal block grant instead of participating in SNAP directly. Under the bill, Puerto Rico's government would create a transition plan, the U.S. Department of Agriculture would approve it, and the full switch would happen 10 years after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: Puerto Rico residents would become eligible for food assistance under the same rules and standards that apply to people in the 50 states and territories like Guam. American Samoa, which currently shares the block grant funding arrangement with Puerto Rico, would continue to receive nutrition assistance funding after Puerto Rico's transition is complete.
ai_sections.why: Puerto Rico would shift from a fixed-funding system to one where benefits are determined by the same nationwide eligibility standards used in the states. The existing block grant program would remain in place during the 10-year transition period.

title: Indoor Air Quality and Healthy Schools Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to create and run a nationwide program aimed at reducing people's exposure to harmful pollutants inside buildings — things like radon, carbon monoxide, formaldehyde, mold, and fine particles. The EPA would publish voluntary health-based guidelines for the most dangerous indoor pollutants, offer grants and technical help to states, local governments, tribes, schools, and nonprofits to improve indoor air quality, and create a voluntary certification system to recognize buildings that do an especially good job protecting occupants' health. Schools and childcare facilities receive particular attention: the EPA would conduct a national assessment of air quality conditions in those buildings and provide targeted guidance and support to help them improve. The bill also asks the National Academy of Sciences to study whether a simple, easy-to-understand indoor air quality index (similar to the outdoor air quality index many people already know) could be created, and it authorizes up to $100 million per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund the overall program. The bill affects a wide range of people — students, parents, teachers, childcare workers, building owners, and the general public — as well as state, local, and tribal governments that could receive assistance under the program.
ai_headline: EPA would fund indoor air quality program at $100M yearly
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: The EPA would run a new program to reduce harmful indoor air pollution, with schools and childcare centers getting special attention.
ai_sections.what: This bill would direct the EPA to create a national program to reduce indoor air pollutants like radon, mold, carbon monoxide, formaldehyde, and fine particles. The EPA would publish voluntary guidelines, offer grants and technical help to governments and nonprofits, and set up a voluntary certification for buildings with strong air quality protections. The bill also calls for a study on whether a simple indoor air quality index could be created, similar to the one already used for outdoor air.
ai_sections.who: Schools and childcare facilities would receive a dedicated national air quality assessment and targeted support. Students, parents, teachers, childcare workers, building owners, state and local governments, tribes, and the general public would all be affected.
ai_sections.why: Indoor air pollutants can harm the health of people who spend time inside buildings, including children in schools and childcare settings. This program would create tools and resources meant to help reduce that exposure.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $100 million per year from 2026 through 2030 to fund the overall program.

title: MORE Act
ai_summary: This bill would remove cannabis (marijuana) from the federal government's list of controlled substances, making it no longer a federal crime to possess, use, or sell cannabis. People who were previously convicted of federal cannabis offenses would have those convictions automatically cleared from their records, and courts would be required to review and dismiss pending federal cannabis cases. A new federal tax — starting at 5% and gradually rising to 8% of the sale price — would be placed on cannabis products sold in the United States, and the money collected would go into a special fund used to provide job training, legal aid, and small business loans to communities and individuals who were most harmed by decades of cannabis enforcement, particularly communities of color. The bill also requires the government to track the demographics of cannabis business owners and employees, and it preserves existing rules that allow employers in transportation, aviation, and other safety-sensitive industries — as well as the federal government — to continue testing workers for cannabis use.
ai_headline: Federal bill would remove cannabis from controlled substances list
