last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committees on Energy and Commerce, Agriculture, Education and Workforce, Ways and Means, Small Business, Natural Resources, Oversight and Government Reform, and Transportation and Infrastructure, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 5068 would remove cannabis from the federal controlled substances list and automatically clear past federal cannabis convictions.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it legal under federal law to possess, use, or sell cannabis. Past federal cannabis convictions would be automatically cleared, and pending federal cannabis cases would be dismissed. A new federal tax on cannabis sales, starting at 5% and rising to 8%, would fund job training, legal aid, and small business loans.
ai_sections.who: Anyone with a past federal cannabis conviction would be affected, as would people with open federal cannabis cases. Cannabis businesses, workers, and communities most impacted by past cannabis enforcement — particularly communities of color — would also be affected. Employers in transportation, aviation, and other safety-sensitive fields, as well as the federal government, could still require cannabis drug testing.
ai_sections.why: Removing cannabis from the federal controlled substances list changes what is and is not a federal crime. Tax revenue collected from cannabis sales would be directed to communities and individuals most harmed by decades of cannabis enforcement.
ai_sections.cost: A federal tax starting at 5% and gradually rising to 8% of the sale price would be placed on cannabis products. Revenue from that tax would go into a special fund for job training, legal aid, and small business loans.

title: CABLE Competition Act
ai_summary: This bill removes the power of local governments (called "franchising authorities") to block or approve the sale, merger, or transfer of a cable TV system operating in their area. Under current law, local governments can require their approval before a cable company sells or transfers its operating license to a new owner; this bill would eliminate that approval requirement entirely. Instead, the new owner simply has to agree to follow the same rules that were already in place for the original cable company, and the cable operator must give the local government written notice at least 15 days before the deal is completed. The bill affects cable companies looking to sell or restructure, potential buyers of cable systems, and local governments that currently oversee cable franchises in their communities. It would apply to existing cable franchises as well as new ones granted after the law takes effect.
ai_headline: Local governments lose veto over cable franchise transfers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4927 takes away local governments' power to approve or block the sale of a cable TV system in their area.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes the right of local governments to approve or reject the sale, merger, or transfer of a cable TV license. The new owner must agree to follow the existing rules, and the cable company must give the local government written notice at least 15 days before the deal closes.
ai_sections.who: This affects cable companies that want to sell or restructure, potential buyers of cable systems, and local governments that currently oversee cable franchises. It applies to both existing franchises and new ones granted after the law takes effect.
ai_sections.why: Local governments would lose their current ability to review or block cable ownership changes in their communities. The only formal role left for local government would be receiving written notice before a sale is completed.

title: TRAPS Act
ai_summary: This bill directs the Secretary of the Treasury to create a government task force within 90 days whose job is to study payment scams — the kinds of fraud that trick people into sending money electronically — and figure out how to better stop them. The task force would bring together representatives from multiple federal agencies (such as the FTC, FBI/Justice Department, and bank regulators), along with people from banks, credit unions, digital payment companies, consumer groups, tech industry associations, and scam victims. Over the course of up to three years, the group would examine scam tactics like fake phone calls, scam texts, and fraudulent websites; look at what other countries have done to fight these scams; and develop consumer education strategies and law enforcement coordination plans. Within one year of being formed, the task force must publish a report for Congress and the public outlining its findings and recommending any new laws or regulations that could help protect consumers, with follow-up annual updates required after that.
ai_headline: Treasury task force would study electronic payment scams
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4936 orders the Treasury to form a task force within 90 days to study payment scams and recommend new consumer protections.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the Secretary of the Treasury to create a task force within 90 days to study electronic payment scams and how to stop them. The group would work for up to three years, then publish a report for Congress with findings and recommendations for new laws or regulations, followed by yearly updates.
ai_sections.who: The task force would include representatives from federal agencies such as the FTC, FBI, and bank regulators, as well as banks, credit unions, digital payment companies, consumer groups, tech industry associations, and scam victims.
ai_sections.why: Without this task force, no coordinated federal effort would exist to study scam tactics like fake calls, texts, and fraudulent websites, or to learn from what other countries have done. The required report creates a public record that Congress can use when deciding whether new laws are needed.

title: PHASE Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs two federal agencies to take steps aimed at reducing traffic deaths and injuries among pedestrians, cyclists, and other people not inside vehicles. First, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) must research and send the Department of Transportation ideas for upgraded traffic control devices — such as improved signals or signage — that help drivers and cyclists without causing distraction. Second, the Department of Transportation must conduct a study focusing on urban areas where pedestrian deaths have risen, looking at physical road changes and technologies like intelligent speed assistance and blind spot detection that could make roads safer. Based on what that study finds, the Department would also run a grant program — funded at up to $5 million per year — that cities, municipalities, and Tribal governments could apply to for money to build safety improvements like better crosswalks, lighting, sidewalks, curb ramps, pedestrian signals, and road buffers, all of which must meet existing federal rules including the Americans with Disabilities Act. The bill primarily affects federal agencies, local governments, and Tribes that apply for grants, and is ultimately intended to benefit everyday pedestrians, cyclists, and other vulnerable road users.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets pedestrian and cyclist road safety
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies would study road safety for pedestrians and cyclists, and cities could apply for grants up to $5 million a year to fund improvements.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs two federal agencies to research and study ways to make roads safer for pedestrians, cyclists, and others outside of vehicles. It also creates a grant program that local governments and Tribes can apply to for money to build physical safety improvements like crosswalks, lighting, sidewalks, and pedestrian signals.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects the National Institute of Standards and Technology, the Department of Transportation, cities, municipalities, and Tribal governments. Pedestrians, cyclists, and other vulnerable road users are the people the improvements would serve.
ai_sections.why: Pedestrian deaths have risen in urban areas, and current road conditions may not be addressing the risk. The research, studies, and funded improvements are aimed at reducing traffic deaths and injuries among people outside of vehicles.
ai_sections.cost: The grant program could award up to $5 million per year. Cities, municipalities, and Tribal governments would apply to receive those funds, which would come from the federal government.

title: To codify Executive Order 14319 (relating to preventing woke AI in the Federal Government).
ai_summary: This bill would turn an existing presidential executive order — Executive Order 14319, titled "Preventing Woke AI in the Federal Government" — into a permanent federal law. Right now, executive orders can be changed or cancelled by any future president, but making it a law would require Congress to act to undo it. The bill's text does not spell out what the executive order itself requires; it simply states that the order "shall have the force and effect of law," so the actual rules and restrictions come from the executive order rather than this bill. The people most directly affected would be federal government agencies and employees who use or develop artificial intelligence tools, since those agencies would be legally bound — not just by presidential directive — to follow whatever the executive order instructs.
ai_headline: Bill would codify Trump's anti-woke AI executive order
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would make Executive Order 14319 on AI in the federal government permanent law, harder to undo than an executive order.
ai_sections.what: This bill would turn Executive Order 14319, "Preventing Woke AI in the Federal Government," into a permanent federal law. Right now, a president can change or cancel that executive order on their own. Making it a law means Congress would have to vote to undo it.
ai_sections.who: Federal agencies and the employees who use or build artificial intelligence tools would be directly affected. They would be legally required to follow the executive order's rules, not just follow them as a presidential directive.
ai_sections.why: Because the bill does not reprint the executive order's text, the actual requirements come from the order itself, not this bill. Turning it into law raises the bar for any future changes, since that would require an act of Congress rather than a presidential decision.

title: CREATE Act
ai_summary: This bill lets film, TV, and other entertainment producers deduct — or "write off" — more of their production costs upfront on their taxes, rather than spreading those deductions out over many years. It doubles the current spending limits that qualify for this immediate deduction: the general cap goes from $15 million to $30 million, and productions in certain lower-income areas get a higher cap of $40 million. Starting in 2027, those dollar limits will automatically adjust each year to keep pace with inflation. The bill also extends this tax benefit, which was set to expire at the end of 2025, through the end of 2030. The people most directly affected are producers and production companies making films, TV shows, and similar entertainment projects in the United States.
ai_headline: Bill would double film production tax deduction caps
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4840 doubles the spending caps for upfront film and TV production tax deductions and extends the benefit through 2030.
ai_sections.what: This bill doubles the spending limits that allow film and TV producers to deduct production costs immediately on their taxes, rather than spreading them over many years. The general cap rises from $15 million to $30 million, and productions in certain lower-income areas qualify for a $40 million cap. The benefit is extended through the end of 2030, and the dollar limits will adjust for inflation starting in 2027.
ai_sections.who: Producers and production companies making films, TV shows, and similar entertainment projects in the United States are most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Raising the caps means more productions may qualify to deduct their full costs right away instead of waiting years. Extending the expiration date from 2025 to 2030 means the tax treatment stays in place longer for the industry.

title: LOAN Act
ai_summary: This bill proposes several major changes to federal student financial aid for current and future college and graduate students, as well as student loan borrowers. It would roughly double the maximum Pell Grant — a form of financial aid that does not need to be repaid — from its current level to $10,000 starting in the 2026–2027 school year, rising to $14,000 by 2031–2032 and adjusting for inflation after that, while also expanding Pell Grant eligibility to certain graduate students, certain immigrant students known as "Dreamers," and recipients of means-tested government benefits. For people who already have or will take out federal student loans, the bill would eliminate origination fees charged when loans are disbursed, end interest capitalization (the practice of adding unpaid interest to your loan balance so that you pay interest on interest), and reduce interest rates for new loans starting July 1, 2026. It would also create two simplified repayment options — a fixed plan and an income-driven plan where monthly payments are capped based on income and family size — and expand and simplify the Public Service Loan Forgiveness program, which cancels remaining loan balances for borrowers who work in qualifying public service jobs.
ai_headline: Bill would double Pell Grants and cut student loan costs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4862 would roughly double the maximum Pell Grant and cut student loan costs for millions of current and future borrowers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would roughly double the maximum Pell Grant to $10,000 starting in 2026–2027, rising to $14,000 by 2031–2032 and adjusting for inflation after that. It would also eliminate loan origination fees, end interest capitalization, lower interest rates on new loans starting July 1, 2026, and create two simplified repayment options. In addition, it would expand Pell Grant eligibility and make the Public Service Loan Forgiveness program easier to use.
ai_sections.who: Current and future college and graduate students, as well as people who already have federal student loans, would be affected. Expanded Pell Grant eligibility would also reach certain graduate students, Dreamer students, and people who receive means-tested government benefits.
ai_sections.why: Borrowers who take out new loans after July 1, 2026 would face different fee and interest structures than borrowers do today. People who work in qualifying public service jobs could find it easier to have remaining loan balances canceled under the expanded forgiveness program.

title: Fisheries Modernization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill expands an existing federal disaster relief program — one that currently helps fishermen recover from fishery collapses — so that it can also cover certain freshwater fisheries, specifically crawfish. Right now, that relief program mainly applies to ocean and coastal fisheries, but this bill adds a new category of eligible disaster cause called an "infrastructure-related cause," meaning that damage resulting from the operation or failure of government-built flood control systems, levees, spillways, or water diversions can now trigger disaster assistance. The bill also specifically names two crawfish species — red swamp crawfish and white river crawfish, both commercially important in Louisiana and surrounding states — as eligible for this relief, and it allows environmental factors like flooding, drought, and water quality problems to be considered when assessing crawfish fishery disasters. This affects crawfish harvesters, commercial fishermen, and subsistence fishers who depend on these freshwater species for their livelihood or food. Existing protections for ocean and coastal fisheries are not changed or reduced by this bill.
ai_headline: Federal fishery disaster aid extended to crawfish
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4800 expands federal fishery disaster relief to cover crawfish harvesters hurt by flood control infrastructure failures.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds freshwater crawfish fisheries to an existing federal disaster relief program that currently covers ocean and coastal fisheries. It creates a new eligible cause called "infrastructure-related," meaning damage from government flood control systems, levees, spillways, or water diversions can now trigger relief. Two crawfish species — red swamp crawfish and white river crawfish — are specifically named, and flooding, drought, and water quality problems can now be considered when assessing crawfish disasters.
ai_sections.who: Crawfish harvesters, commercial fishermen, and subsistence fishers who depend on these freshwater species are affected. Ocean and coastal fishermen are not affected, as their existing protections remain unchanged.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, crawfish fishermen affected by infrastructure failures or environmental events had no access to this federal disaster program. The bill creates a path for those harvesters to receive assistance when such events damage their fisheries.

title: No Surprises Act Enforcement Act
ai_summary: This bill strengthens the rules that protect patients from unexpected medical bills by increasing the financial penalties that health insurance plans and insurers must pay when they break those rules. Specifically, it raises the penalty for certain serious violations — such as improperly billing patients more than their expected share of costs for emergency care or out-of-network services — from $100 per day to $10,000 per incident. It also creates new penalties for insurance plans or medical providers who are slow to pay or refuse to pay after an independent arbitrator has decided what the fair payment should be: the late or non-paying party must pay three times the disputed amount, plus interest. Finally, it requires federal agencies to report to Congress every six months on how many audits were conducted, how many complaints were filed, what enforcement actions were taken, and what the most common violations were, so there is public accountability for how well these protections are being enforced. The bill affects group health insurance plans, health insurance companies, and out-of-network medical providers and facilities — and indirectly benefits patients who receive emergency, non-emergency, or air ambulance care.
ai_headline: Bill raises surprise-billing penalties to $10,000 per incident
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Education and Workforce, and Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4710 raises penalties for surprise-billing violations and slow arbitration payments, and requires federal agencies to report enforcement data to Congress every 6 months.
ai_sections.what: This bill increases the fines that health plans and insurers pay when they break surprise-billing rules. It also sets new penalties for parties who are late or refuse to pay after an arbitrator decides a fair amount. Federal agencies would be required to report enforcement activity to Congress every six months.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly applies to group health insurance plans, health insurance companies, and out-of-network medical providers and facilities. Patients who receive emergency, non-emergency, or air ambulance care are indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Current penalties for serious violations may not be strong enough to stop improper billing practices. Without regular public reporting, it is harder to know whether existing patient protections are actually being enforced.
ai_sections.cost: Fines for certain serious violations would rise from $100 per day to $10,000 per incident. Parties who do not pay after an arbitration decision would owe three times the disputed amount, plus interest.

title: Federal Retirement Fairness Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow federal employees — including U.S. Postal Service workers — to count time they spent working in temporary government positions after December 31, 1988, toward their federal retirement benefits under the Federal Employees Retirement System (FERS). Currently, that temporary service after 1988 does not count toward retirement; this bill would remove that cutoff date so that post-1988 temporary work can be credited. Employees who want to count that time would need to make a deposit (a payment into the retirement system) to receive the credit. The Office of Personnel Management would be required to inform agencies and employees about who is eligible to make such a deposit, and to issue rules putting the new policy into effect.
ai_headline: Federal workers could credit post-1988 temp service toward FERS
last_action_text: ASSUMING FIRST SPONSORSHIP - Ms. Randall asked unanimous consent that she may hereafter be considered as the first sponsor of H.R. 1522, a bill originally introduced by Representative Connolly, for the purpose of adding cosponsors and requesting reprintings pursuant to clause 7 of rule XII. Agreed to without objection.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal workers could count post-1988 temporary government jobs toward retirement if they pay a deposit into the retirement system.
ai_sections.what: This bill would let federal employees count temporary government work done after December 31, 1988, toward their retirement under FERS. Right now, that work does not count. Employees who want the credit would need to make a payment into the retirement system.
ai_sections.who: Federal employees and U.S. Postal Service workers who held temporary government positions after 1988 could be affected. The Office of Personnel Management would be required to notify agencies and workers about eligibility and create new rules.
ai_sections.why: Workers who served in temporary roles after 1988 currently receive no retirement credit for that time. This bill would change that, as long as the employee makes the required deposit.

title: To amend title 18, United States Code, to include rioting in the definition of racketeering activity.
ai_summary: This bill adds the federal crime of rioting to the list of offenses that count as "racketeering activity" under federal law. Racketeering laws — commonly known as RICO — are tools prosecutors use against organized criminal enterprises, allowing them to charge groups of people who collectively carry out a pattern of serious crimes. By including rioting on that list, the bill would allow federal prosecutors to treat organized rioting as part of a racketeering case, meaning that individuals involved in a riot could potentially face RICO charges if their actions are connected to a broader criminal enterprise. This could affect people who participate in riots that prosecutors believe are coordinated or organized, exposing them to the stronger penalties that come with federal racketeering charges.
ai_headline: Federal rioting added to list of RICO crimes
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4620 adds rioting to the federal racketeering law, which could expose riot participants to RICO charges.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds the federal crime of rioting to the list of offenses covered by federal racketeering law, known as RICO. That means organized rioting could be treated as part of a racketeering case under federal law.
ai_sections.who: People who take part in riots that prosecutors believe are coordinated or connected to a broader criminal enterprise could face RICO charges.
ai_sections.why: Federal racketeering charges carry stronger penalties than standard charges, so individuals involved in such riots could face harsher consequences than they would otherwise.

title: Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act
ai_summary: This bill changes federal law to prevent the Federal Reserve — the central bank of the United States — from creating or issuing a digital version of the U.S. dollar, commonly called a "central bank digital currency" or CBDC, either directly to the public or through banks and other financial middlemen. It also bars the Federal Reserve from opening individual accounts, offering financial products or services directly to ordinary people, or using any such digital currency as a tool to carry out economic policy. There is one exception: the prohibitions do not apply to a digital dollar that is open, available without restrictions, and protects users' privacy in the same way physical cash does. The bill primarily affects the Federal Reserve itself, limiting what it is legally allowed to research, develop, or put into practice — and it expresses Congress's view that the Federal Reserve already lacks the authority to create such a currency without a future act of Congress.
ai_headline: Bill would bar Federal Reserve from issuing digital dollar
last_action_text: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would stop the Federal Reserve from creating or issuing a digital U.S. dollar, with one exception for cash-like digital currency.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal for the Federal Reserve to create, issue, or test a central bank digital currency, known as a CBDC. It would also stop the Federal Reserve from opening accounts for regular people or using a digital dollar to manage the economy. The only exception is a digital currency that works like physical cash — open to everyone, unrestricted, and fully private.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly limits what the Federal Reserve is allowed to do. Congress is also saying, through this bill, that the Federal Reserve does not already have the power to create a digital dollar on its own.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the Federal Reserve would be legally blocked from moving forward with any CBDC research, development, or rollout unless a future law changes these rules. Any digital currency that does not match the privacy and openness of physical cash would be off the table entirely.

title: Joint Chiefs Reauthorization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill extends and expands a federal program that funds cooperative land restoration projects on both national forests and nearby private farmland. The program — which pairs the U.S. Forest Service and the Natural Resources Conservation Service — is extended through 2031, giving it roughly eight more years of authorized operation. The bill also broadens what the program can work on, adding wildfire recovery and improving soil and water resources to its existing goals, and it requires the two agencies to better coordinate their plans and share the best available science when making decisions. Projects must align with state-level forest, wildlife, or water priority plans, and any work in protected roadless areas remains off-limits. The people most directly affected are farmers, ranchers, and rural landowners whose property sits near national forests, as well as the federal agency staff and local communities that depend on healthy forests and watersheds.
ai_headline: Forest Service land restoration program extended to 2031
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Agriculture, and in addition to the Committee on Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: A federal land restoration program shared by two agencies is extended through 2031 and expanded to cover wildfire recovery and soil and water work.
ai_sections.what: This bill extends a joint federal program through 2031, giving it about eight more years to fund land restoration projects on national forests and nearby private farmland. It also expands the program's goals to include wildfire recovery and improving soil and water resources. The two agencies involved must better coordinate their plans, use the best available science, and align projects with state-level forest, wildlife, or water priority plans.
ai_sections.who: Farmers, ranchers, and rural landowners whose property sits near national forests are most directly affected. Federal agency staff and local communities that depend on healthy forests and watersheds are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Extending the program means the U.S. Forest Service and the Natural Resources Conservation Service continue working together on restoration rather than operating separately. Adding wildfire recovery and soil and water goals means the program can respond to a wider range of land conditions.

title: Satellite-Based Agricultural Data Act
ai_summary: This bill would add commercial weather services as an approved and prioritized source of data and tools when the U.S. Department of Agriculture funds agricultural research through its Agriculture and Food Research Initiative (AFRI), a competitive grant program. Specifically, it updates existing law so that research on climate change mitigation in agriculture can explicitly include data and tools purchased from private-sector weather companies — such as satellite-based weather forecasting services. The change affects researchers, universities, and other organizations that apply for USDA grants, potentially opening the door for them to use commercially available weather data as part of their funded projects. Farmers and the broader agricultural sector could indirectly benefit if this leads to better research on how to reduce or adapt to the effects of climate change on crops and food production.
ai_headline: USDA grant program may now fund commercial weather data
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4414 would let USDA grant-funded researchers use private-sector weather data, including satellite services, in climate and agriculture studies.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates federal law so that USDA agricultural research grants can explicitly include data and tools bought from private-sector weather companies, such as satellite-based forecasting services. It applies specifically to research on climate change and farming funded through the Agriculture and Food Research Initiative.
ai_sections.who: Researchers, universities, and other organizations that apply for USDA grants would be directly affected. Farmers and the broader agricultural sector could be indirectly affected depending on what the funded research produces.
ai_sections.why: Allowing grant-funded projects to use commercial weather data could change the kinds of tools and information available to agricultural researchers. This may influence the direction and findings of research on how farming can respond to climate change.

title: Tribal Access to Clean Water Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would direct federal money and support toward building, repairing, and maintaining clean drinking water and sanitation systems on Tribal lands, because Congress found that nearly half of households on those lands currently lack reliable access to safe water. It would authorize hundreds of millions of dollars per year from 2026 through 2030 — up to $500 million annually through the Indian Health Service, $100 million annually through the Department of Agriculture, $100 million annually for operating and maintaining water facilities, and $18 million annually through the Bureau of Reclamation — to fund construction, repairs, and ongoing upkeep of water infrastructure. The bill also sets aside money specifically for technical assistance, meaning hands-on expert help to guide Tribal governments and Native Hawaiian organizations through applying for funding, managing water systems, and building the administrative know-how needed to run those systems long-term. Importantly, eligible Tribal and Native Hawaiian recipients would not be required to provide matching funds or prove they cannot afford the projects on their own. The people most directly affected are members of federally recognized Indian Tribes and the Native Hawaiian Community who currently live without safe, reliable water access.
ai_headline: Federal bill targets clean water gaps on Tribal lands
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Natural Resources, and in addition to the Committees on Energy and Commerce, and Agriculture, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4377 would send federal funding to build and repair drinking water and sanitation systems on Tribal lands.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs federal money and support toward building, repairing, and maintaining clean drinking water and sanitation systems on Tribal lands. It also sets aside funds for hands-on expert help to guide Tribal governments and Native Hawaiian organizations in applying for funding and managing water systems. Eligible recipients would not be required to provide matching funds or prove financial need.
ai_sections.who: The people most directly affected are members of federally recognized Indian Tribes and the Native Hawaiian Community who currently lack safe, reliable water access. Tribal governments and Native Hawaiian organizations would apply for and manage the funding.
ai_sections.why: Congress found that nearly half of households on Tribal lands currently lack reliable access to safe water. This bill is a response to that gap in water infrastructure.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize up to $500 million per year through the Indian Health Service, $100 million per year through the Department of Agriculture, $100 million per year for operating and maintaining water facilities, and $18 million per year through the Bureau of Reclamation. These amounts would run from 2026 through 2030.
costChips: USDA and water ops receive $100M/year each

title: Drug and Alcohol Clearinghouse Public Safety Improvement Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill requires trucking companies that operate vehicles weighing 10,000 pounds or more to report positive hair drug test results to a federal database — called the Drug and Alcohol Clearinghouse — that tracks commercial truck and bus drivers who have failed drug or alcohol tests. Specifically, it covers positive results from hair tests given before someone is hired or during random testing, but only when those tests are conducted using an approved testing device and analyzed by a laboratory with specific accreditation. Once reported, a positive hair test result would be treated the same way as an employer having direct knowledge that a driver used drugs, which can affect that driver's ability to work. The U.S. Department of Transportation would be required to write new rules putting this into practice within one year of the bill becoming law. This bill primarily affects trucking and motor carrier companies and the commercial drivers they employ or screen for hire.
ai_headline: Trucking firms must report hair drug tests to federal database
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

ai_sections.tldr: Trucking companies would have to report positive hair drug tests to a federal driver database, with the same consequences as a known drug use violation.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require trucking companies to report positive hair drug test results to the federal Drug and Alcohol Clearinghouse database. It covers hair tests given before hiring or during random testing, but only when the test uses an approved device and an accredited lab. The Department of Transportation would have one year to write the rules for how this works.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects trucking and motor carrier companies that operate vehicles weighing 10,000 pounds or more. It also affects commercial drivers those companies employ or consider for hire.
ai_sections.why: A positive hair test result would be treated the same as an employer knowing directly that a driver used drugs, which can affect whether that driver is allowed to work. Drivers whose results are reported would be entered into a federal database that employers can check.

title: Head Start for Our Future Act
ai_summary: This bill expands the types of work that college students can perform under the Federal Work-Study program — a federal program that helps students earn money for college through part-time jobs. Currently, work-study jobs in "community service" include things like literacy training; this bill would add child development and early learning programs, specifically including Head Start and Early Head Start, to that list of qualifying community service activities. In practical terms, this means college students could use their federally funded work-study hours to work at early childhood programs like Head Start centers serving low-income young children and families. The bill affects college students who receive work-study aid and the early childhood programs that could gain additional student workers.
ai_headline: Work-study funds could flow to Head Start programs
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4318 would let college work-study students use their funded hours at Head Start and Early Head Start programs.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds child development and early learning programs, including Head Start and Early Head Start, to the list of qualifying community service jobs under the Federal Work-Study program. Right now, community service work-study jobs include things like literacy training, but early childhood programs are not on that list.
ai_sections.who: College students who receive Federal Work-Study aid would be affected, as would early childhood programs like Head Start centers that serve low-income young children and families.
ai_sections.why: Early childhood programs could gain access to more student workers. College students would have a broader set of work-study job options available to them.

title: SAWMILL Act
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal loan guarantee program to help small sawmills and wood-processing facilities in rural America get financing to open, reopen, upgrade, or expand their operations. The U.S. Department of Agriculture, working with the Department of the Interior, would first identify federal lands — including tribal forests — that are high priority for ecological restoration work that involves removing trees or other vegetation. Sawmills and wood-processing businesses located within 250 miles of those lands could then apply for government-backed loans, as long as having that facility nearby would meaningfully lower the cost of doing that restoration work on federal land. The total amount the government could guarantee under this program is capped at $220 million. The people most directly affected are rural sawmill owners and operators, as well as communities near federal forests that could see increased restoration activity.
ai_headline: USDA loan guarantees would back rural sawmill expansions
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: The SAWMILL Act would let small rural sawmills apply for federal loan guarantees, capped at $220 million, to open or expand near federal lands needing restoration.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal loan guarantee program for small sawmills and wood-processing facilities in rural areas. To qualify, a business must be within 250 miles of federal lands — including tribal forests — identified as high priority for ecological restoration. The facility must also meaningfully lower the cost of doing that restoration work.
ai_sections.who: Rural sawmill owners and wood-processing business operators would be the primary applicants. Communities located near federal forests could also be affected if restoration activity in those areas increases.
ai_sections.why: Small rural sawmills often struggle to get financing on their own, so a government-backed loan could make it possible for them to open, reopen, upgrade, or expand. Having more local processing capacity near federal lands is tied to how restoration work on those lands gets carried out and at what cost.
ai_sections.cost: The total amount the federal government could guarantee under this program is capped at $220 million.

title: Protecting Patient Access to Cancer and Complex Therapies Act
ai_summary: This bill changes how drug companies pay for the price reductions that Medicare has negotiated on certain high-cost drugs — particularly drugs administered in a doctor's office or hospital outpatient setting, such as cancer treatments. Instead of requiring manufacturers to sell those drugs directly at the lower negotiated price (called the "maximum fair price"), the bill lets manufacturers continue charging their standard price and then pay the difference back to Medicare as a rebate after the fact. Medicare patients who receive these drugs would still pay lower out-of-pocket costs, calculated based on the negotiated price rather than the higher standard price. The rebate money collected from manufacturers would go into the Medicare trust fund. Drug manufacturers who participate in Medicare's drug price negotiation program, as well as Medicare patients receiving these negotiated drugs in clinical settings, are the main groups affected.
ai_headline: Medicare drug rebates replace direct price cuts for Part B
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Instead of selling drugs at a lower price upfront, manufacturers could charge full price and pay Medicare back the difference later.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how drug companies deliver price reductions on certain high-cost drugs, like cancer treatments given in a doctor's office or hospital. Instead of charging the lower negotiated price at the point of sale, manufacturers could charge their standard price and send the difference to Medicare as a rebate afterward. Patients would still pay out-of-pocket costs based on the lower negotiated price.
ai_sections.who: Drug manufacturers that take part in Medicare's drug price negotiation program are directly affected. Medicare patients who receive these high-cost drugs in clinical settings, such as cancer treatment, are also affected.
ai_sections.why: This changes when and how the government collects the savings from negotiated drug prices. The rebate money manufacturers pay would go into the Medicare trust fund.
ai_sections.cost: Manufacturers would pay the difference between their standard price and the negotiated price back to Medicare as a rebate. That money would be deposited into the Medicare trust fund.

title: Contact Lens Prescription Verification Modernization Act
ai_summary: This bill updates the rules that contact lens sellers must follow when verifying a customer's prescription. Most importantly, it requires online contact lens sellers to offer a way for customers to electronically submit a copy of their prescription — and any prescription information sent by email must be encrypted to protect personal health data. The bill also requires that a prescriber's email address be included alongside their name, phone number, and other identifying information used during the verification process. Finally, it clarifies that automated or pre-recorded phone calls no longer count as a valid method for verifying a prescription. These changes primarily affect online contact lens retailers and the eye care providers whose prescriptions those retailers must verify.
ai_headline: New rules tighten contact lens prescription verification
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: Online contact lens sellers must now accept electronic prescriptions, encrypt health data sent by email, and can no longer verify prescriptions by robocall.
ai_sections.what: This bill updates the rules for how contact lens sellers verify a customer's prescription. Online sellers must offer a way to submit prescriptions electronically, and any prescription information sent by email must be encrypted. Automated or pre-recorded phone calls can no longer be used to verify a prescription.
ai_sections.who: These rules affect online contact lens retailers and the eye care providers whose prescriptions those retailers verify. Prescribers must also provide their email address as part of the verification process.
ai_sections.why: Removing robocalls as a valid verification method changes how sellers and eye care providers must communicate during the prescription check. Requiring encryption is meant to change how personal health information travels between parties.

title: Multiple Firearm Sales Reporting Modernization Act of 2025
ai_summary: Under current law, gun dealers who hold a federal license are already required to notify federal authorities whenever they sell two or more handguns (pistols or revolvers) to the same non-licensed customer within five consecutive business days. This bill would expand that existing reporting requirement to cover **all** firearms — meaning the same notification rule would now apply to rifles, shotguns, and any other firearm types, not just handguns. The people directly affected are federally licensed gun dealers, who would have a broader reporting obligation, and federal law enforcement, which would receive more information about multiple firearm purchases. Customers who are not licensed gun dealers and who buy two or more firearms of any type from the same dealer within a five-business-day window would be the subject of those reports. The bill does not ban or restrict any purchases — it only changes what dealers are required to report.
ai_headline: Gun dealers would report all multi-firearm sales to feds
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill expands an existing reporting rule so gun dealers must report multiple sales of any firearm, not just handguns.
ai_sections.what: Right now, licensed gun dealers must report to federal authorities when they sell two or more handguns to the same unlicensed customer within five business days. This bill would expand that rule to cover all firearms, including rifles and shotguns. It does not ban or restrict any purchases.
ai_sections.who: Licensed gun dealers would have a broader reporting obligation under this bill. Federal law enforcement would receive reports, and unlicensed customers who buy two or more firearms of any type from the same dealer within five business days would be the subject of those reports.
ai_sections.why: Federal authorities would receive information about a wider range of multiple firearm purchases than they do today. The change affects what dealers are required to report, not what customers are allowed to buy.

title: Prohibition of Medicaid Funding for Conversion Therapy Act
ai_summary: This bill would ban the Medicaid program from paying for "conversion therapy" — defined as any paid practice or treatment that tries to change a person's sexual orientation or gender identity, including attempts to alter their behaviors, gender expression, or same-gender attractions. The ban would apply to services provided to anyone enrolled in Medicaid, which is the government health insurance program primarily serving low-income individuals and families. The bill makes clear that the ban does not cover therapy that helps someone through a gender transition, or counseling that supports a person's identity exploration and development without trying to change it. Any individual, business, or organization that receives Medicaid money would be prohibited from billing that program for conversion therapy services once the law takes effect.
ai_headline: Medicaid funding for conversion therapy would end under HR 4244
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4244 would stop Medicaid from paying for therapy that tries to change a person's sexual orientation or gender identity.
ai_sections.what: This bill would ban Medicaid from covering conversion therapy, which is any paid practice that tries to change someone's sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, or same-gender attractions. The ban does not apply to therapy that supports a gender transition or helps someone explore their identity without trying to change it.
ai_sections.who: The ban would apply to anyone enrolled in Medicaid, the government health insurance program mainly serving people with low incomes. Any person, business, or organization that receives Medicaid funding would be prohibited from billing the program for these services.
ai_sections.why: Providers who currently bill Medicaid for conversion therapy would no longer be able to do so once the law takes effect. Medicaid enrollees seeking this type of therapy would not be able to use their Medicaid coverage to pay for it.

title: Supporting Trade and Rebuilding Opportunity for National Growth Act
ai_summary: This bill raises the maximum amount that small businesses can borrow through two major federal loan programs run by the Small Business Administration (SBA). For the most common SBA loan program (known as the 7(a) program), the bill doubles the cap on how much the government can guarantee on a single loan — from $3,750,000 to $7,500,000, with a related ceiling raised from $5,000,000 to $10,000,000. It also doubles the maximum loan amounts available through a separate program that helps small businesses finance major purchases like buildings and equipment, raising those caps to $10,000,000. Small business owners who need larger amounts of financing than the current limits allow would be the primary people affected, as they could potentially qualify for bigger government-backed loans than they can today.
ai_headline: SBA loan caps would double for small businesses
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Small Business.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4153 would double the loan limits for two SBA small business loan programs, raising some caps up to $10,000,000.
ai_sections.what: This bill would double the maximum loan amounts available through two federal small business loan programs run by the SBA. For the 7(a) program, the government guarantee cap on a single loan would rise from $3,750,000 to $7,500,000, with a related ceiling going from $5,000,000 to $10,000,000. A separate program that helps small businesses buy buildings and equipment would also see its loan caps doubled, up to $10,000,000.
ai_sections.who: Small business owners who need more financing than the current limits allow would be most directly affected. They could potentially qualify for larger government-backed loans than they can under today's rules.
ai_sections.why: If passed, small businesses seeking large loans would have access to higher federally backed amounts than currently available. Businesses whose financing needs exceed today's caps would have more options through these two SBA programs.

title: Health Care for Energy Workers Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill expands who can authorize medical care for a specific group of sick workers — people who qualify for federal health benefits under a program that covers employees who became ill due to their work at nuclear weapons facilities and other energy-related government sites. Right now, only physicians (medical doctors) can prescribe or order the medical services, equipment, and supplies those workers receive through that program; this bill would also allow nurse practitioners and physician assistants to do so. These providers would need to be acting within the boundaries that their state law sets for their profession and following any additional rules the federal government puts in place. The people directly affected are the energy workers and their families who receive these federal medical benefits, as well as the nurse practitioners and physician assistants who treat them.
ai_headline: Bill expands who can authorize care for nuclear workers
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4122 lets nurse practitioners and physician assistants — not just doctors — authorize care for sick nuclear and energy workers.
ai_sections.what: This bill expands who can order medical services, equipment, and supplies for workers who got sick from their jobs at nuclear weapons facilities and other government energy sites. Right now only doctors can do this. The bill would add nurse practitioners and physician assistants, as long as they follow their state's rules and any federal guidelines.
ai_sections.who: The people directly affected are energy workers and their families who receive these federal medical benefits. Nurse practitioners and physician assistants who treat these workers are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Currently, those workers can only receive authorized care when a physician signs off on it. This change means more types of providers could approve their care.

title: Health Equity and Access under the Law for Immigrant Families Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would expand access to government health insurance programs — including Medicaid, the Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP), Medicare, and Affordable Care Act marketplace plans — to a broader range of immigrants living in the United States. Under current law, many immigrants must wait years before qualifying for these programs, and some are entirely excluded; this bill would remove those waiting periods and restrictions for people who are lawfully present, including those with deferred action status (such as DACA recipients) and others with federally authorized permission to be in the country. It would also give individual states the option — but not the requirement — to extend Medicaid and CHIP coverage even to undocumented immigrants who would otherwise qualify based on income and other criteria. People who currently use an affidavit of support (a financial sponsorship document) to live in the U.S. would no longer have their sponsor held financially responsible for their government health care costs. The bill affects immigrants and their families across the country, as well as state governments that administer Medicaid and CHIP programs.
ai_headline: Bill would open Medicaid and CHIP to more immigrants
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would let most lawfully present immigrants join Medicaid, CHIP, Medicare, and ACA plans without waiting periods.
ai_sections.what: This bill would remove waiting periods and other restrictions that currently block many lawfully present immigrants from enrolling in Medicaid, CHIP, Medicare, and Affordable Care Act marketplace plans. It would also give states the choice — not the requirement — to extend Medicaid and CHIP to undocumented immigrants who meet income and other eligibility rules. Sponsors who sign an affidavit of support for an immigrant would no longer be held financially responsible for that person's government health care costs.
ai_sections.who: Lawfully present immigrants — including DACA recipients and others with federally authorized permission to be in the country — and their families would be affected. State governments that run Medicaid and CHIP programs would also be affected, since they would gain a new option to expand coverage.
ai_sections.why: Currently, many immigrants wait years before they can enroll in these programs, and some cannot enroll at all. This bill would change who is eligible and how financial responsibility is assigned for immigrant health coverage.

title: Designate CAIR as a Terrorist Organization Act
ai_summary: This bill requires the Secretary of State, working with the Attorney General and the Secretary of the Treasury, to formally review whether the Council on American-Islamic Relations (CAIR) — a Muslim civil rights organization — qualifies for designation as a foreign terrorist organization under existing U.S. law. The review must be completed within 90 days of the bill becoming law. After the review, the Secretary of State must report the findings to Congress, and that report must either announce that CAIR has been designated a foreign terrorist organization or explain why it did not meet the legal criteria for that designation. The bill most directly affects CAIR as an organization; a foreign terrorist organization designation under U.S. law carries significant legal consequences, including potential restrictions on financial transactions and criminal penalties for people who provide the group with material support.
ai_headline: Bill would require terrorism review of CAIR
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would require the U.S. government to formally review whether CAIR qualifies as a foreign terrorist organization within 90 days.
ai_sections.what: This bill requires the Secretary of State, along with the Attorney General and the Secretary of the Treasury, to review whether CAIR meets the legal standard for designation as a foreign terrorist organization. The review must be completed within 90 days, and the findings must be reported to Congress. That report must either announce a designation or explain why CAIR did not meet the legal criteria.
ai_sections.who: CAIR, a Muslim civil rights organization, is the group most directly affected by this bill. The Secretary of State leads the review, working with the Attorney General and the Secretary of the Treasury.
ai_sections.why: If CAIR were designated a foreign terrorist organization, it could face restrictions on financial transactions. People who provide the group with material support could also face criminal penalties under existing U.S. law.

title: To direct the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development and the Director of the Bureau of the Census to conduct a study and submit a report about how Federal agencies identify and record cases of housing loss in the United States, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill requires two federal agencies — the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) and the Census Bureau — to work together to study how the federal government currently tracks people who lose their homes in the United States. Within six months of the bill becoming law, they must deliver a report to Congress that identifies and defines different types of housing loss (such as evictions, foreclosures, and disaster-related displacement), determines which types are most common, and examines the data the government already collects on these situations. The report must also evaluate how accurate and up-to-date that data is, and recommend ways the government could do a better job of collecting and organizing it — including identifying any funding or new legal authority that would be needed to act on those recommendations. The bill defines "housing loss" broadly to include court-ordered evictions, informal pressure from landlords that forces someone to leave, foreclosures due to missed mortgage or tax payments, and being displaced by a natural disaster. This effort would primarily affect federal agencies that collect housing data, though its ultimate purpose is to improve the government's understanding of housing instability experienced by people across the country.
ai_headline: HUD and Census Bureau to study housing loss data
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3988 would require HUD and the Census Bureau to study and report to Congress on how the federal government tracks housing loss.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs HUD and the Census Bureau to study how the federal government currently tracks people who lose their homes. Within six months of becoming law, the two agencies must deliver a report to Congress that defines types of housing loss, reviews existing data, and recommends improvements to how that data is collected and organized.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects federal agencies that collect housing data, especially HUD and the Census Bureau. It would also require Congress to receive and review the agencies' findings.
ai_sections.why: Without a clear picture of how and why people lose their homes, gaps in federal data may go unaddressed. This study is meant to help the government understand what information it already has and what it still needs.

title: Connected Vehicle National Security Review Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new government office — the Office of Information and Communications Technology and Services — inside the Commerce Department, and gives it the authority to review, restrict, or block business deals involving technology used in connected vehicles (cars and trucks that communicate wirelessly with networks and other devices) when those deals involve companies or technology linked to China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea. If the office finds that a deal poses an "undue risk" to national security or critical infrastructure, it can require changes to the deal, set cybersecurity conditions, or ban the transaction entirely; if a ban is issued, it must be publicly announced in the Federal Register. The Director of National Intelligence is also required to produce regular risk assessments about threats from these countries' supply chains and share them with Congress. Businesses and individuals who violate the office's orders or rules can face serious consequences, including civil fines of at least $250,000 per violation or up to $1 million and 20 years in prison for willful criminal violations. This bill primarily affects companies that make, sell, or use technology components in connected vehicles, particularly those with supply chain connections to the four named countries.
ai_headline: New Commerce office would vet foreign EV tech deals
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: A new federal office could block or change business deals involving connected vehicle technology tied to China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new office inside the Commerce Department with the power to review, restrict, or block business deals involving technology used in connected vehicles. The office can act when those deals involve companies or technology linked to China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea. The Director of National Intelligence would also be required to produce regular threat assessments and share them with Congress.
ai_sections.who: This bill mainly affects companies that make, sell, or use technology in connected vehicles, especially those with supply chain ties to the four named countries. The new office and the Director of National Intelligence would take on new responsibilities under this law.
ai_sections.why: If the office finds a deal poses an undue risk to national security or critical infrastructure, it can require changes, set cybersecurity conditions, or ban the transaction entirely. Any ban must be publicly announced in the Federal Register.
ai_sections.cost: Businesses or individuals who break the office's rules can face civil fines of at least $250,000 per violation or up to $1 million. Willful criminal violations can result in up to 20 years in prison.
costChips: Up to 20 years prison for willful crimes

title: Runaway and Homeless Youth and Trafficking Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill renews and expands federal funding and programs that provide shelter, counseling, and other support services to young people who are homeless, runaways, or living on the streets. It updates three main types of grant programs — short-term emergency shelters, longer-term transitional housing, and street outreach — by extending grants to five-year periods, raising the minimum and maximum dollar amounts organizations can receive, and adding new required services such as suicide prevention, trauma-informed care, substance use education, and help applying for federal student aid. The bill also creates a new optional grant program specifically for prevention services, aimed at keeping at-risk youth from becoming homeless in the first place, and adds stronger protections against discrimination for youth served by these programs, including LGBTQ youth. It affects runaway and homeless young people across the country (primarily those ages 15–21, with some programs extending to age 25), as well as the local nonprofit and public organizations that serve them, and it authorizes $200 million per year for the main programs and additional funding for outreach and prevention.
ai_headline: Federal homeless youth shelter grants renewed and expanded
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3856 renews and expands federal shelter, housing, and outreach programs for homeless and runaway youth, with $200M authorized per year.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews federal grant programs that fund emergency shelters, transitional housing, and street outreach for young people who are homeless or have run away. It extends grants to five-year terms, raises funding amounts, and adds required services including suicide prevention, trauma-informed care, substance use education, and help applying for federal student aid. It also creates a new optional grant program focused on preventing youth homelessness before it starts, and strengthens protections against discrimination, including for LGBTQ youth.
ai_sections.who: The bill primarily affects young people ages 15 to 21, with some programs reaching up to age 25, who are homeless, runaway, or living on the streets. It also affects the local nonprofit and public organizations across the country that provide services to these young people.
ai_sections.why: Without renewal, existing federal programs supporting homeless and runaway youth would lose their authorization, potentially reducing available services. Adding prevention grants and anti-discrimination protections changes which youth can be served and what kinds of programs qualify for funding.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $200 million per year for the main shelter and housing programs, with additional funding authorized for street outreach and the new prevention program.

title: Bipartisan Background Checks Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require a background check for every firearm sale or transfer between private individuals — not just sales made through licensed gun dealers, as is currently required. Under the bill, if two private people want to transfer a gun, they must first go through a licensed gun dealer, importer, or manufacturer, who would run the standard federal background check before the transfer can happen. The bill includes several exceptions where the background check would not be required: transfers between close family members (such as spouses, parents and children, siblings, grandparents, and aunts/uncles and nieces/nephews) given as gifts or loans, temporary transfers to prevent imminent death or serious harm, transfers at shooting ranges or while hunting when the original owner is present, and transfers that happen through inheritance. This affects any private citizen who wants to give, sell, or lend a firearm to another private citizen outside of those listed exceptions. The bill explicitly states that it does not authorize the creation of a national firearms registry and does not prevent states from passing their own laws on the same subject.
ai_headline: Private gun transfers would require background checks
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 18 would require a background check for all private firearm transfers, with exceptions for close family, emergencies, and inheritance.
ai_sections.what: This bill would extend the federal background check requirement to private firearm sales and transfers, not just sales through licensed dealers. Anyone transferring a gun privately would need to go through a licensed dealer, importer, or manufacturer to complete the check first. Exceptions include gifts or loans between close family members, emergencies involving imminent harm, supervised use at ranges or while hunting, and inherited firearms.
ai_sections.who: This affects any private citizen who wants to sell, give, or lend a firearm to another private citizen outside the listed exceptions. Licensed gun dealers, importers, and manufacturers would be the ones running the checks for these private transfers.
ai_sections.why: Currently, private sales and transfers between individuals do not require a background check the way dealer sales do. This bill would close that gap, meaning more transfers would go through the federal check process before a gun changes hands.

title: Runaway and Homeless Youth and Trafficking Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill renews and strengthens federal programs that fund shelters, counseling, and support services for young people who have run away from home or are experiencing homelessness. It extends grants to local nonprofit and public organizations that provide short-term emergency shelter, longer-term transitional housing, street outreach, and new prevention services — and it specifically adds stronger protections and services for youth who are victims of human trafficking. Organizations receiving funding would be required to serve youth in culturally appropriate ways, track data on trafficking victims and other vulnerable groups, help youth apply for college financial aid, and prepare emergency plans for disasters and public health crises. The bill primarily affects runaway and homeless youth (generally ages 15 to 26), the local shelters and nonprofits that serve them, and the federal agency that oversees these grants. It authorizes up to $200 million per year for most programs and additional funding for street outreach and prevention services through 2030.
ai_headline: Senate bill renews homeless youth shelter grants to 2030
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S3318)

ai_sections.tldr: This bill renews federal funding for runaway and homeless youth shelters and adds stronger protections for trafficking victims through 2030.
ai_sections.what: This bill renews and expands federal programs that pay for shelters, counseling, and support services for young people who have run away or have no home. It adds stronger protections for youth who are victims of human trafficking and requires organizations to provide culturally appropriate services, track data on vulnerable youth, help with college financial aid applications, and prepare emergency plans for disasters and public health crises.
ai_sections.who: The bill mainly affects young people ages 15 to 26 who are homeless or have run away from home, including those who have been trafficked. It also affects the local nonprofits and public organizations that run these programs and the federal agency that manages the grants.
ai_sections.why: Without renewal, federal grants supporting these shelters and services would expire, affecting the organizations and youth who depend on them. The added trafficking protections reflect that homeless and runaway youth face heightened risk of exploitation.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes up to $200 million per year for most programs, plus additional funding for street outreach and prevention services, through 2030.

title: Expanding Seniors Access to Mental Health Services Act
ai_summary: Starting January 1, 2026, this bill would expand the mental health services that Medicare covers when provided by clinical social workers. Currently, Medicare limits what clinical social workers can bill for when treating patients in skilled nursing facilities (like long-term care homes), and those facilities are often required to bundle those costs into a flat payment rather than billing separately — which can reduce residents' access to these services. This bill would remove skilled nursing facility residents from that bundled payment restriction, allowing clinical social workers to bill Medicare directly for their services, just as they can for patients in other settings. It also broadens the definition of covered clinical social worker services to include "health and behavior assessment and intervention" — a category of mental health-related care for people dealing with physical health conditions — in addition to the diagnosis and treatment of mental illnesses. The people most directly affected are Medicare patients living in skilled nursing facilities who need mental health support, as well as the clinical social workers who serve them.
ai_headline: Medicare would cover more social work care in nursing homes
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2026, Medicare patients in nursing homes could get mental health care from clinical social workers billed separately, not bundled.
ai_sections.what: This bill would let clinical social workers bill Medicare directly for services given to residents of skilled nursing facilities, starting January 1, 2026. Right now, those costs are bundled into a flat payment, which can limit access to care. The bill also expands covered services to include mental health support for people dealing with physical health conditions.
ai_sections.who: Medicare patients living in skilled nursing facilities who need mental health support are most directly affected. Clinical social workers who provide care in those settings are also affected.
ai_sections.why: Currently, bundling mental health services into a flat facility payment can reduce how often residents receive those services. Removing that restriction would change how clinical social workers are paid for this care.

title: Supporting Healthy Moms and Babies Act
ai_summary: This bill would require health insurance plans to cover pregnancy-related medical care at no out-of-pocket cost to the patient, meaning no copays, deductibles, or other cost-sharing charges. The covered care would span the entire pregnancy journey — from prenatal checkups and ultrasounds through labor and delivery (including anesthesia and specialist consultations) to a full year of postpartum care afterward, and would also include care related to miscarriage. Mental and behavioral health services connected to pregnancy or new parenthood would be covered too, including for parents who did not physically give birth. This requirement would apply to most private health insurance plans, including employer-sponsored group plans and plans purchased individually on the marketplace.
ai_headline: Private insurers would cover full pregnancy care costs
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Most private health plans would have to cover all pregnancy and postpartum care with zero out-of-pocket costs for patients.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require most private health insurance plans to cover pregnancy-related care at no cost to the patient — no copays, deductibles, or other charges. Covered care would include prenatal visits, ultrasounds, labor and delivery, anesthesia, specialist care, miscarriage care, and a full year of postpartum care. Mental and behavioral health services connected to pregnancy or new parenthood would also be covered, including for parents who did not give birth.
ai_sections.who: This requirement would apply to most private health insurance plans, including employer-sponsored plans and plans purchased through the marketplace. Parents who did not physically give birth would also be eligible for the mental and behavioral health coverage.
ai_sections.why: Patients with covered pregnancies would no longer pay out of pocket for most pregnancy and postpartum services. Insurance plans subject to this rule would be required to absorb those costs instead of passing them to patients.

title: Forage Fish Conservation Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes how the federal government manages small schooling fish — such as anchovies, herring, and similar species — that serve as a critical food source for larger fish, whales, seabirds, and other ocean wildlife. It requires federal fisheries managers to formally define these "forage fish," consider the food needs of predator species (like tuna and marine mammals) when setting fishing limits, and stop new commercial fisheries from targeting currently unmanaged forage fish species until scientists have assessed the potential ecosystem impacts and proper management rules are in place. It also specifically requires that river herring (blueback herring and alewife) and shad (American shad and hickory shad) be added to existing federal fishery management plans for the Atlantic coast, with increased monitoring — including onboard observers or electronic monitoring — on at least half of all fishing trips by mid-water trawl vessels in the Atlantic herring and mackerel fisheries.
