The bill primarily affects commercial fishing vessel operators, federal and regional fisheries management councils, and the Secretary of Commerce, while also having indirect consequences for the broader ocean ecosystem and industries — like recreational fishing and whale-watching — that depend on healthy fish populations.
ai_headline: Federal managers would set limits to protect forage fish stocks
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3714 would protect small schooling fish like anchovies and herring by setting new limits and blocking unregulated commercial fishing of these species.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets new rules for managing small schooling fish such as anchovies, herring, river herring, and shad. It would require fisheries managers to account for how much larger animals like tuna, whales, and seabirds need to eat when deciding how much of these fish can be caught. It also blocks new commercial fisheries from targeting currently unmanaged forage fish until scientists study the ecosystem effects and proper rules are in place.
ai_sections.who: Commercial fishing vessel operators, federal fisheries managers, and regional fisheries management councils would need to follow the new requirements. The Secretary of Commerce would also have new responsibilities, and industries like recreational fishing and whale-watching would be indirectly affected.
ai_sections.why: Small schooling fish are a key food source for many larger ocean animals, so changes to how they are managed can affect the broader ocean ecosystem. Industries that depend on healthy fish and wildlife populations, such as recreational fishing and whale-watching, could see indirect effects as well.

title: IG Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would do two main things related to Inspectors General (IGs) — the independent watchdogs who investigate waste, fraud, and abuse within federal agencies. First, it would create a brand-new Inspector General's office specifically for the Executive Office of the President — the group of agencies and staff that directly support the White House — requiring the President to appoint someone to that role within 90 days of the bill becoming law. Second, it would restrict the reasons why the President can fire most presidentially appointed Inspectors General, limiting removal to only three causes: inefficiency, malfeasance (misconduct), or neglect of duty. This protection would apply to IGs across most major executive branch departments and agencies, though IGs at independent agencies — a specific list of roughly two dozen bodies such as the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, the Merit Systems Protection Board, and the National Transportation Safety Board — would not receive the same removal protections. The bill primarily affects the President's authority over these watchdog officials, as well as the officials themselves and the federal employees and programs they oversee.
ai_headline: Bill would shield most federal watchdogs from firing
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3735 creates a new watchdog office for the White House and limits when the President can fire most federal inspectors general.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new Inspector General office inside the Executive Office of the President, the group of agencies that directly support the White House. The President would have 90 days to appoint someone to that role. The bill also limits the reasons the President can remove most presidentially appointed Inspectors General to three: inefficiency, misconduct, or neglect of duty.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects the President, most presidentially appointed Inspectors General across major federal departments and agencies, and the federal employees and programs those watchdogs oversee. Inspectors General at roughly two dozen independent agencies, such as the Nuclear Regulatory Commission and the National Transportation Safety Board, would not receive the new removal protections.
ai_sections.why: Because removal would be restricted to specific causes, Inspectors General would be harder to dismiss outside those reasons. A new watchdog office would exist where none currently does, covering the agencies and staff that support the White House directly.

title: Fair and Equal Housing Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would update the federal Fair Housing Act to make it illegal to discriminate against someone in housing based on their sexual orientation or gender identity, in addition to the protections that already exist for race, color, religion, sex, disability, family status, and national origin. It defines "sexual orientation" as homosexuality, heterosexuality, or bisexuality, and "gender identity" as a person's internal sense of their own gender, regardless of the sex they were assigned at birth. The protections would also apply if someone is discriminated against because of who they are associated with — for example, a person could not be denied housing because their spouse or roommate is LGBTQ+ — and they would cover situations where someone is discriminated against based on a mistaken belief about their identity. The bill would affect anyone involved in housing — including landlords, sellers, lenders, and real estate agents — as well as LGBTQ+ individuals and others who may face housing discrimination based on perceived or actual sexual orientation or gender identity. It also strengthens existing anti-intimidation protections, meaning it would be illegal to threaten or intimidate someone because they are exercising their fair housing rights related to sexual orientation or gender identity.
ai_headline: Fair Housing Act would ban LGBTQ+ housing bias
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3696 would add sexual orientation and gender identity as protected categories under the federal Fair Housing Act.
ai_sections.what: This bill would update the federal Fair Housing Act to make it illegal to discriminate against someone in housing because of their sexual orientation or gender identity. It also covers discrimination based on who a person is associated with, such as a spouse or roommate, and discrimination based on a mistaken belief about someone's identity. It would also be illegal to threaten or intimidate someone for exercising these fair housing rights.
ai_sections.who: The bill would apply to anyone involved in housing, including landlords, sellers, lenders, and real estate agents. It would affect LGBTQ+ individuals and anyone else who might face housing discrimination based on perceived or actual sexual orientation or gender identity.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, sexual orientation and gender identity are not explicitly protected under the federal Fair Housing Act, meaning people in those situations may have limited legal options if they face housing discrimination. Adding these categories would give affected individuals a federal legal path to challenge such discrimination.

title: VA Employee Fairness Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill removes certain limits on collective bargaining rights for employees of the Veterans Health Administration (VHA), which is the part of the Department of Veterans Affairs that provides health care to veterans. Under current law, specific rules restrict what VHA employees can negotiate over through their unions; this bill would eliminate those restrictions, giving VHA workers broader ability to collectively bargain over workplace issues — similar to the rights federal employees in many other agencies already have. The workers directly affected are the doctors, nurses, and other health care staff employed by the VHA across the country. The bill also makes clear that removing these restrictions does not take away the VA Secretary's existing authority over special pay incentives and faster hiring processes for certain VHA positions.
ai_headline: Bill would expand VHA workers' collective bargaining rights
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3261 would remove current limits on union bargaining rights for Veterans Health Administration health care workers.
ai_sections.what: This bill removes restrictions that currently limit what VHA employees can negotiate through their unions. It would give VHA workers bargaining rights more like those of federal employees in other agencies. The bill also keeps the VA Secretary's existing authority over special pay and hiring processes for certain VHA positions.
ai_sections.who: The workers affected are doctors, nurses, and other health care staff employed by the VHA across the country.
ai_sections.why: Removing these restrictions would expand the range of workplace issues VHA employees can negotiate through collective bargaining. VHA workers would have broader bargaining rights than they currently hold under existing law.

title: Capacity Building for Business Districts Pilot Program Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would create a new federal grant program, run by the U.S. Economic Development Administration, that sends money to experienced nonprofit or similar intermediary organizations so they can in turn help local business district groups in low-income, rural, minority, and Native communities. Those local groups — such as a neighborhood merchant association or a small-town main street organization — could use the support for training, operating costs, advice, and physical improvements to their commercial areas. The grants would be awarded competitively, must last at least two years, and the federal government must spread them across a wide range of geographic areas rather than concentrating them in one region. Organizations that receive the grants must report back annually on how the money was spent, which businesses and districts were helped, and how many jobs were created or kept. The people most directly affected are small and local businesses in struggling communities, the nonprofit organizations that serve those businesses, and the residents who rely on those commercial areas for goods, services, and jobs.
ai_headline: Federal grants would back small-business districts in underserved areas
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.

ai_sections.tldr: A new federal grant program would fund nonprofits that help local business districts in low-income, rural, minority, and Native communities.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal grant program run by the U.S. Economic Development Administration. Grants would go to established nonprofits or similar organizations, which would then support local business district groups with training, operating costs, advice, and physical improvements to commercial areas. Grants must last at least two years and be spread across many geographic areas.
ai_sections.who: Small and local businesses in struggling communities, the nonprofits that serve them, and residents who depend on those commercial areas for goods, services, and jobs would be most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Grant recipients would be required to report annually on spending, which businesses and districts were helped, and how many jobs were created or kept. Without the program, local business district groups in low-income and underserved communities would not have access to this source of funding and support.

title: To amend the Small Business Act to waive the accrual of interest and payments for certain disaster loans for a year, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: If this bill becomes law, small businesses and individuals who receive a federal disaster loan — for disasters officially declared on or after the bill's enactment — would not have to pay any interest or make any loan payments for the first 12 months after receiving the money. During that one-year window, the interest rate on the loan would be set to zero percent, and repayment of the loan's principal would be postponed. This relief applies to loans made through the Small Business Administration's disaster loan program, which helps businesses, homeowners, and renters recover from declared disasters such as floods, hurricanes, or wildfires. After the 12-month period ends, normal loan terms would resume. The change only covers loans tied to disasters declared after the bill takes effect — it does not apply to existing or previously issued disaster loans.
ai_headline: SBA disaster loans would get a 12-month interest-free window
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Small Business.

ai_sections.tldr: Small businesses and individuals with new federal disaster loans would pay zero interest and no payments for the first 12 months after receiving funds.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set the interest rate to zero and pause all payments for the first 12 months on qualifying federal disaster loans. After that year, normal loan terms would resume. It only applies to loans tied to disasters declared after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.who: Small businesses, homeowners, and renters who receive a disaster loan through the Small Business Administration after this bill takes effect would qualify. People with existing or older disaster loans would not be covered.
ai_sections.why: Borrowers would have one year without interest charges or required payments before standard repayment begins. Loans connected to disasters declared before the bill's enactment would continue under their original terms.

title: Improving Seniors’ Timely Access to Care Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill places new rules on Medicare Advantage insurance plans — the private health insurance plans that some people choose instead of traditional Medicare — specifically targeting a practice called **prior authorization**, where the plan must approve a treatment, test, or medication before a patient can receive it. Starting in 2027, these plans would have to report detailed data to the federal government each year — such as how many prior authorization requests they approved or denied, how long decisions took, and how often artificial intelligence was used to make those decisions — and that information would be made publicly available online. Starting in 2028, plans would be required to handle prior authorization requests through secure electronic systems (not faxes or basic online forms) and adopt new protections for enrollees, including regularly reviewing which services actually need prior authorization and giving high-performing doctors the ability to bypass some of those requirements. The bill also gives the federal government more authority to set stricter time limits on how quickly plans must respond to prior authorization requests, potentially as short as 24 hours in some cases. The people most directly affected are the millions of seniors and people with disabilities enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans, as well as the doctors, hospitals, and other providers who treat them.
ai_headline: Medicare Advantage prior authorization rules tightened
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2027, Medicare Advantage plans must report prior authorization data publicly; by 2028, use electronic systems and new patient protections.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets new rules for Medicare Advantage plans around prior authorization, which is when a plan must approve a treatment, test, or medication before a patient can get it. Beginning in 2027, plans must report yearly data to the federal government — such as approval and denial rates, decision times, and how often AI was used — and that data must be posted online. Starting in 2028, plans must use secure electronic systems for these requests, regularly review which services still need prior authorization, and allow high-performing doctors to skip some requirements.
ai_sections.who: Seniors and people with disabilities enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans are most directly affected. Doctors, hospitals, and other health care providers who treat those patients are also affected.
ai_sections.why: More detailed public data could make it easier to see patterns in how plans approve or deny care. Faster electronic systems and tighter response time limits could change how quickly patients and doctors get decisions on care requests.

title: To amend title XVIII of the Social Security Act to provide for certain cognitive impairment detection in the Medicare annual wellness visit and initial preventive physical examination.
ai_summary: Starting January 1, 2026, this bill would require that Medicare's annual wellness visit and the one-time "Welcome to Medicare" physical exam both include a standardized test to check for signs of cognitive impairment, such as memory problems or early dementia. The screening tool used must be one that the National Institute on Aging has approved as suitable for detecting cognitive impairment in a primary care setting, and the doctor must record which tool was used and what the results were in the patient's medical record. This affects Medicare beneficiaries — primarily people age 65 and older — who attend these covered preventive visits, as well as the doctors and other health providers who conduct them. The goal, as stated in the bill, is to catch cognitive decline earlier so that patients and their caregivers can access care planning, treatments, and support services sooner.
ai_headline: Medicare wellness visits would include cognitive screening
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting Jan. 1, 2026, Medicare wellness visits must include a standardized cognitive screening test approved by the National Institute on Aging.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require Medicare's annual wellness visit and the one-time "Welcome to Medicare" exam to include a standardized test for signs of cognitive impairment, such as memory problems or early dementia. The screening tool must be approved by the National Institute on Aging for use in a primary care setting. Doctors must record which tool was used and what the results showed in the patient's medical record.
ai_sections.who: This affects Medicare beneficiaries, mostly people age 65 and older, who attend these covered preventive visits. It also applies to doctors and other health providers who conduct these exams.
ai_sections.why: The bill states that the goal is to catch cognitive decline earlier. Earlier detection means patients and caregivers could access care planning, treatments, and support services sooner.

title: Motorsports Fairness and Permanency Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would permanently allow owners of motorsports entertainment complexes — such as NASCAR tracks, drag strips, and other racing venues — to write off the cost of their facilities for tax purposes over 7 years instead of the longer timeframe that normally applies to commercial buildings. Under current tax law, this faster 7-year depreciation schedule for motorsports venues has repeatedly expired and had to be renewed by Congress, creating uncertainty for track owners planning long-term investments. By making the rule permanent, the bill removes that uncertainty so owners can consistently count on the tax benefit when deciding whether to build, expand, or upgrade their facilities. The primary beneficiaries are businesses that own and operate motorsports venues, though supporters would argue the broader racing industry — including workers and fans — could benefit from continued investment in these facilities.
ai_headline: Senate bill locks in tax break for racing venues
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would make permanent a tax rule letting motorsports venue owners write off facility costs over 7 years instead of longer.
ai_sections.what: This bill would permanently allow owners of motorsports entertainment complexes — like NASCAR tracks and drag strips — to depreciate their facilities over 7 years for tax purposes. That faster write-off schedule has repeatedly expired and needed Congress to renew it.
ai_sections.who: The direct beneficiaries are businesses that own and operate motorsports venues. Supporters also point to workers and fans in the broader racing industry.
ai_sections.why: Because the rule has expired repeatedly, track owners have faced uncertainty when planning long-term investments. Making it permanent means owners can count on the tax treatment when deciding whether to build, expand, or upgrade facilities.

title: To amend the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 to require certain disclosures by institutional investment managers in connection with proxy advisory firms, and for other purposes.
ai_summary: This bill would require large investment firms — companies that manage stocks and other investments on behalf of clients like pension funds or individual retirement accounts — to publicly disclose detailed information about how they vote on corporate shareholder proposals and how much they rely on outside "proxy advisory firms" (businesses that provide recommendations on how to vote). Every covered investment manager would need to file a yearly report explaining how each shareholder vote was cast, what percentage of their votes matched the recommendations of any proxy advisory firm they hired, and how those votes served the financial best interests of their clients. Investment managers with at least $100 billion in assets under management face additional requirements: they must tell customers that they are not required to vote on every proposal, and they must perform and document a financial analysis for any vote that goes against a recommendation from an independent board of directors. The bill essentially aims to make the voting behavior of large investment firms more transparent, ensuring that clients can see whether their manager is making independent judgments or simply following outside advice.
ai_headline: Investment firms face new shareholder vote disclosure rules
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: Large investment firms would have to publicly report how they vote on corporate proposals and how often they follow outside voting advice.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require large investment firms to file yearly reports showing how they voted on corporate shareholder proposals and how closely they followed recommendations from outside proxy advisory firms. Firms managing at least $100 billion must also document a financial analysis for any vote that goes against an independent board of directors, and must tell customers they are not required to vote on every proposal.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to institutional investment managers, such as companies that manage stocks on behalf of pension funds or individual retirement accounts. Firms with at least $100 billion in assets under management face additional requirements beyond the general rules.
ai_sections.why: Clients would be able to see whether their investment manager is making independent decisions or largely following outside advice. This creates a public record of voting behavior that did not previously exist.

title: Farmer to Farmer Education Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs the U.S. Department of Agriculture to build and support networks where farmers, ranchers, and forest owners teach and learn from each other about conservation practices — such as soil health, water management, or sustainable forestry. The Agriculture Secretary would be allowed to sign agreements with a range of organizations — including nonprofits, tribal governments, universities, local governments, and farmer groups themselves — to set up these peer-learning networks, match experienced farmers with mentors, and organize training events. Those organizations could then pass funding along to smaller groups or even individuals to plan and run learning activities, and participants in those events must be paid at market rates. The bill gives priority to serving historically underserved farmers, low-income farmers, and those in high-poverty areas, and requires that assistance be provided in a farmer's native language whenever possible. Within four years, the Agriculture Department must report back to Congress on how the program is working, including how widely conservation practices have been adopted as a result.
ai_headline: USDA would fund farmer-to-farmer conservation networks
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would have the USDA build peer-learning networks so farmers can teach each other conservation practices.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the USDA to create and support networks where farmers, ranchers, and forest owners learn conservation practices from one another. The Agriculture Secretary could sign agreements with nonprofits, tribal governments, universities, local governments, and farmer groups to run these networks, organize training events, and pair experienced farmers with mentors. People who take part in learning activities must be paid at market rates.
ai_sections.who: The program serves farmers, ranchers, and forest owners, with priority given to historically underserved farmers, low-income farmers, and those in high-poverty areas. Assistance must be provided in a farmer's native language whenever possible.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the USDA would have four years to report to Congress on how the program is working and how widely conservation practices have been adopted as a result.

title: Born in the USA Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would block the federal government from spending any money to carry out a January 2025 executive order that directed federal agencies to stop recognizing U.S. citizenship for certain children born on American soil. Specifically, the bill targets Executive Order 14160, which sought to limit birthright citizenship — the principle that nearly anyone born in the United States is automatically a U.S. citizen. The bill's sponsors argue that birthright citizenship is guaranteed by the 14th Amendment to the Constitution and has been upheld by the Supreme Court, and that no executive order can legally override it. This bill would affect children born in the U.S. to parents who are undocumented immigrants or who are in the country on temporary visas, as those are the children whose citizenship the executive order sought to deny.
ai_headline: Bill targets funds for birthright citizenship order
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would stop federal agencies from using funds to carry out an executive order that limited birthright citizenship for some U.S.-born children.
ai_sections.what: This bill would block federal agencies from spending money to enforce Executive Order 14160, a January 2025 order that directed agencies to stop recognizing citizenship for certain children born on U.S. soil. The bill's sponsors say birthright citizenship is protected by the 14th Amendment and has been upheld by the Supreme Court.
ai_sections.who: This bill would affect children born in the United States to parents who are undocumented immigrants or who are in the country on temporary visas, as those are the children the executive order targeted.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, federal agencies could continue acting on the executive order and decline to recognize the citizenship of some U.S.-born children. With this bill in place, agencies could not use funds to carry out that policy.

title: Providing Child Care for Police Officers Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would create a federal grant program — run by the Department of Health and Human Services — that sends money to state, territorial, and tribal child care agencies, which then pass the funds along to law enforcement agencies or groups of agencies to help them set up and run child care programs for the children of police officers. The child care would be specifically designed to match the unusual schedules of law enforcement work, such as overnight shifts, weekends, and irregular hours. At least 20 percent of the grant money must go toward smaller law enforcement agencies with fewer than 200 full-time officers. The program would run from 2026 through 2030, with up to $24 million available each year, and any single applicant can receive no more than $3 million; recipients must also cover a growing share of costs themselves, starting at 10 percent and rising to about 33 percent by the third year. The bill primarily affects law enforcement officers with children, the agencies that employ them, and the child care providers who would offer services under the program.
ai_headline: Federal grants would fund child care for police families
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal grants would fund child care programs built around police officers' irregular schedules, running 2026–2030 with up to $24M per year.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal grant program managed by the Department of Health and Human Services. Money would flow to state, territorial, and tribal child care agencies, which would pass it along to law enforcement agencies to set up child care programs. Those programs would be designed around the irregular hours common in police work, such as overnight shifts and weekends.
ai_sections.who: The program primarily affects police officers who have children and the agencies that employ them. Child care providers who offer services under the program would also be involved.
ai_sections.why: Law enforcement schedules often fall outside the hours covered by standard child care programs. This bill would create a funding path to address that gap.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $24 million per year would be available from 2026 through 2030, and no single applicant could receive more than $3 million. Recipients would be required to cover a share of costs themselves, starting at 10 percent and rising to about 33 percent by the third year. At least 20 percent of grant funds must go to smaller agencies with fewer than 200 full-time officers.

title: INDEX Act
ai_summary: This bill would require investment advisers who manage **index funds and other passively managed funds** — such as the funds that track the S&P 500 found in many 401(k) retirement accounts — to pass shareholder voting decisions back to the individual investors who own shares in those funds, rather than casting those votes themselves. Under current practice, large investment firms like BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street collect and vote enormous blocks of shares on behalf of millions of investors, giving those firms significant influence over major U.S. companies. Under this bill, when those funds hold more than 1 percent of a company's shares, the investment adviser would be required to ask each individual fund investor how they want their proportional share of votes cast, and then vote accordingly. If an investor doesn't respond, the adviser generally cannot vote those shares — with limited exceptions for routine corporate matters or certain votes requiring majority approval, where uninstructed shares may be voted in line with other shareholders. The bill affects investment advisers managing passive or index-based funds, the individual investors in those funds (including ordinary retirement savers), and the companies whose shareholder votes would now reflect a broader range of individual investor preferences rather than the decisions of a small number of large asset managers.
ai_headline: Senate bill shifts proxy votes to index fund investors
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would require index fund managers to ask individual investors how to vote their shares instead of deciding for them.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require investment advisers who manage index funds and similar passively managed funds to pass voting decisions back to the individual investors who own shares in those funds. When a fund holds more than 1 percent of a company's shares, the adviser must ask each investor how they want their votes cast and then follow those instructions. If an investor does not respond, the adviser generally cannot vote those shares, with limited exceptions for routine matters or certain votes requiring majority approval.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects investment advisers managing index funds, including large firms like BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street, as well as the individual investors in those funds, including everyday retirement savers. It also affects the companies whose shareholder votes would now reflect a wider range of individual investors rather than a small number of large asset managers.
ai_sections.why: Right now, large investment firms vote enormous blocks of shares on behalf of millions of investors, giving those firms significant influence over major U.S. companies. This bill would shift that influence toward individual investors.

title: Violet’s Law
ai_summary: This bill requires every federal agency that uses animals in research laboratories to create a program — within one year of the bill becoming law — that allows certain research animals to be adopted or placed in non-laboratory settings once they are no longer needed for research. The animals covered are dogs, cats, nonhuman primates (such as monkeys), guinea pigs, hamsters, and rabbits. To be eligible for release, an animal must be examined by a licensed veterinarian within ten days of placement and certified to be free of infectious disease or physical conditions that could pose a risk to the animal itself, other animals, or the public. Eligible animals can be placed with individual adopters, animal shelters, animal rescue organizations (which must be registered nonprofits), or animal sanctuaries (which must also be registered nonprofits that provide lifetime care, do not breed or sell animals, and do not allow public contact or use animals for performances or research that causes pain or distress). This primarily affects federal agencies that operate animal research facilities and the animals housed in those facilities.
ai_headline: Federal labs would rehome research dogs, cats, and primates
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal agencies that use lab animals must create adoption programs for dogs, cats, primates, guinea pigs, hamsters, and rabbits no longer needed for research.
ai_sections.what: HR 3246, called Violet's Law, requires every federal agency that uses animals in research labs to set up an adoption or placement program within one year of becoming law. The program covers dogs, cats, nonhuman primates, guinea pigs, hamsters, and rabbits. Before any animal can be placed, a licensed vet must examine it within ten days and confirm it poses no health risk to people, other animals, or itself.
ai_sections.who: This law affects federal agencies that run animal research facilities and the animals housed in those facilities. Eligible animals can go to individual adopters, shelters, registered nonprofit rescue organizations, or registered nonprofit sanctuaries that provide lifetime care and do not breed, sell, or use animals for performances or painful research.
ai_sections.why: Without this law, federal agencies have no required process for rehoming research animals once their research use ends. This bill creates a consistent, health-screened pathway for those animals to leave laboratory settings.

title: MOMS Act
ai_summary: This bill creates several new federal programs and requirements centered on supporting pregnant women, new mothers, and their children. First, it directs the federal government to build and maintain a public website (pregnancy.gov) that helps women find nearby resources — such as housing, financial aid, childcare, medical care, and adoption services — based on their ZIP code, while also listing licensed child placement agencies and funding opportunities for pregnancy support centers; the website is explicitly barred from including or funding any organization that performs, refers for, or counsels in favor of abortion. Second, the bill creates a grant program for nonprofit organizations to give pregnant and postpartum women free information and referrals — and sometimes direct services — covering medical care, housing, nutrition, childcare, education, and more, again excluding organizations connected to abortion. Third, it provides grants to health care providers in rural, underserved, and tribal areas to purchase at-home telehealth equipment (such as blood pressure cuffs and pulse oximeters) so pregnant and postpartum women can receive monitoring and care remotely. Finally, it requires states to begin establishing and enforcing child support obligations from a biological father starting as early as the month of conception — if the mother requests it — with protections ensuring paternity testing cannot be done without the mother's consent or in any way that could harm the unborn child.
ai_headline: Bill creates pregnancy.gov and maternal support grants
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3235 creates federal programs for pregnant and postpartum women, including a resource website, grants, telehealth equipment, and early child support rules.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets up four new federal efforts: a public website at pregnancy.gov to help women find local support services by ZIP code, grants for nonprofits to connect women with resources and services, grants for rural and tribal health providers to offer remote monitoring equipment, and a rule requiring states to start collecting child support from biological fathers as early as conception if the mother asks. None of the website, grant, or funding programs may involve organizations connected to abortion.
ai_sections.who: Pregnant women, new mothers, and their children are the focus of this bill. Nonprofits, rural and tribal health care providers, and state child support agencies would take on new roles under it.
ai_sections.why: If passed, more women in underserved areas could access health monitoring and support services from home. States would also be required to change how and when they begin the child support process.

title: Farm Workforce Modernization Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill makes major changes to how farmworkers who are currently in the country without legal status — and the farms that employ them — are treated under immigration law. It would create a new temporary legal status called "Certified Agricultural Worker" for undocumented immigrants who can prove they did substantial farm work in the U.S. in recent years, allowing them and their immediate families to live and work here legally for renewable periods; those with very long work histories could eventually apply for a green card (permanent residence) by paying a $1,000 fee and meeting tax obligations. The bill also overhauls the existing H-2A guest worker visa program, which lets farms hire foreign workers temporarily, by creating a single online system to make that process faster and less paperwork-heavy. It requires farms to use an electronic system to verify that all employees are legally authorized to work in the U.S. It also includes provisions to preserve and build housing for farmworkers, hold overseas labor recruiters accountable, and protect applicants' personal information from being used against them for immigration enforcement purposes.
ai_headline: Farm bill offers undocumented workers a legal path
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, Education and Workforce, and Financial Services, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Undocumented farmworkers who can prove recent U.S. farm work could get temporary legal status, with a path to a green card.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a new temporary legal status for undocumented farmworkers who can show they did substantial farm work in the U.S. recently, letting them and their families live and work here legally for renewable periods. Workers with very long work histories could apply for a green card by paying a fee and meeting tax obligations. The bill also updates the existing guest worker visa program, requires farms to electronically verify workers' legal status, and includes protections for farmworker housing and applicant personal information.
ai_sections.who: Undocumented immigrants currently doing or recently having done farm work in the U.S., along with their immediate families, would be directly affected. Farms and agricultural employers would also face new requirements around hiring verification and guest worker paperwork.
ai_sections.why: Without a change in law, undocumented farmworkers remain at risk of deportation and have no formal path to legal status. Farms that rely on these workers face ongoing uncertainty about their workforce.
ai_sections.cost: Workers applying for a green card would pay a one thousand dollar fee. No other specific costs, funding amounts, or fines are described in the summary.
costChips: No other costs or fines mentioned

title: LOCOMOTIVES Act
ai_summary: This bill would prevent individual states from setting their own emission rules for locomotives (train engines) — both existing ones already in use and new ones. Currently, federal law already blocks states from making emission rules for certain locomotives, and this bill expands that block to cover all locomotives engaged in commercial railroad transportation. In practice, this means that only the federal government (through the Environmental Protection Agency) could regulate how much pollution trains are allowed to emit — no state could impose its own, potentially stricter, standards. The people most directly affected are railroad companies operating freight and passenger trains across the country, as well as the states (like California) that have tried or may want to create their own tougher locomotive emission standards.
ai_headline: Federal government would hold sole authority over train emissions
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would stop states from making their own pollution rules for train engines, leaving that power only with the federal government.
ai_sections.what: This bill would block all states from setting their own emission rules for locomotives used in commercial railroad transportation. Right now, federal law already limits some of that state authority, and this bill would expand that limit to cover all locomotives.
ai_sections.who: Railroad companies running freight and passenger trains across the country would be directly affected. States like California that have tried or may want to create stricter train emission standards would lose the ability to do so.
ai_sections.why: Only the federal government, through the Environmental Protection Agency, could regulate how much pollution trains are allowed to emit. No state could set its own standards, even stricter ones.

title: Save Healthcare Workers Act
ai_summary: This bill would make it a federal crime to assault a hospital worker while they are on duty, if the attack interferes with or limits their ability to do their job. A conviction could result in up to 10 years in federal prison, or up to 20 years if the attacker uses a weapon, causes bodily injury, or commits the assault during a declared public health or presidential emergency. The bill covers workers employed by or contracted with a wide range of medical facilities, including general hospitals, long-term care hospitals, rehabilitation centers, cancer hospitals, children's hospitals, critical access hospitals, and rural emergency hospitals. It also creates a federal grant program, funded at up to $25 million per year for ten years, through which hospitals could apply to the U.S. Attorney General for money to improve workplace safety — for example, by purchasing security technology, training staff in de-escalation, or coordinating with local law enforcement. There is a limited defense built into the bill: a person with a physical, mental, or intellectual disability may avoid conviction if their conduct was a direct result of that disability.
ai_headline: Bill would criminalize assaults on hospital workers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3178 would make assaulting an on-duty hospital worker a federal crime with up to 20 years in prison.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it a federal crime to attack a hospital worker while they are on the job if the attack interferes with their ability to do their work. A conviction could mean up to 10 years in federal prison, rising to up to 20 years if a weapon is used, someone is injured, or the attack happens during a declared public health or presidential emergency. People with physical, mental, or intellectual disabilities may avoid conviction if their actions were a direct result of that disability.
ai_sections.who: This law would apply to workers employed by or contracted with many types of medical facilities, including general hospitals, long-term care hospitals, rehabilitation centers, cancer hospitals, children's hospitals, critical access hospitals, and rural emergency hospitals.
ai_sections.why: If passed, people who assault on-duty hospital workers could face federal criminal charges and significant prison time. Hospitals could also apply for federal grant money to put toward workplace safety improvements.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would create a federal grant program funded at up to $25 million per year for ten years, with hospitals applying to the U.S. Attorney General for those funds.
costChips: Hospitals apply to U.S. Attorney General

title: America the Beautiful Act
ai_summary: This bill extends and expands a government fund that pays for fixing up deteriorating facilities, trails, and other infrastructure in national parks, national forests, wildlife refuges, Bureau of Land Management lands, and Bureau of Indian Education schools. The fund, which was set to expire in 2025, would be extended through 2033, and the annual cap on deposits into it would be raised from $1.9 billion to $2 billion. The bill also encourages people to donate money directly to the fund — for example, when buying a national parks pass online or in person — and gives priority to projects where donations cover at least 15 percent of the costs. In addition, land managers would be required to develop plans to do more routine, preventive maintenance so that backlogs of repairs don't keep growing, and to report to Congress on what steps they are already taking to reduce those backlogs. The people most directly affected are visitors to federal public lands, the employees who manage those lands, and communities near national parks and forests that depend on them for tourism and recreation.
ai_headline: Senate bill extends federal lands repair fund to 2033
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: S 1547 extends a federal repair fund for public lands through 2033 and raises its annual cap from $1.9B to $2B.
ai_sections.what: This bill extends a federal fund that pays for fixing deteriorating facilities, trails, and infrastructure on national parks, forests, wildlife refuges, BLM lands, and Bureau of Indian Education schools. The fund's deadline would move from 2025 to 2033, and the annual deposit cap would rise from $1.9 billion to $2 billion. The bill also lets people donate to the fund when buying a national parks pass, gives priority to projects with at least 15 percent donor funding, and requires land managers to plan for routine preventive maintenance and report to Congress on reducing repair backlogs.
ai_sections.who: Visitors to federal public lands, the employees who manage those lands, and communities near national parks and forests that rely on tourism and recreation are most directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without action, the fund was set to expire in 2025, and repair backlogs on public lands would likely continue to grow. Requiring preventive maintenance plans and congressional reporting is intended to slow how quickly those backlogs accumulate.
ai_sections.cost: The annual cap on deposits into the fund would increase from $1.9 billion to $2 billion. Donations from the public, including optional contributions when purchasing a national parks pass, would also feed into the fund.
costChips: Public donations accepted, incl. park pass gifts

title: Ukrainian Adjustment Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would allow certain Ukrainian nationals who were paroled (temporarily admitted) into the United States after February 20, 2014, along with their qualifying family members, to apply for permanent residency (a "green card") through a streamlined process. To qualify, applicants must pass the same security and background checks required of refugees, and the government must determine that granting them permanent status is not a threat to national safety or security. Applicants would not be charged any fees for the application, and while their application is pending, they cannot be deported or treated as being in the country unlawfully. People who are eligible but do not apply within one year of the program's official launch generally lose the right to use this pathway, though exceptions exist for those who request an extension or have already completed equivalent vetting.
ai_headline: Ukrainians paroled after 2014 could gain permanent residency
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Ukrainians paroled into the U.S. after Feb. 20, 2014 could apply for a green card at no cost, but must apply within one year of launch.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a streamlined path to permanent residency for certain Ukrainian nationals already temporarily admitted to the United States. Applicants must pass the same security and background checks required of refugees. While an application is pending, the person cannot be deported or considered unlawfully present.
ai_sections.who: Ukrainian nationals who were paroled into the United States after February 20, 2014, along with their qualifying family members, could be eligible. People who do not apply within one year of the program's launch generally lose access to this pathway, with some exceptions.
ai_sections.why: If passed, eligible Ukrainians would have a defined window to pursue permanent legal status or risk losing this option. Those who qualify but miss the deadline would generally need to use other legal pathways to remain in the country.

title: Better CARE for Animals Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill strengthens the enforcement of federal animal welfare laws by giving the U.S. Department of Justice (through the Attorney General) new, explicit powers to take legal action against people or businesses that violate those laws — powers that previously rested mainly with the Department of Agriculture. Under the bill, the Attorney General can go to federal court to seek emergency orders to stop ongoing violations, have animals removed or relocated, revoke licenses, and impose fines of up to $10,000 per violation per day; animals involved in violations can also be seized and forfeited to the government, and the person responsible can be charged for the cost of the animals' care. Fines and penalties collected must be used to pay for the temporary care of animals while legal cases are being resolved. The bill also requires the Agriculture Department and the Attorney General to formally agree, within 180 days, on how they will work together and share information — including details about repeat violators whose actions seriously harm animals' health or well-being. The people most directly affected are licensed animal dealers and exhibitors (such as breeders, zoos, and similar businesses regulated under existing animal welfare law), as well as anyone who may temporarily care for animals seized during enforcement actions.
ai_headline: DOJ gains authority to prosecute animal welfare violations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3112 gives the Justice Department new legal powers to enforce animal welfare laws, including seizing animals and fining violators up to $10,000 per day.
ai_sections.what: This bill gives the U.S. Department of Justice new, direct powers to enforce federal animal welfare laws — powers that mostly belonged to the Department of Agriculture before. The Justice Department can now go to court to stop violations, remove animals, revoke licenses, and collect fines. The two agencies must also agree within 180 days on how they will share information and work together.
ai_sections.who: Licensed animal dealers and exhibitors — such as breeders, zoos, and similar businesses — are the people most directly regulated under this bill. Anyone who temporarily cares for animals seized during an enforcement action is also affected.
ai_sections.why: Adding a second federal agency to enforcement means violators face a wider legal response than before. Repeat violators whose actions seriously harm animals' health are specifically flagged for information-sharing between agencies.
ai_sections.cost: Fines can reach up to $10,000 per violation per day. Money collected from fines and penalties must go toward the temporary care of animals while legal cases are being resolved. The person found responsible for a violation can also be charged for the cost of caring for any seized animals.
costChips: Fine money funds seized animal care
Violators may pay animal care costs

title: Canadian Snowbird Act
ai_summary: This bill would allow Canadian citizens who are at least 50 years old to stay in the United States for up to 240 days out of every 365-day period as long-term visitors, which is longer than the 182 days currently available under standard tourist rules. To qualify, a Canadian retiree would need to maintain a home in Canada, own or rent a place to stay in the United States, not be barred from entering the country for other legal reasons, and not collect U.S. federal public benefits. While in the U.S., they generally could not work for an American employer, though they could continue doing remote work for a Canadian employer they already worked for back home. A qualifying retiree's spouse could also stay under the same rules, and having a U.S. residence would not count against them as evidence that they are trying to permanently move to America. The bill also ensures that these Canadian visitors are treated as non-residents for U.S. tax purposes, meaning they would not be subject to U.S. income taxes the way a U.S. resident would be.
ai_headline: Canadians over 50 could stay in the U.S. up to 240 days
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3070 would let Canadians 50+ stay in the U.S. up to 240 days per year instead of the current 182-day limit.
ai_sections.what: This bill would raise the allowed U.S. stay for qualifying Canadian visitors from 182 days to 240 days out of every 365-day period. They could not work for a U.S. employer, but could do remote work for a Canadian employer they already had. They would also be treated as non-residents for U.S. tax purposes.
ai_sections.who: Canadian citizens who are at least 50 years old could qualify, along with their spouses. To be eligible, they would need to keep a home in Canada, have a place to stay in the U.S., and not collect U.S. federal public benefits.
ai_sections.why: Extending the allowed stay changes how long Canadian retirees can be present in the U.S. before they must return home. Treating them as tax non-residents means they would not be taxed on their income the way a U.S. resident would be.

title: Access to Breast Cancer Diagnosis Act of 2025
ai_summary: If this bill becomes law, health insurance plans — including employer-sponsored group plans and individual plans — would be required to cover certain breast exams at no out-of-pocket cost to the patient, meaning no deductibles, copays, or coinsurance. This applies to two types of exams: "diagnostic" exams, which are used when a doctor needs to follow up on a suspicious finding from a screening or other exam, and "supplemental" exams, which are additional screening tests (such as an MRI or ultrasound) ordered for people who have no known abnormality but are considered higher risk due to personal or family history. Both types of exams must be medically necessary and appropriate according to National Comprehensive Cancer Network Guidelines to qualify for this no-cost coverage. Insurers would still be allowed to require prior authorization before approving these exams, and the rule would also apply to grandfathered health plans and high-deductible health plans paired with Health Savings Accounts, with the new requirements taking effect for plan years starting on or after January 1, 2026.
ai_headline: Bill would mandate free breast diagnostic exams
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2026, most health plans must cover follow-up and supplemental breast exams with no copays, deductibles, or coinsurance.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require health insurance plans to cover two types of breast exams at no cost to the patient: diagnostic exams ordered after a suspicious finding, and supplemental exams ordered for people at higher risk due to personal or family history. Both types must be considered medically necessary and appropriate under National Comprehensive Cancer Network Guidelines. Insurers could still require prior authorization before approving these exams.
ai_sections.who: This rule would apply to most health insurance plans, including employer-sponsored group plans, individual plans, grandfathered health plans, and high-deductible health plans paired with Health Savings Accounts. It would affect patients whose plans cover plan years starting on or after January 1, 2026.
ai_sections.why: Without this requirement, patients in qualifying plans may currently face out-of-pocket costs for these types of breast exams. If the bill becomes law, those costs would no longer be charged to the patient for covered exams.

title: Hot Foods Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would allow people who receive food assistance benefits (commonly known as SNAP or "food stamps") to use those benefits to buy hot, ready-to-eat foods — something that is currently not permitted under federal law. Right now, SNAP benefits can only be used to purchase foods intended to be prepared and eaten at home, so items like a hot rotisserie chicken, a warm meal from a grocery store deli counter, or other prepared hot foods are off-limits. Under this bill, hot prepared foods would be treated the same as other food purchases. The change would affect SNAP recipients across the country, as well as retailers who sell hot prepared foods, though the bill also limits which stores can be approved as SNAP retailers based on this new rule — specifically, no more than half of a store's total gross sales can come from hot ready-to-eat foods for the store to qualify.
ai_headline: SNAP benefits could expand to cover hot prepared foods
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2512 would let SNAP recipients buy hot, ready-to-eat foods — like deli meals or rotisserie chicken — which is currently not allowed.
ai_sections.what: This bill would change federal law to allow SNAP benefits to be used for hot, ready-to-eat foods sold at stores. Right now, SNAP only covers foods meant to be prepared at home, so hot prepared items are not allowed. Under this bill, hot foods would be treated the same as any other SNAP-eligible food purchase.
ai_sections.who: SNAP recipients across the country would be affected, as would retailers that sell hot prepared foods. Stores where more than half of total sales come from hot ready-to-eat foods would not be allowed to participate as SNAP retailers under this rule.
ai_sections.why: Passing this bill would expand what SNAP recipients can purchase with their benefits. It would also change which stores are eligible to accept SNAP payments, depending on how much of their business comes from hot prepared foods.

title: Historic Tax Credit Growth and Opportunity Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill changes the federal tax credit that property owners can claim when they renovate a historic building. Under current law, the credit is spread out over five years, but this bill would allow the full 20% credit to be taken in the same year the renovated building is put back into use. For smaller renovation projects — generally those costing up to $3.75 million, or up to $5 million in rural areas — the credit rate would be increased to 30%, and owners of those smaller projects would be allowed to sell or transfer their tax credit to another person or business who can use it. The bill also makes it easier for more buildings to qualify for the credit by loosening one of the eligibility requirements related to how much money has been spent on a building relative to its value, and it removes a rule that currently reduces a building's tax depreciation value by the amount of the credit claimed. These changes primarily affect real estate developers, property owners, investors, and nonprofits involved in restoring historic buildings across the country.
ai_headline: Bill expands historic building renovation tax credits
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Owners who restore historic buildings could claim a larger, faster federal tax credit — and smaller projects could sell that credit to others.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes the federal tax credit for renovating historic buildings in several ways. Owners could take the full 20% credit in the same year the building reopens, instead of spreading it over five years. Smaller projects would get a higher 30% credit rate and could transfer that credit to another person or business.
ai_sections.who: These changes would affect real estate developers, property owners, investors, and nonprofits that restore historic buildings. Smaller projects in rural areas would see additional adjustments under the bill.
ai_sections.why: Allowing the credit to be taken all at once and transferred changes when and how owners and investors receive its financial value. Loosening eligibility rules and removing the depreciation reduction means more buildings and owners could qualify and keep more of the credit's value.

title: Accounting STEM Pursuit Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill adds accounting education to the list of approved activities that schools can use federal grant money for under an existing program that supports student learning and enrichment. Specifically, it would allow schools to spend these grants on teaching students about accounting and raising awareness of accounting as a career. It also specifically calls for expanding access to high-quality accounting courses for students through 12th grade who come from groups that are underrepresented in accounting careers. The bill affects K–12 schools and school districts that receive federal Student Support and Academic Enrichment grants, as well as the students in those schools — particularly those from groups historically less represented in the accounting profession.
ai_headline: Federal K–12 grants could fund accounting education
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2911 lets K–12 schools use existing federal grants to teach accounting and expand access for underrepresented students.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds accounting education to the list of things schools are allowed to spend certain federal grant money on. That includes teaching accounting and raising awareness of it as a career path, with a focus on reaching students from groups that are underrepresented in the accounting field.
ai_sections.who: K–12 schools and school districts that receive federal Student Support and Academic Enrichment grants would be affected. Students in those schools, especially those from groups historically less represented in accounting, are the intended focus.
ai_sections.why: Right now, accounting education is not listed as an approved use for these grants. This bill would change that, giving schools the option to direct existing grant funds toward accounting courses and awareness through 12th grade.

title: Neighborhood Homes Investment Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new federal tax credit designed to encourage developers and builders to construct or substantially renovate homes for sale in struggling, low-income neighborhoods where the cost to build a home is higher than what buyers can afford to pay — a problem the bill calls the "value gap." Builders who sell qualifying homes at affordable prices in these distressed areas can claim a credit equal to the difference between their development costs and the sale price, with additional caps based on a percentage of development costs or national home prices. To qualify, both the neighborhood and the buyer must meet specific criteria: the census tract must have below-average incomes and home values, and the buyer's household income must be at or below 140% of the local median. The bill also includes a smaller version of the credit — capped at $50,000 — for contractors who rehabilitate homes already owned by lower-income residents who live there as their primary home. Each state would receive a yearly credit "budget" (at least $12 million or $9 per state resident, whichever is greater, adjusted for inflation) to be distributed by a state-designated agency, and homeowners who buy a qualifying home and then sell it within five years must repay a portion of the benefit back to that agency.
ai_headline: New tax credit targets homebuilding in low-income areas
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 2854 creates a federal tax credit for builders who construct or renovate homes in low-income neighborhoods where building costs exceed what buyers can pay.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a federal tax credit for builders and developers who build or substantially renovate homes for sale in struggling, low-income neighborhoods. The credit covers the gap between what it costs to build the home and what buyers can afford to pay. A smaller version of the credit, capped at $50,000, is available to contractors who renovate homes already owned and lived in by lower-income residents.
ai_sections.who: Builders qualify if they sell homes at affordable prices in census tracts with below-average incomes and home values, and the buyer's household income must be at or below 140% of the local median. Lower-income homeowners who already own and live in a home as their primary residence may also be covered through the contractor rehabilitation credit.
ai_sections.why: In some neighborhoods, the cost to build a home is higher than what buyers can afford, which can discourage new construction or renovation. This credit is meant to offset that gap, which may affect whether builders choose to work in those areas.
ai_sections.cost: Each state receives a yearly credit budget of at least $12 million or $9 per state resident, whichever is greater, adjusted for inflation, distributed by a state-designated agency. Buyers who purchase a qualifying home and sell it within five years must repay a portion of the benefit to that agency.

title: North American Energy Act
ai_summary: This bill creates a new, standardized federal approval process for building or operating pipelines and power lines that cross U.S. borders with Canada or Mexico. Under the new system, companies would apply to either the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (for oil and natural gas pipelines) or the Department of Energy (for electric transmission lines) and receive a decision within 90 days of completing the required environmental review — unless the agency determines the project is not in the public interest. The bill also eliminates the requirement that the President personally approve these cross-border energy projects through a Presidential permit, replacing that process with the new agency-based system. It affects energy companies that want to build or modify cross-border pipelines or power lines, as well as the federal agencies that review those projects; facilities already in operation when the law takes effect would not need to go through the new process.
ai_headline: Agencies would replace presidents in approving cross-border energy lines
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill replaces presidential approval of cross-border pipelines and power lines with a 90-day agency review process.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a standard federal process for approving pipelines and power lines that cross U.S. borders with Canada or Mexico. Companies would apply to FERC for oil and gas pipelines or the Department of Energy for electric lines, and receive a decision within 90 days after environmental review is complete. It removes the requirement that the President personally sign off on these projects through a Presidential permit.
ai_sections.who: Energy companies that want to build or change cross-border pipelines or power lines would follow the new process. FERC and the Department of Energy would take over the review role previously held by the President. Facilities already operating when the law takes effect are not included.
ai_sections.why: Removing the Presidential permit requirement shifts decision-making authority from the White House to federal regulatory agencies. Companies would have a defined timeline for receiving a decision once environmental review is finished.

title: National Biotechnology Initiative Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill directs the President to create a coordinated, government-wide effort to strengthen the United States' position in biotechnology — the science of using living organisms and biological processes to develop products and technologies. It sets up a new office inside the White House, led by a presidentially appointed Director, to coordinate biotechnology activities across more than a dozen federal agencies, including the Departments of Defense, Agriculture, Health and Human Services, and Energy. Those agencies would work together on things like funding research, streamlining regulations for biotech products, protecting against biological security threats, training workers, and improving international trade relationships for U.S. biotech products. The bill also requires the government to publish a public website with plain-language biotech information and regulatory guidance, produce regular reports and a national strategy, and authorize roughly $132 million over five years to support the new office. The effort is designed to wind down its intensive coordination role after 20 years, shifting to a lighter support function, and it affects federal agencies, researchers, businesses, students, workers, and the general public who interact with biotechnology in any form.
ai_headline: White House office would lead U.S. biotech coordination
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committees on Foreign Affairs, Agriculture, Energy and Commerce, and Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would create a White House office to coordinate U.S. biotechnology efforts across federal agencies for up to 20 years.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs the President to set up a new office inside the White House to coordinate biotechnology work across more than a dozen federal agencies. The office would be led by a presidentially appointed Director and would oversee things like research funding, regulations, worker training, biological security, and international trade for biotech products. The government would also be required to publish a public website with plain-language information and regulatory guidance, and to produce regular reports and a national strategy.
ai_sections.who: Federal agencies including the Departments of Defense, Agriculture, Health and Human Services, and Energy would take part in this effort. Researchers, businesses, students, workers, and members of the general public who interact with biotechnology in any form would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: The coordination role is designed to run intensively for 20 years and then shift to a lighter support function. Without a coordinated effort, the U.S. government's biotechnology activities across many agencies would continue to operate separately.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize roughly $132 million over five years to support the new White House office.

title: Abortion Funding Awareness Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would require states to track and publicly disclose how Medicaid dollars — specifically the portion that comes from the federal government — are spent on services provided by abortion providers. Within 60 days after each fiscal year ends, any state that made such payments must file a report with the federal Department of Health and Human Services and post it on a public state website; the report must list the amount and purpose of each payment, compare it to payments in prior years, and include the number of abortions performed, the gestational age at the time of each abortion, and the method used. The federal government must then compile all state reports into a single summary report, submit it to two congressional committees, and publish it on a federal website within 90 days after each fiscal year. The bill affects state Medicaid programs and any healthcare provider — including organizations affiliated with or under the same ownership as an abortion provider — that receives Medicaid reimbursements from federal funds.
ai_headline: States would log Medicaid funds spent at abortion providers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: States would have to track and publish how federal Medicaid dollars are spent at abortion providers, with details reported to Congress.
ai_sections.what: This bill would require states to track how federal Medicaid money is spent at abortion providers and make that information public. Each year, states must file a report listing payment amounts, purposes, year-over-year comparisons, and details about each abortion including gestational age and method used. The federal government would then combine all state reports into one summary report for Congress and post it online.
ai_sections.who: Any state that sends federal Medicaid payments to abortion providers — or to healthcare organizations connected to them — would be required to comply. The Department of Health and Human Services and two congressional committees would receive the reports.
ai_sections.why: States and the federal government would take on new reporting and publishing responsibilities each fiscal year. Information about how federal Medicaid funds move through the healthcare system would become available to the public and to Congress.

title: Staged Accident Fraud Prevention Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill makes it a federal crime to deliberately cause a crash involving a large commercial vehicle — such as a semi-truck or bus — or to arrange for someone else to do so. Anyone convicted of intentionally staging such a collision could face a fine, up to 20 years in prison, or both; if the staged crash causes serious injury or death to anyone, the minimum prison sentence becomes 20 years. The law would apply to individuals who orchestrate these so-called "staged accidents," which are often carried out to fraudulently collect insurance payouts. One key protection in the bill is that a person cannot be prosecuted under this federal law if they have already been convicted or acquitted for the same act under a state or local law.
ai_headline: Staged truck crashes would become a federal crime
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Federal bill would make staging a crash with a semi-truck or bus a crime punishable by up to 20 years in prison.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it a federal crime to deliberately cause a crash involving a large commercial vehicle, like a semi-truck or bus, or to arrange for someone else to do so. If the staged crash causes serious injury or death, the minimum prison sentence is 20 years.
ai_sections.who: This law would apply to people who plan or carry out staged collisions, often done to fraudulently collect insurance money. A person cannot be charged under this federal law if they have already been convicted or acquitted for the same act under a state or local law.
ai_sections.why: People convicted under this law could face a fine, up to 20 years in prison, or both. Staged crashes involving large commercial vehicles can result in serious injuries or deaths to uninvolved people on the road.

title: Puppy Protection Act of 2025
ai_summary: This bill would set new federal rules for how licensed dog dealers — businesses that breed and sell dogs commercially — must care for their animals. It requires dealers to give dogs solid-floored enclosures of a minimum size based on the dog's length, keep temperatures between 45 and 85 degrees Fahrenheit, provide food at least twice a day and constant access to clean water, and give dogs over 12 weeks old daily access to an outdoor exercise area.
