title: Pell Grant Flexibility Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes how Pell Grant money is calculated for college students with disabilities. Right now, Pell Grant amounts are reduced when a student takes fewer classes than a full-time course load. This bill would let students with disabilities who are approved by their school to take a lighter schedule still receive the same Pell Grant amount as a full-time student, as long as they are taking at least 5 credit hours. The school must first officially determine that the reduced course load is appropriate for that student's disability.

There is one important limit built into the bill. This full-time calculation only applies to how much money a student gets each semester. It does not change the total number of semesters a student is allowed to receive Pell Grants over their lifetime. So students would not be able to use this rule to extend how long they can collect grants overall.

This bill affects low-income college students who have a qualifying disability under the Americans with Disabilities Act and whose school has approved them to take fewer classes because of that disability. It could also affect colleges and universities, which would need to make those official determinations for students. Students without disabilities are not affected by this change.
ai_headline: Pell Grant rules would expand for students with disabilities taking reduced course loads
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: Students with disabilities approved for lighter course loads could receive the same Pell Grant amount as full-time students, starting at 5 credits.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes how Pell Grant amounts are calculated for students with disabilities who take fewer classes than a full-time load. If a school officially approves a reduced schedule because of a student's disability, and the student takes at least 5 credit hours, they would receive the same grant amount as a full-time student. This rule only affects how much money a student gets each semester, not the total number of semesters they can receive Pell Grants in their lifetime.
ai_sections.who: This affects low-income college students who have a qualifying disability under the Americans with Disabilities Act and whose school has approved them to take a lighter course load. It also affects colleges and universities, which would be responsible for making those official determinations.
ai_sections.why: Without this change, students with disabilities who take fewer classes receive a smaller Pell Grant than full-time students. This bill would make their per-semester amount the same as a full-time student's, as long as the school has approved their reduced schedule.

title: Protect Working Musicians Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill lets independent musicians band together to negotiate with big streaming platforms like Spotify or YouTube without breaking antitrust laws. Normally, antitrust laws prevent competitors from coordinating on prices or business terms, because that could be seen as price-fixing. This bill carves out a specific exception so that small, independent music creators can act as a group when dealing with large platforms, giving them more bargaining power than they would have on their own.

The bill covers musicians, producers, mixers, and sound engineers who own their own copyrights and earn less than one million dollars a year in licensing income. It applies to online music platforms that make more than one hundred million dollars a year from music distribution. Under the bill, qualifying independent creators could also collectively refuse to license their music to these platforms, or to AI companies that use music to train their systems. However, the negotiations cannot be only about setting a price, must treat similarly situated artists the same way, and cannot involve outside parties like major record labels.

The people most affected are self-managed, independent musicians who currently have little leverage when dealing with large streaming services. Right now, platforms can largely set their own terms, and individual artists have little choice but to accept them or go unheard. This bill would not change copyright law or streaming rules directly, but it would give smaller creators a legal way to organize and push back collectively on licensing terms they find unfair.
ai_headline: Bill would let indie musicians bargain collectively with Spotify and YouTube
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Independent musicians earning under $1M/year could legally team up to negotiate streaming deals under this bill.

It covers musicians, producers, mixers, and sound engineers who own their own copyrights and earn less than one million dollars a year in licensing income. Groups could also collectively refuse to license their music to platforms or to AI companies that use music for training purposes.
ai_sections.who: The bill applies to independent creators who manage themselves and earn under one million dollars a year in licensing income. It targets platforms that earn more than one hundred million dollars a year from music distribution. Major record labels cannot be part of the negotiations.
ai_sections.why: Right now, individual independent artists have little power when large platforms set their own licensing terms, and creators largely must accept those terms or lose access to listeners. This bill would give smaller creators a legal path to organize together and respond to terms they find unfair.

title: Fair Wages for Incarcerated Workers Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would change federal law so that people who work while incarcerated in prison or jail are covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), the main federal law that sets minimum wage rules. Right now, the FLSA specifically excludes incarcerated workers, meaning prisons and jails do not have to pay them the federal minimum wage. This bill would remove that exclusion, requiring that incarcerated workers be paid at least the federal minimum wage for the work they do, whether the facility is run by a government agency or a private company under government contract.

The bill also includes a specific protection about how that pay can be used. Under current law, employers can sometimes count things like housing and meals as part of an employee's wages. This bill says that for incarcerated workers, prisons cannot count the cost of a cell, food, or court-imposed fees (like filing fees or attorney fees) as part of the wages owed. This means the worker would receive actual money, not just a credit toward costs the facility already charges them. Child support payments, crime victim fund payments, and criminal fines would still be allowed to be deducted.

This bill would directly affect the roughly 800,000 people who work inside U.S. prisons and jails, including those in federal programs like UNICOR, state prison industries, work release programs, and facilities run by private prison companies. It would also affect the government agencies and private companies that run those facilities, since they would face new wage obligations. The bill was introduced in the Senate and referred to committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on.
ai_headline: Senate bill would extend FLSA minimum wage rules to prison workers
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4143 would require prisons and jails to pay incarcerated workers at least the federal minimum wage.

Prisons and jails, whether government-run or privately operated under government contract, would be required to pay incarcerated workers at least the federal minimum wage. The bill also prohibits facilities from counting the cost of a cell, food, or court-imposed fees as part of those wages, though deductions for child support, crime victim funds, and criminal fines would still be allowed.
ai_sections.who: This bill would affect the roughly 800,000 people who work inside U.S. prisons and jails, including those in federal programs, state prison industries, work release programs, and private facilities. It would also affect the government agencies and private companies that operate those facilities, since they would take on new wage obligations.

ai_sections.cost: The summary does not include specific cost figures, but it notes that government agencies and private companies running prisons and jails would face new wage obligations they do not currently have.

title: Fair Wages for Incarcerated Workers Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would require that people who work while incarcerated in jail or prison be covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), which is the main federal law that sets minimum wage and overtime rules for workers in the United States. Right now, incarcerated workers are excluded from those protections, meaning prisons and jails can pay them very little, sometimes just a few cents per hour, with no legal minimum. If this bill became law, incarcerated workers would have to be paid at least the federal minimum wage for the work they do, whether the facility is run by a government agency or a private company under contract with the government.

The bill also includes a specific protection about how wages are calculated. Prisons would not be allowed to count the cost of housing or meals as part of an incarcerated worker's pay, and they could not deduct court-imposed fees like filing fees or public defender fees from wages in a way that reduces what counts as the worker's actual earnings. This is meant to prevent situations where a worker technically gets paid but most or all of it is taken back through deductions before they ever see it.

This bill would affect the roughly 800,000 to 1 million incarcerated people in the United States who perform some kind of work through prison programs, including jobs in facility operations, manufacturing, public works, and work release programs. It would also affect federal and state correctional agencies and private prison companies, which would be required to comply with federal wage laws they currently do not have to follow for this group of workers.
ai_headline: Bill would extend Fair Labor Standards Act to prison workers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8002 would require prisons and jails to pay incarcerated workers at least the federal minimum wage under the same law that covers other U.S. workers.
ai_sections.what: This bill would extend Fair Labor Standards Act protections to people who work while incarcerated, requiring they be paid at least the federal minimum wage. Facilities would not be allowed to count housing or meals as wages, and could not use certain court-imposed fee deductions to reduce a worker's counted earnings.
ai_sections.who: The bill would affect the roughly 800,000 to 1 million incarcerated people in the United States who currently perform work through prison programs. It would also apply to federal and state correctional agencies and private prison companies that run work programs.
ai_sections.why: Right now, incarcerated workers are excluded from federal wage law, so facilities can legally pay them very little, sometimes just cents per hour. This bill would change that by making the same federal minimum wage rules apply to this group of workers.

title: Drones for First Responders Act
ai_summary:
This bill raises import taxes on drones made in China. Starting 30 days after it becomes law, Chinese-made drones would face a 30% tariff. That rate would increase each year, reaching 50% plus a flat $100 fee per drone after five years. The goal is to make American-made and allied-country-made drones more price-competitive against cheaper Chinese options. Starting in 2031, any drone imported into the U.S. would also need to prove that key parts like cameras, radios, and flight computers were not made in China.

The money collected from these new tariffs would go into a special government fund. That fund would then be used to give grants to first responders, farmers and ranchers, and operators of critical infrastructure like power grids and water systems. Sixty percent of the grant money would go to first responders, with the remaining 40% split between farmers and infrastructure providers. The grants could be used to buy or lease drones that are not made by Chinese companies.

The people most directly affected are fire departments, police departments, emergency medical services, and other first responders who currently rely heavily on Chinese-made drones. Farmers who use drones for crop monitoring would also be affected, as would businesses that manage critical infrastructure. American drone manufacturers could benefit if the higher tariffs push buyers toward domestically produced options. Importers and retailers of Chinese drones would likely face higher costs, which could be passed on to consumers.
ai_headline: Bill proposes steep tariffs on Chinese drones to fund first-responder grants
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3786 adds tariffs on Chinese-made drones and uses that money to help first responders, farmers, and infrastructure operators buy non-Chinese drones.
ai_sections.what: This bill places a 30% tariff on drones made in China, rising to 50% plus a flat fee per drone over five years. Starting in 2031, imported drones must also prove that key parts like cameras, radios, and flight computers were not made in China. Money collected from these tariffs goes into a fund that provides grants to buy or lease non-Chinese drones.
ai_sections.who: Fire departments, police, emergency medical services, farmers, and critical infrastructure operators like power grid and water system managers are the main groups affected. American drone makers and importers or retailers of Chinese drones would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Higher tariffs could raise the cost of Chinese-made drones, which many first responders and farmers currently rely on. Importers and retailers may pass those higher costs on to buyers.
ai_sections.cost: Tariff revenue funds grants for first responders, who receive 60% of the money, with the remaining 40% split between farmers and infrastructure providers. Importers of Chinese drones would pay the tariffs, and those costs could be passed on to consumers.

title: Maternal Health Pandemic Response Act
ai_summary:
This bill would direct federal money toward tracking and improving the health of pregnant women and new mothers during public health emergencies like pandemics. It would authorize about $190 million for the CDC and the National Institutes of Health to collect data on how diseases and health crises affect pregnant and postpartum people, study what works to keep them safe, and build up the ability of state, tribal, and local health departments to gather that information. The CDC would also be required to make that data publicly available on its website and update it at least monthly during an emergency, broken down by race, ethnicity, income, location, and other factors, while keeping individual identities private.

The bill would also create a task force made up of federal health officials, state and tribal public health leaders, doctors, midwives, doulas, community organizations, and patients to develop recommendations for how to deliver safe, respectful maternity care during emergencies. Those recommendations would cover a wide range of topics, including telehealth options, mental health support, access to midwives and doulas, screening for domestic violence, infant nutrition, and how to address bias and discrimination in maternity care settings. The CDC would also be required to run public education campaigns so pregnant people, their employers, and their doctors have clear, accurate information during emergencies.

The people most directly affected are pregnant women and new mothers, particularly those from communities that already face higher rates of pregnancy-related death or complications, including racial and ethnic minority groups, low-income individuals, and people in rural or underserved areas. Health care providers, hospitals, midwives, doulas, and state and tribal health agencies would also be affected by the new data requirements and care recommendations.
ai_headline: Federal maternal health bill directs $190M to CDC for emergency data
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would fund data collection and care guidelines to protect pregnant people and new mothers during public health emergencies.
ai_sections.what: This bill would direct federal money to the CDC and National Institutes of Health to track how health emergencies affect pregnant and postpartum people and study what keeps them safe. It would create a task force to develop care recommendations covering telehealth, mental health, doulas, midwives, domestic violence screening, and bias in maternity care. The CDC would also be required to run public education campaigns and post regularly updated data on its website, broken down by race, income, location, and other factors.
ai_sections.who: Pregnant women and new mothers would be most directly affected, especially those from communities that already face higher rates of pregnancy-related death or complications, including racial and ethnic minorities, low-income individuals, and people in rural or underserved areas. Health care providers, hospitals, midwives, doulas, and state and tribal health agencies would also be affected by new data requirements and care recommendations.
ai_sections.why: Without consistent data and care standards, gaps in maternity care during emergencies can go unnoticed or unaddressed. Tracking outcomes across different groups would make those gaps visible and help inform future responses.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize approximately $190 million for the CDC and the National Institutes of Health to carry out these activities.

title: DeOndra Dixon INCLUDE Project Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill directs the National Institutes of Health (NIH) to officially create and run a research program focused on Down syndrome. The program, called the INCLUDE Project, would fund studies on how Down syndrome affects people throughout their entire lives, from childhood through old age. It would also support clinical trials that include people with Down syndrome, research into conditions that often occur alongside Down syndrome (such as Alzheimer's disease and autoimmune disorders), and work aimed at improving daily life for people with Down syndrome and their families. The NIH would be required to make sure different agencies within it are working together and not repeating each other's work.

The bill also requires NIH to regularly check in with patient advocates and others who are directly affected by Down syndrome when deciding what to study. Every two years, NIH would have to send Congress a report describing what research was done, which agencies were involved, and what real-world results came out of that research.

This bill most directly affects people with Down syndrome, their families, caregivers, and the medical and research communities that work with them. It could also matter to people interested in related conditions like Alzheimer's disease, since Down syndrome research may produce broader insights. The bill was introduced in May 2025 and has been referred to committee, meaning it has not yet become law.
ai_headline: NIH must launch INCLUDE Project to fund Down syndrome research across lifespan
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported by the Yeas and Nays: 46 - 0.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3491 would require NIH to formally establish the INCLUDE Project, a research program focused on Down syndrome across all life stages.
ai_sections.what: This bill would direct the National Institutes of Health (NIH) to officially create and run a research program called the INCLUDE Project. The program would fund studies on Down syndrome across a person's entire life, support clinical trials that include people with Down syndrome, and research conditions that often occur alongside it, such as Alzheimer's disease and autoimmune disorders. NIH would also be required to coordinate across its agencies to avoid repeated work and to consult regularly with patient advocates and those directly affected by Down syndrome.
ai_sections.who: This bill most directly affects people with Down syndrome, their families, and caregivers, as well as the medical and research communities that work with them. People interested in related conditions like Alzheimer's disease may also be affected, since findings from this research could apply more broadly.
ai_sections.why: If passed, NIH would be legally required to run this program, report to Congress every two years on research activity and real-world results, and involve affected communities in setting research priorities. Without the bill, no such formal requirement exists.

title: HSA Modernization Act
ai_summary:
This bill makes several changes to Health Savings Accounts, or HSAs, which are special tax-free accounts people use to save money for medical expenses. Right now, HSAs have strict rules about who can open one and how much money can go in. The bill would expand those rules starting in 2026. Veterans who use VA health benefits but do not have a service-connected disability could now open an HSA. People on Medicare Part A due to age could also contribute to an HSA. Native Americans who receive care through the Indian Health Service would no longer be disqualified. People with lower-cost "bronze" or "catastrophic" insurance plans through the ACA marketplace could use an HSA. Health insurance plans that skip the deductible for the first $500 of mental health care would still qualify. And if someone just started their high-deductible plan, they could use their HSA for medical bills from up to 60 days before the account was officially opened.

The bill also makes two financial changes. First, married couples where both spouses are 55 or older could put both of their catch-up contributions into the same single HSA account, which is not allowed under current rules. Second, the cap on how much someone can put into an HSA each year would be raised to match the out-of-pocket maximum allowed under their health plan, which is a significantly higher limit than what is currently set.

This bill mainly affects people who have high-deductible health insurance plans and use, or want to use, an HSA. That includes working-age adults, veterans, Medicare-age adults still working, Native Americans, and married couples near retirement. People who currently cannot contribute to an HSA because of their coverage type would gain new access, and existing HSA holders could potentially save more money tax-free for medical and long-term care costs.
ai_headline: Bill would expand HSA eligibility to veterans, Medicare enrollees and tribal members
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2026, more people could open or contribute to a Health Savings Account, and yearly contribution limits would rise.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes who can open a Health Savings Account and how much money can go into one each year, starting in 2026. It also lets married couples who are both 55 or older put both of their catch-up contributions into one shared HSA, which current rules do not allow. People with certain lower-cost insurance plans could now qualify, and a new 60-day lookback window would let new account holders use HSA funds for bills from just before the account opened.
ai_sections.who: These changes would affect veterans using VA health benefits without a service-connected disability, people on Medicare Part A still working, Native Americans receiving care through the Indian Health Service, people with bronze or catastrophic ACA marketplace plans, and married couples nearing retirement. People who currently cannot contribute to an HSA because of their coverage type would gain new access under this bill.
ai_sections.why: Expanding eligibility means more people could use a tax-free account to set aside money for medical expenses. Raising the annual contribution limit to match the out-of-pocket maximum under a person's health plan means existing HSA holders could potentially store more money tax-free.

title: Universal School Meals Program Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would make school breakfast and lunch free for every child enrolled in a participating school, regardless of family income. Right now, free and reduced-price meals are only available to families who qualify based on income. Under this bill, no student would ever be charged for a school meal, and schools would be forbidden from shaming or singling out children over unpaid meal debt. The government would also reimburse schools for any meal debt families already owe. Reimbursement rates paid to schools would start at $3.28 per breakfast and $5.42 per lunch, and those amounts would go up each year to keep pace with food costs.

The bill also expands free meals beyond the regular school day. Summer meal programs, afterschool care programs, and childcare centers would all be required to serve free meals to children. Schools that buy at least 25 percent of their food from local farms would receive a small extra payment per meal. The bill also updates how the government measures student poverty across several federal programs, since the current method relies on who qualifies for free or reduced-price lunch, a category that would no longer exist.

The people most directly affected are the roughly 50 million K-12 students in schools that participate in federal meal programs, along with their families, school food service workers, and local school districts. Families who currently pay full or reduced price for school meals would no longer receive a bill. Schools and states would take on new paperwork and administrative responsibilities, while receiving higher federal reimbursements to cover the cost of serving every student.
ai_headline: Federal universal school meals bill would end student lunch debt
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would make school meals free for all 50 million K-12 students, regardless of family income.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make breakfast and lunch free for every student in participating schools, no matter what their family earns. Schools could not charge students for meals or single out children with unpaid meal debt. Free meals would also expand to summer programs, afterschool care, and childcare centers.
ai_sections.who: The roughly 50 million K-12 students in schools that take part in federal meal programs would be affected, along with their families, school food service workers, and local school districts. Families who currently pay full or reduced price for school meals would no longer receive a bill.
ai_sections.why: Schools and states would take on new paperwork and administrative responsibilities under this bill. The way the government measures student poverty across several federal programs would also need to change, since it currently relies on who qualifies for free or reduced-price lunch, a category that would no longer exist.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would reimburse schools at $3.28 per breakfast and $5.42 per lunch, with those rates rising each year to keep up with food costs. Schools would also receive a small extra payment per meal if they buy at least 25 percent of their food from local farms. The government would repay schools for existing unpaid meal debt that families already owe.
costChips: Bonus pay for 25% local food purchases
Gov't repays existing unpaid meal debt

title: GUIDANCE Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill is a response to actions taken by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) in May 2025, when the agency withdrew or canceled more than 20 of its own guidelines and rules. Those withdrawn rules covered a wide range of consumer protections, including rules about medical debt collection, overdraft fees, lending discrimination, data security, protections for military servicemembers, and how banks can report information about you to credit agencies. This bill would cancel those withdrawals, essentially putting all those CFPB rules and guidelines back into effect as if they had never been removed.

The people most directly affected are everyday consumers who use financial products like bank accounts, credit cards, loans, and credit reports. For example, rules about how debt collectors can contact you, how banks must handle your personal financial data, and what lenders must tell you about home sale contracts would be restored. Protections for active-duty military members and their families, as well as rules about what banks can do with closed accounts or charge in fees, would also come back into force.

This bill was introduced by Representative Maxine Waters and has been sent to the House Financial Services Committee, where it would need approval before moving forward. It has not yet become law. Whether these protections should be in place is a matter of ongoing debate between those who prioritize consumer oversight and those who prefer less regulation of financial institutions.
ai_headline: Bill seeks to restore 20-plus CFPB consumer protection rules scrapped in May
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would restore more than 20 consumer financial protection rules that the CFPB withdrew in May 2025.
ai_sections.what: This bill would cancel the CFPB's May 2025 decision to withdraw more than 20 of its own rules and guidelines. Those rules covered topics like medical debt collection, overdraft fees, lending discrimination, data security, credit reporting, and protections for military servicemembers. Restoring them would put all those rules back in effect as if they had never been removed.
ai_sections.who: Everyday people who use bank accounts, credit cards, loans, or credit reports would be directly affected. Active-duty military members and their families are also among those covered by the rules this bill would restore.
ai_sections.why: If the bill passes, the withdrawn CFPB rules would be legally in force again, and financial institutions would be required to follow them. If it does not pass, those rules remain withdrawn and the consumer protections they provided stay absent.

title: Flood Mapping Modernization and Homeowner Empowerment Pilot Program Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill tells FEMA to run a pilot program that gives grants to cities and towns to build better flood maps focused on urban flooding. Urban flooding is when heavy rain overwhelms storm drains and causes water to back up into streets, basements, and buildings in developed areas. Right now, official flood maps often miss this kind of flooding. The grants would help local governments use modern technology to create detailed maps that show flood risk building by building, and then make that information available to the public through a website.

The program would award grants to up to 12 communities total, spread across different city sizes and locations, including large coastal cities, mid-sized cities, and smaller cities with populations above 50,000. Each grant can cover up to 75 percent of the project cost, with the local government covering the rest. The total funding authorized is $2 million in 2027 and $5 million in 2028. Communities that receive grants have 18 months to complete their mapping work and then must report back to FEMA on what they learned.

The people most directly affected are homeowners, renters, and business owners in participating cities who would gain access to clearer, more accurate information about their personal flood risk. Local governments and stormwater agencies in eligible cities could apply for the grants. The bill also directs FEMA to report to Congress on the results, with the idea that successful methods could eventually be used more broadly across the country.
ai_headline: FEMA pilot program would fund urban flood mapping in up to 12 cities
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (text: CR S2280-2281)

ai_sections.tldr: FEMA would run a pilot program giving grants to up to 12 cities to build detailed, public urban flood maps building by building.
ai_sections.what: This bill directs FEMA to run a pilot program that funds cities and towns to create detailed flood maps focused on urban flooding, where heavy rain overwhelms storm drains and floods streets, basements, and buildings. Participating communities must make the maps available to the public through a website and complete the work within 18 months. FEMA would then report results to Congress to inform possible wider use of the methods.
ai_sections.who: Homeowners, renters, and business owners in participating cities would gain access to more detailed information about their personal flood risk. Local governments and stormwater agencies in cities with populations above 50,000 could apply for the grants.
ai_sections.why: Current official flood maps often miss urban flooding, which means people may not have accurate information about the flood risk to their specific home or building. This program would test whether newer mapping technology can fill that gap.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $2 million in 2027 and $5 million in 2028 for the program. Grants cover up to 75 percent of project costs, with the local government responsible for the remaining share. Up to 12 communities total may receive grants.
costChips: Grants cover up to 75% of costs

title: Let Kids Play Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets what it calls "vulture investors," meaning private equity funds that buy into businesses and use certain profit-extraction tactics that can harm those businesses. Specifically, it would make it illegal for any private equity firm that has used these harmful tactics, or that has had two or more of its purchased companies go bankrupt within five years, to own, operate, or control any youth sports organization, league, facility, training camp, or related technology platform. Private equity firms that currently have youth sports investments would have to prove they have never used these harmful tactics, or else sell off those investments within two years.

The bill also bans a long list of specific business practices in youth sports, even for private equity firms that are not officially labeled "vulture investors." These include things like forcing families to use specific hotels or travel companies for tournaments, charging hidden fees that are not clearly disclosed upfront, locking youth athletes or clubs into long-term non-cancelable contracts, and taking ownership of athlete health data or video recordings of games. Firms that break these rules can be ordered to repay profits, refund fees, cancel debts they loaded onto sports organizations, and return any data or technology they took.

The bill affects private equity firms that invest in youth sports, the youth sports organizations themselves, and families with children in organized sports. Enforcement would be handled by the Federal Trade Commission and the Justice Department's antitrust division, but state attorneys general and individual families could also sue in court. Fines collected would go into a fund used to lower participation costs, fund scholarships, or help divested youth sports organizations keep running safely.
ai_headline: FTC bill would bar predatory private equity from youth sports markets
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: Private equity firms with harmful financial records could be banned from owning youth sports organizations under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill would bar private equity firms labeled "vulture investors" from owning or controlling youth sports organizations, leagues, facilities, training camps, or related technology platforms. Firms currently holding those investments would have two years to sell them off unless they can prove they never used the harmful financial tactics the bill targets. The bill also bans specific business practices in youth sports, such as forcing families to use certain hotels, charging hidden fees, locking athletes into long-term contracts, and taking ownership of athlete health data or game recordings.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects private equity firms that invest in youth sports, the youth sports organizations they invest in, and families with children in organized sports. Enforcement falls to the Federal Trade Commission and the Justice Department's antitrust division, though state attorneys general and individual families can also take legal action.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, private equity firms with records of harmful financial tactics face no specific restriction on owning or shaping youth sports organizations. The bill creates consequences for violations, including repaying profits, refunding fees, canceling debts loaded onto sports organizations, and returning data or technology taken from them.
ai_sections.cost: Fines collected under this bill would go into a fund used to lower participation costs, fund scholarships, or help youth sports organizations keep running after being divested.

title: Multigenerational Caregiving Data Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the federal government to start counting people who provide unpaid care to multiple generations of family members at the same time. Right now, large government surveys like the American Community Survey and the Current Population Survey do not include questions about this type of caregiving, so there is no reliable national data on how many people do it or how it affects their lives. The bill gives the government three years to add at least one question about multigenerational caregiving to at least one major federal survey. Answering the question would be voluntary.

The bill defines a multigenerational caregiver as someone who provides unpaid care to people from more than one age group, such as a person who looks after both a young child and an elderly parent with a health condition. The care can happen across different households, not just under one roof. Before the question goes live on any survey, the responsible agency must test it to make sure people understand it and that it produces reliable answers.

Two years after the question is added to a survey, the Secretary of Commerce must send a report to Congress summarizing what the data shows, how well the question worked, and whether it should be continued or changed. The report also gets posted online for the public to read. This bill mainly affects federal statistical agencies that run large population surveys, and its findings would eventually be useful to policymakers, researchers, and the millions of Americans who quietly juggle caregiving responsibilities for both younger and older family members at the same time.
ai_headline: Federal government must count multigenerational caregivers in national surveys
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

A multigenerational caregiver is someone who provides unpaid care to family members from more than one age group, such as caring for both a child and an elderly parent, even across different households. Answering the question would be voluntary, and the responsible agency must test it before it goes live.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects federal statistical agencies that run large population surveys, such as those conducting the American Community Survey and the Current Population Survey. Researchers, policymakers, and Americans who provide unpaid care to both older and younger family members at the same time would eventually be affected by the data collected.
ai_sections.why: Right now there is no reliable national data on how many people provide this type of care or how it affects their lives. Two years after a question is added, the Secretary of Commerce must report to Congress on what the data shows and whether the question should be continued or changed, with the report also posted online.

title: Multigenerational Caregiving Data Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the federal government to start collecting data on "multigenerational caregivers," meaning people who are simultaneously caring for someone from more than one age group without being paid. For example, this would include a person caring for both a young child and an elderly parent, or a child and a disabled adult family member. Right now, large federal surveys that track the American population do not consistently ask about this type of caregiving, especially when the people being cared for live in different homes. The bill gives the government three years to add at least one question about this to a major national survey, such as the American Community Survey or the Current Population Survey.

The bill sets some ground rules for how this works. The question must be tested before it is rolled out widely to make sure people understand it and that the answers are reliable. Answering the question is completely voluntary. Within two years after the question starts being used, the government must send a report to Congress and post it publicly online, explaining how well the data collection worked and whether changes should be made.

The people most directly affected are the millions of Americans who quietly take on unpaid caregiving responsibilities for multiple family members at once, sometimes called the "sandwich generation." Right now, policymakers have limited hard data on how many of these caregivers exist, what they earn, or how their health is affected. This bill does not create new programs or benefits; it simply starts gathering information so that future decisions about workplace policy, health care, and family support can be based on better facts.
ai_headline: Federal government must track multigenerational unpaid caregivers in national surveys
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8792 requires the federal government to add a survey question about unpaid multigenerational caregiving within three years.
ai_sections.what: This bill tells the federal government to add at least one question about multigenerational caregiving to a major national survey, such as the American Community Survey or the Current Population Survey, within three years. The question must be tested first to make sure people understand it and that the answers are reliable. Answering is voluntary, and within two years of the question going live, the government must send a report to Congress and post it publicly online.
ai_sections.who: This affects Americans who are unpaid caregivers for people from more than one age group at the same time, such as someone caring for both a young child and an elderly parent. It also affects federal agencies responsible for running large national surveys.
ai_sections.why: Right now, policymakers have limited data on how many multigenerational caregivers exist, what they earn, or how their health is affected. Collecting this information could inform future decisions about workplace policy, health care, and family support.

title: STOP TRUMP ACT
ai_summary:
This bill would block federal money from being used to pay legal settlements or judgments in favor of the President, the President's family members, businesses connected to the President, political appointees, or people who took part in the January 6, 2021 Capitol attack. It specifically targets cases where those parties claim the government unfairly targeted them politically or "weaponized" law enforcement against them. It would also ban the creation of any government fund or commission set up to compensate people who say they were victims of politically motivated government actions.

The bill also restricts the Department of Justice from representing the government in lawsuits where the President stands to personally gain money or political benefits. If any federal money was already paid out in ways that would violate this bill, the government would be required to get that money back. The Treasury Department could recover it by withholding tax refunds, grants, contracts, or other payments owed to the recipient, and the Attorney General could sue to collect it.

This bill would mainly affect the current President and people closely connected to the administration who have filed or might file legal claims against the federal government. It would also affect anyone who received payments through a government "weaponization" compensation program. Ordinary Americans not connected to the President or those specific legal disputes would not be directly affected. The bill was introduced by a Democratic congressmember and is currently in committee, meaning it has not been voted on.
ai_headline: Bill would bar Treasury from paying "weaponization" settlements to Trump allies
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

It would also ban government funds or commissions set up to compensate people who claim they were targeted by politically motivated government actions. If any payments like this had already been made, the government would be required to recover that money.
ai_sections.who: This bill would mainly affect the current President and people closely connected to the administration who have filed or might file legal claims against the federal government. title: Military and Veterans Fuel Discount Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would allow the Secretary of Defense to create a fuel discount program at military exchange stores, which are the gas stations and shops found on or near military bases. The discount would be at least equal to the federal gas tax, which is 18.4 cents per gallon for regular gasoline and 24.4 cents per gallon for diesel. The Secretary could also offer additional discounts to cover state and local fuel taxes. The fuel must be pumped directly into a vehicle the buyer owns, and the program would run through September 30, 2029.

The people who would benefit are anyone already allowed by law to shop at military exchange stores. That group generally includes active-duty service members, military retirees, veterans with certain disability ratings, and their dependents. The discount would be applied automatically at the pump when an eligible person fuels up. Rules would be put in place to prevent fraud and to stop people from buying discounted fuel in bulk to resell it.

The Department of Defense would be required to report to Congress each year on how the program is working, including how much fuel was sold, what the discounts cost, and any problems that came up. The bill does not force the Secretary to start the program, it simply gives permission to do so. It also encourages coordination with other federal agencies that run similar exchange systems, such as those serving Coast Guard members or veterans through the Department of Veterans Affairs.
ai_headline: Pentagon fuel discount bill targets military exchange gas stations
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9027 would let the Pentagon offer a fuel discount at base gas stations, but does not require it to do so.
ai_sections.what: This bill gives the Secretary of Defense permission to create a fuel discount program at military exchange gas stations. The discount would be at least equal to the federal gas tax — 18.4 cents per gallon for regular gasoline and 24.4 cents per gallon for diesel. The program would run through September 30, 2029, and the discount would apply automatically at the pump.
ai_sections.who: Anyone already allowed by law to shop at military exchange stores could receive the discount. That group generally includes active-duty service members, military retirees, veterans with certain disability ratings, and their dependents.
ai_sections.why: If the Secretary chooses to start the program, eligible people would pay less per gallon when fueling their own vehicles at participating base stations. Congress would receive a yearly report on fuel sold, discount costs, and any problems that came up.

title: Caja del Rio Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill would set aside about 85,000 acres of federal land near Santa Fe, New Mexico for conservation and protection. It creates two new protected areas: the Caja del Rio Special Management Area, covering roughly 67,000 acres of National Forest land, and the Caja del Rio National Conservation Area, covering about 18,000 acres of Bureau of Land Management land. The land would be permanently off-limits to new mining, oil and gas drilling, and most new road construction. The government would be required to create management plans within three years, limit where motor vehicles can go, and close down unauthorized roads.

The bill gives Native American tribes a significant role in how the land is managed. Tribes with historical or cultural ties to the area can help shape management plans, contribute traditional knowledge, and access the land for religious and cultural practices like plant gathering and ceremonies. Small portions of the land can even be temporarily closed to the general public to protect tribal ceremonies. Spanish and Mexican land grant communities with historical ties to the area also retain certain traditional uses like hunting, grazing, fishing, and wood gathering.

People most directly affected include residents of the Santa Fe area, hikers and outdoor recreationists, Native American tribes connected to the region, ranchers who currently graze livestock there, and Santa Fe County and city water utilities that draw drinking water from the area. The bill specifically protects existing water infrastructure rights for the city of Santa Fe and Santa Fe County while still requiring that any new construction fit the conservation goals of the protected areas.
ai_headline: Federal bill would protect 85,000 acres near Santa Fe from drilling and mining
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4458 would permanently protect about 85,000 acres of federal land near Santa Fe from new mining, drilling, and most road construction.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets aside about 85,000 acres of federal land near Santa Fe, New Mexico as two new protected areas. New mining, oil and gas drilling, and most new road construction would be permanently banned there. The government would have three years to create management plans and restrict where motor vehicles can travel.
ai_sections.who: Santa Fe area residents, hikers, Native American tribes, ranchers with grazing rights, and city and county water utilities are directly affected. Tribes with cultural ties to the land and Spanish and Mexican land grant communities with historical ties would keep certain traditional access rights.
ai_sections.why: Designating the land as protected means current and future uses like mining and drilling would no longer be allowed there. Water infrastructure for the city of Santa Fe and Santa Fe County would be protected, but any new construction in the area would need to meet conservation requirements.

title: Caja del Rio Protection Act
ai_summary:
This bill would set aside about 85,000 acres of federal land near Santa Fe, New Mexico for special protection. It creates two designated zones: a Special Management Area covering roughly 67,000 acres in the Santa Fe National Forest, managed by the U.S. Forest Service, and a National Conservation Area covering about 18,000 acres of Bureau of Land Management land. The bill also creates a separate withdrawal area that is closed off from mining, drilling, and new land sales. Within three years of passing, federal managers would be required to create detailed plans for how each area is managed, including limiting which roads can be used by motor vehicles and decommissioning roads that are not officially designated.

The bill places strong restrictions on what can happen on this land. New permanent roads would generally be banned, and mining, oil and gas drilling, and most new development would not be allowed. Livestock grazing that already exists could continue under federal oversight. Wildfire management and ecological restoration work would still be permitted. The federal government could also trade some Bureau of Land Management land with the State of New Mexico in exchange for state-owned land within the protected boundaries, as long as both sides agree and the land values are fairly matched.

Several groups are specifically affected. Native American tribes with historical or cultural ties to the area would have guaranteed access for religious, spiritual, and traditional practices, and their knowledge could be included in how the land is managed. Local communities with roots in old Spanish and Mexican land grants would have their traditional uses, like hunting, fishing, wood gathering, and herb collecting, recognized and protected. Santa Fe County and the city of Santa Fe would keep their existing rights to maintain drinking water infrastructure in the area. Recreational users, off-road vehicle enthusiasts, and anyone hoping to develop or mine the land would face new restrictions on access and use.
ai_headline: Federal bill would protect 85,000 acres near Santa Fe under Forest Service and BLM oversight
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8616 would protect about 85,000 acres near Santa Fe, NM by banning new roads, mining, and drilling across two designated zones.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets aside roughly 85,000 acres of federal land near Santa Fe, New Mexico into two protected zones: a Special Management Area of about 67,000 acres in the Santa Fe National Forest and a National Conservation Area of about 18,000 acres managed by the Bureau of Land Management. A separate withdrawal area would be closed to mining, drilling, and new land sales. New permanent roads would be banned, though existing livestock grazing, wildfire management, and ecological restoration work could continue.
ai_sections.who: Native American tribes with ties to the area would keep guaranteed access for religious and traditional practices, and communities descended from Spanish and Mexican land grant holders would have their traditional uses like hunting, fishing, and wood gathering formally recognized. Santa Fe County and the city of Santa Fe would keep their existing rights to maintain drinking water infrastructure, while recreational users, off-road vehicle enthusiasts, and anyone seeking to mine or develop the land would face new restrictions.
ai_sections.why: Federal land managers would be required to create detailed management plans within three years, including decisions about which roads motor vehicles may use and which roads would be decommissioned. The federal government could also arrange voluntary land exchanges with the State of New Mexico if land values are fairly matched.

title: Julius Rosenwald and Rosenwald Schools National Historical Park Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new national historical park honoring Julius Rosenwald and the schools he helped build for Black children in the South during the early 1900s. The park would have locations in four states: a main visitor center near the old Sears complex in Chicago, Illinois, where Rosenwald built his business fortune, plus three restored school buildings in Maryland, South Carolina, and Virginia. Rosenwald partnered with Booker T. Washington to fund and build nearly 5,000 schools between 1912 and 1932, educating over 600,000 African American children during an era when many were denied access to public education. Notable people who attended these schools include John Lewis, Maya Angelou, and Medgar Evers.

The bill also directs the National Park Service to create a nationwide network connecting surviving Rosenwald school buildings and related sites across the country. This network would include federal, state, local, and privately owned properties. The Park Service would produce educational materials, conduct research, put up signs and exhibits, and could offer grants to participating sites. The bill also calls for adding information about Rosenwald's story at two existing national historic sites in Illinois and Alabama.

The people most directly affected are visitors, students, historians, and local communities near the park sites and network locations. Property owners whose buildings join the network would need to allow public access. State and local governments in Illinois, Maryland, South Carolina, and Virginia may enter into partnerships with the federal government to support the park. Funding would come from Congress through the regular federal appropriations process.
ai_headline: Bill would establish Julius Rosenwald National Historical Park across four states
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would create a new national historical park honoring Julius Rosenwald and the nearly 5,000 schools he helped build for Black children in the South.
ai_sections.what: This bill would establish a national historical park with sites in Illinois, Maryland, South Carolina, and Virginia honoring Julius Rosenwald and the schools he helped build for African American children between 1912 and 1932. The National Park Service would also create a nationwide network connecting surviving Rosenwald school buildings and related sites, producing educational materials, research, signage, and exhibits, and offering grants to participating sites.
ai_sections.who: Visitors, students, historians, and communities near park and network sites would be directly affected. Property owners whose buildings join the network would need to allow public access, and state and local governments in the four states may enter into partnerships with the federal government.
ai_sections.why: Creating the park and network would give more people the opportunity to learn about the role these schools played during an era when many Black children were denied access to public education. Property owners who join the network would take on an obligation to provide public access to their sites.
ai_sections.cost: Funding for the park would come from Congress through the regular federal appropriations process.

title: INVEST Act
ai_summary:
This bill would give businesses a tax credit when they hire veterans who have skills or training in renewable energy fields like solar, wind, or hydropower. To qualify, the veteran must have a military job specialty related to renewable energy, advanced manufacturing, welding, or engineering, OR have completed a vocational degree in renewable energy within the past year, OR hold a LEED green building certification. The tax credit only applies to the wages paid to the veteran for work done in renewable energy jobs, not any other work they might do for that employer. It would apply to workers hired after December 31, 2025.

The bill affects two main groups. First, businesses in the renewable energy industry that hire qualifying veterans would pay less in federal taxes. Second, military veterans who have relevant training or credentials would potentially become more attractive job candidates, since hiring them comes with a financial benefit to the employer. The bill also includes rules ensuring that U.S. territories like Puerto Rico, Guam, and others can offer a similar benefit to businesses there.

This is an extension or update to an existing part of the tax code called the Work Opportunity Tax Credit, which already gives employers tax breaks for hiring certain groups who often face barriers to employment. This bill adds a specific category focused on veterans with clean energy skills, reflecting both veteran employment and energy workforce goals at the same time.
ai_headline: New Work Opportunity Tax Credit expansion targets veterans in clean energy hiring
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Starting in 2026, businesses that hire veterans with clean energy skills or credentials may receive a federal tax credit on those wages.
ai_sections.what: This bill adds a new category to an existing federal tax credit program that already rewards employers for hiring certain workers. Businesses would receive a tax credit on wages paid to qualifying veterans working in renewable energy jobs. The credit applies only to wages for renewable energy work, not any other duties the veteran performs.
ai_sections.who: Renewable energy businesses that hire qualifying veterans would see a reduction in their federal taxes. Veterans qualify if they held a military specialty in renewable energy, advanced manufacturing, welding, or engineering, or earned a vocational degree in renewable energy within the past year, or hold a LEED green building certification.
ai_sections.why: Because hiring a qualifying veteran reduces an employer's tax bill, veterans with these credentials may become more competitive candidates for renewable energy jobs. The bill also extends this same tax treatment to businesses operating in U.S. territories such as Puerto Rico and Guam.

title: A bill to amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to exclude from gross income charitable distributions from certain employer-sponsored retirement plans, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill would let people age 70½ or older donate money directly from certain workplace retirement accounts to charity without paying income tax on that money. Right now, a similar tax break already exists for Individual Retirement Accounts (IRAs), but it does not cover most employer-sponsored plans like 401(k)s, 403(b)s, 457(b)s, SEPs, and SIMPLE plans. This bill would extend the same benefit to those accounts. The tax-free donation would be capped at the same dollar limit that already applies to IRA charitable donations, which is $105,000 per year as of 2026, and the money must go directly from the retirement plan to a qualifying nonprofit organization.

The people most directly affected are retirees or near-retirees who are 70½ or older and still have money sitting in a workplace retirement plan rather than an IRA. If this passes, they could send retirement funds to a qualifying charity without that withdrawal counting as taxable income. Employers and plan administrators would also need to handle the mechanics of sending funds directly to charities, which is currently not a standard feature of most workplace plans.

Qualifying charities must be standard public nonprofits. Donor-advised funds and private foundations would not qualify, just as they are excluded from the existing IRA rule. The bill does not change anything for people under age 70½, people who already use the IRA charitable rollover, or people who want to withdraw retirement funds for non-charitable purposes.
ai_headline: Bill would expand 401(k) and 403(b) charitable rollover tax break to match IRA rules
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: People 70½+ could donate directly from workplace retirement accounts to charity, tax-free, up to $105,000 a year.
ai_sections.what: This bill would extend an existing tax break to workplace retirement accounts like 401(k)s, 403(b)s, 457(b)s, SEPs, and SIMPLE plans. Right now, that tax break only covers IRAs. Donations must go directly to a qualifying public nonprofit, and the limit is $105,000 per year as of 2026.
ai_sections.who: People age 70½ or older with money in a workplace retirement plan would be directly affected. Employers and plan administrators would also need to set up a way to send funds directly to charities, which most plans do not currently do.
ai_sections.why: Retirees who kept money in a workplace plan instead of an IRA would gain the same option that IRA holders already have. Donor-advised funds and private foundations would still not qualify, just as they are excluded under the current IRA rule.

title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to exclude from gross income charitable distributions from certain employer-sponsored retirement plans, and for other purposes.
ai_summary:
This bill would let people age 70½ or older donate money directly from certain workplace retirement accounts to charity without paying income tax on that withdrawal. Right now, a similar tax break already exists for Individual Retirement Accounts (IRAs), but it does not cover most employer-sponsored plans like 401(k)s, 403(b)s, 457(b)s, SEPs, and SIMPLE plans. This bill would extend the same benefit to those account types. The tax-free donation amount would be capped using the same limit already set for IRAs, which is currently $105,000 per year, and any amount donated from an IRA would count against that cap.

The people most directly affected are workers and retirees who are 70½ or older and still have money sitting in a workplace retirement plan rather than an IRA. This is common among people who worked for governments, schools, nonprofits, or large employers. Without this bill, if they want to give retirement money to charity, they first have to withdraw it, pay income taxes on it, and then donate it. This bill removes that tax step when the money goes straight from the retirement account to the charity.

Charities that receive these kinds of donations could also see an increase in contributions if the tax benefit encourages more giving. The bill does not apply to donations made to private foundations or donor-advised funds. It would take effect for the tax year beginning after the bill is signed into law.
ai_headline: Bill would extend IRA charitable rollover rules to 401(k) and similar plans
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: Workers 70½+ could donate directly from 401(k)s and similar plans to charity, tax-free, up to $105,000/year.
ai_sections.what: This bill would allow people age 70½ or older to make tax-free donations directly from workplace retirement accounts — such as 401(k)s, 403(b)s, 457(b)s, SEPs, and SIMPLE plans — to eligible charities. That option already exists for IRAs, and this bill would extend it to those other account types. The combined limit for tax-free charitable donations across all account types would be $105,000 per year.
ai_sections.who: People most directly affected are workers and retirees age 70½ or older who still hold money in a workplace retirement plan rather than an IRA, which is common among those who worked for governments, schools, nonprofits, or large employers. Charities that receive these donations could also be affected. The bill does not apply to private foundations or donor-advised funds.
ai_sections.why: Without this bill, someone who wants to donate retirement plan money to charity must first withdraw it, pay income taxes on that amount, and then donate what remains. This bill removes the tax step when money goes directly from the retirement account to the charity.

title: Small Business Investment Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill changes the tax rules for people who invest in small businesses by buying stock. Right now, if you hold "qualified small business stock" for more than 5 years, you may not have to pay taxes on the profit when you sell it. This bill would lower that waiting period to 3 years and create a sliding scale: hold the stock 3 years and exclude 50% of your gains from taxes, 4 years and exclude 75%, and 5 or more years and exclude 100%. It also lets investors who lend money to a small business through a convertible loan, which later turns into stock, count the time they held the loan toward that holding period. Additionally, the bill expands these tax breaks to cover S corporations, a common type of small business structure that was previously left out.

The people most directly affected are individual investors who put money into small startup companies and small businesses. This includes angel investors, venture capital participants, and everyday people who buy stock in qualifying small businesses. It also affects the small businesses themselves, particularly S corporations, since being included in these rules may make it easier for them to attract investors.

The broader idea is to make it more attractive for people to invest in small companies by reducing the tax they owe on profits. Whether you benefit depends on whether you own or invest in a qualifying small business and how long you hold your investment. The bill does not affect people who only invest in large, publicly traded companies through stock markets like the New York Stock Exchange.
ai_headline: Bill eases qualified small business stock tax rules for early investors
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1199 shortens the wait for small business stock tax breaks from 5 years to 3 and adds a sliding scale for how much profit is tax-free.
ai_sections.what: This bill changes the tax rules for people who hold stock in qualifying small businesses. Instead of waiting 5 years to exclude all gains from taxes, investors can now exclude 50% after 3 years, 75% after 4 years, and 100% after 5 years. The bill also lets investors count time spent holding a convertible loan before it became stock, and it extends these rules to S corporations for the first time.
ai_sections.who: Individual investors in small startups and small businesses are most directly affected, including angel investors and everyday people who buy qualifying small business stock. Small businesses themselves, especially S corporations, are also affected because the new rules may change how easy it is for them to attract investors.
ai_sections.why: Lowering the holding period and adding a sliding scale changes the tax cost of investing in small companies at different points in time. Including S corporations means a type of business that was previously left out now falls under these rules.

title: SAM Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill, called the SAM Act of 2026, creates a new federal grant program that would give public transit agencies money to buy and deploy self-driving buses. The program would have $100 million available over several years, with the federal government covering up to 80 percent of project costs. At least 15 percent of the money must go to projects in rural areas. To qualify, a transit agency could partner with a private company, like a bus manufacturer, to bring self-driving bus technology into service.

The bill also requires the government to set up a special testing facility specifically for self-driving buses, separate from the existing facility used to test regular buses. Before any self-driving bus can be purchased with federal money, it must pass safety and performance tests at one of these facilities. The bill also updates the rules so that federal grant money already available through several existing transportation programs can now be used to buy the software that runs these autonomous vehicles, not just the physical vehicles themselves.

This bill mainly affects local and regional public transit agencies, particularly those looking to modernize their bus fleets with self-driving technology. It would also impact companies that build autonomous vehicles and the software that powers them. Everyday riders in cities and rural communities could eventually see self-driving buses on their local routes if their transit agency applies for and receives one of these grants.
ai_headline: Federal SAM Act would fund self-driving bus fleets with $100M in grants
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: The SAM Act of 2026 creates a federal grant program to help public transit agencies buy and test self-driving buses.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new federal grant program for public transit agencies to purchase and deploy self-driving buses. It also requires a dedicated testing facility for self-driving buses and allows existing federal transportation grant money to be used for the software that runs these vehicles, not just the vehicles themselves.
ai_sections.who: Local and regional public transit agencies are the main groups this bill targets, especially those wanting to update their bus fleets. Companies that build autonomous vehicles and their software would also be affected, and riders in both cities and rural communities could eventually see self-driving buses on their routes.
ai_sections.why: Transit agencies that receive grants would be able to bring self-driving bus technology into service on local routes. Rural communities would have a dedicated share of funding available to them, since the bill sets aside a portion specifically for rural projects.
ai_sections.cost: The program makes $100 million available over several years. The federal government would cover up to 80 percent of project costs, with at least 15 percent of total funding required to go toward rural area projects.

title: Medium Transit Intensive Cities Authorization Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill changes how certain federal public transit funding is divided up among cities. Right now, a large chunk of federal transit money flows mainly to the biggest cities. This bill sets aside 1.5 percent of that funding pool specifically for mid-sized cities, meaning urbanized areas with populations between 200,000 and 999,999 people. To qualify for a share of this set-aside, a mid-sized city's transit system has to be performing at or above the level of big cities with over one million people in at least one of six measured categories, such as how many miles passengers travel, how often buses or trains run compared to the population, and how many riders use the system per person in the area.

The money is then divided among qualifying mid-sized cities based on how well their transit systems perform compared to those big-city averages. A city that outperforms large cities in more categories gets a bigger slice. The federal Department of Transportation would use data already collected in the National Transit Database to figure out which cities qualify and how much each one gets.

This bill primarily affects residents and transit agencies in mid-sized American cities, roughly places like Santa Barbara, California or Provo, Utah in size, that already run relatively strong public transit systems. It does not take money away from the biggest cities directly, since the 1.5 percent comes from a broader funding pool before other distributions are made. Riders in qualifying mid-sized cities could see more federal dollars flow to their local bus or rail systems.
ai_headline: Federal transit bill carves out 1.5% for mid-sized city systems
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: Mid-sized cities with strong transit systems could receive a dedicated 1.5% share of federal transit funding under this bill.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets aside 1.5 percent of a federal transit funding pool specifically for mid-sized cities, defined as urbanized areas with populations between 200,000 and 999,999 people. To qualify, a city's transit system must perform at or above big-city levels in at least one of six measured categories, such as passenger miles, service frequency per resident, or riders per capita. Qualifying cities receive a share based on how many of those categories their system outperforms large-city averages.
ai_sections.who: This bill primarily affects residents and transit agencies in mid-sized American cities that already run relatively strong public transit systems. The federal Department of Transportation would use data from the National Transit Database to determine which cities qualify and how much each receives.
ai_sections.why: Riders in qualifying mid-sized cities could see more federal dollars directed to their local bus or rail systems. The 1.5 percent set-aside is drawn from a broader funding pool before other distributions are made, so the largest cities are not directly reduced.
ai_sections.cost: The bill directs 1.5 percent of an existing federal transit funding pool to mid-sized cities, but the summary does not specify a total dollar amount for that pool.

title: Stop Scamming Truckers Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets a specific scam that has been hurting truckers and trucking companies. Right now, private businesses sometimes send letters or emails to truck drivers and carriers that look like they are coming from the federal government, specifically the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA). These fake-looking messages try to get truckers to pay money to register or renew their USDOT number, which is the identification number the government requires commercial truck operators to have. In reality, the government handles this registration itself, and these private companies are collecting fees for something truckers can do directly with the government for free or at a lower cost.

The bill would require any private company sending these kinds of messages to clearly state, in easy-to-read text, that they are not the government and have no government affiliation. The disclaimer must appear upfront, either on the first page of a letter or immediately visible in an email without scrolling. Private companies would also be banned from using government-looking names, seals, or logos, and from suggesting that paying them is required to keep a USDOT number active.

If a trucker or carrier receives a message that breaks these rules, they can sue the company in federal court. They could recover any money they paid, plus between $500 and $5,000 per violation, along with legal fees. The person suing does not have to prove they were actually fooled by the communication, just that the rules were broken. This bill primarily affects commercial truck drivers, small trucking businesses, owner-operators, and the private companies that currently send these types of communications.
ai_headline: House bill targets private firms mimicking FMCSA to charge truckers
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

ai_sections.tldr: Private companies must clearly say they are not the government when contacting truckers about USDOT number registration.
ai_sections.what: This bill sets rules for private companies that send letters or emails to truckers about registering or renewing their USDOT number. These companies must clearly state upfront that they are not the government and have no government connection. They also cannot use government-looking names, seals, or logos, or suggest that paying them is required to keep a USDOT number active.
ai_sections.who: This bill affects commercial truck drivers, owner-operators, and small trucking businesses who receive these messages. It also applies to private companies that currently send this type of communication.
ai_sections.why: Without these rules, truckers may pay private companies for a service they can complete directly with the government at little or no cost.
