Truckers who receive messages that break these rules can sue in federal court and recover money paid, plus between $500 and $5,000 per violation, along with legal fees.
ai_sections.cost: A trucker or carrier who wins a lawsuit can recover any money they paid to a private company, plus between $500 and $5,000 per violation and legal fees. The person suing does not need to prove they were fooled, only that the rules were broken.

title: At HOME Services Act
ai_summary:
This bill creates a two-year test program where hospitals can treat certain Medicare patients at home instead of keeping them in the hospital for observation. Right now, when doctors need to watch a patient closely but are not sure they need to be formally admitted, those patients stay in the hospital under what is called "observation status." This bill would let participating hospitals provide that same monitoring and care in the patient's own home, using a mix of in-person visits and telehealth. Hospitals that want to join must apply and meet safety standards set by the federal government.

The program builds on rules that were already used during the COVID-19 pandemic, when many hospitals got special permission to treat patients at home. It waives certain normal hospital requirements, like having a nurse on-site around the clock, as long as the hospital can show patients are getting the same quality of care they would receive inside a hospital. The government can remove any hospital from the program if it stops meeting the safety and quality standards.

The bill mainly affects Medicare patients, meaning people 65 and older or those with certain disabilities, who would otherwise be placed in hospital observation status. It also affects hospitals that choose to participate. After the two-year program ends, the Department of Health and Human Services must publish a public report comparing patient outcomes, costs, and care quality between home-based and traditional hospital observation. That report is meant to help policymakers decide whether to make the program permanent.
ai_headline: Medicare home observation pilot program set for two-year trial under HHS
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: A two-year test lets certain hospitals care for Medicare observation patients at home using in-person visits and telehealth instead of a hospital stay.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a two-year test program allowing participating hospitals to monitor and treat certain Medicare patients at home instead of keeping them in the hospital under observation status. Care would be delivered through a mix of in-person visits and telehealth. After the program ends, the Department of Health and Human Services must publish a public report comparing patient outcomes, costs, and care quality between the two approaches.
ai_sections.who: Medicare patients who would otherwise be placed in hospital observation status are the people this program would affect, meaning adults 65 and older or those with certain qualifying disabilities. Hospitals that choose to apply and meet federal safety standards may also participate.
ai_sections.why: The report required at the end of the program is meant to give policymakers information to decide whether to make home-based observation care permanent. Any hospital that stops meeting safety and quality standards can be removed from the program.

title: CIRCUIT Act
ai_summary:
This bill adds a new tax credit for companies that manufacture distribution transformers inside the United States. A distribution transformer is the equipment that takes high-voltage electricity from power lines and steps it down to the lower voltage used in homes and businesses. Under the bill, a manufacturer could claim a tax credit worth 10 percent of what it costs them to produce each qualifying transformer. The credit would kick in 90 days after the bill becomes law.

The bill does this by expanding an existing tax credit called the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit, which already rewards domestic production of items like solar panels and battery components. Distribution transformers would simply be added to that existing list.

This mainly affects companies in the United States that make distribution transformers. Indirectly, it could matter to electric utilities and consumers, since there is currently a significant backlog and shortage of these transformers in the country. By making domestic production more financially attractive, the bill aims to encourage more of these transformers to be built in the U.S. rather than imported. Taxpayers generally would not feel a direct effect, though federal tax revenue would be reduced somewhat by the new credit.
ai_headline: Bill adds transformer makers to Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 448 adds a 10% tax credit for U.S. companies that manufacture distribution transformers, starting 90 days after the bill becomes law.
ai_sections.what: This bill creates a new 10 percent tax credit for companies that manufacture distribution transformers inside the United States. Distribution transformers are the devices that reduce high-voltage electricity from power lines to the lower voltage used in homes and businesses. The credit is added to an existing program called the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit, which already covers items like solar panels and battery components.
ai_sections.who: This directly affects U.S. companies that manufacture distribution transformers. Electric utilities and consumers could be indirectly affected, since the country currently has a significant backlog and shortage of these transformers.
ai_sections.why: Making domestic production more financially attractive could lead to more transformers being built in the U.S. rather than imported. Federal tax revenue would be reduced somewhat as a result of the new credit.
ai_sections.cost: Federal tax revenue would be reduced somewhat by the new credit. Individual taxpayers would not feel a direct effect.
costChips: No direct effect on individual taxpayers

title: Concurrent Care for Comfort Act
ai_summary:
This bill deals with Medicare coverage for a specific group of patients: people with end-stage renal disease (kidney failure) who choose to enter hospice care. Right now, Medicare's rules make it difficult for these patients to receive dialysis and hospice care at the same time, because choosing hospice typically means giving up coverage for treatments related to the illness that qualifies them for hospice. This bill would officially allow Medicare to pay for "palliative dialysis" — dialysis used for comfort rather than to treat or cure the disease — while a patient is also receiving hospice care.

Under the bill, Medicare would cover up to 10 dialysis sessions for these patients while they are on hospice. The government would create a separate payment system for dialysis facilities that provide this comfort-focused care, so those facilities get paid outside of the normal hospice payment bundle. The dialysis can happen at a facility or at home. Starting in 2029, the government would review whether the 10-session limit is appropriate and could adjust it based on real-world data and feedback.

This bill mainly affects Medicare patients who have end-stage renal disease, were already on dialysis, and choose to enter hospice — typically people who are terminally ill and have decided to focus on comfort rather than continued treatment. It also affects the dialysis clinics and hospice providers who care for them, as well as Medicare administrators who would need to set up the new payment rules.
ai_headline: Medicare would cover palliative dialysis for hospice patients under new bill
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: Medicare would cover up to 10 comfort-focused dialysis sessions for kidney failure patients already in hospice care.
ai_sections.what: This bill would let Medicare pay for "palliative dialysis" — dialysis used for comfort, not to cure — while a patient is also in hospice care. Medicare would cover up to 10 of these sessions, which could take place at a facility or at home. Starting in 2029, the government would review whether that 10-session limit should be adjusted based on real-world data and feedback.
ai_sections.who: This affects Medicare patients who have end-stage renal disease, were already on dialysis, and have chosen to enter hospice care. It also affects dialysis clinics, hospice providers, and Medicare administrators who would set up the new payment rules.
ai_sections.why: Right now, Medicare rules generally require patients to give up coverage for treatments related to their illness when they choose hospice, which makes it hard to receive dialysis and hospice care at the same time. This bill would create a separate payment system for dialysis facilities providing comfort-focused care, so those facilities would be paid outside of the normal hospice payment bundle.
ai_sections.cost: Medicare would pay dialysis facilities through a new, separate payment system created specifically for this comfort-focused care, outside the standard hospice payment bundle.

title: Constance C. McDaniel Medically Necessary Infant Formula and Donor Milk Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a three-year test program run by the Department of Health and Human Services. The program would help certain families pay for infant formula or donated breast milk when breastfeeding is not possible for medical reasons. The government could provide help through vouchers, reimbursements, or grants to local organizations. Up to $15 million per year would be set aside for the program, and officials would have to report to Congress each year on how it is working.

The program is specifically designed for families who fall through the cracks of existing help. To qualify, a person cannot already be receiving benefits through WIC, the federal nutrition program for low-income mothers and young children. Beyond that, there must be a documented medical reason why breastfeeding is not an option. That could include a mother with a serious illness, a mental health condition, or a physical condition making breastfeeding impossible. It also covers situations like adoption, surrogacy, or the death of the mother. Babies themselves may qualify if they have feeding tubes, digestive problems, latching difficulties, or food allergies.

This bill would affect a relatively narrow group of families — those with genuine medical barriers to breastfeeding who do not already qualify for WIC assistance. Healthcare providers, pediatricians, and OB-GYNs would play a role by diagnosing and documenting the medical need. Donor milk banks that meet federal safety standards could also be involved in supplying milk through the program.
ai_headline: HHS pilot program would cover formula costs for families shut out of WIC
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9007 would create a 3-year federal program to help families pay for infant formula or donor breast milk when breastfeeding is medically impossible.
ai_sections.what: This bill would set up a three-year test program run by the Department of Health and Human Services. It would help eligible families cover the cost of infant formula or donated breast milk when breastfeeding is not medically possible. Help could come through vouchers, reimbursements, or grants to local organizations.
ai_sections.who: The program targets families who cannot get help through WIC and have a documented medical reason why breastfeeding is not an option. That includes mothers with serious illness, mental health conditions, or physical barriers, as well as families formed through adoption, surrogacy, or the death of a mother. Babies with feeding tubes, digestive problems, latching difficulties, or food allergies may also qualify.
ai_sections.why: Without this program, families with medical barriers to breastfeeding who do not qualify for WIC have no dedicated federal option for this kind of help. Healthcare providers would document medical need, and federally approved donor milk banks could supply milk through the program.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $15 million per year would be set aside for the program. Officials would be required to report to Congress each year on how the program is working.

title: Protecting America's Drinking Water from Extreme Temperatures Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes two main changes to an existing federal program that helps mid-sized and large drinking water systems stay reliable. First, it expands the list of threats that water utilities can use grant money to protect against. Right now, the program covers natural hazards and extreme weather events like floods. This bill adds "extreme temperatures," meaning both very hot and very cold conditions, as an approved reason to apply for funding. That could include things like insulating pipes against hard freezes or upgrading equipment that breaks down in extreme heat.

Second, the bill extends the program's funding authorization. The current version runs through 2026. This bill would push that window forward to cover 2027 through 2032, keeping the grant program active for another six years so water systems can continue applying for money.

The people most directly affected are the operators and managers of medium and large community water systems across the country, since they are the ones who apply for and use these grants. Indirectly, it affects the residents those systems serve, because stronger infrastructure means fewer disruptions to tap water during temperature extremes. It does not create a new program or guarantee new money; it simply broadens what existing grant dollars can be spent on and keeps the program running longer.
ai_headline: Federal drinking water resilience grants expanded to cover extreme temperatures
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill lets water utilities use existing federal grants to protect pipes and equipment from extreme heat and cold, and keeps the grant program running through 2032.
ai_sections.what: This bill expands an existing federal grant program so that medium and large drinking water systems can use the money to prepare for extreme temperatures, both hot and cold. It also extends the program from its current end date to cover the years 2027 through 2032. It does not create a new program or guarantee new funding.
ai_sections.who: Operators and managers of medium and large community water systems are the ones who apply for these grants. Residents served by those systems are also affected because the infrastructure those systems maintain delivers their tap water.
ai_sections.why: Adding extreme temperatures as an approved reason for funding means water systems can apply for money to do things like insulate pipes against hard freezes or fix equipment that fails in extreme heat. Extending the program through 2032 means water systems can keep applying for grants for six more years instead of the program expiring.

title: Women’s Health Protection Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal law protecting the right to get an abortion before a fetus is viable, meaning before it could survive outside the womb. It would block states from passing or enforcing rules that make abortions harder to get than other similar medical procedures, require unnecessary in-person visits or tests, ban telemedicine for abortion care, or restrict which doctors can provide abortions. After viability, the bill would still allow abortions when a doctor determines the pregnancy threatens the patient's life or health. The bill would also protect the right to travel to another state to get an abortion and to help someone else do so.

The bill would override state laws that conflict with it, meaning abortion restrictions currently in place in many states could be struck down if this bill became law. Both the federal government and private individuals or doctors could sue in federal court to block laws that violate the bill. If someone wins such a lawsuit, the other side would have to pay their legal fees.

This bill directly affects anyone who is or could become pregnant, as well as doctors, nurses, pharmacists, and clinics that provide abortion services. It also affects state governments that have passed abortion restrictions or bans. The bill was introduced in the House and has been referred to committee, meaning it has not yet been voted on or become law.
ai_headline: House bill would guarantee abortion access before fetal viability nationwide
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 12 would make abortion access a federal right, overriding state laws that restrict it, but has not yet been voted on.
ai_sections.what: This bill would create a federal right to abortion before a fetus can survive outside the womb. It would block states from enforcing rules that make abortion harder to get than other similar medical procedures, ban telemedicine for abortion, or limit which providers can perform them. After that point, abortion would still be allowed if a doctor determines the pregnancy threatens the patient's life or health.
ai_sections.who: This bill directly affects anyone who is or could become pregnant, as well as doctors, nurses, pharmacists, and clinics that provide abortion services. It also affects state governments that currently have abortion restrictions or bans in place.
ai_sections.why: If passed, this bill would override conflicting state laws, meaning many existing state abortion restrictions could be struck down. Both the federal government and private individuals or doctors could sue in federal court to enforce the law, and the losing side would pay the winner's legal fees.

title: AID Youth Employment Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create two new federal grant programs to help young people ages 14 to 24 find jobs. One program focuses on summer employment, and the other supports year-round work opportunities. The federal government would award competitive grants to states, local governments, tribal nations, and community-based organizations, which would then use the money to pay employers part of the cost of hiring eligible young people. Jobs must pay at least the minimum wage, and summer positions must last at least six weeks. The bill would authorize up to $375 million per year for summer jobs and $500 million per year for year-round jobs, running from 2026 through 2030.

To receive funding, organizations would need to build partnerships that include schools, employers, workforce agencies, and social service providers. Programs would be required to offer more than just a paycheck. They would also need to provide mentors, work-readiness training, and connections to education or future job opportunities. Special attention is given to young people facing serious challenges, such as those who are homeless, in foster care, involved in the justice system, or living in high-poverty communities. At least 20 percent of summer funds must go to rural areas, and at least 5 percent must go to tribal areas.

The bill affects young Americans between the ages of 14 and 24, whether they are currently in school, have dropped out, or are simply unemployed. Employers who participate would receive financial help to offset the cost of hiring these workers. The federal Department of Labor would oversee the programs, track results, and report to Congress regularly. Tribal nations would have some flexibility in how they apply and participate. The bill has been introduced in the Senate but has not yet been passed into law.
ai_headline: Senate bill would fund youth jobs through Labor Department grants
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (text: CR S1926-1930)

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 1211 would create federal grant programs to help Americans ages 14–24 find summer and year-round jobs starting in 2026.
ai_sections.what: This Senate bill would create two federal grant programs for youth employment: one for summer jobs and one for year-round jobs. Grants would go to states, local governments, tribal nations, and community organizations, which would use the money to help pay employers who hire eligible young people. Funded programs must also provide mentors, work-readiness training, and connections to education or future jobs. Jobs must pay at least minimum wage, and summer positions must last at least six weeks.
ai_sections.who: Young people ages 14 to 24 who are in school, have dropped out, or are unemployed could be served, with extra focus on those who are homeless, in foster care, involved in the justice system, or living in high-poverty or rural communities. Employers, schools, workforce agencies, and social service providers would also be involved through required partnerships.
ai_sections.why: If passed, the Department of Labor would oversee both programs, track outcomes, and report regularly to Congress. At least 20 percent of summer funds would be directed to rural areas and at least 5 percent to tribal areas.
ai_sections.cost: The bill would authorize up to $375 million per year for summer jobs and $500 million per year for year-round jobs, running from 2026 through 2030.

title: Equal Health Care for All Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make it illegal for health care providers to treat patients differently based on race, national origin, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, disability, age, or religion. If someone believes they received worse care because of one of these characteristics, they could file a complaint with a renamed federal office called the Office for Civil Rights and Health Equity. That office would investigate the complaint, try to reach an agreement between the patient and the provider, and if needed, refer the case to the Attorney General. Patients could also sue in court. Hospitals that repeatedly provide unequal care could be kicked out of Medicare and Medicaid, though not if doing so would reduce access to care in low-income areas.

The bill also requires hospitals to start being measured and financially scored on how equally they treat patients across different groups, starting in 2026. Health care providers and facilities would have to report their patient outcome data broken down by race, age, sex, and other characteristics, and that information would be stored in a government database without personal identifying details. A new Federal Health Equity Commission, with members from both political parties, would monitor progress, hold hearings, and report to Congress and the President each year on how well the country is doing at closing health care gaps.

The bill affects a wide range of people and institutions. Patients from minority, low-income, elderly, or other historically underserved groups would gain new legal tools to challenge unequal treatment. Hospitals and doctors that accept Medicare or Medicaid would face new reporting requirements and potential penalties. Hospitals serving large numbers of low-income patients would be first in line for new federal grants to fund things like bias training, translation services, and efforts to hire more diverse medical staff.
ai_headline: New federal bill bans unequal health care and creates equity scoring for hospitals
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions. (Sponsor introductory remarks on measure: CR S4458)

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would ban unequal health care treatment and let patients file complaints or sue if they believe they were treated worse due to who they are.
ai_sections.what: This bill would make it illegal for health care providers to treat patients differently based on race, sex, age, disability, religion, sexual orientation, gender identity, or national origin. Patients could file complaints with a renamed federal office, the Office for Civil Rights and Health Equity, or take their case to court. Starting in 2026, hospitals would be measured and financially scored on how equally they treat different groups of patients.
ai_sections.who: Patients from minority, low-income, elderly, or other historically underserved groups would gain new legal tools to challenge unequal treatment. Hospitals and doctors that accept Medicare or Medicaid would face new reporting requirements and possible penalties, while hospitals serving large numbers of low-income patients would be first in line for federal grants.
ai_sections.why: Hospitals that repeatedly provide unequal care could be removed from Medicare and Medicaid, unless doing so would reduce access to care in low-income areas. A new Federal Health Equity Commission would track progress and report to Congress and the President each year on how well health care gaps are being closed.
ai_sections.cost: The bill mentions federal grants available to hospitals that serve large numbers of low-income patients, which could be used for things like bias training, translation services, and hiring more diverse medical staff. No specific dollar amounts are given.

title: End the Vaccine Carveout Act
ai_summary:
This bill would make two major changes to how vaccine makers can be sued. Right now, under a law passed in 1986, people who are injured by vaccines generally cannot sue the manufacturer directly in regular court. Instead, they must first go through a special government program called the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program (VICP), which uses a no-fault system to pay claims. This bill would remove that requirement, allowing people to take vaccine makers and administrators directly to court just like they would sue any other company for a defective product. It also removes filing deadlines, meaning people could file injury claims at any time, even for injuries from the past.

The bill also separately removes COVID-19 vaccines from a different legal protection called the PREP Act, which currently shields manufacturers of emergency public health products from most lawsuits. Under this bill, COVID-19 vaccines would no longer be covered by that shield, meaning vaccine makers could face broader legal liability for those products specifically. People could still use the VICP compensation program OR file a lawsuit, but not both for the same injury.

The people most directly affected would include vaccine manufacturers, who could face a much larger volume of lawsuits and financial risk. People who believe they were harmed by a vaccine would gain new legal options. Doctors and pharmacists who give vaccines could also face more legal exposure. Some experts argue that increased liability could affect vaccine availability or pricing, while others argue it gives injured people more options for justice. The bill was introduced in July 2025 and has been referred to committee, meaning it has not become law.
ai_headline: Bill would strip VICP shield and let vaccine injury suits go to court
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

ai_sections.tldr: This bill would let people sue vaccine makers directly in regular court, removing the current requirement to use a special federal program first.
ai_sections.what: This bill would remove the rule that requires people to go through the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program before suing a vaccine maker. It would also remove filing deadlines, so people could sue over past injuries at any time. COVID-19 vaccines would lose a separate legal protection called the PREP Act shield, opening those makers to broader lawsuits as well.
ai_sections.who: Vaccine manufacturers, doctors, and pharmacists who give vaccines could all face more lawsuits as a result. People who believe they were harmed by a vaccine would have new options to take their case to regular court.
ai_sections.why: Removing these protections could lead to a much larger number of lawsuits against vaccine makers and providers. Some experts say this could affect how available or how expensive vaccines are, though others say it gives injured people more paths to seek justice.

title: Ensuring Safe and Toxic-Free Foods Act of 2025
ai_summary:
This bill would change how the FDA handles food additives and chemicals that are labeled "generally recognized as safe," or GRAS. Right now, food companies can declare their own ingredients safe without FDA approval, and that determination stays in place indefinitely. Under this bill, companies that want to use a new food chemical must submit detailed safety information to the FDA, including evidence that the substance does not cause cancer, does not harm reproduction or development, and has no significant toxic effects. The FDA would then review that information publicly, allow 60 days for public comment, and officially approve or reject the safety claim. Substances found to cause cancer or reproductive harm could no longer qualify as GRAS, and no new chemicals that were not already on the market before the bill's passage could automatically receive that status.

The bill also requires the FDA to go back and review chemicals that were previously approved. The agency would need to reassess at least 10 substances or groups of substances every three years, starting within three years of the law taking effect. The FDA could also require food manufacturers to provide data or run new safety tests to support those reviews. Experts hired by companies to vouch for ingredient safety would have to disclose and be free of financial conflicts of interest.

This bill would affect food and beverage manufacturers that use chemical additives, as well as the FDA, which would take on significantly more review work. Everyday consumers could also be affected, since ingredients currently in packaged foods might face new scrutiny or removal if they do not meet the updated safety standards. The bill takes effect two years after it becomes law.
ai_headline: Senate bill would end self-approval of food chemical safety
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 2341 requires FDA review and public comment before any food chemical can be labeled safe, ending company self-certification.
ai_sections.what: S 2341 would require food companies to submit detailed safety evidence to the FDA before any ingredient can be designated generally recognized as safe, or GRAS. The FDA would publicly review that information and open a 60-day public comment period before approving or rejecting the safety claim. Substances linked to cancer or reproductive harm could no longer qualify as GRAS, and new chemicals not already on the market before the bill passes could not receive that status automatically.
ai_sections.who: Food and beverage manufacturers that use chemical additives would face new submission and testing requirements under this bill. The FDA would take on significantly more review work, and everyday consumers could be affected if ingredients currently in packaged foods are removed or reformulated under the updated standards.
ai_sections.why: The bill would eliminate the existing system in which companies declare their own ingredients safe without FDA involvement or a defined expiration on that determination. The FDA would also be required to reassess at least 10 previously approved substances or groups of substances every three years, beginning within three years of the law taking effect.

title: Preventing the Abuse of Immigration Parole Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes the rules for "immigration parole," which is a legal tool that lets the government temporarily allow certain foreigners into the United States while their immigration cases are being decided. Right now, the law says parole should be granted case-by-case for urgent humanitarian reasons or significant public benefit, but the bill's supporters say that rule has not been followed strictly enough. The bill rewrites that section of immigration law to make the case-by-case requirement more explicit and adds a hard cap of no more than 3,000 people granted parole per year starting in 2029. It also blocks parole for people from countries the State Department has labeled "countries of concern" unless the Secretary of State personally approves an exception.

The bill also creates a new legal tool for state governments. State attorneys general could sue the federal government in federal court if they believe the parole rules are being broken in a way that harms their state or residents. The financial harm threshold to bring a lawsuit is set very low, at just over $100, meaning states would not need to prove major economic damage to have the right to sue.

This bill would most directly affect people outside the United States who are waiting to enter and might otherwise have qualified for temporary parole, as well as the federal agencies that manage immigration. It would also affect people from countries on the State Department's "countries of concern" list, who would face an additional barrier. State governments would gain new legal power to challenge federal immigration decisions in court.
ai_headline: Bill would cap immigration parole at 3,000 people a year starting 2029
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 3725 caps immigration parole at 3,000 per year from 2029 and lets state AGs sue the federal government over parole decisions.
ai_sections.what: HR 3725 rewrites the immigration parole rules to make the case-by-case requirement more explicit and sets a hard annual cap of 3,000 parole grants starting in 2029. It blocks parole for people from countries the State Department labels "countries of concern" unless the Secretary of State personally approves an exception. It also creates a new right for state attorneys general to sue the federal government in federal court if they believe parole rules are being broken.
ai_sections.who: The bill most directly affects people outside the United States waiting to enter who might otherwise have qualified for temporary parole, including those from State Department-designated countries of concern. Federal immigration agencies and state governments are also affected, with states gaining new legal standing to challenge federal parole decisions.
ai_sections.why: People who would have qualified for parole under current rules may be denied entry or face longer waits once the annual cap is reached or if they are from a country of concern. The low lawsuit threshold of just over $100 in financial harm means states can more easily challenge federal immigration decisions in court, which could increase litigation against federal agencies.

title: Veterans Border Patrol Training Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a new pilot program giving military service members who are leaving the armed forces a pathway to become Border Patrol agents. It works through an existing Defense Department program called SkillBridge, which already lets troops near the end of their service spend time training for civilian careers while still receiving their military pay and benefits. Under this bill, the Department of Homeland Security would partner with the Defense Department and the VA to offer Border Patrol training as one of those SkillBridge options. The program must be up and running within 180 days of the law passing, and it would automatically shut down after five years unless Congress acts to continue it.

The people most directly affected are active-duty service members who are in the process of transitioning out of the military. They would have a chance to train for a federal law enforcement job before their service officially ends. The bill also tracks participation by reservists, officers, enlisted members, veterans, and even military spouses and dependents, suggesting those groups could be involved in some capacity as well, though the core focus is on transitioning troops.

The broader effects would be felt by U.S. Customs and Border Protection, which is responsible for staffing the Border Patrol. The agency has faced ongoing hiring challenges, and this program is designed to bring in candidates who already have relevant skills from military service. Congress would receive yearly reports on how many people applied, enrolled, and completed the program, giving lawmakers data to decide whether the approach is working before the five-year window closes.
ai_headline: Military veterans would get a fast track to Border Patrol jobs under HR 4160
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Opportunity.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4160 lets troops train for Border Patrol jobs before leaving the military, using an existing Defense Dept program called SkillBridge.
ai_sections.what: HR 4160 would create a five-year pilot program allowing active-duty service members to train for Border Patrol positions before their military service ends. It operates through SkillBridge, an existing Defense Department program that lets transitioning troops pursue civilian career training while still receiving military pay and benefits. The Department of Homeland Security would partner with the Defense Department and the VA to make Border Patrol training available as a SkillBridge option, with the program required to launch within 180 days of the law passing.
ai_sections.who: The primary people affected are active-duty service members who are transitioning out of the military. The bill also tracks participation by reservists, officers, enlisted members, veterans, and military spouses and dependents, suggesting those groups may be involved in some capacity.
ai_sections.why: U.S. Customs and Border Protection, which staffs the Border Patrol, has faced ongoing hiring challenges, and this program is intended to bring in candidates with relevant skills from military service. Congress would receive yearly reports on applications, enrollments, and completions, giving lawmakers data to evaluate the program before the five-year window closes automatically.

title: Flight 293 Remembrance Act
ai_summary:
This bill requires the Department of Defense to build a public database listing the names, ranks, and service details of military members who died in non-combat plane crashes, meaning accidents that happened during regular military operations rather than in battle. The review would cover crashes going back to 1984 using Defense Department records, and even earlier using outside sources. The database must be completed within one year of the bill becoming law.

The bill also requires the government to actively help the families of service members who died in these crashes. This includes making sure families know what benefits and support they are entitled to, assigning a specific government contact person to walk each family through the process, and connecting them with peer support networks so they can share experiences with others in similar situations. The goal is to make it easier for these families to get financial help, counseling, and survivor benefits that they may not have been aware of or had trouble accessing.

The families most directly affected are those who lost a military loved one in a non-combat accident involving a military aircraft, which can include training flights, transport missions, or other routine operations. Two years after passage, the Defense Department would have to report back to Congress on how many families were helped and how well the program is working. The bill also includes standard civil rights protections to ensure no family is treated differently based on race, sex, disability, or national origin when receiving these services.
ai_headline: Defense bill would name every service member lost in non-combat air crashes since 1984
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 1030 would create a public DoD database of non-combat military air crash deaths and assign caseworkers to surviving families.
ai_sections.what: HR 1030 directs the Department of Defense to build a publicly accessible database listing the names, ranks, and service details of military members killed in non-combat aircraft accidents. Records would reach back to 1984 using DoD sources, and further using outside sources. The database must be completed within one year of enactment.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects families of service members killed in non-combat military aircraft accidents, including training flights, transport missions, and other routine operations. The Department of Defense is responsible for building the database, staffing family support, and reporting results to Congress.
ai_sections.why: Families may have had difficulty accessing survivor benefits, counseling, and financial assistance they were entitled to receive. Two years after passage, the Defense Department must report to Congress on how many families were helped and how well the program functioned.

title: End Solitary Confinement Act
ai_summary:
This bill would ban the use of solitary confinement in all federal prisons, immigration detention centers, and any other facilities that hold people on behalf of the federal government. Under the bill, people in these facilities must have at least 14 hours per day outside their cells, including time for group programs, recreation, meals, and visits. Isolation would only be allowed in very limited situations, such as overnight, during brief emergencies, or during a serious facility-wide lockdown, and even then strict time limits apply. Certain groups could never be isolated against their will, including people under 25 or over 55, people with disabilities or mental health conditions, pregnant people, and LGBTQ individuals.

The bill also sets up a watchdog group made up of community members, including people who have personally experienced solitary confinement, to conduct surprise visits to federal facilities and make sure the rules are being followed. Federal agencies would have to publicly report data every three months about who is being isolated and for how long. People who are harmed by violations of the law could sue in federal court and could receive money for their suffering, even without a physical injury.

States and local governments that receive federal law enforcement grants would also feel the effects of this bill. They would need to show that their own prisons and jails follow similar rules or risk losing at least 10 percent of that federal funding. In short, this bill primarily affects people held in any federally run or federally contracted detention facility, the staff who work there, and state and local governments that depend on federal criminal justice grants.
ai_headline: Federal bill would ban solitary confinement and require 14 daily hours out of cell
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 4682 would ban solitary confinement in all federal detention facilities and require at least 14 out-of-cell hours daily.
ai_sections.what: HR 4682 would ban solitary confinement in all federal prisons, immigration detention centers, and any facilities holding people on behalf of the federal government. People held in these facilities would be required to have at least 14 hours per day outside their cells for programs, recreation, meals, and visits. Isolation would still be permitted in very limited situations such as overnight, brief emergencies, or facility-wide lockdowns, with strict time limits in each case.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects people held in federally run or federally contracted detention facilities, the staff who work there, and state and local governments that receive federal criminal justice grants. Certain groups could never be isolated against their will, including people under 25 or over 55, people with disabilities or mental health conditions, pregnant people, and LGBTQ individuals.
ai_sections.why: States and local governments that cannot show their facilities follow similar rules would risk losing at least 10 percent of their federal law enforcement grant funding. People harmed by violations could sue in federal court and receive compensation for suffering even without a physical injury, and federal agencies would be required to publicly report isolation data every three months.
ai_sections.cost: States and local governments that receive federal law enforcement grants risk losing at least 10 percent of that funding if their facilities do not meet the bill's standards.
costChips: States risk losing 10%+ of federal grants
Funding loss tied to solitary confinement rules
Local jails and prisons subject to review

title: A bill to amend chapters 83 and 84 of title 5, United States Code, to authorize an increase of the retirement age for members of the Capitol Police.
ai_summary:
This law changes the retirement age rules for members of the United States Capitol Police, which is the law enforcement agency that protects the U.S. Capitol building and the members of Congress who work there. Before this law, Capitol Police officers were required to retire when they turned 60 years old. Now, the Capitol Police Board has the flexibility to set the mandatory retirement age anywhere between 57 and 62, depending on what the Board decides is appropriate.

The Capitol Police Board is the governing body that oversees the Capitol Police force. This law gives that Board the power to adjust the retirement age within that range, rather than having a fixed age set in federal law. The change applies to officers covered under both of the main federal retirement systems, so it covers essentially all Capitol Police members regardless of when they were hired.

This law directly affects Capitol Police officers and the agency that manages them. It does not apply to other federal law enforcement agencies or to the general public. For officers themselves, it could mean being required to retire a little earlier or a little later than the previous fixed age of 60, depending on what the Board ultimately decides.
ai_headline: Capitol Police mandatory retirement age made flexible under new law
last_action_text: Became Public Law No: 119-95.

ai_sections.tldr: The Capitol Police Board can now set officer mandatory retirement age anywhere from 57 to 62, replacing the previous fixed age of 60.
ai_sections.what: This law replaces the fixed mandatory retirement age of 60 for U.S. Capitol Police officers with a flexible range of 57 to 62. The Capitol Police Board now has authority to set the specific retirement age within that range. The change applies under both main federal retirement systems, covering essentially all Capitol Police members.
ai_sections.who: This law directly affects U.S. Capitol Police officers and the Capitol Police Board, which oversees the force. It does not apply to other federal law enforcement agencies or to the general public.
ai_sections.why: Officers may now be required to retire up to three years earlier or two years later than the previous fixed age of 60, depending on what the Board decides. The mandatory retirement age for Capitol Police is no longer set by federal law but is instead subject to the Board's discretion within the defined range.

title: American Families Gas Tax Relief Act
ai_summary:
This bill would temporarily eliminate the federal gas tax for 120 days after it becomes law. Right now, the federal government charges about 18 cents per gallon on gasoline and about 24 cents per gallon on diesel. Under this bill, those taxes would drop to zero for that period. The president could extend the pause by another 90 days if he decides economic conditions call for it.

To make sure road construction and environmental cleanup programs don't lose funding, the bill requires the U.S. Treasury to replace the lost tax money out of its general budget. That way, the Highway Trust Fund, which pays for roads and bridges, and the Leaking Underground Storage Tank Trust Fund, which pays for cleaning up fuel spills, would still receive the same amount of money they normally get from the gas tax.

The bill also includes a policy statement saying that fuel companies and retailers should pass the savings directly to customers at the pump, not keep the extra money as profit. It gives the Treasury Department authority to enforce that. This bill would most directly affect everyday drivers, truckers, and anyone who buys gasoline, diesel, or kerosene during the holiday period. It would also affect fuel producers, distributors, and retailers, who would be expected to lower their prices accordingly.
ai_headline: Federal gas tax suspended for 120 days under new House bill
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8795 would zero out the federal gas tax for 120 days, saving drivers about 18 cents per gallon at the pump.
ai_sections.what: HR 8795 would temporarily reduce the federal gas tax from about 18 cents per gallon on gasoline and about 24 cents per gallon on diesel to zero for 120 days after enactment. The president could extend the pause for an additional 90 days based on economic conditions. Fuel companies and retailers would be required to pass the savings to customers rather than retain them as profit, with the Treasury Department given authority to enforce that requirement.
ai_sections.who: The bill would most directly affect everyday drivers, truckers, and anyone who purchases gasoline, diesel, or kerosene during the suspension period. Fuel producers, distributors, and retailers would also be affected, as they would be expected to lower prices accordingly.
ai_sections.why: Removing the tax would reduce per-gallon costs for consumers during the suspension window, though the policy statement and enforcement mechanism would determine whether those savings actually reach the pump. The Highway Trust Fund and the Leaking Underground Storage Tank Trust Fund would continue receiving their normal funding levels through Treasury transfers rather than tax receipts.
ai_sections.cost: The U.S. Treasury would replace all lost gas tax revenue from its general budget for the duration of the suspension, ensuring the Highway Trust Fund and the Leaking Underground Storage Tank Trust Fund receive the same amounts they normally get from the gas tax.
costChips: Treasury backfills all lost tax revenue
Leaking Underground Storage Tank Fund kept whole

title: Protecting America’s Small Oil and Gas Producers and Rural Jobs Act
ai_summary:
This bill changes the tax rules for small oil and gas producers, specifically around something called "percentage depletion." Percentage depletion is a tax deduction that lets oil and gas companies write off a portion of their income to account for the gradual using up of an oil or gas well. Right now, the deduction rate for certain low-producing "marginal" wells can go up to 25 percent, and this bill keeps that cap but changes how the rate is calculated based on oil prices. When crude oil prices are lower, producers would get a bigger deduction. It also doubles the daily barrel limit that qualifies for this special tax treatment, going from 1,000 to 2,000 barrels per day. On top of that, the bill removes a rule that currently limits how much of this deduction a company can take based on how much taxable income they earned.

The people most directly affected are small, independent oil and gas producers who operate marginal wells. A marginal well is one that doesn't produce very much oil or gas, often found in rural areas. These wells can be expensive to keep running, especially when oil prices drop, and the current tax rules sometimes make it hard for small operators to stay profitable.

If the bill becomes law, these smaller producers would generally pay less in federal taxes, particularly during periods of low oil prices. That could make it easier for them to keep older, low-output wells running rather than shutting them down. Rural communities near those wells could also be affected, since keeping wells open supports local jobs and tax revenue. The changes would take effect starting with the 2027 tax year.
ai_headline: Tax deduction limit doubled for small oil and gas well operators
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8034 expands percentage depletion tax deductions for marginal well operators, doubling the barrel cap to 2,000/day starting in 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 8034 changes how percentage depletion tax deductions are calculated for small oil and gas producers, linking higher deduction rates to lower crude oil prices while keeping the existing 25 percent cap. The bill doubles the daily production limit eligible for this deduction from 1,000 to 2,000 barrels per day. It also removes the current rule that restricts the deduction based on a company's taxable income.
ai_sections.who: The bill directly affects small, independent oil and gas producers who operate marginal wells, which are low-output wells often located in rural areas. Rural communities near those wells may also be affected, as well operations support local jobs and tax revenue.
ai_sections.why: If enacted, qualifying producers would pay less in federal taxes, particularly when oil prices are low, which could reduce the financial pressure to shut down older, low-output wells. The changes would take effect starting with the 2027 tax year.

title: Norma Ruth Criswell Carpenter & Clovis C. Criswell Grant Parish Restoration Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill makes two changes to an existing federal tax break called the New Markets Tax Credit (NMTC). The NMTC is a program that encourages private investors to put money into businesses and projects in low-income communities by giving those investors a credit on their federal taxes. To qualify for this credit, a neighborhood or census tract generally has to meet certain poverty or income thresholds. This bill adjusts the rules for who qualifies under specific circumstances.

The first change deals with rural counties that have lost a lot of residents over time. Currently, the poverty rate in an area is calculated using all residents, including people living in prisons, nursing homes, or other group facilities. This bill would exclude those institutionalized residents from that calculation. Since prisons and similar facilities can artificially inflate an area's poverty numbers, removing them from the count could change whether a rural area qualifies for the tax credit.

The second change helps communities located in counties where the federal government owns at least 30 percent of the land, such as areas near national forests or parks. Because large chunks of federal land are uninhabited, they can make a county's overall population density look very low, which can affect how poverty rates are measured in census tracts within that county. This bill lowers the poverty rate threshold needed to qualify for the tax credit in those areas, from 20 percent to 15 percent. Land used for military bases or held in trust for Native American tribes does not count toward that 30 percent federal ownership threshold. People most directly affected include investors, businesses, and residents in qualifying rural and federally land-heavy communities.
ai_headline: Senate bill rewrites rural poverty rules for federal tax credit
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4584 changes who qualifies for the New Markets Tax Credit in rural and federally land-heavy counties.
ai_sections.what: S 4584 modifies the New Markets Tax Credit, a federal program that gives investors tax credits for funding businesses in low-income communities. The bill excludes institutionalized residents such as prisoners and nursing home occupants from poverty rate calculations in qualifying rural counties. It also lowers the poverty rate threshold from 20 percent to 15 percent for communities in counties where the federal government owns at least 30 percent of the land.
ai_sections.who: The changes directly affect investors, businesses, and residents in rural counties with population loss and in counties with heavy federal land ownership. Land held for military bases or in trust for Native American tribes does not count toward the 30 percent federal ownership threshold.
ai_sections.why: Excluding institutionalized residents from poverty counts could shift which rural areas meet the eligibility threshold for the tax credit. Lowering the poverty threshold for federally land-heavy counties could expand the number of census tracts that qualify under the program.

title: Tax the Grift Act
ai_summary:
This bill targets a very specific situation: if the President of the United States sues the IRS and wins money from a settlement, anyone who receives a payment from that settlement fund would be taxed at 100 percent on that money. In other words, the government would take back every dollar paid out from such a settlement. The bill also makes clear that those payments cannot be deducted from regular income taxes, so there is no way to reduce or offset the tax.

The bill affects anyone who would receive money from a settlement fund created because the President filed a civil lawsuit against the IRS. In practice, this is a narrow and unusual situation, since it would require the sitting President to personally sue the tax agency. The bill does not affect ordinary taxpayers, normal IRS disputes, or typical lawsuit settlements.

This legislation was introduced in May 2026 and has been sent to the House Ways and Means Committee, which handles tax policy. It has not become law. Because it applies only to this one very specific type of settlement, the number of people directly affected would depend entirely on whether such a lawsuit ever occurs and results in a settlement fund.
ai_headline: Bill would tax at 100% any payout from a presidential IRS lawsuit settlement
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 9075 would impose a 100% tax on any settlement money paid out if the sitting U.S. President sues the IRS and wins.
ai_sections.what: HR 9075 would impose a 100 percent tax on any money paid from a settlement fund created because the President of the United States filed a civil lawsuit against the IRS. Recipients of such payments could not deduct them from regular income taxes, leaving no way to offset the tax. The bill applies only to this one specific type of settlement and does not affect ordinary taxpayers or typical IRS disputes.
ai_sections.who: The bill would affect anyone who receives a payment from a settlement fund arising from a presidential civil lawsuit against the IRS. In practice, the number of people affected depends entirely on whether such a lawsuit ever occurs and produces a settlement.
ai_sections.why: Because the 100 percent tax rate would recapture every dollar paid out from such a settlement, no recipient would retain any of the money received. The deduction prohibition closes any additional avenue that might otherwise reduce the tax burden on those payments.
ai_sections.cost: Recipients of covered settlement payments would face a 100 percent tax rate on those amounts, with no deduction permitted to offset that liability.
costChips: No deductions allowed on those payments

title: Recognizing the critical importance of the United States Special Operations Forces community and expressing support for the designation of SOF Week.
ai_summary:
This resolution is a formal statement from Congress recognizing the importance of the United States Special Operations Forces, which includes well-known units like the Army Special Forces (Green Berets), Navy SEALs, Army Rangers, and several other elite military groups. The resolution does not create any new laws, spend any money, or require anyone to do anything. It is simply Congress going on record to say it values these military units and the work they do.

The resolution also expresses support for "SOF Week," an annual conference held in May that brings together special operations military professionals from the United States and other countries. The 2025 event took place May 5 through 8. Supporting SOF Week in this resolution means Congress is publicly acknowledging the event, not funding or organizing it.

The people most directly mentioned are active and former special operations military personnel and their families. The resolution specifically notes that family members face real challenges due to frequent deployments and dangerous missions. Beyond that, this resolution has no direct effect on everyday Americans since it carries no legal weight and changes no existing law or policy.
ai_headline: House resolution honors special operations forces and backs annual SOF Week event
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

ai_sections.tldr: Congress formally recognizes US Special Operations Forces and SOF Week; the resolution carries no legal weight and changes no law.
ai_sections.what: This congressional resolution formally recognizes the importance of United States Special Operations Forces, including Army Special Forces, Navy SEALs, and Army Rangers. It also expresses public support for SOF Week, an annual conference held May 5 through 8, 2025, that brings together special operations professionals from the US and other countries. The resolution does not create law, spend money, or require any action.
ai_sections.who: Active and former special operations military personnel and their families are the people directly mentioned. Family members are specifically noted as facing challenges from frequent deployments and dangerous missions.
ai_sections.why: Because the resolution carries no legal weight and changes no existing law or policy, it has no direct effect on everyday Americans. Its effect is limited to placing Congress on record in support of these military units and the SOF Week conference.

title: Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act, 2027
ai_summary:
This bill sets the federal budget for the Department of Commerce, the Department of Justice, NASA, and several other agencies for the fiscal year that ends September 30, 2027. It decides how much money each agency gets and sets rules for how that money can be spent. Some of the biggest funding amounts include about $11.4 billion for the FBI, $8.2 billion for the federal prison system, nearly $5 billion for the Patent and Trademark Office, and about $4.7 billion for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, which runs weather forecasting and fisheries programs. It also funds the Census Bureau, the Drug Enforcement Administration, the Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco Firearms and Explosives, and programs that help victims of domestic violence and human trafficking.

The bill touches the lives of most Americans in some way. Anyone who has applied for a patent, checked a weather forecast, bought imported goods, or used the internet has benefited from agencies this bill funds. People in the criminal justice system are directly affected, since the bill funds federal courts, prosecutors, prisons, and the US Marshals. Fishermen, coastal communities, and people in economically struggling areas are affected through NOAA and Economic Development Administration funding. The bill also includes a requirement that the National Weather Service maintain enough staff to protect lives and property, and it blocks federal science funding from going to research done on supercomputers in China.

A few specific policy choices stand out. The bill requires every US Attorney's office to run or join a human trafficking task force. It limits how much of the gun-related budget the ATF can spend unless the agency is processing firearms applications within set time limits. It requires that federal prison programs created by the First Step Act of 2018, which focused on reducing recidivism, receive at least about $409 million. It also says the National Technical Information Service cannot charge people for copies of government reports if those reports are available for free online.
ai_headline: Federal spending bill sets agency budgets through September 2027
last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 567.

ai_sections.tldr: HR 8845 allocates billions to the FBI, federal prisons, NOAA, and other agencies for fiscal year ending September 30, 2027.
ai_sections.what: HR 8845 sets the federal budget for the Department of Commerce, the Department of Justice, NASA, and several other agencies through the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027. It determines funding levels for each agency and establishes rules on how that money may be spent. The bill also includes specific policy requirements, such as mandatory human trafficking task forces in every US Attorney's office and spending conditions tied to ATF processing times for firearms applications.
ai_sections.who: The bill affects most Americans in some way, including people who use weather forecasts, apply for patents, buy imported goods, or interact with the federal criminal justice system. Fishermen, coastal communities, domestic violence survivors, human trafficking victims, and people in federal prison are among those more directly affected.
ai_sections.why: Without a funding bill, these agencies would lack authorized budgets to operate through fiscal year 2027. The specific policy provisions attach conditions to how agencies must function, which shapes outcomes in areas ranging from public safety to scientific research.
ai_sections.cost: The bill allocates about $11.4 billion for the FBI, $8.2 billion for the federal prison system, nearly $5 billion for the Patent and Trademark Office, and about $4.7 billion for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. First Step Act recidivism-reduction programs must receive at least about $409 million.

title: SMART Community Policing Act
ai_summary:
This bill would add new options for how federal COPS (Community Oriented Policing Services) grant money can be spent. Right now, local police departments can apply for these federal funds to pay for things like hiring officers and training. This bill would let departments also use that money to create three new types of programs: mobile crisis teams (where mental health professionals and paramedics respond to certain 911 calls alongside or instead of police), co-responder programs (where a police officer and a behavioral health clinician are paired together to handle mental health situations), and case management teams (where staff follow up with people after a crisis to help them access ongoing services and stick to treatment plans).

The bill is focused on situations where someone calls 911 not because of a crime, but because they are dealing with a mental health crisis, homelessness, substance use, or poverty. The idea is that having trained mental health professionals involved in those calls could lead to calmer outcomes and connect people to help rather than just leaving it to police alone.

People most directly affected would include residents who experience mental health crises and may interact with law enforcement, local police departments that choose to apply for these grants, and mental health and medical professionals who might be hired under these new programs. The bill does not require any department to create these programs. It only allows them to use existing federal grant money for this purpose if they choose to.
ai_headline: Federal police grants could fund mental health crisis response teams
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4575 would let local police use existing federal COPS grants to fund mental health co-responder and crisis team programs.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 4575 would expand how federal COPS grant money can be spent by local police departments. The bill adds three new eligible uses: mobile crisis teams, co-responder programs pairing officers with behavioral health clinicians, and case management teams that follow up with people after a crisis. No department is required to create these programs; participation is voluntary.
ai_sections.who: Local police departments that choose to apply for COPS grants would be directly affected, as would mental health professionals, paramedics, and case managers who could be hired under the new programs. Residents who experience mental health crises, homelessness, substance use difficulties, or poverty and come into contact with 911 systems are also affected.
ai_sections.why: The bill targets 911 calls driven by mental health or social crises rather than crime, where trained non-police responders may produce different outcomes. Adding these options could shift how some departments staff and structure their response to those calls.
ai_sections.cost: The bill draws on existing federal COPS grant funds already available to local departments; no new funding source is created or specified in the summary.

title: Elementary and Secondary School Counseling Act
ai_summary:
This bill would create a federal grant program to help hire more mental health professionals in high-need public schools. The federal government would send money to states, and states would then pass that money to local school districts to recruit and keep counselors, psychologists, and social workers. The goal is to reach recommended staffing levels, such as no more than 250 students per school counselor or 250 students per social worker. The bill would authorize $5 billion for the first year, with more funding in future years. States would need to chip in 20 percent of their grant amount using their own funds.

The program focuses specifically on high-need schools, which are generally schools that serve more low-income students and face greater challenges. School districts that serve the most high-need schools would get priority when states hand out the money. Native American schools run by the Bureau of Indian Education and schools in U.S. territories like Puerto Rico and Guam would also receive a small share of the funding directly. Districts would have to report each year on how many mental health staff they hired and what the student-to-counselor ratios look like.

The people most directly affected would be students at high-need public elementary and secondary schools across the country, who could gain greater access to counselors and other mental health support. School districts and states would also be affected, since they would need to apply for the grants and meet reporting requirements. Mental health professionals such as school counselors, psychologists, and social workers could see more job opportunities in schools if the bill becomes law and funding is approved.
ai_headline: Federal bill would fund mental health staff at high-need schools
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: S 4618 would send $5B in year one to states to hire counselors and mental health staff at high-need public schools.
ai_sections.what: S 4618 would create a federal grant program funding states to recruit and retain counselors, psychologists, and social workers in high-need public schools. States would distribute the money to local school districts, with priority given to districts serving the most high-need schools. Target staffing ratios include no more than 250 students per school counselor or per social worker.
ai_sections.who: Students at high-need public elementary and secondary schools would be most directly affected, along with the school districts and states that apply for grants and meet reporting requirements. Mental health professionals such as counselors, psychologists, and social workers could see expanded job opportunities in schools.
ai_sections.why: Districts receiving funds would be required to report annually on mental health staff hired and student-to-counselor ratios. Schools serving Native American students through the Bureau of Indian Education and schools in U.S. territories such as Puerto Rico and Guam would receive a direct share of funding under the bill.
ai_sections.cost: The bill authorizes $5 billion for the first year, with additional funding in future years. States must contribute 20 percent of their grant amount using their own funds.
costChips: States must match 20% with own funds
More funding authorized in future years

title: SCREEN for Type 1 Diabetes Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill directs the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) to run a nationwide public awareness campaign about type 1 diabetes. The campaign would teach people about the early warning signs of type 1 diabetes, explain how and where to get screened, and share information about managing the disease after a diagnosis. The CDC would spread this information through TV, radio, social media, the internet, and trusted community figures. Materials would be created in multiple languages and tailored to different communities, especially those seeing the fastest rise in type 1 diabetes cases.

The bill also provides grants to state, local, and tribal public health departments to help them connect people in their communities with screening services. Schools, colleges, community health centers, and doctors' offices would all receive educational materials. The CDC would be required to work with medical organizations, schools, nonprofits, and community groups when designing the campaign to make sure the information is accurate and effective. Congress would receive a progress report within one year of the law taking effect.

This bill would most directly affect people who are at risk for type 1 diabetes or who have not yet been diagnosed, as well as their families. It would also impact doctors, school staff, and community health workers who would receive new educational resources. The program is authorized to spend up to five million dollars per year from 2027 through 2031.
ai_headline: CDC would run national type 1 diabetes awareness drive under Senate bill
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

ai_sections.tldr: Senate bill S 4612 directs the CDC to run a national type 1 diabetes awareness campaign funded at up to $5M per year from 2027 to 2031.
ai_sections.what: S 4612 directs the CDC to conduct a nationwide public awareness campaign covering early warning signs, screening locations, and disease management for type 1 diabetes. The campaign would reach the public through TV, radio, social media, the internet, and trusted community figures, with materials in multiple languages tailored to communities with the fastest-rising case rates. The bill also provides grants to state, local, and tribal public health departments to connect residents with screening services.
ai_sections.who: People at risk for or not yet diagnosed with type 1 diabetes, and their families, would be most directly affected. Doctors, school staff, and community health workers would also receive new educational resources, as would schools, colleges, community health centers, and doctors' offices.
ai_sections.why: Requiring the CDC to coordinate with medical organizations, schools, nonprofits, and community groups is intended to ensure the campaign's information is accurate and effective. Congress would receive a progress report within one year of the law taking effect, creating a formal accountability checkpoint.
ai_sections.cost: The program is authorized to spend up to five million dollars per year from 2027 through 2031.

title: ASSIMILATION Act
ai_summary:
This bill, called the ASSIMILATION Act, would make major changes to how the United States handles legal immigration. It would eliminate the diversity visa lottery, which currently gives 55,000 green cards per year to people from countries with low immigration rates to the US. It would also cut back on family-based immigration, so that US citizens could no longer sponsor parents, adult children, or siblings for green cards. Instead, parents could only come on a temporary visitor-type visa with no path to permanent residence. The bill would shift the system toward a "merit-based" model, where work visas go mainly to people who earn high salaries, work in certain needed occupations, or have exceptional skills. H-1B work visas would be capped at 50,000 per year, and the wage requirement for those jobs would be raised significantly. The bill would also end Optional Practical Training, the program that lets international students work in the US after graduation.

The bill would make it harder to become a US citizen. The waiting period for naturalization would double from five years to ten years. Applicants would need to show a higher level of English ability, prove they paid all their taxes, and show they did not receive most government benefits during that time. The bill would also redefine birthright citizenship so that children born in the US would only automatically be citizens if at least one parent is a citizen or a legal permanent resident.

The bill would affect several other groups as well. People seeking asylum would face tougher rules, including a bar for those who passed through another country on the way to the US without first seeking protection there. Sponsors who bring immigrants to the US would need to earn twice the poverty level and post a $20,000 bond. People who overstay their visas could face criminal charges and fines. The bill would also limit the president's ability to create broad immigration relief programs, such as deferred action policies, without approval from Congress.
ai_headline: ASSIMILATION Act would cut family visas, double naturalization wait to 10 years
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

ai_sections.tldr: The ASSIMILATION Act would overhaul legal immigration by cutting family visas, ending the diversity lottery, and doubling the naturalization waiting period to 10 years.
ai_sections.what: The ASSIMILATION Act would eliminate the diversity visa lottery, which currently issues 55,000 green cards per year, and end family-based sponsorship for parents, adult children, and siblings of US citizens. It would shift toward a merit-based work visa system, cap H-1B visas at 50,000 per year, end Optional Practical Training for international students, and redefine birthright citizenship to apply only when at least one parent is a citizen or legal permanent resident.
