title: Scratch Cooked Meals for Students Act of 2026
ai_summary:
This bill would create a pilot grant program run by the U.S. Department of Agriculture to help school districts prepare more meals from scratch using fresh, unprocessed ingredients instead of pre-packaged or heavily processed foods. The program would award competitive grants to school food authorities, meaning the local agencies that manage cafeteria programs. Each grant would last two school years, and up to $20 million per year could be approved for the program from 2027 through 2031. Schools that receive grants could use the money to train cafeteria staff, buy new kitchen equipment, update facilities, pay workers for the extra labor involved, and educate students about food through things like taste tests and cooking lessons.

Not all schools would have equal chances of receiving a grant. The program gives top priority to districts where the most students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, meaning lower-income communities would be first in line. Priority is also given to school districts that run their own food service rather than outsourcing it to a private company, and to districts where cafeteria workers have or are working toward a union contract or similar labor agreement. Schools that do not yet meet those conditions can still qualify if they commit to meeting them before the grant ends.

This bill mainly affects public school students, school cafeteria workers, and school administrators across the country. Students in lower-income districts stand to benefit the most since those schools are prioritized. Cafeteria employees could see changes in their job duties, training opportunities, and potentially their wages. The bill is currently in the early stages and has only been introduced in the Senate, so it has not become law yet.
ai_headline: Senate bill would fund scratch cooking in school cafeterias
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: A Senate bill would give USDA grants to schools to cook fresh meals from scratch, prioritizing low-income districts, with up to $20M per year available.
ai_sections.what: Senate bill S 4599 would create a pilot grant program at the U.S. Department of Agriculture to help school districts shift from pre-packaged or heavily processed foods to meals made from scratch using fresh, unprocessed ingredients. Grants would last two school years each and could fund staff training, kitchen equipment, facility updates, additional labor costs, and student food education. The program would run from 2027 through 2031 and has only been introduced in the Senate so far.
ai_sections.who: The program would affect public school students, cafeteria workers, and school administrators nationwide. Students in lower-income districts would be first in line, as the program gives top priority to schools where the most students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch.
ai_sections.why: Cafeteria workers could see changes in job duties, training opportunities, and potentially wages. Districts that outsource food service to private companies or lack union contracts for cafeteria workers would receive lower priority unless they commit to changing those conditions before the grant ends.
ai_sections.cost: Up to $20 million per year could be approved for the program, covering the years 2027 through 2031.

title: GROUSE Act of 2026
ai_summary:
The GROUSE Act would create a new federal program that pays private landowners to restore wildlife habitat on their forest land. If a landowner's forest was recently logged, is currently being logged, or was damaged by a natural disaster like a wildfire or tornado, they can apply to sign a contract with the U.S. Department of Agriculture. Under that contract, the government would cover 75 percent of the costs to plant native trees, shrubs, and other vegetation that benefits upland birds and wildlife, such as grouse, quail, and similar species. The contracts last up to five years, and landowners must work with a wildlife biologist or forester to create a habitat restoration plan.

There are limits on how much land qualifies. A landowner can only receive payments for up to 250 acres or 5 percent of their eligible forest land, whichever is smaller. The land must also be at least 10 acres and must be privately owned, non-industrial forest land. The government can also provide hands-on technical help to landowners who need guidance carrying out their restoration plan.

This bill mainly affects small and mid-sized private forest landowners across the country, particularly in rural areas where logging and natural disasters are common. It would also benefit hunters, wildlife enthusiasts, and conservation groups who care about upland bird populations. The program would be run through the USDA and built into the existing framework of the Food Security Act of 1985, which already manages several farmland and conservation programs.
ai_headline: Federal program would pay landowners to restore bird habitat on logged forests
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

ai_sections.tldr: The GROUSE Act would fund 75% of habitat restoration costs for private forest owners whose land was logged or disaster-damaged, up to 250 acres.
ai_sections.what: The GROUSE Act would create a federal program paying private forest landowners to restore wildlife habitat on recently logged, actively logged, or disaster-damaged land. The government would cover 75 percent of costs to plant native trees, shrubs, and vegetation that supports upland birds like grouse and quail. Contracts last up to five years and require a habitat restoration plan developed with a wildlife biologist or forester.
ai_sections.who: The program applies to small and mid-sized private, non-industrial forest landowners, particularly in rural areas. Hunters, wildlife enthusiasts, and conservation groups focused on upland bird populations would also be affected.
ai_sections.why: Upland bird and wildlife populations in logged or disaster-affected forests could increase as native vegetation is restored under government-funded contracts. The program would be administered through the USDA within the existing framework of the Food Security Act of 1985.
ai_sections.cost: The federal government would pay 75 percent of restoration costs per contract. Payments are limited to no more than 250 acres or 5 percent of a landowner's eligible forest land, whichever is smaller, and the parcel must be at least 10 acres.
costChips: Max 250 acres or 5% of eligible land
